### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-02T02:59:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61466>

For goodness sake people, we can talk about bodily discharge etc - but we seem to shy away from politics.

I'll open.

I hate both candidates. I voted for Romney in mail in for the sheer sake of change. I would have voted with enthusiasm for Ron Paul. I think our elected officials are are utter failures for not fighting to change the electoral and party system.

Our system prevents the best leaders and candidates from rising to actually be on the ballot.

I also think you vote does not always count. I probably haven't voted in most elections as objectively speaking my vote was pointless. Why bother.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-02T13:45:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61506>

Ok I'll bite. As I've gotten older my conservative views have skewed more toward the center and I suddenly have found my self sitting in the libertarian camp. However I can't fully back a libertarian candidate b/c my job is tied to supporting the DOD & military.

So considering there's a high probability ill be in a precarious job position in the next 6 mths courtesy of Obama's proposed budget for the next 4yrs (yes I've seen it b/c we're programming for it right now) and the proposed personnel cuts in the DoD the dc metro area is looking at a jump in unemployment if govt contractors by close to 40%. Yeah 40%. What ppl don't realize is that there is a 3rd economy that's getting ready to take a nose dive & it impacts all 50 states & the 4 US territories

I do not like Romney at all but he's my best chance for keeping my job

Johnson & Obama have nothing to offer me & my family economically so I'm resigned to vote for some one who I don't like or respect. But then again I feel the same way about POTUS & Johnson

If I weren't in a precarious economic position I'd write in John Gault & Howard Roark

### BJK77 — 2012-11-02T14:06:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61507>

Politics never makes me uncomfortable. The fact that some people can't carry on a conversation with differing opinions about issues shows how clearly arrogant many in our society are.

I consider myself to be an independent. I refuse to tow any party line. I think it forces you to be narrow minded. Anyone who is a staunch party member will overlook any fault of their candidate because it's all about winning, not about choosing the 'best' person. And when I say 'best' I'm referring to an individual's character, not only their position on the issues. There are issues in which I agree more with the position of the Democrats and there are issues where I tend to side with the Republicans. I have voted both Democrat and Republican in my life and have never made my decision based on one or two issues alone. How someone can vote for a pro-life / pro-choice candidate purely because of their position without looking at that person's honesty and integrity is beyond me.

There are, of course, certain issues that rank higher for me, but overall I base my decision more on my assumption of where the individual's heart is - their honesty, integrity, character and the people they choose to surround themselves with. Unfortunately that eliminates 99% of the politicians out there from being a good choice for me

I would have loved to vote for Ron Paul. He's the only one that has the balls to truly take a stand and stick with it. And the only one with the balls to say we screwed up this country and need to go back to the traditional values we were founded on. Since my vote for Paul wouldn't mean squat, my next choice would be Johnson; again, that vote won't mean squat. I'd love to say I need to vote for him just because the other two are dimwits, which I believe, but based on the latest Benghazi incident and many others, I can never in good conscience vote for Obama. He has yet to show me that he has even the capability of being honest and forthright, let alone the intelligence to surround himself with good people.

### Michael — 2012-11-02T15:17:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61515>

> **Jack Kruse;63227 wrote:** The president is like the Queen of England except with a penis........and I got a feeling she is more of man than either one of these two candidates.

I should think Prince Charles would be pretty much in favour of the ethos of Epipaleo.

He has a strong and abiding interest in the countryside and in organic farming and gardening. The socialist TV satire puppet show _Spitting Image_ back in the 80s used to satirize him by having him talk to plants, which tells you something about his interests.

He's also an acomplished watercolourist, but his interest in the countryside isn't prissy in the way that might imply. He's the kind of bloke who'd spend hours stalking stags on the moors in bad weather; he also broke his nose playing rugby; and his skiing approaches foolhardy.

He currently has a considerable business selling organic food through the Waitrose supermarket chain (part of teh John Lewis partnership). I don't think the venture's primarily about making money, or even about making the food he thinks we should all eat available, so much as it's about ensuring that the land is farmed in the way he thinks it should be.

He also got together with architects to build a "model village" at Pundbury in Dorset, using local materials and traditional designs of building in harmony with the landscape. He's taken a fair bit of abuse for that from the people who make their living selling brutalist architecture to the rest of us. Someone tried to click the classwar button by saying he wanted "neo-classical for the nobs and pitched roofs for the common folk". That kind of rhetoric writes itself, doesn't it? I think one can probably buy it by the yard somewhere.

Charles also seems spiritually open - he's the kind of man who would be a deep and sincere member of the Anglican communion and happy to take part in the traditional forms of worship of his church, but who would also be interested in what the Dalai Lama was saying. He was a great friend of Laurens van der Post, who was interested in Jungian psychology and ethnography - in fact Charles is, like Laurens, something of a defender of the Bushmen.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-02T17:42:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61557>

Voting is tricky and involves some interesting ethical dilemmas. Do you do what is good for your personally or for society in general? Do you vote for short-term impact or for solutions to long-term issues? The biggest problem I see with our system (at least president/congress/senate) is that once they get in office, they will do anything to stay and be re-elected. Any desire to work for the good of the country goes out of their heads.

I don't think it really matters whether Obama or Romney is elected this go-round. Neither one will be a true leader who gets us to focus on long-term problems (climate change, national debt, widespread human-caused poor health, social security/medicare) which are going to destroy us in a matter of years. To fix those problems will require someone to give us the straight, unvarnished truth and persuade us to sacrifice. Ain't nobody gonna want to sacrifice, especially if they think some other guy is getting a better deal.

The only solutions that will work are local. [http://www.ellenlaconte.com/life-rules-the-book](http://http://www.ellenlaconte.com/life-rules-the-book)

Cavemam...i thought nonchalant was banned too! I was totally perplexed as to what transpired that I couldn't see on my phone.

I could be wrong but I really think the tide is turning. So many people my grandfathers age were hard line repubs or dems, no in between.

I know a few holdouts my age who claim democracy, but the large majority are libertarian or constitutionalist. They may vote repub for lack of a third option for certain things, but I think the days of the two party system really are gone. We just have to get the mechanics caught up to the population. How the mechanics would be designed for that tpe of "no party system" is to much for my little brain at the moment.

This may be a regional thing, it's just my observation. We are very rural here, and the recession didn't really hit as hard as it did elsewhere, mostly because we were already depressed. Once it did hit though it was with a vengeance because nearly everyone is or works for small business. Family farms and the like.

On a totally unrelated note...went to see Argo last night....once again, just like after watching The Stoning of Soraya M, I am so very, very thankful to live in this country. I worry about getting enough seafood and too much light after dark...they worry about far more. It's a good show though. Not really an Affleck fan, but he did a good job.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-03T18:07:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61791>

> **PrimalPam;63518 wrote:** I voted "Not Obama" too. I'd have voted for a 3rd party candidate, but my problem in doing so is that I'm afraid it just takes away votes from the main parties. Since the likelihood of a 3rd party getting elected is so slim, I stay within the 2 major parties so that one of them at least has a clear chance of winning.
>
>
>
> I really wish there'd be a greater depth and breadth to the Libertarian party though...
>
>
>
> In any event, I do feel blessed that we CAN vote.

IMO, the way the country is going won't change much whether we elect a democrat or republican. Neither will work on the big, gnarly, long-term problems. Voting third-party sends a strong message that the electorate is dissatisfied with the "two" choices. Imagine, if all the people who don't like either major candidate voted third party, we might actually get some attention. There won't be revolutionary change at first, but if we could get 10-15% this election, then more people would be emboldened next time around. However, if we keep choosing the lesser of two evils, that's what we'll end up with -- evil.

It's all smoke and mirrors, that's the thing. It's just like selling cars. There's the absolute bottom line of what the car actually cost the dealer, but everything above and beyond that, all the financing and "rebates" and "incentives" (oh hai, stimulus anyone?!) are just creative accounting. I am an accountant for a living (dealing with the government, funnily enough). You can bet your sweet bippy that when the gov't comes calling and wants to audit, we have to have all our i's dotted and our t's crossed...but when it comes to the American people wanting to know where our tax dollars are going? Oh, it's far too complicated. We could never understand. \*eye-roll\*

edited to remove a bunch of specifics about my job that would support my argument but that might get me in trouble, and since I need my job to, you know, buy fish and bacon and 99% Lindt bars, I figure it's better to not run my mouth. For once.

I'm pro-life & I support a woman's right to choose abortion

I support looser gun control

I support gay rights

I'm a staunch supporter of Israel and their ability to defend themselves against Iran and other extremist Islamic states

Climate change - not sold when you look at the geological record - Mother Nature will correct it when she feels it is time

Reducing the deficit will require ppl to give up entitlements, cutting programs & some tax hikes

Equal pay will never happen by force in the private sector. The only way it's going to happen is when more women shun traditional rolls and break the glass ceiling. Where it is a big problem is at lower level jobs that aren't governed by minimum wage

There are some issues that the moral right & moral left will never agree upon and from my perspective should be left out of politics completely

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-04T15:27:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61954>

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-04T15:34:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61956>

> **Anna K.;63725 wrote:** Agree, that's the problem, nobody wants to compromise. We need both, increase the taxes and reduce the spending.
>
>
>
> And military spending and military jobs is like and entitlement program too. We still have soldiers in Europe and Japan. Why? We don't need to spend more then the next 10 countries combined on military, especially traditional military like tanks, for example.

Economic security is tied to national security, which is tied directly to military strategy - which includes overseas bases. Here's the interesting thing - in anticipation of the withdrawl from both Iraq and Afganistan the DOD has initiated a draw down of all personnel - this hasn't been directed by POTUS - this is the DOD seeing the changing environment. and the need to change how it operates. additionally several of the overseas bases have been slotted to close w/in the next couple of years - as a result of the last BRAC in the mid 2000's. If you want to talk about tanks - the Army has requested permission to get rid of thousands of tanks - which would save $Billions in storage, and maintenance costs - but congress wont let them - and these aren't even the current tanks in use - they're Korean WAr and Vietnam area tanks. The Army is the only service that doesn't have a major weapon system upgrade since their last effort was canceled and rightly so - Yet the air force has purchased 2 different fighters - made by the same company that do the exact same job? Yeah Fraud, Waste and Abuse IMO -and I'm former Air Force. Is all of this necessary? probably not

but can we pull back to the isolationism of pre - WW1? unfortunately not - we entered a slippery slope when Clinton established the US Military as a police force for the world back in 1993. Whats sad is the politicians haven't studied history - we've just relearned the lessons of Vietnam - and only one man understood what we're doing: Gen Patreaus. We continue to make the same mistakes - because we forget to look to our past. you cant have a successful and secure economy w/o a well thought out national security strategy, that includes military. Are there ways to do that w/o DOD being a bloated beast - yes - but you have to remove the entrenched leadership that's use to running DOD on contingency budgets....

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-04T15:47:17+0200

my fathers family is morman, however, my grandfather left the faith b/c of the injustice he saw against others. The requirement to go on a mission is for personal growth and devotion to your own faith - and great if you can convert others. Regardless of faith - there will always be people attempting to convert you. that's their intent/focus - you can't let that rule your life.

as for the SCJ - these men and women - while they may have certain political leanings, their job is to evaluate a case against LAW, not impose their personal morals on a case. while yes those morals may influence their perspective - it all comes down to wether a law stands within constitutionality or not. The SC is responsible for keeping both the President and Congress in Check - while we may not like their decisions - they are made w/impartially based upon where something stand w/respect to constitutional law

### Michael — 2012-11-04T18:12:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62020>

> **ealachan;63614 wrote:** I did say I was a socialist...that's about as liberal as it gets, innit? LOL

Not as different as it might seem. LOL

Just flicking around seeing what was on the site wasting time. I'm not going to argue about U.S. politics, which mean nothing to me. But here's something of more general interest.

So these are both modern ideologies and both based on abstract absolutes, right? In the case of the first it's _liberty; in the case of the second, _equality_.

Get a mental image - a load of people standing around. Each has a circle around him. That's the area of his individual liberty: no-one can step inside that without violating his freedom.

But hang on ... each circle must be the same_ size or each person's liberty can't really be guaranteed, so the concept of liberty already contains the concept of equality.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-06T22:03:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62420>

I don't think it's simply a woman's rights issue. It's a woman's symbiotic lifeline to another person. There is not clarity with the majority or science on when "life" starts and if or if not that organism is as valuable to the society as a POTENTIAL sentient being vs an actual sentient being.

I think it's re-opening as an issue, because all the children of the first generation living with legalized abortion are now having children. For many, that simply changes the value they put upon that potential sentient being. It did for me, and it's a very common switch of POV. I was all for abortions right up until I saw that 1st ultrasound.

It's not a question on IF society is ok with eliminating a sentient being (war), but simply a fight over the circumstances in which that is o.k.

Until we perfect the electronclouderator that can pinpoint if or if not a mass of cells is hooked up to that thing called life, arguing if it's life or not is a straw man.

We are ok with taking life, so that is irrelevant.

We need to determine if we are ok with doing it to protect the freedom of a singular woman.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-06T22:06:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62421>

> **Anna K.;64233 wrote:**
>
>
>
> Remember the mandatory ultrasounds that you have to do before the procedure?

This one doesn't seem that draconian to me. Either it is life or it isn't. If you are getting an abortion, it's because you are either of the mind that it isn't - or you understand that you are taking a life to preserve yours. Educating people (the mothers and fathers) is something that society does everywhere for the sake of the social order. God knows we have to sit through all kinds of emotional drivel growing up as the price to pay for admission to the tribe.

What is it about this that seems so out of line?

And, to add - I'm not asking because I already KNOW an answer and just looking to give it. I'm asking because you are a very reasonable, cogent and logical poster and I'm very interested!

### ealachan — 2012-11-06T22:16:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62423>

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-06T22:34:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62425>

> **ealachan;64237 wrote:** I'm sorry, but if that's NOT a woman's rights issue, I don't know what the hell WOULD be.

If the government were trying to take away your ability to vote because you are a woman.

If the government were trying to prevent you from driving because you are a woman.

Society gives the above by it's wholly man-made, sometimes arbitrary, fabricated construct. Nature, not society gave women the child bearing end of the stick. The ability to have a clean and safe abortion is part and parcel of the fabricated societal construct - thus subject to it's whims.

I'm against it for me. I would do everything I could to educate friends and loved ones on what they are doing - but I think we sacrifice potential all the time for much lesser causes than a particular woman's freedoms, so it doesn't make sense to prevent this procedure.

Of course, before I had kids - I used to think "the damn things don't even have a consciousness until about age 6, so I'm for 12th trimester abortions". Thankfully, one of the wisdoms life has taught me is there is rarely a black and white answer, and it's the fool that doesn't learn what those who blazed the trail before them are willing to share. When I have not had a particular experience, the next best thing to do is learn from those that have.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T15:00:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62505>

I went to bed very early last night because I knew what was coming. Instead of fighting against the grain in medicine the die is now cast. The world in which I play is forever changed and it matters little what I think about it any longer. The people have spoken. But one thing is crystal clear now that no party can deny based upon what is coming in 2014. We must rely on us.......and our ability to adapt now that the country has spoken. The ability to attain greatness and make forward progress with positive transformative change dies on the vine the moment we try to emulate someone else. Be you, the world needs your insight. Since our country wants mediocrity in healthcare........we need to strive for optimal in ourselves to overcome that obstacle. The rut for patients just got deeper. So it means your personal education about health is now paramount to avoiding what is coming.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-07T17:26:04+0200

I think that one is changing fast. There will probably be plenty born in the next 50 years that are much hardier with our neolithic conditions.

Plus, information is now traveling into our consciousness at such an accelerated rate - I think we don't know how that is going to wire the brains of the future.

An example: Our childhood is remembered through a multi-sensory combo of feelings plus the rare random photo. Children of today, in the small families and hover-parents who document their every word and 1st crap are SURELY going to be a completely different psychological/socioloical beast. I think there is a new world coming. It should be fascinating - and I'm a little excited.

### kathylu — 2012-11-07T18:12:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62569>

Jack, the paradigm is not shifting fast enough to prevent the oncoming health disaster that awaits most people. Our children and grandchildren may stand a chance. The current generation...not so much. That is independent of proposed changes in the heath care system. It's not like the entire world will wake up tomorrow and realize that "Gee, it sounds good to go for a swim in the frozen lake after my brekkie of 2 dozen oysters in seaweed and duck fat. Then I'll check my supply of candles for tonight because I gave up all artificial light since that 2012 AMA position statement that no one else paid attention to. Then after an hour or two of meditation, yoga and my twice weekly sprints, I'll walk (barely clothed) through the snow (checking my game traps along the way) to my 4 hour low-stress creative job hand carving dulcimers. Boy, I'm glad I gave up that 80 hour per week investment banking job to adopt an ancestral lifestyle!"

Flippancy aside, managing the results of the rampant neolithic diseases occurring may be the best most people will be able to hope for for the next 30 years. Until what you are telling us slips into conventional consciousness, if it ever does. Those of us who know will survive and teach our children, families and friends. Unfortunately, I fear it will be painfully slow.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-08T00:51:49+0200

people are responsible for themselves, and fully capable of making their own decisions. Why do some insist on making the choices for others?

peer regulation is far more effective than state control, as well as being broader in scope.

### Inger — 2012-11-08T01:17:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62739>

It is just like in Finland.

Here you will need to wait half a year to get you caries filled in your teeth if not doing it privately and paying all by yourself. The same with other health care. In comparison to Hamburg (Germany) where I lived before, the Doctors here know very little. And you need to wait... long. Very long.

To me all this matters little, because I took my health in my own hands long ago.

It only makes me more aware, how important it is not to be dependent on the heath care. So I do all I can not to be.

The Russian, that often come to eat/visit as tourists in the restaurant I work in, they are a different species. They are used to have to rely on their own. I can sense how different they are. I like it. They are used to have to take care of their own.

One Russian friend of my parents told me visiting here a week ago.. His mom grew up in Sibiria (Northern Russia). And when she had a cold or was sick, she always got into the ice cold lake, submerging her whole body. The next day she was healthy again. He/she did not knew anything about CT or the science behind. They just knew out of experience, it worked.

The Russian people have a lot of old wisom still. Because they have no other choise. No hospitals or doctors around.

I have seen where that leads to, especially when doctors are not professional (wich is too common here sadly). One family friend died last spring of lyme desease. A number of doctors have stopped accepting Medicare patients. My bro-in-law had a heck of a time finding someone. The doc we saw (who we paid cash) said Medicare pays him 25% of his fee. Every year, Congress threatens to cut Medicare spending, but backs down. Ain't gonna happen. The best part is they tell us they have suddenly, magically, found $500 Billion (that would be $500,000 million to those playing at home) in fraud that they will now do something about. Why did it have to come to this before they decided to tackle fraud? Because it is the government and government generally does nothing efficiently. For proof, visit your local DMV and try to get in and out in 30 minutes. More like 3 hours around here. How in the world can the same number of doctors serve 60 million more patients?

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T02:09:49+0200

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T02:15:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62777>

> **Anna K.;64586 wrote:** how is that possible?
>
>
>
> they will force you to take statins?
>
>
>
> ban you from eating fish?
>
>
>
> what exactly are you afraid of?

Ask the people in New York who can't order their own health tests. People in Canada can't get a full thyroid panel. Doctors few and far between in New Zealand. With 60 million more patients with "insurance" and no more doctors, I foresee a crisis. More docs are already declining to take Medicare patients, others are fully self-pay and you're on your own to file a claim with your carrier. We are creating a divide in health care - the wealthy will simply pay out of pocket and get immediate service and everybody else will get in line and hope/pray to see someone.

Part of the problem is that people have lost the meaning of insurance. They now want "health maintenance" - pay a monthly fee and get all the care & services you want at no cost. No incentive to take care of yourself when you can just go to the doc and get loaded up with free meds.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T04:10:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62808>

> **Anna K.;64620 wrote:** LOL DMV is run by the local government, so blame your neighbors...
>
> in my place it's 30 min...

OK, let's try another example - USPS (and don't say it's not a government entity; its budget and rules are controlled by Congress). Ever been in there, waiting in a long line, only to find it's time for one of the clerks to go on break so the wait gets even longer. Another example - Social Security - lines are long there. That kind of service is typical from a government agency. You might also get that with a private company but at least in that case, you can vote with your feet and go somewhere else.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T04:14:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62809>

> **Anna K.;64646 wrote:** my point is republicans blame the Federal gov all the time, saying local will be better, well, state is local

Certainly state is more local than federal but I agree with the premise that lobbying your local agency (city, county) is far easier than battling the state which in turn is easier than going up against the Feds. That's all playing out here. A local group of citizens got a referendum on the city election which banned fracking within the city, going against state regulations which determine where it will be permitted. State voters also voted to legalize pot which of course goes against federal law. I'd much rather take my chances lobbying my friends and neighbors than trying to get through to a bunch of career politicians in DC.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-08T04:27:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62813>

> **Anna K.;64630 wrote:** how is obmacare = no longer have control over my health???
>
>
>
> they will force you to take statins?
>
>
>
> ban you from eating fish?
>
>
>
> what exactly are you afraid of?

I have options today b/c I could leave the VA and find a progressive doc who still takes insurance in the DCMetro area. Before Obama care passed close to 60% of docs in our area stopped taking insurance - I noticed this migration began back in 2004. It's even harder now to find docs that take ins those same docs are also not taking in new patients

So if no one take your insurance or will take you on as a new patient w/o insurance . You get kaiser or Tricare /VA medical care where they only run basic tests & if your labs look normal even if you have symptoms of say hypothyroid you get told your ok and move along If you don't fit in their box of how they are told to practice medicine - you will find its hard to get appointments you'll be flagged as a problem patient If you do get a dx your only medication options are synthetic hormones. If you have asthma they won't try to determine what causes it or how to help prevent attacks but send you home w/inhaled steroids & tell you you'll die if you don't take them. If you have migraines they give you triptans even though your medical records indicate you don't tolerate the drugs.

Im guessing you've never been seriously ill Or have bad health in general I hope you stay healthy, never have to have a medicalized birth (b/c in a medicalized l&d ward in the us the average c-sect rat is close to 40% and climbing) or have a disease that kaiser/Tricare/VA determine its too expensive to consider a drug to treat it or too outside the box

Come see me in 20 yrs after you've run the gaulent of Obama care. We can compare notes about our experiences in a system that doesn't value n=1.

Your sadly mistaken if you think the government dictating health care will lead to better care and greater market options.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-08T07:33:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62838>

> **Lyndra;64616 wrote:** Ask the people in New York who can't order their own health tests. People in Canada can't get a full thyroid panel. Doctors few and far between in New Zealand. With 60 million more patients with "insurance" and no more doctors, I foresee a crisis. More docs are already declining to take Medicare patients, others are fully self-pay and you're on your own to file a claim with your carrier. We are creating a divide in health care - the wealthy will simply pay out of pocket and get immediate service and everybody else will get in line and hope/pray to see someone.
>
>
>
> Part of the problem is that people have lost the meaning of insurance. They now want "health maintenance" - pay a monthly fee and get all the care & services you want at no cost. No incentive to take care of yourself when you can just go to the doc and get loaded up with free meds.

Upfront confession: I have not read the ObamaCare document and I'm not going to.

From far away on the other side of the world, it seems that the problem isn't 'socialised' medicine vs 'capitalist' medicine but that you've voted for a monolithic mindset about medicine. Everyone will get the same thing, in the same way. Free thinking outside the system - by patient or doctor - will not only be discouraged (as it already was) but will be mostly impossible. Doctors are shifting into job-keeping/a\*\*-covering mode and patients must follow along if they are going to get treatment at all. So 'everyone' gets healthcare, but no one gets healthy.

My DH goes to the VA for medical care. He's had 2 heart attacks, and has injuries from Vietnam that still bother him today. VA care is not perfect, but he is very satisfied with his care there. ### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T19:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62939>

> **Anna K.;64665 wrote:** while I agree that USPS sucks and need to be changed, the question is why do you go there? you have competition, fedex, ups, etc... why use the government run agency that sucks???
>
>
>
> I disagree with the premise that everything gov run automatically sucks, and private rules. I've had many bad experiences with private enterprises as well.

Only USPS will deliver letters to my house. USPS is the only option when sending to a PO box. I won't say all government is bad and all private enterprise is good. Nothing is that black & white. However, after spending 25 years employed by the state, I saw all the bad that comes with a highly regimented system. Hiring & firing, procurement, all with ridiculous layers of bureaucracy. Try to hire someone in less than 3 months (unless it's an emergency temp). Try to fire someone unless you have undisputable cause (theft caught on video tapes or child porn on the computer makes it easier). What comes with government is layers and layers of red tape. That can be true in private companies but they generally end up going under (unless of course they get a nice bailout and aren't forced to make necessary changes).

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-08T20:15:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62961>

All I can say is I hope everyone who thinks Obama and Obama care is just wunnerfull is right, and I'm wrong.

I havent seen anything the govt has taken over as better, more effective, or safer (hello fda safety record, I'm looking at you, among others). Regulation is more expensive. Period.

The fact is, we STILL have "KL" capsules being administered, it's just patented KL with a fancy name and a few studies showing that it was marginally more effective than a placebo.

It might kill you slowly and painfully after 8 weeks, but hey, the studies lasted for six weeks, and there were no side effects, so rock on!

Universal health insurance is NOT going to fix anything. Until people buy into their own N=1, and quit expecting a Ferrari performance on Cheetos and Mountain Dew, nothing will change. It will cost more, and be less effective. But when the majority of the country can't explain the difference between a democracy and a republic, and don't know which the US was intended to be, and why, I guess it isn't that surprising after all.

My beef, however, is people blaming that problem on a political party or specific elected official. Jimmy Carter didn't invent Cheetos. The gross mis-education of doctors with regard to nutrition has been going on for decades, and it wasn't any one political party that caused it.

### SueC — 2012-11-08T21:15:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62972>

Conventional medicine has never been great at treating chronic disease, that's up to us to take responsibility for our lives. But I sure would like to be able to get trauma care without being worried about losing my home.

### Stavity — 2012-11-09T13:32:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63101>

I am seeing two specialists and actually finding my experience with them to be better than ever before. No more than 5 minutes wait time all four visits. They are better then average doctors, very efficient and most of all respectful. One doctor even called me to explain one of my tests more thoroughly. Though, I find it strange they look more like business men than doctors--no more white coats. I like it!!!

But, as much as I like them and respect their opinion, I won't be using their treatment. No way, a year of being sick when I am actually starting to feel better than I have in 4 years? Now, how to handle explaining my position without shooting myself in the foot since I am not sure I can turn health around.

> **KiwiLauren;64677 wrote:** ......, it seems that the problem isn't 'socialised' medicine vs 'capitalist' medicine but that you've voted for a monolithic mindset about medicine. Everyone will get the same thing, in the same way. Free thinking outside the system - by patient or doctor - will not only be discouraged (as it already was) but will be mostly impossible. Doctors are shifting into job-keeping/a\*\*-covering mode and patients must follow along if they are going to get treatment at all. So 'everyone' gets healthcare, but no one gets healthy.

this is an excellent point, that adds clarity to otherwise circular 'arguments' that are occurring.

the entire premise of government provided health care being beneficial is that it knows what's 'right' in the first place. the result is medicine that is monolithic and cost efficient rather than efficacious.

Remember that the health of the nation is the concern of government, not the individual health of the patient. There is a big difference between a doctor employed by an individual, and one employed by the government on your behalf.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-11T01:32:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63476>

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-11T01:32:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63477>

The number one reason for bankruptcy is medical expenses. The number one reason for short-sales on houses is medical expenses causing a loss of house.

What I see is although on the surface, looks awful - and to most going through it, it IS awful because of both bankruptcy and losing your house have such social stigma, but in reality it's such a lifesaver of body and spirit for those that go that route because their medical expenses are too high.

I've seen too many people, once they get to the other side of that adventure end up experiencing both a new found sense of value for what's important in life and a place of freedom without any debt on their plate. I've been fortunate enough to help many people through having their house foreclosed or shortselling and almost exclusively a year later after the bankruptcy or foreclosure they are at such peace. It's inspiring really.

so what I see with our current system is this:

1\. we have something in place to deal with trauma and emergency medicine

2\. we have something in place to absolve the individual for the responsibility of that expense. Yes, it's more injury and insult on top of the trauma, but all in all it's better than not having it.

It seems that insurance premiums are really a tax to fund the socialized nature of our current system - and yes the haves get a better experience and benefits.

I do not think medical care should be something given only to those who can afford it. . .I think our country is wealthy enough to cover medical care just as we cover infrastructure items.

The problem with our current system is administrative expenses. . .

So I have not read obama care stuff. .. and all I know of it is the headlines I read. It does look to me like it's just adding yet ANOTHER layer of administrative expense and "pork belly" if you will, which doesn't seem like the right direction to me.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-11T01:36:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63478>

Also, does anybody really expect every person that is a US citizen to get EQUAL quality of care by right? Nothing in nature works that way. No matter what the system, some do better than others. I guarantee every single person reading this is benefiting from being a "have" in the scope of all the humans on earth.

In light of that, I think the 'ideal' would be to provide a base level of care through taxation and let the rest go as nature intended.

### chocolate — 2012-11-11T01:49:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63485>

> **kathylu;65308 wrote:** Yes, but only if you qualify due to income. If you are over the limit (125% of the poverty line?), no coverage. I know, it happened to my sister some years ago (accident, surgery, long recovery, no insurance). She didn't qualify for medicaid, but didn't have enough money to pay. She had to declare bankruptcy because of the unexpected medical bills.

Your sister was discriminated against. The money should be repayable over a long term. My son was dealing with a high deductible for fat blob the size of a bing cherry on the back of his neck. He couldn't believe as a certified bean counter that he couldn't just get a few prices from a few docs about how much it would cost to remove it, at least a ball park (especially since he could send the picture and size on the iphone. We were afraid that he would go to a garage doctor. He didn't pay for anesthesia to get his wisdom teeth out.) Prices should be knowable.

In California, if you have nothing but kids... you are covered and covered very well. If you have insurance, it dictates your care and your copay is non negotiable. They run an asset check before they talk money in collections. i.e. if you piss away your money and time and get injured, you pay nothing. If you have a house or two, they'll take one and let you live in the other till you die. Then they'll take it. It's as messed up as student aid. In other countries, any one can borrow to go to school. In this country, some people borrow, some people free ride. Some people's parents borrow and lose their house. One of my son's friends went to a prestigious art school and finally understood what was wrong with liberalism when he lost one of his friends to just that situation. He was riding on needs based scholarships and grants. His friend was riding on loans and had to quit school to help repay the loans on the house after his dad lost his job. People live in government housing and get a free ride to school as well. If it was a matter of getting what you paid for, maybe people would get really involved. We get what what we pay for. Humans fed like livestock getting the illnesses livestock get when they outlive their growth. And we pay to sustain those illnesses.

### stacyj — 2012-11-11T02:17:47+0200

If you want that, then it is your responsibility to carry enough insurance to provide for a catastrophic event. Check your car insurance med pay, and adjust it accordingly. Universal health care is not the issue here. As an accident victim- most of my care has been covered by my medical insurance, and what has not been has been covered by liens against future settlement. Mine, and my family's medical care is well in excess of 6 figures, not coming close to bankruptcy yet.

### Glamazon — 2012-11-11T02:58:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63514>

I was recently dropped by my health insurance when I lost my job. I am considered uninsureable due to previous co morbidities like high blood pressure and sleep apnea. So I go to the state. I can get very low cost insurance from them with a 10k deductible. Insurance won't cover my cutting edge health needs anyway(epi paleo labs/meds are not covered). All states have plans for the uninsureable. Some won't cover for cancer but just about everything else goes. I just need it for catastrophic reasons. I can get some medicaid coverage for traumatic brain injuries after discharge.

RE: medical payment coverage. In CO, the primary insurer pays first and then sues the auto insurance co. for re-imbursement. I can't remember the name of the particular state statute. Auto ins. generally covers about a week or less in the ICU. People can creep up to a 500k bill in just a few days. We used to have to notify the cost analysis dept when we hit the first 100K. Now, in a short time people can easily peak at 500k. Miracles can happen in the ICU and they are expensive.

I have had patients who have stuck around for several months in the ICU awaiting to meet acute rehab requirements for transfer to a lower level of care. Sometimes the hospital can't transfer because a rehab center won't take a patient until they have medicaid in place. This is after all the other kinds of benefits have run out. It's a mess to navigate. It is horrendous stress on the patient and their family.

We used to be a no fault state but that was overturned several years ago.

>
> Wrens teach their eggs to sing
>
>
>
> Teaching embryos the password for food helps parents avoid having to feed imposters.
>
>
>
> ZoÃ« Corbyn
>
>
>
> 08 November 2012
>
>
>
> Baby fairy wrens won't get fed unless they sing the secret password they learned in the egg.
>
>
>
>
>
> Mothers usually set about teaching their offspring the moment they're born. But the females of one Australian bird can't wait that long.
>
>
>
> Superb fairy-wren (Malurus cyaneus) mothers sing to their unhatched eggs to teach the embryo inside a 'password' — a single unique note — which the nestlings must later incorporate into their begging calls if they want to get fed.
>
>
>
> The trick allows fairy-wren parents to distinguish between their own offspring and those of the two cuckoo species that frequently invade their nests. The female birds also teach their mates the password.
>
>
>
> Fairy-wrens were known to discriminate against cuckoo nestlings on the basis of their foreign begging calls1, says Sonia Kleindorfer, an animal behaviourist at Flinders University in Adelaide, who led the work. But it wasn't known that wren nestlings learned the passwords before hatching.

[http://www.nature.com/news/wrens-teach-their-eggs-to-sing-1.11779](http://http://www.nature.com/news/wrens-teach-their-eggs-to-sing-1.11779)

If you want to see a hateful racist, you should look at the picture of that fairy wren.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-11T05:09:17+0200

### chocolate — 2012-11-11T20:44:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63664>

The argument I was trying to make is that a few of us pay for everyone, no matter what. The prices are not knowable because it is based on your assets. Why isn't there a menu with a price list? The poor doctors try to make up for being cheated by medicare on our backs. Its all craziness. Why can a medical supplier at a hospital get 100 bucks for a cane that costs twenty at Walgreens and a doctor has to fight to get 50 bucks for an office visit while paying a staff of 7?

Why do I get the feeling that the 100 buck cane is still going to be around but the staff of 7 isn't? Its all broken. The best hours for clinics are kept by the pot clinics in California. They are scheduled for the working class. The doctors are pretty good and they are glad to take time with you (so I hear). The professionalism and thoroughness is something talked about. Why is that? Is it because they are actually getting paid a fair price for their time without the huge overhead? Little hard to come up with harm over herb and malpractice.... but then, the something for nothing crowd isn't going to those docs.... Well, actually they are. They are just paying because thats the way the game is played.

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-11-11T20:49:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63666>

Most people on this forum agree that our "health care" system in the US is severely broken. ObamaCare (whether you support or oppose it) is as John Boehner, Speaker of the House, said "is the law of the land".

Most people resist change, even when stuck in a bad rut. ObamaCare represents change. So the logic should be if a system is broken, then change is needed.

Many people who oppose ObamaCare are predicting what will happen. In reality, nobody knows all of what will happen. One of the things that makes the US a great country is the ability to change and move on.

I agree with Cavemam ... we need to accept the past, and move on to the future. **What our health care system will look like in 2014 or 2016 is a story yet to be written.**

I predict lots of creativity with a greater emphasis on alternative methods .... HEY, that sounds like the creative ideas shared on Dr. Kruse's blog and forums. Starfish march on.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-11T20:50:01+0200

> **chocolate;65568 wrote:** The argument I was trying to make is that a few of us pay for everyone, no matter what. The prices are not knowable because it is based on your assets. Why isn't there a menu with a price list? The poor doctors try to make up for being cheated by medicare on our backs. Its all craziness. Why can a medical supplier at a hospital get 100 bucks for a cane that costs twenty at Walgreens and a doctor has to fight to get 50 bucks for an office visit while paying a staff of 7?
>
> Why do I get the feeling that the 100 buck cane is still going to be around but the staff of 7 isn't? Its all broken. The best hours for clinics are kept by the pot clinics in California. They are scheduled for the working class. The doctors are pretty good and they are glad to take time with you (so I hear). The professionalism and thoroughness is something talked about. Why is that? Is it because they are actually getting paid a fair price for their time without the huge overhead? Little hard to come up with harm over herb and malpractice.... but then, the something for nothing crowd isn't going to those docs.... Well, actually they are. They are just paying because thats the way the game is played.

That brooches another factor. Illustrated by medical care vs pot dispensing. Malpractice. It's real. Mistakes mean people die. The greatest thing going now in medicine is the amazing trauma care and sheer number lives saved on a daily basis on things that used to be sure death. Or the tragedy when something SHOULD be a simple/easy fix but due to malpractice someone dies.

I'd have a hard time not killing someone who killed my child due to malpractice - how does one work under those conditions? No room for error. Very few are cut out for it me thinks. This is why I don't begrudge high salaries for the ones that can.

I agree with Joann, the HMO was the beginning of problems with the healthcare system. I thought it was great, at first. I didn't have to pay $10 to see the doctor, the visit was "free". And I didn't have to fill in the forms to get reimbursed for expenses. Now there is no way I could afford to pay for most of the costs in the first place, nevermind reimbursements.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-12T02:12:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63738>

I think many people see "broken" as meaning unaffordable, either in terms of paying for insurance or getting coverage if you have some pre-existing condition and can't get in under a group policy. In my view, it's more a matter that the people are broken. So many people have neolithic diseases that need regular monitoring & medication and are not going to be cured by any CW doctor or pharmaceutical company. It looks to me like we're on a collision course with bankruptcy. It's estimated that caring for one person today with diabetes costs $7K/year. By 2030, it's expected that 1/3 of Americans will be diabetic.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-12T02:15:23+0200

### Lyndra — 2012-11-12T02:20:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63741>

> **Nonchalant;65652 wrote:** I agree with Joann, the HMO was the beginning of problems with the healthcare system. I thought it was great, at first. I didn't have to pay $10 to see the doctor, the visit was "free". And I didn't have to fill in the forms to get reimbursed for expenses. Now there is no way I could afford to pay for most of the costs in the first place, nevermind reimbursements.

Now that I have a high deductible plan, I am woefully aware of the cost of care. A couple of years ago, I had a UTI and went to my doctor's office for a test and Rx for antibiotics. I paid my $30 co-pay and a few weeks later, got bills for over $600, a combination of the doctor's fee, facility fee (they bill separately), and the urine test itself. Had I known the cost in advance, I would have gone to some urgent care place where I bet it would have been way cheaper. I know of no way to get a cost estimate in advance, though.

### LinD — 2012-11-12T03:42:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63758>

This is a FB post from a doctor friend of mine:

Today I attended the opening session of the 2012 AMA Interim Meeting in Honolulu, Hawaii. These opening sessions are largely ceremonial with awards and speeches. Always a bit too self-congratualory for my taste, but some egos need to be stroked.

The two main addresses were from AMA President Dr. Jeremy Lazarus, of Colorado. He emphasized the achievements of the AMA since the last meeting and called on members to continue to stay the course of healthcare reform initiated but the Affordable Care Act. He went on to talk about how physicians should be selfless and consider the greater good of society.

Dr. Lazarus was followed by Dr. Madera, the Executive VP who outlined the strategies of the AMA going forward. He emphasized the need for physicians to consider the economic implications of their actions. At one point he was attempting to explain how better health would improve the economy, something that no one would argue with. However, he challenged physicians with the task of ensuring both individuals' health and the nations economic wellbeing. Near the end of his remarks he used the phrase "physiologic benefit" referring to our patients and "our nations GDP" in the same sentence. He might as well have been reading from the Communist Manifesto. When he concluded his remarks, as is the custom, the Delegate dutifully stood and applauded. All but a few, who like me, sat silent, stunned at what we had just heard.

To cap off this afternoon session the Speaker of the House, Dr. Andy Gurman outlined some specific things that would be coming forward soon for the House to consider. One was the need to "revise" AMA's current Code of Medical Ethics, to make them more relevant to the changing world in which we live.

It seems pretty clear to me that the AMA recognizes that many of their actions actually run counter to the time honored ethics of the medical profession, so rather than reconsider those actions and policies they have simply decided to change the rules to fit their agenda.

Even before today, I was planning on this being my last AMA meeting, but after what I heard this afternoon I'm finding it difficult to even return tomorrow to fulfill my obligation to represent the American Society of General Surgeons. The only thing I can hope to accomplish at this point is to gather more ammunition that can be used by other organizations like Docs4PatientCare and Association of American Physicians and Surgeons to fight back against this obvious assault on American values of Freedom and Liberty.

Just yesterday I started rereading Ayn Rand's "Atlas Shrugged" but I have put it down. Today, I lived it, and many of my colleagues figuratively said "Who is John Galt?"

### Entelechy — 2012-11-12T04:18:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63770>

2\. people come to the US when they can't get services in their own country?

true. people drive from here to the US all the time paying out of pocket for MRI's rather than wait months to get one. Cancer treatment protocols are limited here, and people pay out of pocket to go to the US for treatment. (i.e. 10 times as many options in neoplastic medications) then of course the infamous Shona Holmes fiasco.

3\. the 2000 WHO index is written with a strong political ideology as a bias?

was run by Gro Harlem Brundtland, the prime minister of Norway.

She is clear about the ideological factors she thinks are important: “Where health and responsiveness are concerned, achieving a high average level is not good enough: the goals of a health system must also include reducing inequalities, in ways that improve the situation of the worst-off. In this report attainment in relation to these goals provides the basis for measuring the performance of health systems.”

4\. the US tops out in most categories once the biases are adjusted?

A WHO discussion paper states, “we consider only the distribution, not the level, as there is no consensus on what the level of health spending should be.” Equal results, not necessarily good results, are the focus.

### Glamazon — 2012-11-14T06:36:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-64174>

> **Nonchalant;65748 wrote:** I tried to find the website but failed. I read an account where a woman needed an x-ray, I think for an arm, and she comparison shopped. It took persistence, because the hospital admins didn't really know what to tell her, but she finally got a price, in the ten thousand range I think. Then she called another, said the price that she got, and they quoted a lower price. Like bargaining for a car, she called another and another, and got it down low, like $100 or less.

Hospitals will give discounts, sometimes substantial ones, if you pay in cash up front. Always ask. I have done some auditing for hospital charges. I was amazed at varying prices charged. One day I came across $15k for snake antivenom vial and a particular patient used 10 vials. Also hospital bills are notoriously inaccurate. I have seen more than a few times men charged for a urine pregnancy test and it wasnt because the doc was trying to rule out cancer. Even basic supplies are all screwed up. I have seen hundreds of thousands of dollars of missed or inappropriate or wrongly assigned charges as well.

### ColdBren — 2012-11-14T06:49:36+0200

This works for MRI's Ct scans etc....good intentions just doomed everyone.

I think its only in California that the vet racket is so ridiculous.

### Endless — 2012-11-14T17:10:44+0200

How different is 'ObamaCare' from what we already have here in Canada? I am low income, so I pay nothing for my medical insurance. I pay nothing for any testing or treatment unless it is considered cosmetic or not medically necessary. I do pay for prescriptions. The downsides I can see are difficulty getting certain tests unless your doc is proactive, and sometimes long waiting lists for certain surgeries, notably joint replacements.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-15T06:29:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-64421>

Look everyone, government intervention will ruin every market that it touches. Why? Because human behavior and all the inputs of all the things going on in the world are too complex for them to predict and control. This is assuming that politicians are well intentioned and have your best interest at heart. In reality, they're in someone's pocket and they ain't close to being on your side. If you're a socialist or communist look at China to see how central planning works out. Right here, right now, is where we all need to unite (and I'm not talking geeks and non-geeks). Without government intervention we would be able to form our own networks and associations (if we so chose) to contract for healthcare. In fact, why not start now? Act boldly in the face of this mandate. Get together with your co-workers, family, friends and find the doctors in your area that think this is an abomination too. If you're scared about being able to afford healthcare out of pocket figure out a way to contract with them, as a group, for their services.... This isn't a new idea. If you need inspiration see here: From Mutual Aid to the Welfare State: Fraternal Societies and Social Services, 1890-1967 [http://www.amazon.com/From-Mutual-Aid-Welfare-State/dp/0807848417/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1352952990&sr=8-1&keywords=david+beito](http://http://www.amazon.com/From-Mutual-Aid-Welfare-State/dp/0807848417/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1352952990&sr=8-1&keywords=david+beito)

# Thread: These Aborigines were not hunter-gatherers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/these-aborigines-were-not-hunter-gatherers.4630/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-02T08:43:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/these-aborigines-were-not-hunter-gatherers.4630/post-61499>

I posted this earlier, but it was in the middle of another thread, and maybe it's worth one of its own.

The researcher says that much of the life of the Australian Aborigines remains a mystery. Their way of life has gone, and they kept no records. What we do have is accounts by early white Australians, and the picture they reveal is astounding. They were making use of the fish. But they weren't just fishing opportunistically in a low-tech manner.

They had constructed fish traps from rocks on the river in the Murray-Darling Basin. It also seems they had balks of timber running along the river to channel it. They had built systems of ponds. These, he says, were used as holding areas. (The same as what we'd call a "stew pond" in a medieval monastery, I suppose.) The fish from these were sometimes used for meetings where tribes met to negotiate and socialize.

**The researcher says he had thought of the Aborigines of this area (the Murray-Darling River basin) as hunter-gatherers, but he now believes they were primarily people who managed water resources.**

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;iqANpeOCwGU\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iqANpeOCwGU\[/video\]\[/video\]

More videos on this here:

[http://australianriverrestorationcentre.com.au/mdb/troutcod/index.php](http://http://australianriverrestorationcentre.com.au/mdb/troutcod/index.php)

# Thread: Dietary intake of fish and PUFA, and clinical depressive and anxiety disorders
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-intake-of-fish-and-pufa-and-clinical-depressive-and-anxiety-disorders.4632/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-02T09:12:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-intake-of-fish-and-pufa-and-clinical-depressive-and-anxiety-disorders.4632/post-61501>

Australian study just published in the _British Journal of Nutrition_:

> ... those in the second tertile of DHA intake were nearly 70 % less likely to report a current depressive disorder compared to those in the first tertile. The relationship of DHA to anxiety disorders was linear; for those in the highest tertile of DHA intake, the odds for anxiety disorders were reduced by nearly 50 % after adjustments, including adjustment for diet quality scores, compared to the lowest tertile. Those who ate fish less than once per week had higher GHQ-12 scores, and this relationship was particularly obvious in smokers. **These are the first observational data to indicate a role for DHA in anxiety disorders**, ...

[http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=8711172](http://http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=8711172)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-02T15:25:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-intake-of-fish-and-pufa-and-clinical-depressive-and-anxiety-disorders.4632/post-61521>

The military is taking a lead on this in the USA......with the looming budget cuts in the DOD they need to do something to make soldiers more fit for duty when there is less of them around.

# Thread: 11/2 Tip of the day to save some money and get nutrients:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-02T15:18:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-61517>

Fish cheeks! Get some. I think they are part of the secret sauce of Inger's MHS. They are awesome and generally dirt cheap in oriental/asian stores for making soup. I have been chowing them religiously for 5 years in recipes. In my opinion, it is the best part of meat on the entire fish. Any fish has them but a bigger fish has the bonanza of size. Cheek sashimi rocks.

How to do you get to the cheek? Once the fish is cooked, it will be found in a cavity exactly on the side of the face of the fish where a cheek would be! You can peel back the skin, and there you will find a fish medallion of meat there waiting for you to eat.

### ealachan — 2012-11-02T17:11:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-61550>

I learned the beauty of fish cheeks on a beach in Belize 10 years ago. Fresh-caught red snapper roasted over a fire pit dug in the sand, with coconut husks thrown on the coals as the fish cooked. It was in the ocean literally 20 minutes before it was in my mouth. Best fish I've ever had in my life.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-02T20:36:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-61600>

Fish 'wings' are popular in New Zealand and very cheap... they are basically the bigger fins of the fish (often salmon) which are then typically smoked.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-05T03:01:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-62121>

For now I'm sticking to oysters, clams, mussels, squid, crab, and then whatever fish (loveeeee salmon) and usually basa. These are easy for me to get and I make sure I get the shellfish as the primary source for my meals---since I know these are on the top of the epi-paleo Rx. Since I love the taste of all seafood it's not really tough or difficult to choose one over the other, so going by nutrient density is easy for me to follow. I must say though, I think my favourite fish is salmon (if cooking/preparing it on my own). If out on vacation and have better access, I love grilled snapper or -insert any Caribbean fish-. Mmmmmmm....fish!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-05T03:03:04+0200

O, and I won't forget my love for seaweed. Lately I've been enjoying it as toasted. Must be a winter thing, feels more comforting as opposed to during the summer I preferred it as a salad (cold). lol

### Dean6789 — 2018-02-15T06:00:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-234789>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=61517)
>
> Fish cheeks! Get some. I think they are part of the secret sauce of Inger's MHS. They are awesome and generally dirt cheap in oriental/asian stores for making soup. I have been chowing them religiously for 5 years in recipes. In my opinion, it is the best part of meat on the entire fish. Any fish has them but a bigger fish has the bonanza of size. Cheek sashimi rocks.
>
>
>
> How to do you get to the cheek? Once the fish is cooked, it will be found in a cavity exactly on the side of the face of the fish where a cheek would be! You can peel back the skin, and there you will find a fish medallion of meat there waiting for you to eat.

Hey Jack, Are you finding any wild fish at the Asian stores?

# Thread: Ideas to Make a Home more OPTIMAL.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### freshveggies — 2012-11-02T15:25:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61520>

HI, I am asking for ideas that you all have to make a home more optimal. Also, if you could start from scratch and build --what would you put in a new home to make it more optimal?

For instance, right now I use a lamp in my bathroom with a yellow bulb so I can shower and put on makeup without turning the regular light on . How to make that better?

Need soaking tub. Maybe a cool cave like Dr. K

Other ideas for more optimal? Fireplace to give a nice light?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-02T16:17:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61531>

Omg I have so much to say on this! My son is out of school tho and it will be late - one word - off gassing.

### freshveggies — 2012-11-02T16:31:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61536>

> **Cavemam;63284 wrote:** Omg I have so much to say on this! My son is out of school tho and it will be late - one word - off gassing.

I agree--my head is spinning. I am looking into products to not off gas---like using exterior grad plywood indoor instead of the interior grade.

what about pex pipes-- Copper ones tend to get pinholes in them with out city water. (actually I will now be country and on a different water supply that is better ) They have been phased out.

I bought a book called Healthy Construction Guidelines.

Looking into sunplans to gain solar heat, but not create a hotbox. Need thermal mass inside too.

Anyone done this or studied this.

Beside healthy home, I am looking to think outside the box and look for optimal. But I for one don't have all the answers, that is why I am asking you all.

### ealachan — 2012-11-02T16:31:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61537>

If it were me building my optimal dream home, I'd have a passive solar set-up on a south-facing slope. I'd have tons of windows so that I could have as much natural light as possible all day long. Big DEEP soaking tub in the bathroom, another one outside on the patio. I'd want several functional fireplaces inside the house and out - one in the kitchen to cook on, as well. My bedroom windows would be east-facing so that I got sunlight first thing every morning, and my patio outside would be west-facing so that I could sit out and watch the sun set every night.

### freshveggies — 2012-11-02T16:36:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61539>

> **ealachan;63290 wrote:** If it were me building my optimal dream home, I'd have a passive solar set-up on a south-facing slope. I'd have tons of windows so that I could have as much natural light as possible all day long. Big DEEP soaking tub in the bathroom, another one outside on the patio. I'd want several functional fireplaces inside the house and out - one in the kitchen to cook on, as well. My bedroom windows would be east-facing so that I got sunlight first thing every morning, and my patio outside would be west-facing so that I could sit out and watch the sun set every night.

A woman after my own dreams. Yes, I finally found that lot. On the edge of town, in the country facing south with nothing but prairie on the south and west. Bedroom to be NE corner to stay cooler. Lots of windows to the south with the proper overhang to not get the hot summer heat.

I have to look into fireplaces. This will also be small and optimized. I do have a budget that I have to live with. I will put my money in cabinets that don't off gas and hardwood floors in all rooms except bath and laundry.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-02T16:42:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61540>

I like the double-hung windows so that you can leave the window a few inches open at the top and still not allow rain in.

A wood-burning cookstove is so much fun to cook on!

I like the tip about exterior plywood. Keep them coming! Any preferences on insulation?

### nonchalant — 2012-11-02T16:48:16+0200

We looked at cabinets, which are so expensive. They have very attractive exteriors, but inside is particle board! DH has decided to make some, instead of buying prefabricated. Much, much cheaper.

Hmmm....optimal bathroom. How about an icemaker attached to the bathtub? LOL

Irony - lots of people respond to dream-stuff like imagining they are goin to have parties and entertain all the time, when they just don't. Then resources go into imaginary entertainment and they stick their bedrooms up on the side of the top floors with horrid lighting (bright outside artificial lights + lack of fresh air and sunlight. . .not to mention the lack of energy efficiency.

### Jonathin — 2012-11-02T20:18:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61594>

The issue of air quality can be largely solved by using an air exchanger. They have been in use in Japan for some time. They are a fairly small unit that exchanges outside air for inside air and conserves most of the heat. Ideally, my best options are to use [faswall](http://http://faswall.com/) construction, natural clay plaster on the inside, poured adobe floor and integral rocket wood stove plus passive solar design and real lumber where necessary. And a nature-based water treatment system that uses biology and movement.

Another concern is the ambient EMF radiation especially in higher population areas. If you think light is bad for us, we are exposed to about 200 million times the levels due to manmade EMF transmitting devices (according to an expert that I have confidence in). Obviously it comes from outside but like pollution the inside levels can be worse - cell phone, wifi, electric wiring. Something to consider. Lifestyle choices are a big issue.

### freshveggies — 2012-11-02T20:32:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61598>

They are a fairly small unit that exchanges outside air for inside air and conserves most of the heat. Ideally, my best options are to use [faswall](http://http://faswall.com/) construction, natural clay plaster on the inside, poured adobe floor and integral rocket wood stove plus passive solar design and real lumber where necessary. And a nature-based water treatment system that uses biology and movement.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Another concern is the ambient EMF radiation especially in higher population areas. If you think light is bad for us, we are exposed to about 200 million times the levels due to manmade EMF transmitting devices (according to an expert that I have confidence in). Obviously it comes from outside but like pollution the inside levels can be worse - cell phone, wifi, electric wiring. Something to consider. Lifestyle choices are a big issue.

Thank you Jonathin. I have read about the plaster inside. Your design sounds good. I will be looking into air exchangers, but I know there are different kinds and I need to figure some of that out. I do think that the architect with sunplans helps with that too.

I am very much into reducing emf's as I am a very sensitive person. No wifi in my house. I will be further away from neighbors in the new location so that will be better. Do you now about how to lower the emf's coming from house wiring. I have read about putting the wires in a pipe in the bedroom and or using romex wires?
