# Thread: Great Rift Valley Kids
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Entelechy — 2012-11-02T15:37:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61524>

reposting from the future of education thread, I think it deserves discussion in it's own right.

The goal: to see if illiterate kids with no previous exposure to written words can learn how to read all by themselves,

â€œI thought the kids would play with the boxes. Within four minutes, one kid not only opened the box, found the on-off switch â€¦ powered it up. Within five days, they were using 47 apps per child, per day. Within two weeks, they were singing ABC songs in the village, and within five months, they had hacked Android,â€￾

[Link](http://http://www.technologyreview.com/news/506466/given-tablets-but-no-teachers-ethiopian-children-teach-themselves/)

Ironically.... One village is called Wonchi, on the rim of a volcanic crater at **11,000 feet; the other is called Wolonchete, in the Great Rift Valley**.

The question is, would SAD kids fair as well in such an experiment? or have we evolved to the point where only training can take place, rather than education?

### nonchalant — 2012-11-02T16:58:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61545>

That sounds like a great experiment! They also fixed the camera that had been disabled, and also customized the desktop, though OLPC had installed software to freeze the desktop settings.

### Melinda — 2012-11-02T18:14:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61567>

Thanks for linking to that. What a world changer giving tablets to the unschooled. As a homeschooling family, my goal is to have my children be self learners. I'm going to have them read that link.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-02T21:40:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61611>

This is unschooling at its finest. kids learning on their own

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-02T22:42:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61621>

Great post and wonderful to think about with our own, 'modern' children. What I was struck by was the belief (or at least the suspended belief) that this learning was possible. How much do we limit our children's learning (and our own) by thinking something isn't possible. We self-limit in so many ways. And we then share those limitations with those we are responsible for. I remember, when I was in grad school, a professor saying to me that her favourite interns were the students. "They have so much hope and they don't know enough to be certain of anything... so they have the best results." Buddhists call this 'don't-know mind.' It is considered a very intelligent state of being. There is so much pressure to 'know'... yet if we already know, then we are by definition limited to what we can find.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-02T23:03:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61623>

OH NO, they're getting too much screen time and we must put those tablets away until after they can do 100 multiplication problems in 10 minutes!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T00:01:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61626>

LOL.........I thought it.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-03T00:09:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61629>

my thought was this is a way to keep the poor man down! Give them the magic boxes that only teach them what we want them to know. . . .why not just hand out a monsanto gift bag with those

### Entelechy — 2012-11-03T03:47:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61696>

Not sure I understand Cavemam.. do you think it 'improper' to provide them with tablets and internet access?

I suppose there is an argument to be made for non-interference, as that really is the overarching base principle being discussed in regards to education, but I have difficulty imagining why voluntary access to information would ever be construed as a negative in any culture. (Propaganda and intentional misinformation aside)

What am I missing?

### Entelechy — 2012-11-03T03:53:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61700>

thinking about the 'wow' moments with my kids...

they aren't the times they demonstrate mastery of anything i have shown or told them.

They are the times when they have discovered things on their own, beyond what I could ever teach them.

Like playing chess with my son, I look up and see him grinning at me... he has me in three moves, and I never saw it coming. He was 12.

now at 14 he thrashes me consistently, and has never read a Chess book. The things he comes up with, wow moments.

now, if he would only eat fish..... lol

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-03T12:05:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61737>

I didn't think they had internet access. They had preloaded games etc to allow them to learn to read without a teacher. I understand what Cavemam is alluding to. Those kids only have whatever information has been provided. What they learn depends on who's providing the information.

Reminds me of the schoolbooks for Afghani kids. [http://supportdanielboyd.wordpress.com/usa-printed-textbooks-support-jihad-in-afghanistan-and-pakistan/](http://http://supportdanielboyd.wordpress.com/usa-printed-textbooks-support-jihad-in-afghanistan-and-pakistan/)

### Entelechy — 2012-11-04T04:05:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61910>

I see, and agree that there is always bias, and will always be bias. (with or without Internet access)

This fact is what inspired my statement in the preceding thread, that

"a government run public education system is incompatible with the values of a free society, as it's interest by natural emergence is not education but in social change and cultural conformity",

My childhood Canadian textbooks tell a different version of the war of 1812 that the US version, survivors write the history books.

The advantage of OLPC versus a corporate or govt. agency, is they don't appear to have an overtly ulterior motive other than their expressed mandate.

OLPC bases it's model Seymour Papert's theory of constructionism, the idea that knowledge is best constructed through the act of sharing and collaborating. The tablet is a tool for engaging in creative, intellectual work.

Whether this method of guiding a childs intellectual development in a prescribed direction is beneficial or not, is up for debate, but I'm still enthralled at the capacity of these kids.

Anyone who has modified an app, rooted an android device, or even just explored the file structure can attest at how arcane it can be even to someone familiar with other operating systems.

Now Imagine not being able to read at all. not understanding the structure, and being handed a tablet in Chinese characters or Arabic abjad.

5 monrhs later you have hacked the safeguards put in place by professional developers.

Beneficial, or deleterious... it's impressive, and I can only imagine the exponential development to master such a wide variety of disparate skills in so short a time.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T05:11:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61918>

Seymor Papert! I forgot that he was part of OLPC.

I owe a lot to the man.... when I was little I did a lot of turtle logo and it layed a great foundation for the higher math I did not to mention computer programming.

Edit: Actually, I think "laying the foundation" is the wrong thing to say. By having unstructured access to the computer I was able to learn ways of thinking that allowed me to do well in math and programming.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Anyone have experience with Cyrex Labs Array 5 Systemic Autoimmune Reactivity Screen?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-have-experience-with-cyrex-labs-array-5-systemic-autoimmune-reactivity-screen.4636/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheHolyCat — 2012-11-02T15:57:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-have-experience-with-cyrex-labs-array-5-systemic-autoimmune-reactivity-screen.4636/post-61528>

Here's a link to give you an idea of what's included in the test panel: [http://www.truehealthlabs.com/systemic-autoimmune-reactivity-screen-cyrex-labs-array-5/](http://http://www.truehealthlabs.com/systemic-autoimmune-reactivity-screen-cyrex-labs-array-5/)

So, any thoughts or experiences that anyone would like to share regarding this test panel, or Cyrex in general?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-02T19:24:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-have-experience-with-cyrex-labs-array-5-systemic-autoimmune-reactivity-screen.4636/post-61577>

I know this is a new screen they are doing. Apparently it was being worked on for a few years to bring it to market. IIRC, it has only been out for a year or less. Can't wait to hear if anyone has had that done and what they think of it.

I had Array 4 done and it was enlightening. It tested cross-reactivity for wheat with other items. I would like to test again this year to see if anything has changed. Cyrex customer service was great and as a lab I think they are good. They have a unique offering although small but much needed in the market.

### popcornsally — 2012-11-04T02:34:07+0200

# Thread: seasonality and sex
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-sex.4638/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-02T16:28:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-sex.4638/post-61535>

I was thinking about the way in which a sense of the seasons must have pervaded people's consciousness in the past - and also how seasonality affected people's behaviour perhaps mostly unconsiously.

Anyway, I remembered how in _Kabloona_ the entire Eskimo village, to the anthropologist's amazement, was seized with something like a fever of sexual feeling.

I think one might speculate that the seasons had a bigger effect on Arctic dwellers, because day-length changes so much there. Then, all of a sudden, I remembered that line from Locksley Hall - that spring was when

> a young man's fancies lightly turn to love

http://www.ficml.org/jemimap/voy/colony/Locksley\_Hall.html

Presumably, Tennyson couldn't have used that line, if it had sounded ridiculous to his contemporaries.

Human fertility may be year-round but one suspects that interest may have waxed and waned somewhat around the year in the past. After all, why dance round a maypole in May rather than February - besides the fact that February would be a bit cold for that?

It also occurs to me that this is another area of human life that commerce has tried to annex. Sex is one of the main things used to advertise products. (The other, of course, is snobbery.)

### AKMan — 2012-11-02T18:03:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-sex.4638/post-61565>

It would make sense for babies to be born in March when fresh food is available for nursing mothers. That would make June the optimal month to 'fancy love'.

# Thread: epi-paleo in popular culture... movies, documentaries, music and books
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-in-popular-culture-movies-documentaries-music-and-books.4639/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Entelechy — 2012-11-02T16:34:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-in-popular-culture-movies-documentaries-music-and-books.4639/post-61538>

As quality input is required for change,

I thought a thread to capture peoples favourite examples could act as a resource.

Movie.

[The Snow Walker](http://http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0337721/),

the female star,Annabella Piugattuk, is a real Inuk, who was raised in the traditional culture.

there are scenes of eating a still wriggling raw fish, eating fat and bits of meat while butchering a caribou....

the movie shows the deep connection with nature that allows them to survive in the barren tundra.

for those who prefer text, the book the film is based on is called "Walk Well, My Brother" by Farley Mowat.

Farley has written numerous books about the northern Canada such as People of the Deer, Lost in the Barrens, and Tundra: Selections from the Great Accounts of Arctic Land Voyages. I've not read a Mowat book I've not gotten something out of.

# Thread: Placeboâ€™s Effect May Depend on Your Genes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/placebo%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-effect-may-depend-on-your-genes.4641/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jonathin — 2012-11-02T20:30:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/placebo%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-effect-may-depend-on-your-genes.4641/post-61596>

This adds another dimension to the process of manifesting self-change.

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

from: [http://deskarati.com/2012/10/26/placebos-effect-may-depend-on-your-genes/#more-24199](http://http://deskarati.com/2012/10/26/placebos-effect-may-depend-on-your-genes/#more-24199)

**Placeboâ€™s Effect May Depend on Your Genes**

Posted on October 26, 2012 by Deskarati

Your response to placebos, or dummy medicine, may depend on your genes, according to a new study. People with a gene variant that codes for higher levels of the brain chemical dopamine respond better to placebos than those with the low-dopamine version. The findings, reported online Oct. 23 in the journal PLoS One, could help researchers design medical studies that distinguish the placebo response from the underlying effect of a medicine â€” the real aim of drug trials.

â€œThis is a possible way to discern who is going to be a placebo responder or nonresponder in a clinical trial,â€￾ said study co-author Kathryn Hall of Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center in Brookline, Mass.

People report feeling better after receiving a placebo, such as a sugar pill or fake treatment, for conditions ranging from chronic pain to Parkinsonâ€™s disease. But only some patients respond strongly, and thereâ€™s no way to predict who will improve on a placebo. A few studies have provided clues. Differences in versions of the catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) gene, which determines levels of dopamine in the brainâ€™s prefrontal cortex, are linked to differences in reward-seeking and pain perception. People with the high-dopamine version, or allele, of the COMT gene feel pain more acutely and seek rewards more strongly than those who have the low-dopamine copy.

That led the researchers to wonder whether the gene modulates placebo response. To find out, Hall and her colleagues analyzed DNA from 104 patients with irritable bowel syndrome who were randomized to one of three groups: One was told they were on the waiting list for treatment, another received a placebo in the form of seemingly real, curt acupuncture, and the third group received fake acupuncture from a caring, warm practitioner who looked patients in the eye, asked about their progress, and even touched them lightly, Hall told LiveScience.

Patients with the high-dopamine version of the gene felt slightly better after seeing the curt, all-business health-care provider that gave placebo acupuncture. But they were six times as likely to say their symptoms improved with a caring practitioner as those with the low-dopamine gene, who didnâ€™t improve much in any group. The findings suggest that medical studies called clinical trials could identify treatment versus placebo effect by grouping patients by gene variant, Hall said. Knowing up front the level of placebo effect for a clinical trial could reduce the cost of the trial significantly by using fewer participants, for instance, she said.

People with the high-dopamine allele of the gene may do well on the placebo with the nurturing treatment because they are generally more attuned to their environments, said University of Michigan psychiatrist Jon-Kar Zubieta, who was not involved in the study.

â€œIt speaks about an interaction between the environment and the gene,â€￾ Zubieta said. â€œItâ€™s very possible that individuals with this allele are more able to process those positive environmental cues.â€￾ Via LiveScience

### SeaHorse — 2012-11-03T14:22:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61741>

Hi again all....I've gone back to non-posting mode recently, not because I don't have anything to say (!) But because I'm trying to spend more of my day away from the computer and that is because I'm feeling so much better!!! So, as I said to Dr. K in my last consult....I'm a success story. I've learned and taken to heart so much from this forum that I want to go out and get back to work with my clients and horses. This time last year I was in total dread of the coming winter....not so much this year. In fact I'm still jumping in our pond two or three times a week besides stepping up the cold showers.

Some starfish may swim away for awhile in grateful appreciation that they can! So thanks to all of you and Dr. K for being here. And I'm still reading up on new posts etc.....won't give that up for anything.

Cheers, SeaHorse

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-03T14:30:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61745>

Not that i think this is all about me, but I am often struck that as i got these diseases a forum and a site like this appears to allow me to heal myself and help others. Thanks to all and especially DR. K.

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-03T15:58:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61761>

It's helped me immensely to read others' journeys here. When you first started writing about CT, I thought, well I'll never do that...too "out there". But everyone started writing about their experiences, so I thought, ok, electronic peer pressure here...I'll jump in the icy water!

It's the combination of the blogs + the comments + your responses to the comments + the forum = change.

And I love to hear the differing opinions and the open discussions. Each N=1 might be different, depending on our different conditions to which we came here, and hearing the discussions really helps me.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T16:13:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61765>

Every N-1 has a different context.......and that is a large point I make her daily.......just because one person has an ED or hashi's should not be the green light for you to apply their N-1 to your situation........this is the biggest error I see on the internet in Paleo sites and forums.......and no one says it aloud so people stop and think...........If I told you to jump off a bridge you would not but if you had Hashi's and I mentioned something to person X everyone seems to think if they have Hashi's they should try it........bad thinking.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T17:02:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61779>

I look at this forum as an extension of my own family in some ways.........

FAMILY teaches the habit of sharing. Running a successful FAMILY is an art and requires sophisticated skills. If you are happy in your personal life, your chances of success in your career or job are greatly increased in this life. Be thankful 19 days before family is celebrated in your house. If you're thinking about it now it means a lot more.

I have a vested interest in keep my family well.........so in this respect you are all in my family.

### Jonathin — 2012-11-03T18:09:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61792>

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-03T18:15:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61797>

> **Jack Kruse;63546 wrote:** Be thankful 19 days before family is celebrated in your house. If you're thinking about it now it means a lot more.

Actually been thinking about family a lot, as I'll be seeing them all very soon. I've tried "starfishing" with them, but things barely get through. It kills me to watch diabetic mother eat "healthy Triscuits" because that's what the ADA says, or quad-bypass + multiple stent father eat lowfat cheese because that's what his cardiologist says. Or 5-mile-a-day running-addicted sister keep running like that because Runner's World Mag says so.

Every time I see them, I try to share more, but I do give so much thanks that I DO have them in my life to annoy them! LOL.

# Thread: That NDE
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T08:57:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-61725>

A (hostile) column on it I stumbled across. I don't know it adds anything, but it finishes with a Zen story that has all the characteristic pithiness of the genre:

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/9598971/Is-the-afterlife-full-of-fluffy-clouds-and-angels.html](http://http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/9598971/Is-the-afterlife-full-of-fluffy-clouds-and-angels.html)

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-11-05T06:33:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62150>

A sceptic about afterlife, but not a dead sceptic. I believe this is one of those stories you chose to believe or not. This guy was trying to make the point for non-believers.

Whether you believe in the afterlife or a life force that survives the mortal body the Doctor's experience is facinating.

What does come next?

### Michael — 2012-11-05T08:55:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62159>

> **ljbprrfmof;63948 wrote:** A sceptic about afterlife, but not a dead sceptic. I believe this is one of those stories you chose to believe or not. This guy was trying to make the point for non-believers.
>
>
>
> Whether you believe in the afterlife or a life force that survives the mortal body the Doctor's experience is facinating.
>
>
>
> What does come next?
>
>
> There's no need for anyone to take the NDE as the man who experienced it took it, as has been said here before. However, that column was written with a sneer, wasn't it? And that seems a bit unnecessary. I didn't think he added anything of moment, but I liked the Zen story at the end, and that's mainly why I posted it.
>
>
>
> Here is that columnist, saying at length in his appallingly superior manner would could be said in half-a-dozen words:
>
>
>
> They are nearly always pleasant and deeply reassuring in a saccharin-soaked way. Lots of spinning down warm, dark tunnels to the sound of celestial music; lots of trips along country lanes lined with hedges, towards the light of a welcoming cottage at the end of the road; lots of tumbling down Alice-in-Wonderland rabbit holes, but without the damaging effects of gravity.

In point of fact, there are negative NDEs as well. That's kind of interesting. I guess it might show our consciousness, or perhaps I should say unconscious, might be as ready to accuse as soothe us.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T02:22:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62467>

As I recollect the story, the physician was not religious, at least prior to the NDE.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-11-07T13:26:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62501>

I believe that is correct.

### caroline — 2012-11-07T13:49:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62502>

love the zen story at the end too

### kathylu — 2012-11-07T18:19:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62575>

I am happy for him. It must be wonderful to look forward to the certainty of a blissful eternity after death. I just see a long sleep.

### ealachan — 2012-11-07T18:36:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62580>

> **kathylu;64414 wrote:** I am happy for him. It must be wonderful to look forward to the certainty of a blissful eternity after death. I just see a long sleep.

I see becoming worm fodder. Worms gotta eat, too...

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-07T18:54:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62589>

I hope I'm non-toxic enough for some worms to want to eat me when i die!

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-07T18:55:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62590>

I've seen the other side. It was as my MIL was dying. It was totally completely unexpected. We had driven 2days to get to Boise to be with her at the end. I walked into her room and ... the walls disappeared and I "saw" a vast open area with clusters of people (lots and lots of people) standing around. They appeared as tapered columns of golden light. I looked to my left and saw one standing near my MIL's bed and I knew instantly it was her late husband.

My only comment at the time was a spontaneous "Wow there's a lot of people in this room!"

### kathylu — 2012-11-07T19:08:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62592>

> **DarleenMB;64429 wrote:** I've seen the other side. It was as my MIL was dying. It was totally completely unexpected. We had driven 2days to get to Boise to be with her at the end. I walked into her room and ... the walls disappeared and I "saw" a vast open area with clusters of people (lots and lots of people) standing around. They appeared as tapered columns of golden light. I looked to my left and saw one standing near my MIL's bed and I knew instantly it was her late husband.
>
>
>
> My only comment at the time was a spontaneous "Wow there's a lot of people in this room!"

As I said, I really hope so...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-07T19:12:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62593>

> **DarleenMB;64429 wrote:** "Wow there's a lot of people in this room!"

This is actually a common comment by people who are near death. I find it absolutely fascinating that you experienced this and you were amongst the living!

### Michael — 2012-11-07T19:48:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62605>

> **Nonchalant;64282 wrote:** As I recollect the story, the physician was not religious, at least prior to the NDE.
>
>
> Yes and no.
>
>
>
> Although I considered myself a faithful Christian, I was so more in name than in actual belief.

[http://www.thedailybeast.com/newsweek/2012/10/07/proof-of-heaven-a-doctor-s-experience-with-the-afterlife.html](http://http://www.thedailybeast.com/newsweek/2012/10/07/proof-of-heaven-a-doctor-s-experience-with-the-afterlife.html)

It seems the experience, whatever it was, deepened his belief. AFAIK, that's not unusual.

What did seem special about this one was that "the neocortex, was inactivated". Later Jack dropped a possible explanation for that. So I guess that put us back to square one: neither more (nor less) remarkable than any other experience of the sort. In retrospect, I guess that would be so.

To be honest, although I did love the Zen story otherwise the columnist in _Telegraph did rub me up the wrong way. I think he thinks he knows and I don't think he can. What the subject says sounds sentimental to the columnist, but then if it did have some content worth knowing, what's to say _this_ man can transmit that ... or even that it can be put into words? When I read William James's the Varieties of Religious Experience_ I remember being somewhat amused by his recounting the story of a Nonconformist preacher shouting, "Woe to the bloody city of Lichfield!" because as he approached Lichfield he saw red everywhere. That really does sound like something organic. But ruling out every story that doesn't fit a preconceived view of things? There are some remarkable stories, for example, from remote Scottish islands of the "second sight" - someone might walk into a pub and see a man covered with water and fall into a faint; the man would be drowned shortly after.

# Thread: Video: Aboriginal Eel Farming
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T09:20:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61728>

This video is only here thanks to Coriander who pointed me to it in an earlier thread.

(Requires the Adobe Flash player.)

This is another video that seems worth a thread of its own.

It's another reminder that if we simply guess what aboriginal peoples did to feed themselves - they "must have" done this or that - we can miss the mark by a long way.

This video refers to people who are now mostly vanished. But for the work of these archaeologists we might never have known about these sophisticated techniques:

> The Lake Condah region in south-west Victoria shows evidence of a network of channels and rock structures used by the Indigenous inhabitants for trapping eels. **If the dating of the fish traps is correct, they would be some of the most ancient structures of this type in existence.** The Budj Bim National Heritage Landscape, which includes Lake Condah, was included in the National Heritage List in 2004, and World Heritage listing is being sought. ...
>
>
> [http://www.abceducation.net.au/videolibrary/view/indigenous-eel-farming-50](http://http://www.abceducation.net.au/videolibrary/view/indigenous-eel-farming-50)
>
>
>
> Radiocarbon dating of marsh sediment by Monash University scientists indicated that the fish traps may be up to 6,800 years old. Evidence that the inhabitants had smoked the eels came from an analysis of sediments taken from beneath hollow trees by Australian National University scientists using mass spectroscopy and gas chromatography.

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-03T14:51:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61753>

Michael, you come up with the most interesting stuff. Thanks!

### Michael — 2012-11-03T17:41:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61788>

Thanks! But the credit for that one's due to Coriander.

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-04T00:08:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61867>

Here's an article with a little more information. [http://www.theage.com.au/articles/2003/03/12/1047431092972.html](http://http://www.theage.com.au/articles/2003/03/12/1047431092972.html)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-04T01:31:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61877>

Thanks, Coriander. Great read.

Btw, do you eat eel? I see it regularly in both the fish shops and supermarkets here but have never bought it. I know there is both fresh water and salt water but know next to nothing about eating, cooking, nutritional value, etc.

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-04T02:58:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61893>

No, it's never even occured to me. My only experience of eel is seeing it dragged out of a man-made lake in nets; considered vermin, they were taken away like garbage.

Now I'm wondering what it tastes like!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-04T03:09:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61899>

Ok, I'm going to buy some when I'm next at the fish shop! A quick google says super high in DHA, B vitamins, vitamin A and D, minerals, phosphorus etc. in the meantime Ill start hunting recipes ( web keeps talking about its 'distinct' taste).

### Michael — 2012-11-04T09:22:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61932>

Rick Stein reckons smoked eel would have to be in the world's top ten foods:

> ... the skin peeled off at the last minute and the fillet thinly sliced and served with a warm potato salad, a few dandelion leaves and some snipped chives.
>
>
> An old Cockney favourite was jellied eels.
>
>
>
> Stein gives a recipe for that, based on some he had in Leigh-on-Sea in Essex:
>
>
>
> **Serves 4-6
>
> 900 g (2 lb) eels, killed and skinned
>
> Pared zest and juice of 1 lemon
>
> 3 bay leaves
>
> 4 cloves
>
> 8 black peppercorns
>
> 4 teaspoons salt
>
> 1 small bunch curly parsley, chopped
>
>
>
> Method**
>
> Cut the spines out from the top and bottom edge of the eels, then cut the eels into 4 cm (1 1/2 inch) pieces. Put the pieces in a large saucepan with the lemon zest, juice, bay leaves, cloves, peppercorns and salt. Add enough cold water just to cover, then bring to the boil and simmer for 20 minutes. Transfer the eels and their cooking liquor to a bowl and leave to cool.
>
>
>
> Stir in the chopped parsley and divide the mixture between 4-6 small pots. Cover and chill until the jelly has set, then serve.

Rick Stein's _Seafood Lover's Guide_, a journey round the coast of Britain and Ireland.

http://www.amazon.com/gp/aw/d/0563488719

### diane — 2012-11-04T13:23:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61941>

I love ungainly which is BBQ eel sushi. I don't think I've seen eel raw in sushi restaurants - just BBQ.

# Thread: New Zealanders urged to take an at-home omega-3 blood test
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-zealanders-urged-to-take-an-at-home-omega-3-blood-test.4652/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T09:31:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-zealanders-urged-to-take-an-at-home-omega-3-blood-test.4652/post-61729>

> Omega-3 health expert and consultant to the World Health Organization, Stuart Tomc, is urging New Zealanders to take an at-home omega-3 blood test in a bid to improve public health and save the national health system millions of dollars per year.
>
>
>
> Tomc says the typical New Zealander consumes far too much omega-6s, leaving them chronically lacking omega-3s and more susceptible to diabetes, heart disease and obesity.
>
>
>
> Tomc arrived in New Zealand this morning ... He will explain how a simple at-home blood test will enable people to see what their omega-3 and omega-6 levels are and adjust their diet accordingly to achieve the proper balance. By doing this, Tomc says peopleâ€™s health will improve, alleviating pressure on the health care system.
>
>
>
> "The root of most health issues stem from inflammation," says Tomc. ...
>
>
>
> ... Tomc noted that todayâ€™s Western diet, which has more processed foods than at any other time in history, has completely thrown essential fats out of balance. "We over-consume the inflammatory omega-6s which has left us chronically lacking omega-3s. This makes us more susceptible to conditions like diabetes, heart disease, and metabolic syndrome (i.e. obesity). For example, the average American eats 10 times more omega-6 fatty acids than omega-3, and as a consequence, the United States has the highest rate of obesity in the world. New Zealand also has a high rate of obesity with one in three people overweight, and one in four adults obese." ...

[http://www.voxy.co.nz/health/us-expert-urges-omega-3-testing/5/139234](http://http://www.voxy.co.nz/health/us-expert-urges-omega-3-testing/5/139234)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T17:03:34+0200

# Thread: Interesting podcast on foods that cause AI problems
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-podcast-on-foods-that-cause-ai-problems.4653/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T10:24:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-podcast-on-foods-that-cause-ai-problems.4653/post-61731>

This is a podcast where author J. J. Virgin is interviewed about her new book, which advises cutting out the 7 foods to which people are most commonly sensitive. This she says she's found frequently results in weight loss as people's inflammation drops.

Anyway, I almost didn't listen. You lnow what it's like with these podcasts. Someone's got a booklaunch, and they've obviously been phoning round, and they're on Jimmy's show and Abel's ... and Dr. Lo's ... and everyone else's in the ancestral health space. However, I did listen, and \*this\* version of the interview was different and interesting.

As well as the usual, the interviewee told a story about her son, who'd just been struck by a hit-and-run driver, leaving him with multiple fractures and brain damage. In the hospital they told her he would die. They said his heart needed blood-thinners, but if they gave them the bleeding from his brain would kill him. They said there were few places in the country that could do anything, but if he were airlifted \*that\* could kill him. So she asked them what would happen if he were \*not\* airlifted, and they said he'd die. She said: "Why are we standing aroundn then?" and began ringing hospitals herself.

The boy was flown to the best hospital in the country for this sort of thing. The surgeon came out, took her hand, and said, "I've got it in hand. Your son will be all right." He did come round and is now recovering.

She said if she hadn't been in possession of her full energies she doesn't know how she could have coped. This is the point of not eating all the processed crap - not weight-loss, but being able to be there and functioning whatever life throws at you.

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/11/02/the-virgin-diet-w-jj-virgin](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/11/02/the-virgin-diet-w-jj-virgin)

### Inger — 2012-11-03T12:05:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-podcast-on-foods-that-cause-ai-problems.4653/post-61738>

> She said if she hadn't been in possession of her full energies she doesn't know how she could have coped. This is the point of not eating all the processed crap - not weight-loss, but being able to be there and functioning whatever life throws at you.

This.

Thank you Michael! Now I need to go and listen to the podcast!

### caroline — 2012-11-06T02:12:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-podcast-on-foods-that-cause-ai-problems.4653/post-62292>

Thanks Michael - that was very interesting.

# Thread: Seaweed smoothies
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T15:31:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61758>

What are people doing in this line?

I tried this:

1 can organic coconut milk (said 400 ml)

About 1/4 cup drained, pre-soaked seaweed

1 tablespoon cocoa powder

1 teaspoon raw honey

A few drops vanilla extract

A pinch cinnamon

A pinch chilli

Just like Inger said, you don't taste the seaweed. What I did taste most was the coconut - which I don't actually like much. It probably didn't need the honey, as there was no sense of needing sweetening. (I kind of like the idea of the honey, though, since it seems reductive to ban it as a "sugar" when there are supposedly literally hundreds of compounds in it.)

I guess something like this could be quite healing - except the chilli perhaps.

Anyone tried any other combinations?

### Optimalbound — 2012-11-03T20:10:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61821>

Thanks for sharing, Michael. What kind of seaweed did you use?

### Glamazon — 2012-11-03T20:44:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61828>

I find most veggie tastes disappear in a smoothie if I put enough cocoa or berries or both in it. Maybe a slight grass aftertaste. I just use water.

### Michael — 2012-11-04T09:38:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61933>

> **Glamazon;63599 wrote:** I find most veggie tastes disappear in a smoothie if I put enough cocoa or berries or both in it. Maybe a slight grass aftertaste. I just use water.

I think I'd have probably preferred just water.

@OptimalBound This packet was a pretty much "let's see" choice from a bewildering variety in a good health-food shop. It was sold under the Clearspring label. I think that's a Welsh company, but the sea vegetables seem to have been wild-harvested off the French coast - says "Product of France". It's dulse, sea lettuce, and nori.

I didn't realize till I opened the packet, but they're shredded really fine with the idea that you can sprinkle them over a salad without needing to soak them.

### Optimalbound — 2012-11-04T10:56:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61939>

Thanks, Michael. I've found some old recepies with bladderwrack that I intend to try out. Wild bladderwrack grows all along the coast where I live. Might try it in a smoothie, also. Will report back if I find anything interesting.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-06T18:27:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-62371>

I was thinking of going a different path with some seaweed.... when I get a new blender (mine broke).

Seaweed

Bone broth

Coconut Oil

Cultured Coconut Milk

sauteed leeks + garlic

\+ oregano and/or rosemary and/or cilantro, or whatever else I have from the garden or on hand.

Black pepper and maybe some cumin

I'm thinking it would come out tasting a lot like a cream of leek soup....

drink it when I'm taking my curcurmin....

Or is it only a "smoothie" if it's sweet or sweetish.... I don't know.

### Inger — 2012-11-06T20:16:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-62395>

RiverNeighbour, I think that is an excellent idea!!! I will try this out too. Look, I have been drinking plain seaweed smoothies now, without anything just soaked seaweeds and water, because I thought it tasted so delish by itself.. but now I want to optimize it further, with some superhealthy spices, onion/garlic etc.

Like a salty soup! Could be cold or warm. Wow.. now you got my brain running..lol I need to experiment with this tomorrow for sure!

# Thread: Is cold water splashing outside effective as CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### gkalmens — 2012-11-03T16:01:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61762>

I would like to try splashing cold water on my body while standing in a swimsuit outside when it is below 40F (5C). Is anybody doing it? Is it effective compared to ice baths? How long should such sessions last?

\- Gene

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T16:08:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61763>

is it better than nothing? yes. Is it optimal like getting in the pacific ocean? not close.

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-03T16:15:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61767>

For me, believe it or not, sitting in the cold water (50-52 F) is actually easier than cold-water splashing or showering. Just submerging my entire body actually feels better.

When I first read about CT, I, too, splashed some cold water, and then I tried to take cold showers. Brrr, and not pleasant. At all. So one Saturday when I was bored, I dumped my entire freezer's icemaker full of ice into my tub, took a meat thermometer with me, and just jumped in. It was about 58F. I was so surprised that, while GETTING in was tough, once I was fully IN, it was actually really nice.

### ealachan — 2012-11-03T16:19:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61768>

Splashing is like torture for me! I'd way rather just get in and stay in.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T16:49:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61775>

i agree with the ladies too

### gkalmens — 2012-11-03T23:58:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61866>

I don't have a problem taking cod baths and I stand them pretty easily. I did it last spring; in summer I soaked in Lake Michigan for 30 min when it was cold enough. I take cold showers in the morning and before bed plus after gym. I also walk every weekday to the train station and back (25 min each way) in very light clothing (I've added gloves lately though). I thought to add something less time-consuming to my late fall-winter routine than cold baths. Given the answers, I will continue with cold baths. Thanks!

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-04T01:24:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61876>

I think if you're going to add to the cold baths, face dunks would be great....elliciting the mammalian dive reflex.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-11-04T03:50:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61907>

> **Jack Kruse;63542 wrote:** i agree with the ladies too

Me three!!

### gkalmens — 2012-11-04T05:14:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61919>

I hold ice packs fresh from the freezer to my face as long as I can stand it. At the same time, I can breathe freely; with face dunking I need to hold my breath. I wonder whether ice packs are an acceptable substitute for face dunking.

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-04T15:41:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61959>

> **Randychaps;63680 wrote:** Me three!!

me 4 full immersion

### philerup — 2012-11-05T09:26:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-62165>

full immersion is superior to any other method imo. i used all methods and only get a real good shiver after my cold baths. the shiver lasts quite a while.

### Adria — 2012-11-05T16:57:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-62204>

Watching my animals.. they will not swim in the cold weather but will purposely run through the water, splashing! We have 20's here now and snow and (my lab especially) loves to run in the shallow water in the creek behind our house. Once frozen, he'll try to break the ice at the water's edge and plunge his legs into the cold. They're not interested in the big lake or in swimming til spring.

# Thread: Minnow trap demonstrated by Jack Hargreaves
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minnow-trap-demonstrated-by-jack-hargreaves.4659/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T17:48:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minnow-trap-demonstrated-by-jack-hargreaves.4659/post-61789>

This is man was brought up in the country with animals, qualified as a veterinary surgeon, and then went into radio and TV in the early days. He tried to document the ways of the old way of life in the country that he'd known as a boy before it finally disappeared.

Here he is showing a "minnow trap" he bought in France, where he says he reckoned people were still taking pains to get their own dinners. He thinks they probably used it catch fish like whitebait. He also shows a wicker crayfish trap.

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;\_sGRHPGHSYg\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=\_sGRHPGHSYg\[/video\]\[/video\]

Most of the old film with Jack in was thought to have been lost or destroyed, but some has turned up now. He can be found in short clips on YouTube showing country cider-making, butchering, bantam-keeping, and so on.

[http://www.youtube.com/results?search\_query=jack+hargreaves](http://http://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=jack+hargreaves)

# Thread: Hello everybody!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-11-03T20:00:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61818>

We were hit by Sandy so we live very much Kruse like these days. It is very dark and cold every day ! Running water is our only luxury. Thanks God for that!

No cell phone, no internet service, so I missed a lot on this forum. I just got a chance to get on a computerto post this for a few minutes. Hopefully, our power should be restored by Nov. 11th., I hope.

Have to fetch some gas somewhere. Life is very simple right now. We have to concentrate on the basics.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-03T20:03:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61820>

Good luck, you are missed - hope recovery is helped by the back to nature forced on you

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T22:00:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61844>

Destiny you will be better for it......I promise you. I went through this with Katrina.

### caroline — 2012-11-04T02:03:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61884>

Big hugs to you and your family - thinking of you.

### kathylu — 2012-11-04T04:14:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61912>

Absolutely thinking of you! So many people here (Washington/Baltimore) really sighed with relief that the storm didn't come 50 miles further south...then we would have been in a heap of hurt. As it is we still have thousands of folk without power in Maryland, though (fortunately) I'm not one of them.

### Destiny — 2012-11-14T01:59:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-64147>

Thank you, all! We are back in the wired world. I am just happy to have it behind me. Unfortunately, there are still many people who cannot say that.

### caroline — 2012-11-14T07:23:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-64179>

> **Destiny;66104 wrote:** Thank you, all! We are back in the wired world. I am just happy to have it behind me. Unfortunately, there are still many people who cannot say that.

Being wired is great isn't it - missed you. xo

### Destiny — 2012-11-14T13:16:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-64216>

> **caroline;66138 wrote:** Being wired is great isn't it - missed you. xo

Missed you too! Yes, I feel connected with the world again !

### CarolMorris — 2012-11-14T17:56:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-64274>

Hugs to you and your family...so sorry you had to go through that...

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-14T21:18:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-64312>

glad you made it through unscathed... hope all is well....looking fwd to your next update...

# Thread: Time for LHT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-lht.4662/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-03T20:50:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-lht.4662/post-61829>

Been doing some thinking and realized that the large majority of LS signs are here.

Sleep is awesome, even with a puppy that must go out 1-2x a night- wake easily in the am and feel ready to rule the world.

Pants are fitting way differently, scale is down only three pounds, but I look as though I've lost ten.

Much calmer, more serene, aside from random CT induced weirdness

Sweating was never really an issue for me...was always pretty well under control.

Hunger is under control...I still BAB, but I don't care to eat at all after about noon.

No cravings to speak of, unless you count salmon as a craving!

**Body temp is pretty consistently 99+...isn't that supposed to drop?**

Quit wearing makeup aside for lipstick and mascara...don't need it except from CT-acne, which is like clockwork these last couple weeks.

Getting fairly cold adapted, warm feels off to me, warm beverages feel out of kilter...like warm water tastes in summer.

I'm having the urge to LHT. But i dont want to screw it up. But everyone crows about a returning libido as a major LS sign....No sign of a libido here.

More Testing is on the list but as I've been out of work this year more than I've been employed and the doc I went to favors 24 hour urinary labs over serum and, well, I blew my test money before I started. Nothing to do about it now but save up and start over, although I did find I am way low on progesterone and have skewed 2/16 est ratios. So not totally wasted, just mostly.

Should I go ahead with LHT or wait just a bit? I'm over thinking this, I suspect, but Any insight is much appreciated!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Pine kernels
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T21:39:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-61837>

I bought a packet of organic pine kernels today. Not sure what to do with them yet, but it seemed like a nice change.

I just looked at the side of the packet, and it says they're from China! Long way for pine kernels to come. I wonder who certifies them. The Chinese may say they're organic, but then _they_ say they're wanted in Tibet.

I'm thinking sprinkle on salads. I think you can make pesto with them, too.

### Endless — 2012-11-04T23:23:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62092>

Are those the same as pine nuts? I've used them toasted on salads, they're yummy. REALLY high in O6.

### Michael — 2012-11-05T09:06:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62161>

I think it would be the same thing under another name.

### Michael — 2012-11-05T09:12:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62163>

Looks like you can use them with fish:

> This is a classic Venetian dish which has been given to me by our Editor, Sarah Randell. Sfogi (sole) is pronounced ‘sforgey’. It is a lovely sweet and sour combination. ...

[http://www.deliaonline.com/recipes/cuisine/european/italian/sfogi-in-saor.html](http://http://www.deliaonline.com/recipes/cuisine/european/italian/sfogi-in-saor.html)

### Endless — 2012-11-05T09:39:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62168>

I guess use will depend on ones omega 3/6 ratio, but I have always bought fresh nuts and seeds at a fall dale and made mixed roasted nuts containing walnuts, almonds, pecans, pine nuts, pumpkin seeds, sunflower seeds and macadamias. I add some garlic powder and tamari soy sauce and they are to die for. Looking for a new soy-free flavor panel, suggestions welcomed!

### Inger — 2012-11-06T11:16:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62325>

I have been eating around 200 grams wild Siberian "pine nuts" (Cedar/Cedrus) / day, the last 4 days. I just eat them plain, they are totally delicious!

Sometimes I have sprinkeled them on salads too. But to me they are so delicious I want to enjoy them just plain.

I usually munch on some whole organic cucumber at the same time..

### Michael — 2012-11-06T11:56:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62326>

200 g?

_\[Exit Inger, pursued by a squirrel\]_

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-06T15:40:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62353>

Absolutely toast them. if you like them raw you will Love them toasted. Dry pan no oil medium heat until they start to brown and smoke. When I was a vegetarian they were my go to bacon replacement. # Thread: off the wall but....  Milking cows and Fishoil supps
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/off-the-wall-but-milking-cows-and-fishoil-supps.4665/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-11-03T21:53:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/off-the-wall-but-milking-cows-and-fishoil-supps.4665/post-61840>

Dietary Fish Oil Supplements Modify Ruminal Biohydrogenation, Alter the Flow of Fatty Acids at the Omasum, and Induce Changes in the Ruminal Butyrivibrio Population in Lactating Cows1,2,3

[http://jn.nutrition.org/content/early/2012/06/26/jn.112.158576.abstract](http://http://jn.nutrition.org/content/early/2012/06/26/jn.112.158576.abstract)

> Four lactating cows fitted with ruminal cannulae and fed a grass silage-based diet were used in a 4 Ã— 4 Latin square with 28-d periods to investigate the effects of incremental dietary fish oil (FO) supplementation (0, 75, 150, or 300 g/d) on the flow of fatty acids at the omasum and **populations of rumen bacteria capable of biohydrogenation. **FO decreased silage intake and ruminal volatile fatty acid concentrations and promoted an increase in molar butyrate and propionate proportions at the expense of acetate.** Extensive ruminal biohydrogenation of 20:5(n-3) and 22:6(n-3) resulted in corresponding increases in numerous 20- and 22-carbon unsaturated fatty acids at the omasum. Omasal flow of several 20-, 21-, and 22-carbon all-cis (n-3) PUFA exceeded the intake from FO. Supplements of FO also induced a dose-dependent decrease in 18:0 and increased trans 18:1 and trans 18:2 flow at the omasum. Trans-11 was the major 18:1 intermediate in digesta, while FO induced quadratic increases in trans-10 18:1 flow, reaching a maximum of 300 g/d. FO had no substantial influence on omasal flow of CLA. Results suggest that one or more fatty acids in FO inhibit the reduction of trans-18:1 and trans-18:2 intermediates by ruminal microorganisms. qPCR based on 16S rRNA genes in omasal digesta indicated that key Butyrivibrio spp. declined linearly in response to FO. Dose-dependent increases in ruminal outflow of biohydrogenation intermediates containing one or more trans double bonds in response to FO has major implications for host metabolism and the nutritional quality of ruminant foods.**

### chocolate — 2012-11-03T21:57:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/off-the-wall-but-milking-cows-and-fishoil-supps.4665/post-61841>

> ABSTRACT Acetic and propionic acids, produced by

>

>

>
> (92 Ã‚Â±38, 3.9 Ã‚Â±1.9 and 2.3 Ã‚Â±1.6 Ã‚Â¿imol/L).There
>

>
> rum acetateropionate ratio and total cholesterol (r
>

>

>

# Thread: Ancient tools made from seashells
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-04T10:08:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/post-61935>

If you think you know you need to look for stone tools, what might you miss? ....

Here is an article "Shell Tools Rewrite Australasian Prehistory" from 2009:

> Dr Katherine SzabÃ³, of the University of Wollongong, has challenged the orthodox view that the early inhabitants of Australasia lacked sophisticated tool-making skills by showing that shells were the basis of their technology, rather than bone or stone. ...
>
>
>
> ... The shell tools \[these are ones from eastern Indonesia\] had been carefully chosen and shaped and **were, in fact, much more complex to produce than the stone tools,’ she says. ...
>
>
>
>
>
> Australasia’s high diversity of marine molluscs makes shell the logical choice of raw material, along with organic materials such as bamboo, for tools and weapons in a region that lacks easily worked stone. Despite this, shell is not a material that anyone has looked at in detail nor understands very well**. ...

[http://www.world-archaeology.com/news/shell-tools-rewrite-australasian-prehistory/](http://http://www.world-archaeology.com/news/shell-tools-rewrite-australasian-prehistory/)

Unfortunately, the full article is behind a paywall.

The archaeologist seems to specialize in molluscs and sea-travel:

[https://researchers.anu.edu.au/researchers/szabo-ka](http://https://researchers.anu.edu.au/researchers/szabo-ka)

>
> I think the writing's on the wall now. There's some good archaeological evidence out there. It's also clear that so many health parameters are affected by the nutrients that are most abundant in seafood, particularly DHA and iodine.
>
>
>
> Our past is tied up with the sea and rivers - as the source of the nutrients for encephalization (as Cunnane persuasively argues, as you showed us), as a continuing source of easily-caught food, as the route out of Africa and across the globe ...
>
>
>
> This alone:
>
>
>
> Fish was a vital food source for prehistoric peoples. Not only is fish highly nutritious, and often relatively easy to catch (not requiring stone tools for either the hunting or eating process), it should be remembered that although many fish were small, several species of prehistoric fish were much larger than modern day equivalents, some even growing as big as a large mammal, so that a single catch could provide a substantial food source for a number of people.
>
>
>
> There were over 28,000 species of fish available to prehistoric peoples.

[http://www.uwhg.org.uk/reports/uwhg\_meetings/01\_apr\_10/01\_apr\_10.html](http://http://www.uwhg.org.uk/reports/uwhg_meetings/01_apr_10/01_apr_10.html)

# Thread: Whaling in Arctic Prehistory
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whaling-in-arctic-prehistory.4669/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-04T10:15:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whaling-in-arctic-prehistory.4669/post-61936>

Here's another article from _Current World Archaeology. I used to subscribe to their sister-magazine _Current Archaeology_, but there are so many calls on one's money... I've never taken Current World Archaeology_.

This story is from May 2008.

> Members of a Russian-American team investigating the early history of whaling have made a spectacular find at Unâ€™enâ€™en, near the modern whaling village of Nunligran, on Russiaâ€™s Chukotka Peninsula. Right at the end of their 2007 season they uncovered an ivory carving, approximately 0.5m in length, engraved with scenes of men in umiaks (wooden framed boats covered in seal or walrus hide) harpooning whales. The carving was found sealed by the roof timbers of a collapsed structure that has been carbon dated to 1,000 BC. ...
>
>
>
> The US Co-Director, Daniel Odess of the University of Alaska Museum of the North, said **â€˜The importance of whaling in Arctic prehistory is now becoming clear. Prehistoric settlements were situated and defended specifically so that people could hunt whalesâ€™.**

So there we are: there were settlements near Bering Strait specifically for the purpose of whaling a millenium before Christ (and doubtless before that).

[http://www.world-archaeology.com/news/whaling-in-arctic-prehistory/](http://http://www.world-archaeology.com/news/whaling-in-arctic-prehistory/)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-04T17:21:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universe-activity-influencing-human-behavior.4677/post-62003>

The great flu outbreak in the early 20th century was preceeded by a massive solar flare that got to the Earth and altered the genetic code of the virus in the ocean and in one year lead to the great pandemic that killed millions. It was the poster example of Brain Gut 2. I absolutely think ionizing radiation changes our behavior because of its effects on Progesterone and BDNF and eventually to the cortisol DHEA melatonin axis. Everything is tied together if you look for it. Evolutionary medicine is all based upon a fractal design template.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-04T17:25:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universe-activity-influencing-human-behavior.4677/post-62006>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish\_flu](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_flu)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-04T17:35:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universe-activity-influencing-human-behavior.4677/post-62008>

A “Chizhevsky Super Event” is named after famed Russian scientist Alexander Chizhevsky (1897-1964) who founded “heliobiology” (study of the sun’s effect on biology) and “aero-ionization” (study of effect of ionization of air on biological entities). Chizhevsky was also was noted for his work in “cosmo-biology", biological circadian rhythms and hematology.

Now if you think this is bunk consider what just happened in NYC and NJ: The Chizhevsky Super Event theory predicted this storm all the way back in March because of the solar maximum that occurred on 3/23/2012. His work is where I found out about the viral alterations in the oceans by the sun and how humans are impacted by it.

Chizhevsky is most notable for his use of historical research (historiometry) techniques to link the 11 year solar cycle, Earth’s climate and the mass activity of peoples, research that was confirmed by Western scientists earlier this decade in two important studies titled Amplifying the Pacific Climate System Response to a Small 11-Year Solar Cycle Forcing and Synchronized Northern Hemisphere climate change and solar magnetic cycles during the Maunder Minimum.

Important to note there are the “four components” of Chizhevsky's eleven year cycle and their approximate lengths: 1) a three year period of minimum activity characterized by passivity and autocratic rule; 2) a two year period during which masses begin to organize under new leaders and one theme; 3) a three year period of maximum excitability, revolution and war; 4) a three year period of gradual decrease in excitability until the masses are apathetic.

Of these “four components” our world has now entered into the Third Period: A three year period of maximum excitability, revolution and war. Sounds like Iran and Isreal no?

According to Chizhevsky’s research, RAS scientists state in this report, the defining characteristics to be looked for when our world enters into a Third Period “Super Event” timeline is a rapid overturning of existing weather phenomena combined with an equally sudden major changing of the global social-political power structures all occurring during a period of Solar Maximum. Can you super storm Sandy?

Interesting to note about the current Solar Maximum we are in was the 23 March 2009 scientific article titled Space Storm Alert: 90 Seconds From Catastrophe published by New Scientist that eerily describes somewhat exactly what is now occurring in the United States following their East Coast region being struck by what is being called a “derecho” storm that, in part, says:

“It is midnight on 22 September 2012 and the skies above Manhattan are filled with a flickering curtain of colourful light. Few New Yorkers have seen the aurora this far south but their fascination is short-lived. Within a few seconds, electric bulbs dim and flicker, then become unusually bright for a fleeting moment. Then all the lights in the state go out. Within 90 seconds, the entire eastern half of the US is without power.” Remember the crazy Aurora in NY and then two months later Sandy comes.......

According to NOAA's Storm Prediction Center web site, a derecho (pronounced “deh-REY-cho”) is a widespread, long-lived wind storm that is associated with a band of rapidly moving showers or thunderstorms.

Chizhevsky’s research, however, states that derecho storms are but a precursor to the much more damaging events associated with a “Super Event”, a finding made even more terrifying with the 2008 news that NASA's five THEMIS spacecraft had discovered a breach in Earth's magnetic field ten times larger than anything previously thought to exist. Solar wind can flow in through the opening to “load up” the magnetosphere for powerful geomagnetic storms.

RAS scientists in this report blame this “loading up” of the magnetosphere caused by solar flares for causing not only the catastrophic Russian floods, but also our present global weather chaos, to include: 1.) The unrelenting heat wave hitting the United States that has caused at least 30 deaths, hundreds of forest fires and billions in crop damage. 2.) The torrential floods and rain continuing to batter the United Kingdom. 3.) The catastrophic floods that have hit India leaving nearly 100 dead and millions homeless. 4.) The historic and severe drought that has hit northern and central Mexico. 5.) The historic drought that has ravaged Brazil’s northeast.

Most important to note about a “Chizhevsky Super Event”, this report concludes, is that while no one is able to predict exactly what the “main event” may be, the historic record leaves no doubt whatsoever that it is coming with terrifying suddenness and will most certainly upend the entire world as we know it.

Of particular concern for this particular “Chizhevsky Super Event”, say RAS scientists in this report, would be the sudden outbreak of global war combined with an equally catastrophic global flu outbreak such as that which happened last century during the Solar Maximum decade of 1910-1918 that saw the outbreak of World War I and the Spanish Flu Pandemic that combined killed estimated 500 million people. There ya go Cavemam!

Not known to but a few researchers was that in 2001 Canadian researchers warned that influenza epidemics are more likely to sweep the globe when the sun develops spots and sends its excess energy barreling toward Earth such as it is doing now.

### chocolate — 2012-11-04T17:41:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universe-activity-influencing-human-behavior.4677/post-62011>

My grandmother allegedly died of the Spanish Flu. She was issued a death cert and to be buried before sundown. Her brother sat by her side with a shot gun until she came to and deliriously spoke in the night of crossing the great divide. I guess you could call it a shotgun wake. She woke up hungry and ok the next morning. Pretty neat. Everyone thought the river brought it. We had the only well in town above the river level and people drew water from it and left cigarettes and smoked and joked terribly afterwards and when I was a kid, you had to skinny dip with certain kids because they would be beaten if their folks knew they had been in the river. The fear was huge.

And the solar flares are freaky.

### Michael — 2012-11-04T18:27:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universe-activity-influencing-human-behavior.4677/post-62024>

The Spanish Flu was what was behind Oliver Sacks' _Awakenings_, wasn't it?

[http://www.oliversacks.com/books/awakenings/](http://http://www.oliversacks.com/books/awakenings/)

I recall being staggered to read that more people died from the flu epidemic than in the Great War (1914-1918) just preceding.

Then I was staggered all over again to learn a few years after that the War probably played a part in the epidemic. Influenza jumps between humans, birds, and pigs. How did the Allied armies in WWI solve the logistic problem of feeding all those troops? They set up vast farms in France - bacon and eggs for the troops ... i.e. hogs and chickens in large numbers and close confinement ...

> The people who actually know something about the Maya would be spluttering their coffee over the PC with laughing:
>
>
>
>
> > "This is thinking that, in truth, has nothing to do with Mayan culture," said Alexander Voss, an anthropologist at the University Of Quintana Roo, a state on Mexico's Caribbean coast. "This thing about looking for end-times is not something that comes from Mayan culture."
>
>
>
> Quite. The roots of this way of thinking are obvious enough:
>
>
>
> [http://oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/apocalypse](http://http://oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/apocalypse)
>
>
>
> It's coming from a culture that has not always taken the Revelation of St. John the Divine with the grain of salt it deserves. IIRC, that book nearly wasn't in the canon - and perhaps it's a shame it was. Not that the genre isn't older - see the book of Daniel. But you have to know how to read the genre - it's not so much "prophecy" as oblique commentary. It's a way of grumbling about the present under a cloak.
>
>
>
> Other cultures tend to think in cycles not unfolding stories heading in straight lines to a destination:
>
>
>
> Braswell compared the Mayan calendar, with its system of cycles within cycles, to the series of synchronized wheels contained in old, analogue car odometers.
>
>
>
> "The Maya long count system is like a car odometer," Braswell wrote. "\[The odometer on\] my first car only had six wheels so it went up to 99,999.9 miles. That didn't mean the car would explode after reaching 100,000 miles."

[http://www.foxnews.com/science/2012/10/01/experts-meet-to-debunk-mayan-calendar-end-world-stories/](http://http://www.foxnews.com/science/2012/10/01/experts-meet-to-debunk-mayan-calendar-end-world-stories/)

Calm down. The car won't explode, fortunately.

Sunlight would become a rarity for life.........things would radically change.

a variance of even 0.1% is 1.74 petawatts.

every, nuclear, hydro electrc, thermal powerplants, all cars, trucks, warships, toasters and birthday candles all together put out 0.016 petawats of power,

a hurricane, 0.1 petawatts

### Entelechy — 2012-11-05T05:28:26+0200

just looked it up,

The Canadian Journal of Infectious Diseases. 2001, shows correlation between flu pandemics and the 11-year sunspot cycle with a 4fold higher incidence of pandemics at solar peaks. Some of the epidemics studied where in years 1729, 1830, 1918, 1957, 1968 and 1977, all years with statistically significant higher than normal solar activity.

due to the small sample size, the statistical confidence in correlation over random chance is only 98%

**Posts:** 1

### chocolate — 2012-11-04T20:55:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-b-12-i-cant-find-a-good-study-just-blurbs-dsplaces-b-12.4680/post-62067>

[http://curezone.com/forums/am.asp?i=1530791](http://http://curezone.com/forums/am.asp?i=1530791)

> Excessive vitamin C intake is also known to displace vitamin B12 from the body. Vitamin B12 is essential for the maturation of blood cells. Deficiencies of B12 lead to a problem known as macrocytic anemia. This condition leads to the formation of abnormally large red blood cells, with impaired ability to carry oxygen. Decreased oxygen levels may cause fatigue, muscle weakness, shortness of breath, and possibly heart arrhythmias.

If anyone knows of a good study... please share
