# Thread: Growing Pains....  and the Gut
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T22:17:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62079>

My daughter is having growing pains. Specifically pains in her legs in the middle of the night. I'm convinced that it is gut related.... She does have gut issues and as we work on them I notice that the growing pains come when something impacts the gut situation (like eating a food that she's sensitive to).

Anyone have any thoughts / resources / ideas? My initial research hasn't really found anything useful.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-04T22:29:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62080>

My daughter used to have this, too. I didn't notice any correlation with food (but I wasn't paying very good attention back then). She recently had 'sore legs' again (same spot as you mention) and I rubbed magnesium oil over them. This stopped the pain. In her case, I think it was likely related to magnesium deficiency - and this recent 'test' somewhat confirms that. It's not directly gut related, but magnesium is certainly a building block to health, gut included.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T22:58:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62089>

This makes sense... Did it relieve the pain immediately?

I just got some minerals to add to her water figuring that minerals might be an issue. I should see about some magnesium oil too....

### kathylu — 2012-11-05T00:54:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62099>

> **RiverNeighbor;63866 wrote:** This makes sense... Did it relieve the pain immediately?
>
>
>
> I just got some minerals to add to her water figuring that minerals might be an issue. I should see about some magnesium oil too....

Don't waste money on Mg oil. Buy a bag of Epsom salts and dissolve some in warm water until it won't dissolve any more (forming a saturated solution). Use it just like Mg oil (which is a saturated solution of Mg chloride). With epsom salts you get sulfur too (magnesium sulfate)!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-05T01:35:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62105>

> **kathylu;63877 wrote:** Don't waste money on Mg oil. Buy a bag of Epsom salts and dissolve some in warm water until it won't dissolve any more (forming a saturated solution). Use it just like Mg oil (which is a saturated solution of Mg chloride). With epsom salts you get sulfur too (magnesium sulfate)!

I couldn't actually afford 'real' mag oil. What I did was make it from nigari crystals the way Dali Dula suggested. I know there is more in there than just mg, but don't know what the content of the other minerals is.

Yes, RN, the pain subsided quite quickly. The growing pains stopped totally once we started supplementing with oral mg every night, too.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-05T05:27:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62145>

Thanks ladies!

I already give her epsom salt baths..... Tomorrow I'll try some concentrated epsom salt water and do a wrap on her. I'll tell her it's her princess spa day or something. Maybe I'll do it and get my son to do it too. Put our swimsuits on and do it in the yard....

I actually tried giving her some of my Mg tonight (disolved in water)... but she spit it out. She's just not old enough to be able to do it because it might help.

We also tried some Mg Oil spray (it wasn't expensive here.... ), but she went into an itching frenzy.

I was able to get lots of broth and probiotic foods into her today so hopefully we'll get back on track working on her gut and we'll get this resolved.

But I seem to not tolerate them very well in other ways. So now I'm stuck (literally!). I just found out I have an unbilical hernia, and it bothers me more when I'm backed up (or bloated - which is great immediate motivation to avoid the foods that would help with the constipation).

I'm eating mainly sockeye salmon, steelhead trout, scallops, some beef, ghee, a serving of cabbage/carrots, and dark chocolate. Keeping to these foods keeps the bloating at bay. I know my T3 is sub-optimal (low-midrange during the day, low overnight), but not totally horrendous. Doctor won't re-test until mid November.

I'm having surgery on the (umbilical hernia in a week). I would love to get a grip on this little problem before then!

# Thread: Importing webinars into iTunes?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/importing-webinars-into-itunes.4683/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-11-05T02:08:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/importing-webinars-into-itunes.4683/post-62111>

Hello, has anyone out there been able to import a webinar from the computer onto iTunes for import into a phone etc? I can't seem to be able to....with any of the .mp3 files. Hm.....

I so would love to have them with me on the road.

### shilohman — 2012-11-05T04:44:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/importing-webinars-into-itunes.4683/post-62140>

> **hope2conceive;63889 wrote:** Hello, has anyone out there been able to import a webinar from the computer onto iTunes for import into a phone etc? I can't seem to be able to....with any of the .mp3 files. Hm.....
>
>
>
> I so would love to have them with me on the road.

Bring up iTunes

click File->click 'Add to Library'

Navigate to where your .mp3 resides

Open it

Now that you have the .mp3 in your Library do:

In iTunes on the left column you will see Library and under that you will see

Music

click Music

and look for the mp3 that you opened, since the webinar mp3s do not have the any tags

you will not see a lot of information.

click to select the mp3, then click file and then New Playlist from Selection

You should now see the name of the mp3 under Playlist in iTunes

You now can double click on the name and rename it if you want to make the name more meaningful.

Hope this helps.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-05T04:48:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/importing-webinars-into-itunes.4683/post-62141>

Al I sent you a link over skype......make sure Kathy watches it.......very important.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-great-example-of-not-accepting-the-diagnosis-youre-given.4685/post-62122>

An inspiring story of how one woman didn't accept her diagnosis of a learning disorder, and now helps others learn.

Barbara Arrowsmith-Young is the Director of Arrowsmith School and Arrowsmith Program. She holds a B.A.Sc. in Child Studies from the University of Guelph, and a Master's degree in School Psychology from the University of Toronto (Ontario Institute for Studies in Education). Arrowsmith-Young is recognized as the creator of one of the first practical applications of the principles of neuroplasticity to the treatment of learning disorders. Her program is implemented in 40 schools internationally.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-11T15:40:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adrenal-fatigue-rx-brain-gut-16.4686/post-63583>

Huck I almost felt the same. I some how got some infection so I'm treating that atm. I'm also heavy loading my digestion. My focus is stomach acid. This is not based on anyone else's info except for my Sx, overall life Sx and tests. So I work diligently to correct this imbalance. I know that even though I eat super clean doesn't mean I'm magically exempt from any disease or illness coming my way, just means way better resiliance against them and wayyyyy less negative effects. I have to keep in mind my own peculiar 'defects' that my body/genes came in from the start.

I will give my phone as an example. It kept shutting off by itself. I ran some low level tests to determine what could be wrong. One thing I noticed is it seemed to last longer when plugged in. So I determined it could be something with the battery. I went to order a battery (not pay until it arrives) and then test new battery to see if that is the cause. In the interim I still felt like this was not exactly the explanation. It was not really perfect with the plug either...just a little better. So another time when I pulled the battery out I decided to look around on phone itself. (I figured to myself it could be a defect on the hardware and in a place I can't access). I did notice however that four metal things were there and it looked like one was a little crooked. It didn't line up with the other three. I wanted to be careful but I decided to nudge it just slightly. I did. The phone works now. I found the true defect. The battery ends up working just fine. (I know there's a lifetime on it, but for now it is still working).

So how this works to this thread is that I think we can fix a lot of external problems like overall hormones and sometimes forget our own little defects which need to be addressed otherwise we see reversal of health again despite Epi-paleo and circadian cycles. (Albeit they really DO help a lot and make things way less damaging). Please be sure to always identify what is your personal defects as well as what your strengths are.

Here's a personal example. On the surface I was thinking that I only had hormone troubles (primarily the main issue last year that I was most concerned about). Well it turns out that I fixed any surface hormone problems in terms of excess estrogen and the like. I know that this is not my underlying defect though. I know that digestion (for one example) is more of an underlying defect for me since birth. I look at the kinds of things that affected me at birth and childhood versus now. Very similar findings. Even at the onset of puberty I did not have trouble with PMS, or hormones giving trouble. I had a regular period. I did not gain excessive weight, no cramps, or mood swings. I just gained period and breasts. It was not until 3 years later that I started to have more in common with other people about cramps etc. Even still, not until 19 did I for the first time have REALLY bad cramps. So anyways what this means is that hormone imbalance is/was not my primary defect. It only seemed to be 'affected' as a result. So now I sit with reversed hormonal issues and a new focus on my personal digestive issues etc.

Long post so thanks for reading.

### caroline — 2012-11-12T01:37:42+0200

### Michael — 2012-11-05T09:02:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-midden.4688/post-62160>

New Zealand.

Kind of fun - for no particular reason, except that it again reminds us that there are huge piles of shells around:

> The discovery of a cluster of pipi shells has halted a million-dollar roading project.
>
>
>
> Archaeologists and iwi representatives have inspected the midden of shellfish remains, which were found in the side of a hill about 3km from McLaren Falls Rd.
>
>
>
> The cluster of shells was found about a month ago by contractors who were undertaking work to make McLaren Falls Rd wider and safer for vehicles.
>
>
>
> The finding has set back the completion of the project, angering some residents.
>
>
> [http://www.bayofplentytimes.co.nz/news/delays-after-shells-found-in-bank-anger-residents/1576692/](http://http://www.bayofplentytimes.co.nz/news/delays-after-shells-found-in-bank-anger-residents/1576692/)
>
>
>
> I guess one could drive the road through there without delay, forget one saw the shells, and go back to eating a McDonalds in a bun with fries and a coke.
>
>
>
>
>
> ... the average American eats 10 times more omega-6 fatty acids than omega-3, and as a consequence, the United States has the highest rate of obesity in the world. New Zealand also has a high rate of obesity with one in three people overweight, and one in four adults obese.

[http://www.voxy.co.nz/health/us-expert-urges-omega-3-testing/5/139234](http://http://www.voxy.co.nz/health/us-expert-urges-omega-3-testing/5/139234)

# Thread: Low carb diets rocked in the Stone Age
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-diets-rocked-in-the-stone-age.4689/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T09:09:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-diets-rocked-in-the-stone-age.4689/post-62162>

This story is about a year old, but anyway ...

They found what's been found before: that the Neolithic in Northern Europe did _not_ mean everyone suddenly eating a lot of grain. In this case they looked at residues on pottery:

> Analyses of 6,000-year-old pottery reveal the culinary habits of Neolithic residents of todayâ€™s Denmark and Germanyâ€™s Baltic coast.
>
>
>
> Traces of meals show a surprising penchant for fish, shellfish and marine mammals, also after plant cultivation and animal husbandry became well established in the region.
>
>
>
> Transcribed to current fads the finds indicate that Stone Age people retained a lifestyle that continued to include a low carbohydrate diet based on hunting, fishing and gathering.

[http://sciencenordic.com/low-carb-diets-rocked-stone-age](http://http://sciencenordic.com/low-carb-diets-rocked-stone-age)

# Thread: Giant clams
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/giant-clams.4691/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T12:39:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/giant-clams.4691/post-62177>

Again, not a new story - but definitely an interesting one:

**Current Biology, Volume 18, Issue 17, 1349-1354, 9 September 2008

> Giant clams are among the most spectacular but also the most endangered marine invertebrates. Their large size and easy accessibility has caused overfishing and collapse of the natural stocks in many places and local extinction in some of the species \[1,2\]. The diversity of giant clams is extremely low because of reliction in this Tethyan group \[3,4\]. The latest additions of living species date back almost two decades \[5,6,7\], fixing the number of extant Tridacna at seven species \[3\]. Here, we report the discovery of a new species of giant clam: Tridacna costata sp. nov. features characteristic shells with pronounced vertical folds, is genetically distinct, and shows an earlier and abbreviated reproduction than its Red Sea congeners. This species represents less than 1% of the present stocks but up to >80% of the fossil shells. The decline in proportion and shell size (20) indicates overharvesting \[8\] dating back to the early human occupation of the Red Sea >125,000 years ago \[9\]. This earliest depletion reported so far of a shallow-water megafaunal invertebrate has important ramifications for human dispersal out of Africa \[10\].

**

> Its oversight in one of the best-investigated reef provinces \[11,12,13\] illustrates the dearth of knowledge on marine biodiversity.

[http://www.cell.com/current-biology/retrieve/pii/S096098220800969X](http://http://www.cell.com/current-biology/retrieve/pii/S096098220800969X)

These would be easily harvested from shallow water, and would provide plenty of nutrition - they really are pretty large. This is a species of Tridacna costata:

# Thread: Tiny ocean microbes don't just wait for food, they go after it
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-ocean-microbes-dont-just-wait-for-food-they-go-after-it.4692/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T15:12:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-ocean-microbes-dont-just-wait-for-food-they-go-after-it.4692/post-62197>

> A life spent drifting in the currents, eating whatever food they bump into, was once the presumed fate of most microbes living in the ocean.
>
>
>
> But researchers are starting to re-imagine the lives of these tiny microorganisms, which are half the size (or smaller!) of red blood cells. New evidence indicates many ocean microbes are active swimmers, following chemical trails toward food "hotspots." ...

[http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/49648845/ns/technology\_and\_science-science/](http://http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/49648845/ns/technology_and_science-science/)

# Thread: Fat cat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T17:53:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62212>

A literal one â€¦

Milo had to be put on a diet because he got jammed in the cat-flap:

> "He does want food quite a lot. You have to not give in to him because he will pester you. But he goes out more now and heâ€™s much more active."

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[http://www.malverngazette.co.uk/news/10023966.Fat\_cat\_becomes\_a\_streamline\_feline/](http://http://www.malverngazette.co.uk/news/10023966.Fat_cat_becomes_a_streamline_feline/)

I guess this is interesting, because (I assume) Milo won't have been overdoing the carbs. It really does seem to be a case of his eating too much. I guess if we really understood why he was pestering for more food when he'd already eaten we'd know something worth knowing.

### Endless — 2012-11-05T18:42:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62215>

Cats are very easily addicted, and cat food manufacturers take full advantage of this! They can even become addicted to the shape of their kibble! One of my cats was amazingly difficult to transition to a raw food diet and off of his beloved dry food. When he got a chance to indulge he would literally gorge himself until he vomited and then eat more. He became overweight on kibble. All my cats are now on raw and they no longer overeat.

### Michael — 2012-11-05T19:34:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62221>

> **Endless;64016 wrote:** Cats are very easily addicted, and cat food manufacturers take full advantage of this! They can even become addicted to the shape of their kibble! One of my cats was amazingly difficult to transition to a raw food diet and off of his beloved dry food. When he got a chance to indulge he would literally gorge himself until he vomited and then eat more. He became overweight on kibble. All my cats are now on raw and they no longer overeat.

Thank you. That's interesting.

I'm deeply sceptical of the "hyperpalatibility" theory of obesity ... and yet it seems to explain what you've seen with your cat. I'd love to know what Milo was eating. Only a few years ago, it was all tinned meat. Now I suppose dried biscuits are a fair slice of the market. No can to open, and it wouldn't smell. Was that what he got? It's a shame we don't know.

I suppose if he's on the dried food we have to ask what they're putting in it and whether they're using cereals as a cheap filler. So is it the "palatability" of the cat food, or is it carbs in it? But the interesting thing is that Milo was begging for more and your cat would "gorge himself until he vomited and then eat more". That does sound more like an addiction scenario.

Here was I wondering whether it was the food (in one way or another) or maybe disruption of sleep and untimely electric light - or not getting cold enough in winter ...

You reckon the manufacturers deliberately spike the cat food then, working on the basis that, although the cat's not buying, so that that might not seem to be worth it, the owner probably can't resist it's importunity?

### diane — 2012-11-05T21:41:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62248>

> **Michael;64024 wrote:** You reckon the manufacturers deliberately spike the cat food then, working on the basis that, although the cat's not buying, so that that might not seem to be worth it, the owner probably can't resist it's importunity?

Yes - because owners feel good when their pets are excited and eat their food quickly. Then they'll buy it again because their pet likes it. I trust very few pet food manufacturers - and a lot of this is based on discussions and information from people who have been selling petfood for many years.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-05T21:56:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62254>

+1, definitely our experience with our cats!

Two years ago one of our cats was hit by a car. When she was able to walk again, she refused the dry kibble and started stealing raw meat from the dog's bowl. She had lost half her body weight and it was touch and go. We had never fed her raw meat. It was like some sort of survival instinct kicking in. She started gaining her weight back, her coat went full and shiny (it had been falling out) and the broken pelvis stopped limiting her mobility.

What was equally amazing was that we decided to follow her cue and feed all the cats raw meat. The other two (who were chubby and big kibble-overeaters) initially refused to eat it. For several days they meowed like crazy and got really cranky. Finally, they ate. In the course of the following year, they both lost heaps of weight.

So... the same diet caused the two overweight to drop weight and the one underweight to gain. They are all three at a healthy weight now and their raw diet persists. Now they fight over who is first to the bowl!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-05T22:09:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62255>

I loved my cats. So much I calorie restricted them with only the best of foods. They did not go outside and risk death or disease. They got plenty of exercise. Both were potty trained (seriously), but able to protest that procedure by peeing on my laundry when I wasn't looking.

So, when we got to year 20 and had super old cats that threw up all the time and were super moody and frankly annoying - we were starting to wonder if their time was here. They were profoundly cantankerous. Waking me up multiple times a night, killer staring the baby, meowing in the most obnoxious way.

I had those cats since COLLEGE. They had moved from Arkansas to San Diego to Boston to Houston to Colorado. They were long lived and energizer bunny.

As the years went on, cat number two made it to 23 - we just put him down this year, and the doc had many suggestions of alternatives to that. I just wasn't going to give meds to a 23 yr old cat.

The last 2 years were a misery for all - hubby and I anxiously looked for signs that the devils housecat was near then end. As we know, sleep is important and we were not gettin it per kitty.

Probably why this year is my best health year - the darn cat finally departed.

I love cats, the next one I get eat's what he wants in the quantity he wants and if it's cat-crack tasty so be it. He will run outside and suppliment with self provided critters. Most likely he will be one fat, happy short lived thing. And that is the way I want it.

I wonder if my son will take that approach with me. . . "JUST GIVE HER MORE WINE AND CHEETOS - SHE LOVES IT!"

\*\*\*if you think I'm a shameful example and cruel terrible animal rights violating person - I suggest you calorie restrict your cats and do all you can for their health. Note, there is a bonus - less food means less litter cleaning. Please call me when they hit the vomit stage so I can enjoy paying it forward.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-06T00:08:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62272>

> **Cavemam;64061 wrote:**
>
> So, when we got to year 20 and had super old cats that threw up all the time and were super moody and frankly annoying - we were starting to wonder if their time was here. They were profoundly cantankerous. Waking me up multiple times a night, killer staring the baby, meowing in the most obnoxious way.
>
> The last 2 years were a misery for all - hubby and I anxiously looked for signs that the devils housecat was near then end. As we know, sleep is important and we were not gettin it per kitty.

Cavemam, I had a similar experience with two cats. One was convinced that for 20 years we had been trying to kill her. She seldom relaxed in our presence. We fed them dry food, mostly. The older they got, the stranger they got. I feel bad now, knowing that they were suffering from malnutrition. Guess dressed-up corn only goes so far for an obligate carnivore.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-06T00:15:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62273>

> **Nonchalant;64079 wrote:** Cavemam, I had a similar experience with two cats. One was convinced that for 20 years we had been trying to kill her. She seldom relaxed in our presence. We fed them dry food, mostly. The older they got, the stranger they got. I feel bad now, knowing that they were suffering from malnutrition. Guess dressed-up corn only goes so far for an obligate carnivore.

Yea, I think back when I first got them. . .20+ yrs ago science diet was the be all end all on feeding. And the dry-only mantra. What I find fascinating, and wonder how it applies to us. . . .they were very long lived and never sick. We didn't have to do one real vet visit their entire lives. . just the shots before airplane rides.

They for sure got stranger as older, I was frankly scared of the last one a little bit. The last year he was just a sack of bones and he could sneak up and you wouldn't realize he was on you. Then he would just stare.

Not all the years are good ones! We might take care to remember that for ourselves.

### Michael — 2012-11-06T09:17:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62321>

> **diane;64054 wrote:** Yes - because owners feel good when their pets are excited and eat their food quickly. Then they'll buy it again because their pet likes it. I trust very few pet food manufacturers - and a lot of this is based on discussions and information from people who have been selling petfood for many years.

It's about time that someone did an expose of what pet food manufacturers are up to then.

It would certainly make entertaining prime-time TV, although some stations might not want to broadcast it on account they might lose commercials for pet food.

Some background talking about Pottenger's experiments; add lots of shots of overweight animals, and point out that doesn't occur in the wild; reveal what's actually in the food. Throw in a bit of Tom Naughton style humour. You'd have a winner.

If that is what's going on what it amounts to cheating people out of money - creating a situation by chemical means where people end up buying more of something than they need - and knowing they've been fleeced and manipulated is something people cannot stand.

It's also interesting to know that zoos that have fed gorillas on "gorilla biscuits" instead of real food have ended up with sick and overweight gorillas:

[http://blog.cleveland.com/metro/2011/07/new\_diet\_for\_apes\_at\_cleveland.html](http://http://blog.cleveland.com/metro/2011/07/new_diet_for_apes_at_cleveland.html)

### Inger — 2012-11-07T09:37:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62494>

So what did this story tells us, if we really listen?

Maybe we should eat more raw too...

I have a 13 yo cat and I feed her mostly raw (the occasional canned tuna when I had no time to go shopping for her, and precooked shrimps wich are her favorit food) and 100% carnivore. She is an amazing cat. Very clever, very healthy.

Anyone that struggles with overweight.. start to eat raw. I bet you will loose the weight fast...!

(This is also my own Nr1.)

### Michael — 2012-11-07T12:26:08+0200

### Michael — 2012-11-07T12:44:27+0200

Why is this not a surprise?

calories in >>>

cardio exercise

### Inger — 2012-11-07T19:21:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62597>

> **Cavemam;64402 wrote:** Or don't. I'm firmly under the idea that a short lived cat is best for all!!!

Yeah.. I totally agree.. if you feed your cat something else than a fresh carnivore diet..lol

When cats get weird foods they get weird for sure. My cat was having pains when peeing when I got her 5 years back. She was fed catfood from the store by her previous owner. I started barfing her and soon there was no pain at all anymore when going to the toilet. She is the most amazing cat now. So wild.. so clever! And full of love and peace. She always wants me to go early to bed, and wants me to wake up early too..lol. It is like she knows my needs.. She loves to look into the sun too with me together. IDK, I will be sad for sure when she is gone..

Cats need to have access to fresh grass too BTW. At least once in a while.

### Linz — 2012-11-08T14:28:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62865>

Thirty years ago in the UK 'least cost' feeding was heavily emphasised in agricultural education and computer programmes were rapidly developing to reduce costs further and further. Most farm animals don't need to live long lives so the health problems weren't obvious (and what harm could it do to us?) and farmers' concerns that ingredients were not being declared - just general energy and protein levels and some nutrient content - were ignored.

Now read the ingredients on any can or packet of food for our carnivorous pets that we hope will live long and healthy lives. If only it was raw v. cooked...... how much meat, bone etc of any description is included? And back to least cost, most milk, most meat. How much was included in our poor herbivores' diets when extra protein could increase output?

I sent a letter to a major animal food company questioning how the ingredients were likely to affect my cat's health. Their response, a voucher entitling me to several free cans!!

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-08T15:40:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62875>

I lost my kitty Zeke two weeks ago. I was just thinking about his mom this morning and how it was really amazing to watch her hunt birds and mice (farm cat) and how sometimes she felt like dropping one off on the doorstep for us. One time I had been fishing in the creek and caught a carp. I brought it up to throw on the compost heap and she brought her litter of kittens (a litter before Zeke's) and you should have seen them all work on eating that fish!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Hack these three labs, please?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### maggie — 2012-11-05T19:04:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62216>

Would really appreciate any/all comments on the following:

Pregnenolone, MS 81/ng/dL (Range: Adults:

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-05T19:45:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62223>

The pregnenolone range from your lab has probably adjusted for your age. Usually down as you get older. I think Dzugan uses a range that is more useful to feeling good and for your body. Preg is the precursor to a lot of other hormones down the chain.

Your FT3 is super duper low. You poor thing, you must be exhausted all the time. Basically with a ratio of 14.2 your body is converting the FT4 into rT3 instead of FT3. You also may not have enough FT4 in your system but w/o labs that is a little harder to determine. You definitely have something going on with your thyroid. I would highly suggest autoimmune thyroid antibodies testing to see if you have Hashimotos (TPO & TgAB).

These labs as they are only tell you bits and pieces of the story. In your situation, I would guess there is more going on than just what is showing up here. More labs are probably necessary. I hope that helps you a little....

### maggie — 2012-11-05T20:22:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62227>

Thanks very much, Patty Cakes. It makes a lot of sense that the lab would adjust for age (I'm 70). Dzugan of course doesn't do that.

These are labs I had done after a consult last week with Dr. K, so they're not the only ones. If you think I look exhausted from the T3 levels, you shoulda seen the cortisol and melatonin numbers! Completely in the toilet. The thyroid antibodies looked OK, but there may be something brewing with dairy, so you're right, there's more to the story--probably hashi's which wasn't revealed by the thyroid antibodies because I haven't been eating grain for 5+ years.

I had these additional thyroid labs done because the doc thought that my very high cholesterol would be related to reverse T3 + low free T3 preventing cholesterol conversion to pregnenolone. Looks like the doc hit a home run here, as he did with the Adrenal Stress Index + melatonin test. Sheesh, what did we do before Dr K?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-05T20:29:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62229>

I know, right? Gotta love it when you find something that works, like this amazing full body investigation we are all doing.

Sounds like you are on your way to figuring it out and now you know what to work on ..... I hope you have a good doc who will recognize what is going on. You'll feel like your 50 when you get this all worked out!!! Good luck!

### vkiernan — 2012-11-05T20:45:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62234>

> **Patty Cakes;64026 wrote:** Your FT3 is super duper low. You poor thing, you must be exhausted all the time. Basically with a ratio of 14.2 your body is converting the FT4 into rT3 instead of FT3. You also may not have enough FT4 in your system but w/o labs that is a little harder to determine. You definitely have something going on with your thyroid. I would highly suggest autoimmune thyroid antibodies testing to see if you have Hashimotos (TPO & TgAB)

Patty, Please help me understand if her t4 is converting to rt3, why isn't that number high? In fact, it is very low. Is her body able to rid itself of it? What am I missing here? Thanks much.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-05T20:53:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62237>

She probably doesn't have much FT4 to convert in the first place. At least that was what I was thinking originally. She is probably super low on all her thyroid hormones. I think it is about balance between all of the thyroid entities. But it does show her body is still converting improperly. At least that is my interpretation. Does that sound right?

Sorry Maggie about talking about you in the third person on your thread.

### maggie — 2012-11-05T21:01:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62239>

Talk on! I don't mind at all, and I appreciate the input.

I had more complete labs done a month ago, but I think that they were skewed because I took all my supps about 2 hours before the blood draw (as suggested by LEF). T4 came back within range, but T3 was high--out of range, so I dropped down 1/4 grain. I probably should have had the whole panel redone this time, but wasn't thinking about T4... just wanted the t3/rt3 ratio. darn.

Reason I think last month's numbers on the blood test were skewed is that DHEA-S came back on that one over 1000, while on the saliva test for the ASI, DHEA\_S came in low. I also took oral DHEA in the am before the blood test.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-05T21:09:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62240>

I think T3 peaks in your system around 1.5-2 hours after ingestion and then starts to drop off. SO you probably tested when it was at it's highest. In my opinion, FT3 testing is difficult when you are taking T3. I take my last T3 at around 3 pm (sometimes I add a dab later). I tested the following morning at 7:30 and I was around 2.2. But I hadn't had any T3 in my system for over 12 hours and I think it only lasts around 8 hours so it should be low. But like how you tested it should be high.

I wonder if anyone knows the best time to test FT3 when taking T3?????? I have read that it's a bugger to figure that out and may be an individual thing based on one's own system and that a person should go by how they feel.

### maggie — 2012-11-05T21:13:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62242>

Aaah, love all this good information. Thank you. Should have mentioned that I take Armour, but had just started a compounded form, and I'm sure I was absorbing the granules in the gel capsule a lot better/faster than the armour tablet. At least I felt that way right after I switched to the compounded. So I think you're right that taking it at 6 would have poked my levels up by a little after 8.

### JanSz — 2012-11-06T02:56:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62297>

> **Maggie;64031 wrote: Thanks very much, Patty Cakes. It makes a lot of sense that the lab would adjust for age (I'm 70). Dzugan of course doesn't do that.
>
>
>
> These are labs I had done after a consult last week with Dr. K, so they're not the only ones. If you think I look exhausted from the T3 levels, you shoulda seen the cortisol and melatonin numbers! Completely in the toilet.** The thyroid antibodies looked OK, but there may be something brewing with dairy, so you're right, there's more to the story--probably hashi's which wasn't revealed by the thyroid antibodies because I haven't been eating grain for 5+ years.
>

>
> I had these additional thyroid labs done because the doc thought that my very high cholesterol would be related to reverse T3 + low free T3 preventing cholesterol conversion to pregnenolone. Looks like the doc hit a home run here, as he did with the Adrenal Stress Index + melatonin test. Sheesh, what did we do before Dr K?

Post all your labs.

# Thread: Chinese building massive DHA facility
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T19:51:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62225>

> A microalgae DHA production project was carried out by Qingdao Langyatai (Group) Co Ltd and Qingdao Institute of Bioenergy and Bioprocess Technology, and launched in Jiaonan to extract DHA from microalgae.
>
>
>
> It is located in Jiaonan Seaside Industrial Park covering an area of 2.67 hectare, the overall floorage of which is about 11, 940 square meters. With proprietary intellectual property rights, it is mapped out to achieve the DHA production scale of 1000-t/a and turn into the largest national microalgae DHA production base in three years.
>
>
>
> DHA is one of the major components of the human brain, which can be extracted from ocean fish or microorganism with DHA, such as microalgae. As the main DHA source in the past, producing DHA from fish oil is simple and cheap. It faces a difficult position due to water pollution and fishery resources depletion. Ocean microalgae is the major substitution of marine fish; therefore it has a broad market for the future.

[http://qingdao.chinadaily.com.cn/2012-10/26/content\_15850378.htm](http://http://qingdao.chinadaily.com.cn/2012-10/26/content_15850378.htm)

### ruby — 2012-11-06T06:29:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62316>

They expect DHA to be a money spinner. Problem is, as with loads of things in China, to milk this cow dry corners will be cut, for sure.

Funny thing, though, China is always quoted by the pro-carb brigade as the crowning proof that rice and grains are innocuous if not downright essential for health. But in my experience, the Chinese always consider rice and grain products "fillers", something to have at the end of the meal to top up real food in case it was not enough. Scoffing on rice has always been a financial resources issue not a dietary/health choice issue.

### Michael — 2012-11-06T09:02:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62319>

Maybe it'll be going in the rations of the Red Army. LOL

### Michael — 2012-11-06T15:35:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62352>

I shouldn't make jokes, should I?

The Chinese Army is reporting the US DOD trial - and doubtless looking on with interest:

[http://www.chnarmy.com/html/2012-10/29886.html](http://http://www.chnarmy.com/html/2012-10/29886.html)

They probably read Jack's blogs and all.

### ruby — 2012-11-07T01:52:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62455>

"The Chinese Army is reporting the US DOD trial - and doubtless looking on with interest"

Seems more likely they are thinking along the lines of "gee, how behind are they?"

They are quite on the ball with this stuff, they just don't make it public. Not unike their northern neighbours

# Thread: Fritdjof Nansen
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fritdjof-nansen.4696/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T21:26:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fritdjof-nansen.4696/post-62243>

I like all these quotes:

> Never keep a line of retreat: it is a wretched invention.
>
>
>
> I demolish my bridges behind me - then there is no choice but forward.
>
>
>
> It is better to go skiing and think of God, than go to church and think of sport.
>
>
>
> War will cease when men refuse to fight.
>
>
>
> Never stop because you are afraid - you are never so likely to be wrong.
>
>
>
> The difficult is what takes a little time; the impossible is what takes a little longer.
>
>
> [http://galaxyofquotes.sumobaby.net/authors\_quotes.php?authorId=6](http://http://galaxyofquotes.sumobaby.net/authors_quotes.php?authorId=6)
>
>
>
> I was wandering through our county town a couple of weeks ago and a second-hand bookshop had in the window a copy of the first edition in English translation of _Farthest North. Wish I could have afforded it.
>
>
>
> An amazing man - in exploration, in diplomacy, in science.
>
>
>
> Wikpedia only mentions Nansen and science with reference to the expeditions in the Fram_:
>
>
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fridtjof\_Nansen#Scientist\_and\_polar\_oracle](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fridtjof_Nansen#Scientist_and_polar_oracle)
>
>
>
> But there's much more:
>
>
>
> Did you think that Fridtjof Nansen only led expeditions to the Arctic? If you did, you were mistaken. Welcome to the world of neuroscience at the Faculty of Medicine. Here, Nansen's legacy is everywhere.

[http://www.med.uio.no/klinmed/english/research/news-and-events/news/2011/nansen-brain-legacy.html](http://http://www.med.uio.no/klinmed/english/research/news-and-events/news/2011/nansen-brain-legacy.html)

[http://www.apollon.uio.no/english/articles/2008/hjernen-nansen.html](http://http://www.apollon.uio.no/english/articles/2008/hjernen-nansen.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-06T02:58:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fritdjof-nansen.4696/post-62299>

interesting read to say the least of this man's life.

### Inger — 2012-11-06T10:33:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fritdjof-nansen.4696/post-62322>

Yes, intresting. My dad is from Bergen. I need to ask him what he knows of Nansen. I loved this quote too, the last sentence especially..

> We all have a Land of Beyond to seek in our lifeâ€”what more can we ask? Our part is to find the trail that leads to it. A long trail, a hard trail, maybe; but the call comes to us, and we have to go. Rooted deep in the nature of every one of us is the spirit of adventure, the call of the wildâ€”vibrating under all our actions, making life deeper and higher and nobler.\[152\]

### caroline — 2012-11-06T13:01:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fritdjof-nansen.4696/post-62332>

> **Inger;64132 wrote:** Yes, intresting. My dad is from Bergen. I need to ask him what he knows of Nansen. I loved this quote too, the last sentence especially..

# Thread: Staying optimal while traveling
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-11-05T21:46:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62249>

I used to have to travel fairly often for work. Thankfully the economy took a nose dive about the same time as my health (ok, no - I'm not thankful that the economy sucks, but the timing was kind of advantageous for me, just sayin') and due to budget cuts I really only had to make a few overnight trips a year. For the most part they've all been within driving distance (< 8 hours or so) and were only for 2 nights, which made things easier.

I'm now headed to Orlando for business the week after Thanksgiving and will be gone for a full week (4 nights business + 3 nights pleasure!). Any suggestions to help with staying on the right track?

I'm already planning tons of seafood, of course. I'm staying in a condo so I'll have a full kitchen. I'm going to try to hit Daytona Beach once or twice for a dip since it's only about an hour from Orlando.

I'm just a little nervous about the whole trip messing with my routine. In one regard I think it may actually be good for me - a chance to relax and unwind. DH is coming down for the last 4 nights and we'll make a mini-vaca out of it, which no doubt will be great for us. On the other hand, I feel like I've finally got this lifestyle down with a good routine - CT first thing in the AM before work, lights out at dark, etc, etc. I hate to stray from that.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-06T00:55:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62284>

Bring your sleep mask and orange goggles.

I try to get a room far from the automobiles. It seems that people like to beep their car alarms all night long.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-06T01:38:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62288>

For the most part they've all been within driving distance (< 8 hours or so) and were only for 2 nights, which made things easier.
>

>

>
>
>
> I'm just a little nervous about the whole trip messing with my routine. In one regard I think it may actually be good for me - a chance to relax and unwind. DH is coming down for the last 4 nights and we'll make a mini-vaca out of it, which no doubt will be great for us. On the other hand, I feel like I've finally got this lifestyle down with a good routine - CT first thing in the AM before work, lights out at dark, etc, etc. I hate to stray from that.

Routine, schmootine. Enjoy yourself!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-06T02:38:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62294>

I stay cold and I eat no differently......sleeping on the road is the roughest for me......so I turn the room into an icebox. When my wife is not around it is harder for me to sleep.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-06T02:57:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62298>

I bring enough food for the trip and rarely eat out. Seaweed snacks, canned salmon, bacon, grilled hamburgers, etc. I use my sleep mask and orange glasses like Nonchalant suggested. While every thing I do while traveling is not optimal (staying up too late and business meetings-no glasses there!) I try to keep up the good work but don't obsess about it.

### caroline — 2012-11-06T10:39:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62323>

> **Jack Kruse;64101 wrote:** I stay cold and I eat no differently......sleeping on the road is the roughest for me......so I turn the room into an icebox. When my wife is not around it is harder for me to sleep.

I think the biggest reason I think you are so cool \[apart from your great brain and what you share with all of us\] is the high regard you have for your wife. I love that.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-06T13:26:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62334>

I've taken 2 business trips since embracing optimal.

1st time around the hotel we stayed in provided a full breakfast of eggs bacon sausage w/the room rate. WIN for the rest of my meals I chose only foods that were meat/veggies -sine we were in Texas that was pretty easy

2nd time around I brought oysters & sardines bpc (brought my own grounds, ghee, coconut oil, camping coffee maker & stick blender). I ate fresh gulf shrimp for lunch every day oysters & sardines for BAB & dinner. And damn was I hot. I kept my room AC at 66 and it was still too hot

Slept like a baby but then again I was at an on airport hotel and I always sleep better when I'm in the pattern.

### BJK77 — 2012-11-06T21:07:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62406>

Thanks for all the tips and advice. I think I'll make an effort to reap the benefits of both being away and relaxing while also (hopefully) sticking to my routine as much as possible. Will definitely pack the orange glasses and some candles . . . they'll come in handy when DH is down anyway

Truth is, we're usually in bed these days by 8:30 anyway! LOL! Sooo, it's not like we're party animals. I'm pretty sure we'll still crash early. I'm just hoping the first few days there on business don't mess with me too much. I'm sure I won't sleep well, so I'll really have to focus on diet and cold.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T15:40:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62508>

Im getting ready to travel and I have to decided to embark on a new biohack.........fasting for the entire trip. It eliminates the question and I want to see how it works.......I'll let you know on Nov 10th how it went.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62509>

Wow, that's interesting...How long will you be traveling?

# Thread: The 7th most important thing about... everything
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Barry — 2012-11-06T01:12:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/post-62285>

I've been busy running and sorting out my career (more on that to come), but this post is a little different. I think it applies to most people, it applies to me, and I'm sure it applies to Jack

Its summed up here by Steve Jobs

â€œYour time is limited, so don't waste it living someone else's life. Don't be trapped by dogma - which is living with the results of other people's thinking. Don't let the noise of other's opinions drown out your own inner voice. And most important, have the courage to follow your heart and intuition. They somehow already know what you truly want to become. Everything else is secondary."

To put this into more perspective, consider what you read here on this forum and what I have already posted about my sport and training. Health is ultimately about happiness. Your work and life dictates that happiness. It doesn't matter about how many kilo's of fish you had last month or how many grammes of carbs you had. Like I said before, I'm all about the nutrition, but I;ve realized that nutrition is only an "add-on" to your general make up. In other words, nutrition doesn't control your existence. Your brain controls your existence. How you think and what you think, and more importantly, the manipulation of your thoughts are governed by how you live. If you live a life of discontentment, stress etc.... then nothing else really matters. You are destined to be unhappy.

So when it comes to work and living and pursuits, follow your instinct. Don't let the noise of so called "know it all" paleo experts and personal trainers tell you what you should do. You need to tell you what YOU like doing. It doesn't matter if that means working in some low paid unusual job, and it doesn't matter if that means doing some sort of exercise that doesn't conform. What matters is that you are following your own inner voice and truly believing and enjoying how you live and what you do. You see, people want you to be like them and to stay that way. They don't like when you do things differently and it works. So don't rely on the opinion of others and doubt yourself. Make it on your own.

"Beyond the walls of intelligence, life is defined"

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-06T13:54:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/post-62340>

I stumbled into what I'm good at w/regards to work. It was pure luck that I happens to meet a distinguished leader in logistics in the fall of my senior yr. I went back after Thanksgiving break and changed my career selection to transportation, logistics, logistics planning & supply. I was 1of 2 classmates that got their 1st choice! The other guy got a pilot slot I became a transportation & logistics plans officer. About 9 yrs ago I wasn't doing what I loved and I was miserable. It took the death if my grandfather suddenly to jolt me out of what I was doing. 2 months later I began working with my current company doing logistic analysis and planning. I'm very fortunate that I get to do what I love every day. I'm one of very few women who have carved out a niche in logistics.

What's amazing is how much I'm improved my performance living an optimal life. I rarely have the days where my job sucks -and when I do I get over it rather quickly b/c those little annoyances don't matter. I'm sharp and in focus every day. I'm faster at completing analysis - such that my co-workers ask me how I do it - I point to my lunch of seafood.

Its amazing how things fall into alignment when your working from a place of being optimal.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T01:57:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/post-62459>

> **Barry;64092 wrote:**
>
> You see, people want you to be like them and to stay that way. They don't like when you do things differently and it works. So don't rely on the opinion of others and doubt yourself. Make it on your own.

Thank you for this, Barry. I know you'll find yourself in the career you want.

### caroline — 2012-11-11T07:35:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/post-63548>

Thanks Barry for posting that. I was lucky enough to work at something I loved\[and was really good at\] - with the person I loved for 35 years. Doesn't get much better than that I think.

this is nothing to do with CT. when im in my room and it is cold, my right hand gets cold faster than my left.

actually my whole body is okay and remains in perfect sync in terms of feeling cold, just that my right hand feels colder.

i have to get a light partial glove so my right hand doesnt get too cold.

can anybody explain if anything is wrong or is this normal? i am left handed by the way.

# Thread: Endangered River Turtle's Genes Reveal Ancient Influence of Maya Indians
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endangered-river-turtles-genes-reveal-ancient-influence-of-maya-indians.4703/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-06T16:21:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endangered-river-turtles-genes-reveal-ancient-influence-of-maya-indians.4703/post-62357>

From August 2011

> A genetic study focusing on the Central American river turtle (Dermatemys mawii) recently turned up surprising results for a team of Smithsonian scientists involved in the conservation of this critically endangered species. Small tissue samples collected from 238 wild turtles at 15 different locations across their range in Southern Mexico, Belize and Guatemala revealed a "surprising lack" of genetic structure, the scientists write in a recent paper in the journal Conservation Genetics. ...
>
>
>
> "We were expecting to find a different genetic lineage in each drainage basin," explains the paper's main author ... "Instead, we found the mixing of lineages. ... Despite appearing isolated, the genetic data showed the different turtle populations had been in close contact for years.
>
>
>
> "But how?" the researchers wondered.
>
>
>
> The best possible explanation, GonzÃ¡lez-Porter and her colleagues say, is that for centuries humans have been bringing them together. The turtles have been used as food, in trade and in rituals for millennia, widely transported and customarily kept in holding ponds till they were needed.
>
>
>
> "For centuries, this species has been part of the diet of the Mayans and other indigenous people who lived in its historic distribution range," the scientists point out in their paper. "D. mawii was a very important source of animal protein for the ancient Mayans of the Peten (Preclassic period 800-400 B.C.)â€¦. And it is possible that these turtles were part of the diet of the Olmec culture more than 3,000 years ago." ...
>
>
> [http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/07/110722112106.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/07/110722112106.htm)
>
>
>
> Interestingly, the turtle seems to have been seen as sacred:
>
>
>
> !e turtle carapace scene shows a youthful
>
> deity figure splitting out of a sea shell on
>
> a plate. !is resurrection from a giant turtle
>
> carapace is one of the truly important moments
>
> in Mayan cosmology.

[www.maya-archaeology.org/...archaeology.../30\_Sacred\_turtles\_in\_Mayan\_art\_and\_iconography\_Revue\_Magazine-Guatemala-August-2011.pdf](http://http://www.maya-archaeology.org/...archaeology.../30_Sacred_turtles_in_Mayan_art_and_iconography_Revue_Magazine-Guatemala-August-2011.pdf)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-excavations-will-reveal-diet-of-skokomish-sklallam-and-suquamish-tribes.4705/post-62361>

That should be an interesting report when it comes out. It should be no surprise that these people were exploiting both land and water resources - exactly what remains to be revealed:

> "A shell midden is really refuse accumulations from people living there, so there is a lot of information in those deposits about their diets â€” fish bones, deer bones, elk bones, seals, things like that," Lewarch said.
>
>
>
> He called it a time capsule that goes back 700 to 800 years. ...
>
>
>
> "It's concrete evidence that is expressed today orally in the traditions of the tribes that use Hood Canal. We know people hunted and fished and collected shellfish. This can actually tell us what species they collected and what kind of fish they fished for."

[http://www.kitsapsun.com/news/2012/nov/04/shell-midden-could-affirm-tribes-stories-of-hood/](http://http://www.kitsapsun.com/news/2012/nov/04/shell-midden-could-affirm-tribes-stories-of-hood/)

# Thread: Video:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video.4708/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-06T20:58:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video.4708/post-62404>

Video:

> Caves in South Africa reveal remnants of a nutrient-rich, seafood diet that my have contributed to early man's cognitive ability.
>
>
> [http://video.nationalgeographic.com/video/news/ng-today/112811-prehistoric-caves-ngtoday/](http://http://video.nationalgeographic.com/video/news/ng-today/112811-prehistoric-caves-ngtoday/)
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Discoveries in South Africa are important in this area. See:
>
>
>
>
> > Over the last forty years, South Africa has played a prominent role in recording the earliest evidence of shellfish in and substantial expansion of the early human diets as shown by several Middle Stone Age (MSA) coastal sites along the west and south coasts. ...
> >
> >
> >
> > **Possible cognitive implications of shellfish gathering as a reflection of modern behavior are also suggested.**
>
> **
>
>
> [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248410001296](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248410001296)
>
>
>
> This cave is at Pinnacle point:
>
>
>
> At PP13B the evidence for symbolic behavior comes in the form of scraped and ground ochre (a naturally occurring bright red rock) that may have been used to form a pigment for body painting. This is similar to more complex ochre utilization known from Blombos Cave slightly further to the west at roughly 70,000 years ago.\[4\] **These discoveries contradicts the classical hypothesis that the modern behaviour emerged only 40,000 years ago and was reached through a "large cultural leap".**\[1\] The harsh climate and reduced food resources may have been why people moved to the shore at Pinnacle Point, where they could eat marine creatures like shellfish, whale, and seal**.\[5\]

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinnacle\_Point](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinnacle_Point)

# Thread: Waterâ€™s Edge Ancestors
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-06T21:16:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/post-62412>

This is a good round-up of work at Pinnacle point and elsewhere by _Science News.

> In a cave hugging South Africaâ€™s lush southern coastline, Curtis Marean suspects he has cornered a wily Stone Age crew that brought humans back from extinctionâ€™s brink. These plucky refugees of continent-wide desolation were able to pull off such a stunning evolutionary turnaround because they got lucky. A coastal oasis near the bottom of the world spread its sheltering arms in the nick of time.
>
>
>
> Marean proposes that it was there, where the Arizona State University archaeologist now conducts excavations, that **humankindâ€™s mental tide turned sometime between 164,000 and 120,000 years ago. Seaside survivors learned to read the moonâ€™s phases in order to harvest heaps of shellfish â€” **brain food extraordinaire â€” during a few precious days each month when ocean tides safely retreated. ...
>
>
> Lots more. The full article is well worth a read.
>
>
>
> Another good short quote:
>
>
>
>
> > ... **some scientists now argue that red meat has been oversold as a dietary staple.**
> >
> >
> >
> > At a meeting of the American Association of Physical Anthropologists, held in Minneapolis in April \[this was 2011\], researchers argued that ancient menus focused heavily on food from lakes, rivers and oceans. New work presented at the meeting pointed to lakeside fishing in East Africa nearly 2 million years ago, the shoreline shellfish harvesting among Homo sapiens at Pinnacle Point starting more than 160,000 years ago and sea voyages to Pacific Ocean islands by an unlikely group of New World settlers around 12,000 years ago.
>
> **
>
> > ...
>
>
>
> As well as the Arizona State University people ([and see website here](http://http://iho.asu.edu)), a researcher from the University of Oregon in Eugene is quoted. And so is South African anthropologist David Braun of the University of Cape Town:
>
>
>
> â€œA large rock is probably all that ancient hominids would have needed to catch fish,â€** Braun said at the anthropology meeting. Even simpler fishing techniques may extend deep into primate prehistory. Orangutans have been found to snatch catfish from shallow ponds bare-handed and chase the finned snacks out of deeper water with poking sticks

[http://www.sciencenews.org/view/feature/id/332828/title/Waterâ€™s\_Edge\_Ancestors](http://http://www.sciencenews.org/view/feature/id/332828/title/Waterâ€™s_Edge_Ancestors)

Isn't it interesting to know that ourang-outangs will do this?

It's kind of embarrassing that Cordain can be found saying from time to time that he works closely with archaeologists and anthropologists. Clearly he doesn't really_ pay that much attention to what's going on in that world or paleo would know what these people are doing and saying. He wouldn't still be pushing the ninety-year-old savannah hypothesis. When did you last see a mention of Pinnacle Point on a paleo site? If it ever has popped up on a major paleo site it's done so very quietly and briefly.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T04:17:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/post-62479>

Very interesting article. NIce find, Michael.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T04:22:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/post-62480>

Michael I have been saying that for a while.......Guys that protect the paleo franchise are the ones who want me to be silent.......but the science makes my words bolder.

### Michael — 2012-11-07T08:46:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/post-62493>

I don't mean to grumble about people in the movement. There's a bit too much of that already on many paleo sites. Just to mention what they seem to be missing (and perhaps imply some people shouldn't make quite such large claims and take quite such a confident tone). It's difficult not to point at online resources like that and not see the incongruity of what they're saying.

It's not just Cordain. Robb's the same. Again, I don't want to make personal criticism. As a person, he seems a nice bloke. But how many times has he metaphorically speaking "dined out" on the story of hitting an elk with a spear-thrower dart on "I, Caveman"? I don't get U.S. TV but as I understand it, they took him and some others to a high plateau and ... well, lets's stop there. He obviously didn't say to them: "A more realistic scenario would be to set us down near a well-stocked river. Or on a seacoast with plenty of shellfish and regularly visited by marine mammals." He wouldn't have said that, because he wouldn't have known.

I don't think "cavemen" would set up camp somewhere _away from_ resources. Why would they do that? The picture of throwing missile weapons at a venture even seems problematic to me. Doubtless it was a way of procuring food at times, but I'm sure hunting must much of the time have been much more conceptually developed and far more organized. There's a classic tale of travel in 18th century Canada. Ray Mears has discussed it on TV before - Samuel Hearne's journey. At one point they stop to hunt and spend some days in the forest filling gaps with brushwood and felling trees in other places. They then drive the deer into this arrangement which funnels them into a killing zone. And that's just a few men on a journey.

Throwing a missile at a venture at a four-footed animal is probably what the average person pictures "cavemen" doing.

This gets even more questionable when we start thinking about, say, Homo erectus, let alone Australopithecines.

Strangely, J. R. R. Tolkien seems to have intuited the answer to how you feed yourself without much infrastructure.

Gollum, of course, is a solitary creature. (His real name, Smeagol, is cognate with an Anglo-Saxion word meaning selfishness, which is a linguistic game on Tolkien's part.) He's fallen out of society. He's got no shops to go to; he's not connected with a social network in which people are growing, trading, preparing, cooking, selling, and serving foodstuffs. He's got no tools. He hasn't even got a tinderbox. What can he do? He can catch fish with his bare hands and devour them raw.

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;fYiCPmwOV4A\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fYiCPmwOV4A\[/video\]\[/video\]

A small hominid with, at this point, limited strength and intellectual abilities and little or no speech would be in much the same position.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: low vit D = longevity.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vit-d-longevity.4712/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-07T01:29:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vit-d-longevity.4712/post-62449>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/11/121105130355.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/11/121105130355.htm)

Previous studies have shown that low levels of vitamin D are associated with increased rates of death, heart disease, diabetes, cancer, allergies, mental illness and other afflictions. However, it is not known whether low levels are the **cause or a consequence** of these diseases.

### janagram — 2012-11-07T02:02:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vit-d-longevity.4712/post-62460>

Oil™ contains nothing but 100% pure sea buckthorn from the Himalayan mountains. Unlike most other products, it ---

I copied this from an ad....just wondering: what is sea buckthorn, and what is it doing in the Himalayas? there are no beaches up there.

### ruby — 2012-11-07T02:37:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vit-d-longevity.4712/post-62472>

Hippophae rhamnoides is a native plant throughout Europe, including Britain, from Norway south and east to Spain and Asia to Japan and the Himalayas. It is grown as an agricultural plant in Germany, France, Finland, India and China. China is the largest agricultural producer. The origin of the plant is Nepal and it migrated to other parts of Eurasia after the last ice age.

The fruits of sea buckthorn are used in a wide variety of products. Due to difficult harvest conditions and long ramp-up time of 6 to 8 years buckthorn is a relatively expensive raw material. Especially in France (southern Alps) sea buckthorn is commonly sold as fruit juice or as an ingredient in non-alcoholic and alcoholic mixed beverages. Other uses include the berries to be processed as fruit wine or into liquor as well as jam. Buckthorn tea is also made out of the fruits and originates from India.

The fruits have a very high vitamin C content, on average exceeding the vitamin C content of lemons and oranges.

# Thread: Raw Fish/Meat for beginners (me)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-11-07T02:35:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62471>

I want to start eating raw fish/meat. Sadly I've only had raw oysters (once), and I know I need to move more in this direction. And I'm ready/willing. But I want to start with easier things (no lamb's heart in a jar just yet for me). I want to start with foods I can source easily to get me started (I often stop before I start when the initial step is a big one (like sourcing fresh/unfrozen raw grassfed lamb's heart). I want choices that are less likely to make me sick, because I know my gut probably still leaky (given the long list of foods I still react to).

Things I am considering:

Ceviche - can I make this at home, with wildcaught sockeye salmon (previously frozen) from the grocery store?

Steak tar tar - anyone made this? I have frozen grassfed ground beef in the freezer...

Anything else? I need beginner choices...

### kathylu — 2012-11-07T02:59:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62473>

You don't want to use previously ground beef for tartare, too much risk of bacterial contamination. I would get a piece of tenderloin or eye round (actually more flavorful than tenderloin) and hand chop, grind or mince it (food processor) myself, shortly before eating it. Here's a good recipe, just skip the french fries:

INGREDIENTS

14 oz. trimmed center-cut beef tenderloin

1 Â½ tbsp. Dijon mustard

2 egg yolks

Â¼ cup canola oil

6 tbsp. salt-packed capers, rinsed, drained, and minced

2 tbsp. minced, pitted green NiÃ§oise olives

2 tbsp. minced parsley

1 Â½ tsp. Worcestershire sauce

Â¼ tsp. hot sauce, such as Tabasco

4 cornichons, minced

1 small yellow onion, minced

Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper, to taste

French fries, for serving

Mixed salad greens, for serving

INSTRUCTIONS

1\. Place beef in the freezer to firm, about 30 minutes; this will make it easier to chop finely. Meanwhile, whisk mustard and egg yolks in a large bowl; while whisking constantly, slowly pour in oil to create a mayonnaise. Add capers, olives, parsley, Worcestershire, hot sauce, cornichons, and onion, and season with salt and pepper; refrigerate flavorings until ready to use.

2\. Remove beef from freezer and cut into Â¼" cubes. Transfer beef to bowl of flavorings and stir to combine. Keep beef mixture chilled until ready to serve.

3\. To serve, divide beef mixture into 4 equal portions, and shape each into an oval disk on a serving plate. Serve immediately with fries and mixed greens, if you like.

### kathylu — 2012-11-07T03:02:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62474>

Ok...I'm still afraid of E. coli O157, I admit it! So, I'd rather put a little extra work in and remain safe.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T03:15:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62475>

Bigknitwit, do try the seviche. The smaller you cut up the salmon, the faster it will be ready. Just squeeze limes, add seafood, chill and wait. When it feels firm, like it has been cooked, it is done. If you don't like the taste of lime, rinse before eating. If the pieces are large, you can cut them in half and make sure they appear to be cooked all the way through.

I don't blame you about the raw oyster. The first time I had it, I had ... difficulty.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T03:17:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62476>

> **kathylu;64289 wrote:** Ok...I'm still afraid of E. coli O157, I admit it! So, I'd rather put a little extra work in and remain safe.

I agree with you, kathylu. I remember my grandmother tasting ground beef before she purchased it from the butcher, but that was another era.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-07T03:33:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62477>

Something really easy to try (and you don't have to make it) is to get cold smoked fish. Because it's been cold smoked and no heat used, it has all the properties of raw (but still has the smoky taste). We use this regularly, often wrapped in nori. Yum.

I also do the ceviche techniques with the raw meat... I eat raw heart weekly (but it is really easy to get here - in every supermarket and very cheap)... I add lemon juice and spices/herbs (along with garlic, onions, etc) and let it sit for a couple of hours or even overnight.

# Thread: Despite the superiority of low-carb we still promote low-fat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/despite-the-superiority-of-low-carb-we-still-promote-low-fat.4715/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-07T04:23:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/despite-the-superiority-of-low-carb-we-still-promote-low-fat.4715/post-62481>

[http://www.doctorslounge.com/index.php/news/hd/33379](http://http://www.doctorslounge.com/index.php/news/hd/33379)

Study finds both low-carb and low-fat diets help overweight people reduce inflammation.

**The people on the low-carb diet, however, lost more weight**

On average, they shed 28 pounds compared to an average loss of 18 pounds for those on the low-fat diet. The study also showed the low-carb group had a more significant drop in BMI and belly fat.

Despite that finding, both diets helped reduce inflammation.
