### ruby — 2012-11-13T06:31:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-fast-and-live-longer.4717/post-63964>

"It should be known that the three-meals-a-day custom is really a modern one, and is not universally practiced even today. So far as history records none of the nations of antiquity practiced it. At the period of their greatest power, the Greeks and Romans ate only one meal a day. For more than a thousand years the one-meal system was the rule in two countries that could raise armies of men every one of whom would have made his fortune as a modern athlete--men who marched for days under a load of iron (besides clothes and provisions) that would stagger a modern porter. The Romans of the Republican age broke their fast with a biscuit and a fig or two, and took their principle meal in the cool of the evening. Among the many things that have been offered as an explanation for their physical, mental and moral decline has been their sensuous indulgence in food which came with power and riches.

Herodotus records that the invading hosts (over five millions) of the Persian general Xerxes, had to be fed by the conquered cities along their lines of march. He states as a fortunate circumstance the fact that the Persians, including even the Monarch and his courtiers, ate one meal a day.

The Jews from Moses until Jesus ate but one meal a day. They sometimes added a lunch of fruit. The Hebrew scriptures say: "Woe unto the nation whose princes eat in the morning." In the oriental world today extreme moderation, as compared to the American standard, is practiced.

During the zenith period of Grecian and Roman civilization monogamy was not as firmly established as the rule that a health-loving man should content himself with one meal a day, and never eat till he had leisure to digest, i.e., not till the day's work was wholly done. The evening repast--call it supper or dinner--was a kind of domestic festival, the reward of the day's toil, an enjoyment which rich and poor refrained from marring by premature gratifications of their appetites."

The average English family adopted the habit of eating three meals a day during the reign of Queen Elizabeth. Andrew Borde, a physician who lived during the reign of Henry VIII, wrote that: "Two meals a day is sufficient for a rest man; and a laborer may eat three times a day; and he that doth eate ofter lyveth a Beestly lyfe. Breakfast as a regular meal is little heard of, though probably most men started the day with a draught of ale and some bread.

Barely two centuries ago, the first meal of the day in England was taken about noon. Breakfast was an unrecognized meal and it originated in the practice of ladies taking an early dish of chocolate before rising. The ancient Greeks--the finest of people, physically and mentally, that ever lived--ate but two meals a day. The same was true of the ancient Hebrews and it is the custom of some of the best fighting races in India today.

From "The Hygienic System: Orthotrophy", by Herbert M. Shelton

Read more: [http://chestofbooks.com/health/natural-cure/The-Hygienic-System-Orthotrophy/Two-Meals-A-Day.html#ixzz2C4c60yss](http://http://chestofbooks.com/health/natural-cure/The-Hygienic-System-Orthotrophy/Two-Meals-A-Day.html#ixzz2C4c60yss)

### caroline — 2012-11-13T09:11:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-fast-and-live-longer.4717/post-63974>

I have found that calorie restriction is really working for me all of a sudden. I am not sure that that is what I should call it and I don't think it is quite IF but it is working great for the first time in my life. What I am doing is.... Breakfast is 2 Bullet proof coffees with butter and CO and sometimes i eat extra butter and sometimes add in a little bit of protein \[fish /MHS\] if there is something left in the fridge. I wait until I am a little hungry so it might be around 10;30. I then have dinner around 3:30 or 4:30 - Almost always fish. I really find I don't like meat anymore or digest it very well. I seem to be eating quite a bit less and feel very satisfied altho I eat until full. I throw in raw oysters and shrimp and Inger's salmon a few times a week. I have always eaten alot of eggs but I had some today and they really didn't agree with me - I was bloated and felt kind of sick and uncomfortable. I have had an intuition lately that i shouldn't have eggs any more - turned out to be right. This has sort of come together from bits and pieces I have learned from all of you! Thanx!!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-13T18:10:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-fast-and-live-longer.4717/post-64043>

Ruby - awesome post. I think the behaviors of the successful peoples are where we should look for inspiration. Note - food was a pleasure though, and has been for most societies. I suggest trying to remove that from the equation may be a human nature mismatch.

### ruby — 2012-11-14T01:47:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-fast-and-live-longer.4717/post-64145>

Thank you. Yes, pleasure by all means but the good thing is it seems this pleasure is amplified after a fast, a point collective wisdom has been driving home for quite some time

So there are two ways to boost pleasure - by having more and more food, or by having less of it. And one seems more healthy than the other

The best sauce for food is hunger - Socrates

The best seasoning for food is hunger; for drink, thirst - Cicero

A hungry stomach rarely despises rough food - Horace

Hunger sweetens beans - Latin proverb

Hunger is a good kitchen to a cold potato - Scottish proverb

Hunger is the best cook - German, Polish proverb

Hunger is the best sauce - English proverb

There's no sauce in the world like hunger - Miguel de Cervantes (1547-1616)

Make hunger thy sauce be thou never so nice, For there shalt thou find none other king of spice.

\- Alexander Barclay (c. 1476-1552)

Appetite is the best sauce - English proverb

Hunger is the best pickle - Benjamin Franklin

> Researchers have found some of the earliest signs of Alzheimer's disease, more than two decades before the first symptoms usually appear.
>
>
>
> Treating the disease early is thought to be vital to prevent damage to memory and thinking.
>
>
>
> A study, published in the Lancet Neurology, found differences in the brains of an extended Colombian family predisposed to develop an early form of Alzheimer's ...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T16:45:41+0200

you eat Epi-paleo and guess what? Beta amyloid goes bye bye..........Just sayin'

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T16:55:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/post-62523>

> "Brain scans of 20 people with the mutation, aged between 18 and 26, already showed differences compared with those from 24 people who were not destined to develop early Alzheimer's.
>
>
>
> The fluid which bathes the brain and spinal cord also had higher levels of a protein called beta-amyloid."

So their brains are already compensating for poor nutrition that early in life? Can't it just steal resources from other parts of the body? Perhaps, if it were a deficiency. But when it is being assaulted with carbs or fructose or MSG or whatever, it has to defend itself somehow. Enter beta amyloid.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T16:59:20+0200

No......neurons can adapt for a while......but ultimately they swell and get overcrowded in the cytosol and protein folding is altered and beta amyloid is the result. Go re read the blog now is your protein bent or how PD and AD are related......and then think about whay you read here and what I said........AD is 100% and Epigenetic disease of modern diets.

### chocolate — 2012-11-13T03:35:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/post-63932>

> **Jack Kruse;64363 wrote:** No......neurons can adapt for a while......but ultimately they swell and get overcrowded in the cytosol and protein folding is altered and beta amyloid is the result. Go re read the blog now is your protein bent or how PD and AD are related......and then think about whay you read here and what I said........AD is 100% and Epigenetic disease of modern diets.
>
>
> Cornell Researchers Find That Starvation or Overeating Affects the Genome
>
> By Christie Rizk on Nov 07, 2012
>
>
>
> It’s well known that overeating or malnutrition affects the human body's ability to function. Now genetics researchers are going beyond physiology to delve into how nutrition impacts the genome. Some in the emerging field of nutrigenomics are investigating how diet alters the way DNA is transcribed into RNA. Othes are looking into how nutrients influence gene expression.
>
> Recently, the Qian lab found that the same sets of transcripts can produce completely different kinds of proteins when subjected to different nutrient or stress signals. The scientists were most surprised by their discovery that too few or too many nutrients can cause messenger RNA to start and stop synthesizing proteins at different locations, thereby changing the makeup of the final protein product.
>
>
>
> "Starvation totally resets the whole translation machinery,” Qian says, by selectively translating part of the mRNA to help the cell survive. But, he adds, "if you have too many nutrients, it can do the opposite and suppress parts of the gene translation and up-regulate the other parts," leading to obesity and related diseases.

[http://nygenome.org/blog/cornell-researchers-find-starvation-or-overeating-affects-genome](http://http://nygenome.org/blog/cornell-researchers-find-starvation-or-overeating-affects-genome)

# Thread: Kids as Starfish
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-11-07T18:03:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62559>

Two stories I had to share with yâ€™all cause I know youâ€™ll appreciate them much more than any of my real-life friends/acquaintances!

My 8-year old son came home from school yesterday and said the teacher was handing out Jolly Ranchers for treats for doing well on their spelling test. Incidentally, DS got a 17/12 â€“ 141%. There were 5 challenge words that counted as bonus points and he got them all right. Anyway, he got the best score in the class and the teacher told him he had first pick and could choose 5 candies. He said â€œno thanks.â€

Of course the teacher was shocked and the kids all told him he was crazy. One asked him why he wouldnâ€™t want them. He told them all about the chemicals used in the food dyes and went on about how we donâ€™t eat crap that isnâ€™t real food â€“ he defined this for them as food thatâ€™s eaten without being pumped full of junk and made in a factory.

A little girl asked him later at lunch if her strawberry milk had chemicals in it to make it red like the Jolly Ranchers and he read the ingredients on the bottle and showed her where it listed Red 40. He said she put it down and went back in the line to get a water bottle.

Now, if an 8-year old can help to educate his peers about this why canâ€™t adults get it together and set the example for their kids?!?!

Next little story: 12-year old daughter woke this morning not at all happy about the outcome of the election. Weâ€™re generally fiscally conservative/socially liberal people, so we can swing either way in an election. This particular year weâ€™ve been talking quite a bit about Obamacare, so sheâ€™s up to speed on what that is and what it may mean for us and everyone. We talked about it a little this morning and she said â€œThis is why Dr Kruse was put in our lives. Weâ€™re lucky that we know enough to hopefully never need a doctor again if we just live like weâ€™re supposed to.â€ She often repeats Jackâ€™s mantra â€œwhen you know better, you do better.â€ I love that she is getting it

So keep plugging away with your kids. It will sink in! We're still not perfect and the kids certainly haven't drank any MHS's, but I have to say they are pretty darn cooperative and willing to try just about anything I put in front of them as long as they understand WHY.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T18:37:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62581>

That's great! Turning down 5 jolly ranchers, wow! Glad he is explaining it to his peers. I guess when you aren't filled with preconceived notions, it is easier to listen to something different.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-07T18:41:58+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T19:35:53+0200

Pure awesomness seeing two starfish created.........and the most remarkable thing is their little friend behavior was changed on dime over milk...........kids get it.........I cant get adults to get it........after 18 months of blogging. Amazing........made my yr really.

### BJK77 — 2012-11-07T19:59:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62611>

> **Jack Kruse;64441 wrote:** Pure awesomness seeing two starfish created.........and the most remarkable thing is their little friend behavior was changed on dime over milk...........kids get it.........I cant get adults to get it........after 18 months of blogging. Well, thanks so much to you Jack for sharing all your knowledge and wisdom. The time you spend on creating starfish and enabling us all to take charge of our own health is amazingly generous. My daughter was thrilled to read your lengthy response to her question yesterday about flexibility. It blew her mind that so much is related to hormones. I've been drilling that into her over and over, but I think reading your response yesterday just tied it all together perfectly for her.

She has now committed to taking cold baths every other day and, knowing her determination, I'm pretty confident she'll stick with it! Her new favorite meal is a shrimp/bacon/avocado salad that we all love. She'll eat this daily if I make it! Considering she wasn't a fan of seafood before, I'd call that success

### BJK77 — 2012-11-08T01:52:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62769>

> **Shijin13;64535 wrote:** I hope that chaos & havoc grow into this as they get older!

Gretchen,

With the example you're setting for your kids, there's no doubt they will be absolute rock stars when they get older  I wish I would have been able to start mine younger, but hey, now that I know better, I'll do better and make up for lost time!

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-08T04:35:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62817>

> **BJK77;64608 wrote:** Gretchen,
>
>
>
> With the example you're setting for your kids, there's no doubt they will be absolute rock stars when they get older  I wish I would have been able to start mine younger, but hey, now that I know better, I'll do better and make up for lost time!

I hope they do this. I worry about dd next yr when she goes to school. At such a young age they can be influenced by teachers w/misguided information and peer pressure (can we all say Disney princesses? - they're not in my house but they are in her world of friends). I want to raise smart children who can think critically

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-07T18:16:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62573>

Definitely NOT southern California! We have horrible water with fluoride and chlorine added to the supply, the weather is too hot, and our beaches get contaminated by sewage coming up from Mexico (every now and again). Then again, there is an abundant supply of organic food and it's fairly easy to find some cuts grass fed beef in the grocery store.

I'm sure those of you out there with expansive thinking minds are going to come up with some fantastic worldly sites to live!

### Lyndra — 2012-11-07T18:56:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62591>

Depends on what else you're looking for - job, school, ability to grown/raise your own food, inhabitability when the revolution comes, etc. You can always live next to a lake or stream if you want want. Go up in elevation so you have CT year-round. Not knowing what you really want - I'd say Wyoming or Montana but then I don't prefer crowded locales. But that's only thinking in US-terms - I have no idea where I'd choose if the world were my oyster.

### Inger — 2012-11-07T21:09:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62642>

I am thinking.. the Lofoten Islands in north Norway where my mom comes from.

The family have a cottage there up in the moutains. It is so beautiful. So much fish in the surrounding sea. So much seaweeds, bladderwrack every where. I am thinking this must be a really healthy place to live for sure... My mom is very healthy and slim even if 60 yo and beeing a mom of 15 kids, she was swimmimg a lot in the cold Atlantic as a kid, she told me they were totally blue as kids but still were swimmimg the whole summer..lol It does not get very warm there in summer eather. I guess they were quite cold adapted. I am thinking I got my love to cold water from my mom.

She told me they had to go skiing to school in deep snow, very far. Every day. No school busses or anything. They had fish too on the menu quite often. But no one was eating seaweeds tho. I would if I move there one day. I am thinking about moving to Norway anyways. They have more and better fish there. The Baltic sea is polluted for sure, not so the Atlantic. You can go fishing wherever you want for free, and you get a lot of fish!!! Big fishes! I need to move there. My brother has invited me to live by him he lives alone in a small own house he buyed. I am considering it for real. I have nothing that keeps me here really. In Norway you get Jobs easy, they have only around 3% of their population unemployed. You get crabs there too, they are easy to catch by yourself. And mussels. And fresh raw shrimps. Talk about heaven...

### prAna303 — 2012-11-08T14:07:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62864>

Right where i am now...

Many of my old friends move to the big cities, abroad and away. I have stayed here since birth. I love my hometown because i have adopted around it.

To the south i have the mountains with endless trails, boulders to climb and the sun to adore. Just 5 minutes from my door.

To the north, very much the same as south. About 10 minutes away.

To the east i have the sea with plenty of fishing and freediving. Give me 10 minutes and i hit that element.

To the west i have the deepest of forests. Here i can run for hours and never meet a single human.

I live in the elements for up to 10 hours a day and when i want to listen to great classical music i can go to the church for free. I can watch art, dance, music and other nice stuff in my hometown. ItÂ´s great.

On top of this i get four seasons with minus 25 degrees in the winters and plus 25 degrees in the summer.

But the most important things is: You see the world as you are.

### stacyj — 2012-11-08T15:26:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62874>

Anywhere not in a densely populated area, with room to grow my own vegies, have some livestock, have room for my family......kind of like the 380 acres I was raised on in Nev....

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-08T15:49:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62876>

I love the idea of completely re-thinking houses. They've evolved through the whims and necessity of the building industry. I think there is tons of room for improvement there.

I am on the hunt constantly for the perfect "forever" house to renovate and area. I have a great architect who's mom lives off in the mountains without electricity and Architect HATES it - but she did her mom's house and she can think out side the box.

So far the working design has a basement type sleeping chamber room. A landing area/entry to sleeping rooms with a small functional potty/brush your teeth kind of bathroom. No shower/bath etc there.

The "sleeping chambers" are sort of on basement level - depends on site (radon issues in mountains). Each chamber is much smaller than a normal bedroom, with a built in bed, low ceiling on one side and a half-walk-out large window on the other side. This is can be opened for fresh air flow, but should remain relatively quiet because of being mostly underground. It is also set back a few inches so black drapes/blinds on timers will be in front of it. Should be super energy efficient and easy to to the 50 degree sleeping temp. Blinds scheduled to come up with the sun. . large window is a mini-garage door, so it actually OPENS and lets in real air and light when desired.

No closets and none of the extras like cable/tv/whatnot. One outlet by the bed for light. . .and a vented "candle place" (smaller version of fireplace) for fire for lighting should that be what is desired.

Walk up the landing steps and to one side is the giant "dressing" area. Kind of a triangle. The dressing area has a washer and dryer IN it, with sitting room, dirty clothes storage, shoes, large mirrors. One side has a door coming from the outside and a mudroom function (snowy, sandy wet clothes and boots can come off here), One side has a door going through to the house bathing facility and the third side transitions out to the public space.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-08T16:02:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62877>

The new thing in houses is for everybody to have a complete stand alone life and every bedroom is large and a master. The newer nicer bedrooms are like little hotels so nobody has to interact or share. There is lip service to a "great room" kitchen type thing - but the expensive houses mean all the grownups work and all the kids are booked with activities. It's barely more than a fast food joint.

It drives me nuts!!!

My more modest clients are much happier people/families than my 1% ers. The de riguer 1% life is lonely and it seems to be a parasitic organism.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-08T16:11:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62879>

ok, now I want to work on house design all day. Damn you internets!!!

Let's crowd source this baby!!! As you go through today, make a mental list of what you do during the day ranked by time. Example:

Cook - 30%

Internet - 15%

TV - 2%

sleep - 20%

shower - 5%

toilet -2%

that kind of thing.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T19:49:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-62606>

[http://www.nytimes.com/imagepages/2006/08/14/science/sciencespecial2/20050815\_EVO\_GRAPHIC.html](http://http://www.nytimes.com/imagepages/2006/08/14/science/sciencespecial2/20050815_EVO_GRAPHIC.html)

Only Turkey bests us.........

Modern healthcare needs an epistemologic foundation.

Chemistry has it

Physics has it.......

Biology does not............

It must change.

This site is the that change agent...........teach your kids. It is our only hope to save ourselves from extinction by bad choices.

DO NOT BELIEVE. This has got to be one of those "bad study" type things. I want to know details on methodology of survey.

### ealachan — 2012-11-07T20:20:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-62630>

I buy it, 100%. I know too many hardcore fundamentalists that are super nice people but that truly believe evolution is totally made up B.S. I respect their right to believe that if they want to, of course...but, yeah. I don't find those figures hard to swallow AT ALL.

### Michael — 2012-11-07T20:58:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-62640>

> **Cavemam;64464 wrote:** DO NOT BELIEVE. This has got to be one of those "bad study" type things. I want to know details on methodology of survey.

That aside, it's a skewed sample. It's The West plus Turkey. And Turkey comes bottom. It would.

"The West" essentially = Europe + Europe's diaspora ... in this case minus Australasia. And modern Japan really counts as the West

But what if you included non-Western countries other than Turkey? Would they agree? Would they even understand the question?

In some ways the U.S. isn't like the rest of the West, because it was neither overrun in the political/military upheavals of the twentieth century (like much of continental Europe) nor pretty much bankrupted by those upheavals like Great Britain. The cultural landscape of Europe would look different if it were not for that. Then you have to ask larger questions - like what's the _downside of having the kind of culture Europe has nowadays? There are interesting demographics here, too. People on the right - such as Mark Steyn - would point out that religious societies seem to maintain population numbers - as the U.S. does up to a point. Demographically, Europe is dying and what replaces it will likely be a _different_ religious culture that has rather less_ respect for science.

### Inger — 2012-11-07T21:37:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-62646>

Yeah. When talking about religiousity my heart bleeds. My almost whole family is very religious.They are polite and always want to listen to my health advice, after all they feel my energy and can see I do well. But. There is the big BUT. GOD is the big guy after all. He will save us. Food does not really matter after all. So they have no consistency, they try a little, but then fall into the old patterns, because God will resolve our problems we just need to pray. Or believe more. While we have the holy spirit we are saved anyways. It is not all about food. But they keep suffering. They need their coffee and cakes after their meetings. I need to not start to talk about this because it hits a point that hurts big time in me. I have seen people suffer bad because of their religious fanatic believes. My sisters husband that are very religious is at the edge of prostate cancer in young age and with 7 kids. But God will help, no need to change their very bad eating habits. My heart bleeds. You can never win an argumantation with someone that believes they have the holy spirit and you have not. It is doomed to fail. So I have learned to not even start it.

But my heart still bleed. Especially for the children. They are not even allowed to go out and play before having a prayer hour with their parents. They so much want to go out, and how healthy would it be! But no.. OMG I have cried alone in my room after facing this. But I can do nothing. Yes I can. I can take them with me after their prayer hour and show them all the beauty in the nature. OMG I can tell you the kids loved it. Me too. I enjoyed every second. Very beautiful memories. I so hope I have planted some good seeds in the kids for the future..

Mmmm.. I wonder if I talk totally out of concept here. If, I beg you all my pardon. I just enjoyed some nice Malbec.. and that might have an impact..

My cat wants my attention.. she is laying on the floor talking to me with her feets up in the air..lol

### Entelechy — 2012-11-07T21:45:40+0200

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-07T22:06:36+0200

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-08T17:21:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-62893>

What a sad survey. I live in Texas, and I grew up in the heart of the Bible Belt, so I do not believe these figures are skewed.

I happen to be single these days, and there's an online dating site I was on called OKCupid. They have all these "matching" - type questions; once can answer zero or up to about 1000 questions, and the site supposedly "matches" you. Everything from eating habits to sex.

Anyway, the one set of questions I always looked for first to see how a guy answered were the evolution-type questions:

\--Do you believe in evolution?

\--Should creationism be taught in schools?

Etc.

Any "incorrect" answers to these questions caused me to automatically delete these guys from my search. By "incorrect", I mean the "No" to the belief in evolution, or the "yes" to teaching creationism, etc.

Trouble was, I had only about 5% of the men left. Literally. So I got off the site, and I'm still single.

Which is bigger, the earth or the sun?

I'd say about 75% of the people said the earth. And they were not joking. So sad.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-09T21:49:33+0200

Inger, this reminds me of a joke that a priest once told me. It goes like this (bear with me):

"A man is in his house when the town he's in begins to flood. As the streets fill with water the police come by and tell him to get out of his house. He says, No, God will save me. Then the water gets higher and people come by in a boat. He is now trapped on the second floor of his house. Get in, they tell him. No, he says, God will save me. The water continues to rise and he is now on the roof. A rescue helicopter appears and tries to winch him up to safety. He again refuses, saying God will save him. He then drowns. When he gets to heaven he is angry and says to God, "I believed in you. Why didn't you save me?!?!" God tells him: "I sent the police, a boat and a helicopter. What more did you want?"

From my understanding and my reading of religious texts, working with the notion of God in your life is an act of co-creation. In other words, there are always forces beyond your control; however, there are always choices within your control as well.

When people abdicate their personal choice I do not think this is because they trust that God will take care of them. It is often that they fear taking personal responsibility. It is hard to do. Yet we all must carve out our own way in life, regardless of whether we hold strict religious beliefs or not. And religion is just one of many walls we can hide behind. In the end, we cannot hide from life no matter how hard we try.

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-09T23:37:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-63220>

I have had no personal relationship with God or Devil, but lately I am mighty impressed with the Sun.

# Thread: Report on Vit-D biohack,   My N-1.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-07T20:10:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/report-on-vit-d-biohack-my-n-1.4723/post-62618>

I have absorption issues due to a bypass.

I tested Vit D on 4/26, It was 21.

I spent the summer in the sun, well over 1 hr per day on average.

I also supplemented with 20-30iu a day with a variety of oral methods and brands.

I tested Vit D on 9/7, It was 21.

I bitched and whined and complained and had an idea. I did a homemade vaginal insert of 20 iu a day from about the end of September through last week.

I tested Vit D on 11/1, it was 77 in a range of 30-100. Topquartilebaby!

\*\*\*The two medical professionals I told about it said no to this. Not sure if because they didn't think it would work, or because they thought there might be some vaginal harm. Vajayjay seems the same to me.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-07T23:53:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/report-on-vit-d-biohack-my-n-1.4723/post-62699>

TMI ALERT. I shall use ephemisms to trick the googles.

When I went sunning down south, I utilized the gelatin capsules that had olive oil in them. I used a pin to poke some holes in it so the oil/d mix would leak out.

These holy capsules would check into the hotel in groups of 4-6 with 5K iu each when ever I remembered during each day. Time was variable.

Some words of advice: This is not a mess free endeavor. Many capsules would try to escape and they did like to run for their life.

I also tried the pure liquid I found that had Vit-d in olive oil, but they were like sprinters and ran out faster than I thought would do any good for the cause.

# Thread: Oh! ho! We strike gold again
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-07T20:16:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/post-62624>

I always thought the bow and arrow was late in coming. Mesolithic - that's what I was taught.

Maybe not. New post on the Smithsonian blog today. Pinnacle Point again:

> The bow and arrow is an ancient weaponâ€”going back at least 71,000 years, a study published in Nature suggests. Archaeologists working at South Africaâ€™s Pinnacle Point cave site uncovered a collection of tiny blades, about an inch big, that resemble arrow points, likely belonging to prehistoric bow and arrows or spear-throwers. The researchers say the discovery is further evidence that humans (Homo sapiens) started to act and think like modern people early in their evolution. ...
>
>
> [http://blogs.smithsonianmag.com/hominids/2012/11/early-bow-and-arrows-offer-insight-into-origins-of-human-intellect/#comments](http://http://blogs.smithsonianmag.com/hominids/2012/11/early-bow-and-arrows-offer-insight-into-origins-of-human-intellect/#comments)
>
>
>
> Golly, I wonder what might have caused that jump to where people started to "started to act and think like modern people". ... could it be down to consuming fish, shellfish, and marine mammals?
>
>
>
> Pinnacle Point, where they could eat marine creatures like shellfish, whale, and seal.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinnacle\_Point](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinnacle_Point)

This might possibly be an "I told you so" moment for Jack. LOL

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T23:33:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/post-62692>

Michael........you are becoming my favorite starfish........This stuff can help change many of the thinkers out there who espouse bacon and elk first. When eat better we learn better. #Epipaleo it forward!

### Michael — 2012-11-08T08:20:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/post-62847>

More here, including a quote from the project director:

> "Every time we excavate a new site in coastal South Africa with advanced field techniques, we discover new and surprising results that push back in time the evidence for uniquely human behaviors," said co-author Curtis Marean, project director and Arizona State University professor in the Institute of Human Origins
>
>
> [http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/11/07/small.lethal.tools.have.big.implications.early.modern.human.complexity](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/11/07/small.lethal.tools.have.big.implications.early.modern.human.complexity)
>
>
>
> In this version it says the microliths may be for arrows to be shot from a bow or for darts to be thrown from a spear-thrower.
>
>
>
> Another comment on the importance of these coastal sites in Africa:
>
>
>
>
>
> "This is why continued and well-funded fieldwork in Africa is of the highest scientific priority if we want to learn about what it means to be human, and where and when it happened," said Marean.

Science News has reproductions, showing how the microliths may have been hafted:

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### ruby — 2012-11-08T08:46:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/post-62851>

When the Sea Saved Humanity:

Key Concepts

At some point between 195,000 and 123,000 years ago, the population size of Homo sapiens plummeted, thanks to cold, dry climate conditions that left much of our ancestors' African homeland uninhabitable. Everyone alive today is descended from a group of people from a single region who survived this catastrophe.

The southern coast of Africa would have been one of the few spots where humans could survive during this climate crisis, because it harbors an abundance of shellfish and edible plants.

Excavations of a series of sites in this region have recovered items left behind by what may have been that progenitor population.

The discoveries confirm the idea that advanced cognitive abilities evolved earlier than previously thought—and may have played a key role in the survival of the species during tough times.

[http://www.nature.com/scientificamerican/journal/v303/n2/full/scientificamerican0810-54.html](http://http://www.nature.com/scientificamerican/journal/v303/n2/full/scientificamerican0810-54.html)

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-1297765/Last-humans-Earth-survived-Ice-Age-sheltering-Garden-Eden-claim-scientists.html](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-1297765/Last-humans-Earth-survived-Ice-Age-sheltering-Garden-Eden-claim-scientists.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-08T14:57:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/post-62869>

Awesome Ruby........

# Thread: parallel analogy to what Jack is driving for in the future of health.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9

### Entelechy — 2012-11-07T20:47:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62637>

community based health, driven by collective passion and love.

This forum is like the Shinto shrine, and Perl support.

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Xe1TZaElTAs&feature=related\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;Xe1TZaElTAs\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Xe1TZaElTAs&feature=related\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;Xe1TZaElTAs)

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-07T22:39:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62667>

beautiful... absolutely beautiful

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T23:15:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62679>

The website is not work to me.......it is my passion. I never thought about in the terms in the You tube video.......but one of our members (Jason) sent me an email a few months ago just shooting the \*\*\*\*.......and he asked me what this site was to me.......my comment to him seemed to affect him and he sent me back a reply that I pondered deeply.......because I had not quite thought about what he was asking or how he was feeling about the site........and when I read his reply I got what he meant........What he meant was in that You tube clip..........

### Inger — 2012-11-07T23:17:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62680>

Yes... nice Video. I like this community.

It feels like home.

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-08T00:41:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62718>

It is truly astonishing to contemplate the depth and possibility within the truth in this clip.

"Ask yourself not 'what's the business model?' but 'do the people who like it take care of each other?' That turns out to be the better predictor of longevity."

Based on that, this community, and Jack's work, will be around for a very long time.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-08T00:50:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62725>

Coriander........you get it but some others here dont........you should see some of the complaints. This site offers tons of free stuff.......and they still want more for free. Our gov't creates this entitlement.......but peoples lives and behaviors create the demand for the service. The goal here is teach people just how much control they really have over their own health........when they are clueless about it.

### BJK77 — 2012-11-08T01:50:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62767>

I am so grateful for the knowledge that is passed along to us. I love knowing that this community is full of individuals all on similar, yet different journeys to improving ourselves. I love that it's a "family" where we are given so much for so little - wisdom, encouragement, support, etc. I often wish I could post more and be more active on the forum, but I just don't feel I have the time right now. I do try to contribute as much as I can just as a way of paying it forward for all the wonderful benefits I've gleaned from being here.

# Thread: Seaweed and the Sick Dog
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-and-the-sick-dog.4728/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2012-11-08T02:25:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-and-the-sick-dog.4728/post-62782>

Walked into quite the mess tonight. It appears our lovely Jack Russell decided to have a little snack today of dried seaweed. Must have jumped up onto the island in the kitchen and snagged the entire bag.

He obviously ate some and then just as obviously expelled it from his stomach. Anyone know if seaweed is "bad" for dogs?

The irony of it all...just had new tile installed on the entire first floor last week. Also hired a service for a "deep clean" on Monday.

Little bugger is lucky we love him!

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T04:20:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-and-the-sick-dog.4728/post-62811>

ASPCA runs a poison control hotline you could try if you're really worried. They may charge you a fee, though. Here's their list of foods not to feed your pets: [http://www.aspca.org/pet-care/poison-control/people-foods.aspx](http://http://www.aspca.org/pet-care/poison-control/people-foods.aspx)

### fitness@home — 2012-11-08T12:41:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-and-the-sick-dog.4728/post-62861>

> **Lyndra;64650 wrote:** ASPCA runs a poison control hotline you could try if you're really worried. They may charge you a fee, though. Here's their list of foods not to feed your pets: [http://www.aspca.org/pet-care/poison-control/people-foods.aspx](http://http://www.aspca.org/pet-care/poison-control/people-foods.aspx)

Thanks for the info Lyndra. He seems to be his happy hyper self this morning and is currently pestering the cat.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T19:32:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-and-the-sick-dog.4728/post-62946>

Glad he's back to normal! I had a scare once with one of my dogs getting into some chocolate and the poison control line was very helpful. Now I know how to make them barf on command (hint: it involves hydrogen peroxide).

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-08T07:56:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-will-not-end-well.4730/post-62844>

[http://www.guardian.co.uk/lifeandstyle/wordofmouth/2012/nov/07/edible-deodorants-not-to-be-sniffed-at](http://http://www.guardian.co.uk/lifeandstyle/wordofmouth/2012/nov/07/edible-deodorants-not-to-be-sniffed-at)

### caroline — 2012-11-08T08:21:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-will-not-end-well.4730/post-62848>

like swallowing an air freshener ??? - wow!

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-12T19:19:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-will-not-end-well.4730/post-63839>

Do read the comments below that article. They are quite entertaining such as the one about an edible product that makes your bowel movements smell like freshly baked cinnamon rolls.

I remember reading something about a chemical called geranyl-geraniol awhile back. Geraniol is said to stunt tumors and have some health benefits, and is a constituent of roses and plants containing citronella. Hmm.. that candy might keep mosquitos away, but it is also a bee attractant.

Geranylgeraniol, part of the melavonate pathway, is a diterpine and an intermediate in the synthesis of vitamins E and K, and geraniol is a monoterpine.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11572758](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11572758)

# Thread: Undernourishment and overexercise in women
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/undernourishment-and-overexercise-in-women.4731/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-08T08:50:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/undernourishment-and-overexercise-in-women.4731/post-62852>

I listened to Sean Croxton's Underground Wellness podcast yesterday and was surprised to find an item on this. Hot on the heels of the _Fat Burning Man episode on the woman who pushed herself into a dangerous and dysfunctional state over food and exercise comes this.

> Did you know that exercise, along with undernourishment, was can do serious -- and sometimes irreparable -- damage to a woman's body?
>
>
>
> Female Athlete Triad Syndrome -- disordered eating and/or undernourishment, loss of menstruation, and bone loss -- is a growing concern in athletic programs and gyms across the country.

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/11/07/female-athlete-triad-w-dr-kelly-austin](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/11/07/female-athlete-triad-w-dr-kelly-austin)

Sean himself says that as a personal trainer he found that many women were deeply envious of very thin women in the gym whose abdominal muscles were visible through the fat layer. I've always thought it a little strange how some men go on about this on paleo boards, but _women_ wanting this for themselves! The girl on Abel's show had said this was what she wanted. The interviewee says that any woman whose fat percentage is that low is probably already doing damage to herself, because it would lower the amount of estrogen available, which would affect bone health. She says often the first sign of a cascade of unfortunate phenomena that many women notice is stress fractures; however, they often don't connect these to anything else, and neither sometimes even do their doctors. This ought to be an immediate sign to be asking questions about diet and exercise and taking a hormone panel.

I can't think of any photographs of male hunter-gatherers that would show them as having a very low fat percentage.

And with women - look for example at the Theodore de Bry engraving of seed-planting. This would be among Virginia Indians who would have done some hunting and gathering, some fishing, and dabbled a little in agriculture. I should think these women would be living about as healthy and natural an outdoor life as any woman would be likely to be. Their forms are soft and rounded, and I doubt any ever thought, like Coco Chanel, that one could "never be too rich or too thin".

[http://www.encore-editions.com/florida-indians-planting-seeds-of-beans-or-maize](http://http://www.encore-editions.com/florida-indians-planting-seeds-of-beans-or-maize)

What ought to be slightly disturbing for the ancestral health movement is that it ought to be a route out of these unhealthy practices and dysmorphic body images, but for some women it seems to be a route in_.

### caroline — 2012-11-08T09:04:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/undernourishment-and-overexercise-in-women.4731/post-62854>

Michael - I just love reading your posts - I can't figure out how you come up with all this stuff! You really are a star fish!

### Michael — 2012-11-08T09:25:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/undernourishment-and-overexercise-in-women.4731/post-62855>

> **caroline;64693 wrote:** Michael - I just love reading your posts - I can't figure out how you come up with all this stuff! You really are a star fish!

Thanks!

Sean's stuff is worth checking out if you don't know him. He's got loads of energy and enthusiasm:

[http://undergroundwellness.com](http://http://undergroundwellness.com)

When i get some spare time I mean to check out his YouTube videos, because I believe he's got a ton of those, too.

### caroline — 2012-11-08T09:33:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/undernourishment-and-overexercise-in-women.4731/post-62856>

# Thread: New research on autism
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-research-on-autism.4732/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-08T10:45:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-research-on-autism.4732/post-62857>

> The study found a protein which helps brain cells transfer data through neurological pathways called synapses was mutated in autism sufferers, offering a likely explanation for their cognitive and behavioural difficulties.
>
>
>
> Principal investigator Johanna Montgomery, from Auckland University's Centre for Brain Research, said the mutated protein, called Shank3, provided exciting possibilities in the search for autism treatments. ...
>
>
> [http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/healthnews/9663181/Genetic-mutation-discovered-in-people-with-autism.html](http://http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/healthnews/9663181/Genetic-mutation-discovered-in-people-with-autism.html)
>
>
>
> What's a bit disappointing is that there's nothing in this short piece suggesting the problem is anything other than genetic. That's reinforced by the sentence at the end that puts the spike in numbers down to "more efficient diagnosis".
>
>
>
> She said the condition was becoming more prevalent, partly due to more efficient diagnosis, with studies estimating it affects about one-in-82 children.

the Telegraph doesn't link to any other resources. It say the paper was published in The Journal of Neuroscience.

EDIT:

Rather more information given here:

[http://www.nzdoctor.co.nz/news/2012/november-2012/08/research-gives-clues-to-autism-mechanism.aspx](http://http://www.nzdoctor.co.nz/news/2012/november-2012/08/research-gives-clues-to-autism-mechanism.aspx)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Hafting microliths
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hafting-microliths.4733/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-08T12:23:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hafting-microliths.4733/post-62860>

The hafting arrangements for these may have been quite complex. Here's a nice blog article on some Scandinavian examples. At least sometimes it seems microliths may have been embedded further down a missile shaft to act like barbs. They may also have been used for tiny chisels - microburins.

[http://hazelnutrelations.wordpress.com/2012/01/31/hafting-microlith-scandinavia-alps/](http://http://hazelnutrelations.wordpress.com/2012/01/31/hafting-microlith-scandinavia-alps/)

### Michael — 2012-11-08T21:50:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hafting-microliths.4733/post-62979>

Here's something else you might use microliths for - a harpoon/fishing-spear:

[http://www.gov.im/mnh/collections/archaeology/mesolithic/microliths.xml](http://http://www.gov.im/mnh/collections/archaeology/mesolithic/microliths.xml)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Ancient Irish Fish Trap
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-irish-fish-trap.4734/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-08T16:07:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-irish-fish-trap.4734/post-62878>

This one is built of timber and said to be one of the finest in Europe. It only came to light relatively recently, but will probably be washed away and may not even be recorded fully owing to lack of funds:

> One of the finest examples of a medieval fish trap in Europe is going to be washed away before its secrets can be recorded, scientists have claimed.
>
>
>
> A 700-year-old giant wooden weir, once used by monks, cannot be fully preserved because it is exposed to the forces of nature.
>
>
>
> And with budget cuts hitting the Heritage Council there is no money to properly analyse and record the huge structure. ...

[http://www.independent.ie/national-news/ancient-fish-trap-will-be-lost-to-the-tide-2546317.html](http://http://www.independent.ie/national-news/ancient-fish-trap-will-be-lost-to-the-tide-2546317.html)

There's a photo of a wooden Irish fish trap here - near the bottom of the page. Just a few stakes now. Not sure how old this one is, but they seem to have uncovered five substantial fish traps in Dublin alone, going back to Stone Age times:

[http://irisharchaeology.ie/2011/05/dublin-the-prehistoric-city/](http://http://irisharchaeology.ie/2011/05/dublin-the-prehistoric-city/)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Orang-utan fishing

### Michael — 2012-11-08T17:18:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orang-utan-fishing.4736/post-62892>

> Orangutans living in Borneo scavenge fish that wash up along the shore and scoop catfish out of small ponds for fresh meals, anthropologist Anne Russon of York University in Toronto reported on April 14 at a meeting of the American Association of Physical Anthropologists. Over two years, **Russon saw several animals on these forested islands learn on their own to jab at catfish with sticks, so that the panicked prey would flop out of ponds and into a red apeâ€™s waiting hands.
>
>
>
> â€œIf orangutans can do this, then early hominids could also have practiced tool-assisted fishing,â€￾ Russon said.** ...
>
>
>
> Fish, even more than red meat, contains fatty acids essential for human brain growth. ...

[http://news.discovery.com/animals/orangutans-fishing-primates-fish-110418.html](http://http://news.discovery.com/animals/orangutans-fishing-primates-fish-110418.html)

I tried searching YouTube under "orang-utan fish" and it looks like there may be some film of this, although haven't checked it out yet.

Try the same search in Google under "video" (the general "video" link not the "YouTube" one and there's what looks to be film of an orang-utan using a stick to fish with.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-08T17:30:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orang-utan-fishing.4736/post-62896>

I've seen clips of this on discovery channel - regarding the orphaned orangutans. I wonder if the deforestation we're causing in their natural habitat is driving them to adapt to other food sources???? what happens if orangutans survive that devestation, and uses shellfish/seafoods from their surrounding water ways? could we see another great ape arise, to compete for resources 250K yrs from now? very Planet of the apes....

### Michael — 2012-11-08T17:41:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orang-utan-fishing.4736/post-62902>

You've seen it!

It's a real clincher, isn't it? If they can do it, why not a hominid? It becomes ridiculous to put "the beginning of fishing" at the date of the earliest fishhooks.

EDIT:

Does this video show it? I can't get it to load:

http://current.com/entertainment/wtf/88923772\_orangutan-hunts-fish-with-spear.htm

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-08T18:51:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orang-utan-fishing.4736/post-62931>

I can't see video at work... but this was the show I saw it on...

[http://animal.discovery.com/tv/orangutan-island/conservation/adopt.html](http://http://animal.discovery.com/tv/orangutan-island/conservation/adopt.html)

one of the older - less socially acceptable orangutans was the one who did it... and he actually went about it like people do for sport here in the US called Noodling... [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noodling](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noodling)

soon some of the younger males and one female started emulating him...

# Thread: THIS is how you fight our enemies.

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-08T17:43:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-62904>

Not saying these are our enemies, just illustrating a point. Just give your enemy a rope.

[http://edition.cnn.com/2012/11/08/world/meast/kuwait-obesity-fast-food/index.html?hpt=hp\_c4](http://http://edition.cnn.com/2012/11/08/world/meast/kuwait-obesity-fast-food/index.html?hpt=hp_c4)

### Glamazon — 2012-11-11T01:40:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63480>

Terrorism, american style. Our food culture is deadly.

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-11T05:41:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63537>

Today in the supermarket I had to visit the aisle of death. One side is soda floor to ceiling facing chips floor to ceiling. 80 ft long. I had to buy chips for a party. I stood at one end in awe and said, "The aisle of death!"

### nonchalant — 2012-11-11T23:58:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63692>

Nope, that aisle is the baking aisle. Sugars & sweetners of all types, various flours and wholegrain flours, and lots of processed oils...

Used to be my favorite area of the store.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-12T00:01:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63693>

So, I see lots of peoples kitchens and look in their pantry. . .even "healthy" seeming people. I've yet to find anybody that has any quantity of actual food. Even the healthiest have larders full of death.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-12T00:49:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63716>

Lol larders full if death. I love that!!!

### Lyndra — 2012-11-12T01:44:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63733>

> **Cavemam;65604 wrote:** So, I see lots of peoples kitchens and look in their pantry. . .even "healthy" seeming people. I've yet to find anybody that has any quantity of actual food. Even the healthiest have larders full of death.

You won't find much food in our pantry. Some canned seafood, coconut & olive oil, olives, capers, and a few canned veggies for emergencies but that's about it. Our fridge, on the other hand, is crammed full. I wish we could get a bigger one but we are space-limited there. One positive note - I recently recovered some cabinet space when I realized I could toss the toaster - never gonna use that puppy again.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-12T01:48:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63734>

> **Dali Dula;65410 wrote:** Today in the supermarket I had to visit the aisle of death. One side is soda floor to ceiling facing chips floor to ceiling. 80 ft long. I had to buy chips for a party. I stood at one end in awe and said, "The aisle of death!"

50% of the "food" aisles are that way. Look at the cereal aisle - how many different processed sugar & grain products can they produce and still manage to differentiate & market them? Don't forget the frozen food aisles, filled with ready-to-eat junk and a myriad of differently prepared potatoes. And last, there's always the bakery section - filled with cookies & cake of all kinds & colors and more bread products that you can imagine. It's really amazing how much space is dedicated to junk. It does make it easier to shop, though. I used to go up and down every aisle, but now I hit the produce & fish, maybe pickup some eggs and frozen veggies and I'm outta there.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-13T08:16:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63969>

Ever notice the hot food case at the truck stop? Everything they display has been deep fried and dark brown in color! Just point out which brown shape you want, the long skinny one, the square one, or the bumpy round one.

If its your lucky day, the attendant might give you 2 shriveled brown pieces for the price of 1 cause they were "cooked" more than 6 hours ago. MMMM MMMM GUUD!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-13T18:13:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-64046>

My food shopping now is not the entertainment it used to be for sure. I kinda miss it!

### Inger — 2012-11-14T09:34:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-64191>

> **Cavemam;65999 wrote:** My food shopping now is not the entertainment it used to be for sure. I kinda miss it!

Hmmm.. To me shopping have turned even more entertaining. Now I know what I want.. and it is like going hunting! Sometimes I find stuff that makes me feel like I fouln gold..lol

It is more challenging now but that is what makes it fun too!

### Destiny — 2012-11-14T14:15:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-64221>

### renwoo — 2012-11-08T21:16:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs-please.4741/post-62973>

I am a 40 yo female. I have Hashimoto's and was on synthroid for 7 years up until 3 days ago and now I am on Nature throid 1 grain (so far). I am exhausted, very overweight, terrible periods (Gyno put in an IUD 6 weeks ago and my period has pretty much now disappeared). Hair is falling out a rapid rate, muscle aches, irritability... I take 50,000 vit d 2 x a week.

Glucose, serum 79 mg/dL (65-99)

BUN 15 mg/dL (6-24)

Creatinine, Serum .69 mg/dL (.57-1.00)

eGFR 109 mL/min/1.73 >59

BUN/Creatinine ratio 22 9-23

Sodium, serum 137 mmo1/L 134-144

Potassium, serum 3.8 mmo1/L 3.5-5.2

Chloride, serum 101 mmo1/L 97-108

Carbon dioxide total 23 mmo1/L 20-32

Calcium, serum 9.6 mg/dL 8.7-10.2

Protein, total, serum 7.6 g/dL 6.0-8.5

Albumin, serum 4.3 g/dL 3.5-5.5

Globulin, total 3.3 g/dL 1.5-4.5

A/G ratio 1.3 1.1-2.5

Bilirubin, total 0.2 mg/dL 0.0-1.2

Alkaline phosphatase,s 56 IU/L 25-150

AST (SGOT) 23 IU/L 0-40

ALT (SGPT) 20 IU/L 0-40

Iron Bind. Cap. (TIBC) 391 ug/dL 250-450

UIBC 315 ug/dL 150-375

Iron, serum 76 ug/dL 35-155

Iron saturation 19 % 15-55

Vit d 25-hydroxy D2+D3 45 ng/mL 32-100

Vit D2 28 ng/mL 32-100

Vit D3 17 ng/mL 32-100

MTFHR single mutation A1298C

Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) Ab 82 IU/mL 0-34

Antithyroglobulin Ab 22 IU/mL 0-40

Thyroxine (T4) free, direct 1.30 ng/dL 0.82-1.77

Reverse T3, serum 37.3 ng/dL 13.5-34.2

DHEA sulfate 144.4 ug/dL 60.9-337.0

Testosterone, serum 18 ng/dL 8-48

TSH 165 UIU/ML 0.450-4.500

Triiodothyronine (T3) 100 ng/dL 71-180

Vit B12 1003 pg/mL 211-946

Ferritin, serum 14 ng/mL 13-150

Free T3 2.8 pg/mL 2.0-4.4

Magnesium, RBC 5.1 mg/dL 4.2-6.8

(plasma not separated from cells)

thank you for reading

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-09T00:52:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-hs-crp.4742/post-63025>

Yes.......cellular energy is nto working optimally yet.......if your TG spiked it is a sign of Mg depletion, L-carnitine issues, lack or recycling of D Ribose, and very low CoEnzy Q10. This is also know as paleo flu or low carb flu.

### hope2conceive — 2012-11-09T01:13:38+0200

# Thread: Let's talk about sex baby, let's talk about you and me, let's talk about sex . . .

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 348
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### curves64 — 2012-11-08T22:39:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-62988>

Iâ€™ve been wanting to start a thread about sex and sexual energy for a while so here goes. Iâ€™m particularly interested in hearing from peri and/or post-menopausal women who have not taken the BIHT route but are using the epi-paleo RX, circadian biology and/or other Dr. K-inspired ideas to reestablish their sexual energy. Mine took a dive about 4 years ago following a series of super stressful events that happened within a short period of time. I was probably already in adrenal fatigue but those events pushed me over the edge. Although my overall energy and cognitive function are definitely coming back online and it looked like it would for a while, my sexual energy has not yet returned to itsâ€™ former glory (I like to say I was a 12 on a scale of 1-10 but am now a 5 or 6). I'm not sure. Maybe I just need to give it more time. If anyone has any words of wisdom or personal experience to share, Iâ€™d love to hear all about it.

### curves64 — 2012-11-08T23:41:11+0200

Without daily erections, Penile tissue begins to die and suffocate for fresh O2 Not to mention the release of Oxytocin from orgasim
>
>
>
> Maybe somebody should open a Paleo Swingers Club - call it the "cave" lol

I know this experience is different for men and women. I'm all for "using it," and "use it" daily, if not twice a day lol. I just miss the intensity and frequency of my orgasms. They were such a vital part of who I was/am that I hate to think of living for another 50 years with that part of me being mediocre.

### ealachan — 2012-11-08T23:45:47+0200

### ealachan — 2012-11-08T23:50:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63007>

curves64, I'm not peri-menopausal and I'm not on any BHRT, so I can't speak to that...but I will say that after a few months of epi-paleo, I don't need nearly as much "convincing" from the hubs to get it on as I used to. There was a time when even him leaning in to kiss me just made me cringe because I knew where he wanted it to lead and I just DIDN'T want anything to do with it. It hurt his feelings, it hurt my feelings, it was a bad scene. Things are MUCH better now. He's happier, I'm happier, the only one that's kind of bummed about everything is the dog because he gets kicked off the bed a lot more nowadays...

### curves64 — 2012-11-09T00:21:03+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-09T00:49:49+0200

### curves64 — 2012-11-09T00:58:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63026>

I'm beginning to see that as you further align with the natural rhythm of your body by paying attention to circadian biology and feeding it the food it was designed to eat, you naturally align with your confidence, your sexual energy and your intuition. One flows from the other effortlessly.

Can't wait to hear more of your thoughts on the topic.

### ealachan — 2012-11-09T01:42:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63035>

> **curves64;64860 wrote:** Love it!!!!
>
>
>
> Ealachan, would you say that you feel more confident and connected to your body, or is it that your sexual energy has increased or both? I love hearing your story. Thank you.

Both I guess, now that you've made me think about it. My confidence has certainly been helped somewhat by losing a bit of weight, but I also certainly feel more desire to actually do the deed as well. Also, I've never been unable to orgasm, but I certainly have noticed that it doesn't seem to take quite as much time / effort now as it used to.

While I'm at it (no pun intended), I'd like to point out something else interesting I've noticed lately: my husband seems to be able to tell when I'm in the mood without me saying anything. I don't know if I'm emitting pheromones or what - it's the damnedest thing. I'll sit there minding my own business and happen to think of something that makes me think of sex, even briefly, and like 5 minutes later there he is, suggesting we get busy!

### diane — 2012-11-09T01:48:14+0200

I'm perimenopausal, not on BHRT (yet - although trying). I can't say I've noticed much of a dip in sexual energy, even before doing the things here. But, I can say that it's been increased even more by epi-paleo, getting my proper sleep, circadian rhythms. And after a cold bath? Get the fire extinguisher out!

### kathylu — 2012-11-09T04:08:55+0200

Darleen, there is nothing to be embarrassed about! It's not like you have to stare at us over morning coffee. Funny story...hubby and I spent 2 weeks on St. Martin a few years ago, really nice btw. Lots of nude beaches on the French side...and you know how we Americans are about going "au naturel", but the hotel we stayed at was on Cupecoy Beach, clothes not required. Early in the vaykay, my husband stands up, declaring "I will never see even one of these people again in my life!" and that was the end of clothes on the beach for him for the rest of the trip! ### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-09T07:05:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63086>

I wonder if one's sex drive - male or female - is meant to be constant as was implied in earlier posts. Given that we understand our ties to seasonality in eating, light cycles and temperatures and given that the environmental conditions into which a baby is born would likely affect the child's survival (and what the mother was able to provide), wouldn't our libido wax and wane according to the seasons somehow. This is certainly true for other eutherian mammals. This is also perhaps a link as to why some of you are finding that the cold stimulates libido... 9 months later, voila!

For example, this article:

Current Women’s Health Reviews, 2007, 3, 182-189

_Season of Birth Effects on Reproduction in Women_

excerpt 1:

In many parts of the world, the external environment shows pronounced variations with season and therefore the season of birth may contribute to early developmental

processes. This seasonal variation concerns a variety of environmental factors (e.g., photoperiod, climatic parameters, food quality and quantity) and maternal factors (e.g. hormonal and metabolic parameters, infections, behavioural variations). However, most of these variables are highly inter-correlated, leaving it very difficult to ascertain single causative factors influencing early development. At high latitudes, winter, for instance, is associated with factors such as low temperature, short photoperiod together with low levels of ultraviolet radiation, vitamin deficiencies \[12\], high risk of infectious diseases \[13,14\], or increased risk of obstetrical complications \[15\]. Although modern life has considerably diminished the seasonal variation of most of these factors (owing to heating, air condition, artificial light or the year round supply of fresh fruit and vegetables), it has not completely vanished.

excerpt 2:

Numerous studies report that the season of birth influences early development of individuals with long-term consequences for a wide range of parameters such as body height \[e.g., 16, 17\], longevity \[e.g., 18, 19\], susceptibility to diseases \[e.g., 20-22\], as well as several parameters of performance \[e.g., 23, 24\]. Expectedly, those parameters investigated both in the Northern and the Southern hemisphere, exhibited a seasonal variation depending on the month of birth, shifted by half a year between the two hemispheres \[18, 25-27\], whereas no birth month variation was found in equatorial regions where seasons are absent \[28\].

excerpt 3:

Owing to the fact that glucocorticoids are among the key hormones controlling fetal development \[115\], and due to the considerable interplay between the hypothalamus-pituitaryadrenal- (HPA-) and the HPG-axis \[116\], an influence of prenatal stress on reproductive development of the fetus is conceivable. It is well established that the HPG-axis is affected by the HPA-axis, with corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) exerting inhibiting effects on gonadotropinreleasing hormone (GnRH) secretion, and glucocorticoids inhibiting gonadotropin and sex steroid secretion \[117\]. In addition, CRH receptors are present in most female reproductive tissues \[118\]. Emerging evidence further indicates that the interaction between the HPA- and HPG-axis may be modified by early environmental conditions \[119\], supporting the view that prenatal stress might be involved in birth season effects on reproductive traits.

### Inger — 2012-11-09T09:11:49+0200

Caroline I do guess Jack is right.. I always was a bit on the "guy" side in my thinking, maybe because my brother was my best friend as a kid IDK, maybe it impacted my thinking.

I always enjoy sex. It plain feels so really good...huh!

Now.. the problem I have instead is orgasm. I have never had a problem getting an orgasm by myself, and I at least one have every day in the morning (I can tell you coffee with cardamom as I drink it every morning makes one get quite a strong desire...lol). But... if I am feeling not totally safe with a partner I can forget about it. I will enjoy sex anyways but I will not get an orgasm. I wonder why this is.

I suffered quite a lot in my marriage it beeing so sexless. And I never had an orgasm with hubby.. I was not feeling safe. It is very sad indeed. Now.. one day I am confident, when I am getting more optimal, there will be someone out there to share my life with who has a huge sex desire..lol

The Kruse Protocol have made my libido even stroger. It was never weak but now I sometimes really wish it would not expand further because without partner it is not so fun..

Somehow men sense the womens libido I guess.. and I get quite a lot attention, also from guys in their 20ies too and it is somehow..weird?

### ealachan — 2012-11-09T17:47:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63130>

> **caroline;64929 wrote:** I am so glad you started this thread - I was too chicken!!! I hope Dr. K does a blog about this soon. There is something I don't quite understand - In a consult - Dr. K told me to stop thinking like a woman - and just do it. I get that \[oxytoxin release etc and the big pharmacy in the brain\] but - a guy's switch is always on shall we say but - a woman's not so much. It seems like such a
>
> missmatch to me so I need to understand this better - pleeeeeease

I think I get the "just do it" philosophy better lately when it comes to sex. I used to get myself so psyched out about "omg I really don't feel like doing it right now, I'm tired and this and that and blah blah a million other things", and then I COULDN'T do it because I couldn't shut my brain up. My husband and I actually had a big coming-to-Jeebus session about it because basically it was really hurting his feelings that I was turning down his advances constantly. I decided at that point that I'd try to just shut my head up and be more receptive sometimes...not in the sense of, you know, laying back and thinking about England while he got his kicks type of thing...but just trying to be more open to the idea of having sex and having FUN while doing it, rather than getting freaked out about it...and it really helped a lot. Also, I found that being honest with him, ie: "that feels nice but it's not getting me where I need to go, let's try this instead", has made the whole process SO much less stressful for both of us. He's not sitting there feeling like he's ineffective, and I'm not sitting there reviewing my grocery list because I'm bored....LOL

### curves64 — 2012-11-09T18:16:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63135>

For most of my life I've had a raging libido and I've always received a lot of male attention. It's not that I am the most beautiful woman in the world but at the age of 55 I still easily easily attract the attention of men in all decades of life. I think it's because I love all things sexual and I welcome and enjoy their attention and I'm confident in my sexuality. I'm picky who I date, but not long ago, I had a very handsome 29 year old lover just for the fun of it. The problem is that chronic stress and menopause diminished my sexual energy by half. I could probably "get by" with things as they are but who the hell wants that! My current bf (whom I love and adore) was shocked to learn that my sexual energy was decreased. I'll never forget the look on his face when I told him. It was a combination of sheer disbelief and delight. lol

I'm reading CT6 and trying to further connect all the dots....
