# Thread: paleo-era homosexuality?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 38
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-11-10T00:03:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63231>

Let me preface this by saying I am not interested in debating the **morality**of homosexuality here.

The "let's talk about sex" thread got me thinking about this, and I didn't want to hijack that thread, so I started a new one. What I'm interested in talking about is whether or not anyone thinks that homosexuality existed in paleo-era humans. There are certainly plenty of animals that display homosexual tendancies in the modern era, even though homosexuality from a purely procreational standpoint appears to be a disadvantage (ie: no passing on of genes).

Part of me thinks that without the constraints of a society trying to make paleo-era folks feel like they needed to "fit in", that they were likely free to have sex with (and love) whoever they wanted. Another part of me says sure, that's all well and good, but back when it was all about whether or not man survived at all, wouldn't the biological emphasis be on making every copulation "count", in the procreational sense?

I think it's an interesting contrast. Discuss, if you'd like.

### shah78 — 2012-11-10T00:15:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63232>

Let me answer your question with a question. Do you think the heterosexual sex was confined to only genital to genital contact. Take away four thousand years of Western and (even Eastern ) organized religions and I imagine you would have found lots of Paleo (and even early neolithic) people really getting" their non-procreative freak on". Why would homosexual contact be any more "taboo". Taboos are soooooo neolithic!

### Huck — 2012-11-10T02:11:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63250>

Humans and culture go together.

Culture is developed over hundreds and thousands of years based on what works. It's not always right, but it is based on what people believed to be truth. Over time, we learn that some of these truths are either not true or are no longer pertinent, but parts of culture stand the test of time.

A pet peeve of mine is that our current culture in the US and the western world, is to quick to throw away cultural values and norms without a serious analysis of the consequences of such action. For example, I object to gay marriage because something in our past culture determined that it was wrong. Before I am willing to accept gay marriage, I want to know why our culture said it was wrong and why that is a wrong conclusion. I believe that culture is actually the passing of wisdom from one generation to the next.

Although I am not a religious person, I feel the same way about religion. Religious morality has served mankind for thousands of years, so some of it must be working for us. I hate to see it totally rejected by so many in our society when it has served a great purpose.

Most people around the world just try to get by. Customs and religion give them a framework to live by. So my question is, why was homosexuality labeled as taboo by all major religions and all or most modern cultures?

### shah78 — 2012-11-10T02:28:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63253>

With all due respect, a lot of hand waving to justify bigotry. They did similar hand waving regarding slavery down in Dixie back in 1820 and around Munich in 1938. If you don't like homosexuals, blacks or Jews, just say it. Don't hide behind "deep" pondering over moral and/or cultural issues. All obfuscations leading to the same result:declarations and acts of hatred against insular minorities.

### LinD — 2012-11-10T02:35:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63254>

> **Huck;65112 wrote:**
>
>
>
> So my question is, why was homosexuality labeled as taboo by all major religions and all or most modern cultures?

Because major religions' are based on the Holy Bible, and God's Word says it (homosexuality) is an abomination.

Also, the parts 'fit' between man and woman. Yay!

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-10T02:49:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63255>

There can be no doubt that homosexuality existed in the paleo era. It has survived hatred, vilification, and legislation. It will continue as long a there are human beings to love and be loved.

I can't imagine that it would ever have been a threat to survival of our species. Homosexuality is just one of a variety of sexual expression. I think that bisexuality would have been far more common; I mean, if you want to get it on, who's the closest attractive unguarded/willing partner might be the only consideration. Fun is fun after all; and it's what we humans gravitate to.

I'd be interested to know how the Kitavans viewed same sex relationships, given the sexual freedom with which they were raised. Because if you don't know where babies come from, it surely can't matter who you have sex with.

### Huck — 2012-11-10T02:57:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63256>

> **shah78;65115 wrote:** With all due respect, a lot of hand waving to justify bigotry. They did the similar hand waving regarding slavery down in Dixie back in 1820 and around Munich in 1938. If you don't like homosexuals, blacks or Jews, just say it. Don't hide behind "deep" moral and/or cultural obfuscation.

No need to be insulting. I have nothing against gays. As someone trained in Sociology, I have a respect for culture, custom, and religion and value them as a stabilizing influence on society.

That is how I look at cultural events and actions. What is the benefit to society of gay marriage? What is the downside? Are civil unions a better alternative?

These type of issues should not be decided based on emotion. but on the benefit to society. Slavery was obviously not in society's best interest and was corrected in the western world, but still practiced in other parts of the world. Our culture isn't perfect, but it is self correcting over time. I see no reason to be in a hurry to change cultural mores that have been accepted for thousands of years.

In the US we are all equal under the law. That overrides any cultural or religious beliefs we may have. But whether gay marriage is purely a cultural and religious question or a legal question is yet to be settled.

On the other hand, I believe in empirical data and when culture is proven wrong, it should be changed.

### ealachan — 2012-11-10T03:07:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63259>

> **Coriander;65118 wrote:** There can be no doubt that homosexuality existed in the paleo era. It has survived hatred, vilification, and legislation. It will continue as long a there are human beings to love and be loved.
>
>
>
> I can't imagine that it would ever have been a threat to survival of our species. Homosexuality is just one of a variety of sexual expression. I think that bisexuality would have been far more common; I mean, if you want to get it on, who's the closest attractive unguarded/willing partner might be the only consideration. Fun is fun after all; and it's what we humans gravitate to.
>
>
>
> I'd be interested to know how the Kitavans viewed same sex relationships, given the sexual freedom with which they were raised. Because if you don't know where babies come from, it surely can't matter who you have sex with.

That's what I'm curious about. Like you said, on the one hand, fun is fun and we gravitate to it, but on the other hand I don't think it's a question that many of us are truly hard-wired to want to further the species. I'm just genuinely curious if we naturally evolved with the ability to understand that sometimes sex is just sex for the sake of fun, or if that's a more "modern" way of thinking. Switching between genders of sexual partners was certainly a pretty common practice in Roman and Greek times, and surely before. I wonder if it's just always been a part of us, like it seems to be with other animals, or if we basically had to evolve enough of a brain to get past the biological imperative of furthering the species.

### shah78 — 2012-11-10T03:12:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63262>

Dear Huck,The beauty of the gay rights debate is that people of our generation (and older) will sooner than later die off and the empirical data debate will simply go "puff". Young people have almost no hangups on this subject.

### kathylu — 2012-11-10T03:13:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63263>

> **Huck;65119 wrote:** N
>
> In the US we are all equal under the law. That overrides any cultural or religious beliefs we may have. But whether gay marriage is purely a cultural and religious question or a legal question is yet to be settled.

Huck, that's not really true concerning gays, is it? Aside from the emotional and social aspects of marriage, gays in a committed relationship have no legal status or protection in most states. The obvious example is that they have no right to make medical decisions for one another. There are many other examples. Civil unions do not equal marriage, they only approximate it...and they don't eliminate the aura of "2nd class citizen". Smacks of the "separate but equal" racial claptrap of the 50's and 60's. Gays are people too...I don't see any good reason to restrict their legal rights.

### kathylu — 2012-11-10T03:15:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63265>

> **shah78;65125 wrote:** Dear Huck,The beauty of the gay rights debate is that people of our generation (and older) will sooner than later die off and the empirical data debate will simply go "puff". Young people have almost no hangups on this subject.

You're right. The "writing is on the wall", as they say.

### shah78 — 2012-11-10T03:20:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63266>

Yes Kathylu," we are all equal", but some of us are "more equal" than others.

### Huck — 2012-11-10T03:27:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63268>

> **kathylu;65126 wrote:** Huck, that's not really true concerning gays, is it? Aside from the emotional and social aspects of marriage, gays in a committed relationship have no legal status or protection in most states. The obvious example is that they have no right to make medical decisions for one another. There are many other examples. Civil unions do not equal marriage, they only approximate it...and they don't eliminate the aura of "2nd class citizen". Smacks of the "separate but equal" racial claptrap of the 50's and 60's. Gays are people too...I don't see any good reason to restrict their legal rights.

The purpose of marriage was to sanctify the family unit, man and woman, for the purpose of raising children. Many, me included, dislike stealing the term marriage to mean something different than what it has meant in culture and religion.

Civil unions give gays and anyone else, all the legal protections of marriage, but preserves the term marriage in its historical usage. So civil unions resolve any legal issues. The fight over what it is called is a cultural debate, not a legal one.

### Michael — 2012-11-10T09:45:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63329>

> **LinD;65117 wrote:** Because major religions' are based on the Holy Bible,

Buddhism isn't. If you take a wider geographical and historical perspective you find that Buddhism has actually been the world's most prolific religion, holding sway over pretty densely populated areas of S. E. Asia for a very long stretch of time.

Don't fall into perspectival errors about what is and is not of historical and cultural relevance just because of where you happen to live.

Buddhism, of course, expects similar standards of sexual behaviour to Christianity (and in addition frowns on alcohol). It hasn't always got them: Japanese Buddhist monasteries were noted for homosexual activity at one time.

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-10T14:25:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63343>

Getting back to evolutionary or survival function there are species of lizards where the males become homosexual when food in the environment becomes scarce. I think this is genius. It allows the males to remain reproductively viable while not actually reproducing. When conditions become right they become hetero again. I think there may be is a similar mechanism at play in humans related to population.

> > Marriage (also called matrimony or wedlock) is a social union or legal contract between people called spouses that creates kinship.\[citation needed\] The definition of marriage varies according to different cultures, but is usually an institution in which interpersonal relationships, usually intimate and sexual, are acknowledged. Such a union is often formalized via a wedding ceremony. In terms of legal recognition, most sovereign states and other jurisdictions limit marriage to two persons of opposite sex or gender in the gender binary, and some of these allow polygynous marriage. Since 2000, several countries and some other jurisdictions have legalized same-sex marriage. In some cultures, marriage is recommended or compulsory before pursuing any sexual activity.

### N~Doro — 2012-11-11T03:15:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sex-sub-forum-topic-three-fetish.4760/post-63517>

> **Cavemam;65330 wrote:** Ok peanut gallery! As much as I'm dying to know all about your particular kinks. . .I was sort of hoping for a little talk about the BRAIN WIRING for these things.

Ok, so I recently read a theory speculating that foot fetishes may be caused by the way our sensory maps are located on the brain. The area for feet sensory processing is usually right next to the gonad sensory processing area (as in, physically right next door on our brain). This might be because in the womb the feet are usually pulled up near the nads as the brain is developing the ability to sense those areas. A little cross firing/wiring and you have some uber nice sensations when you see/think about/... feet. i think the book I was reading was The Brain that Changes Itself by Norman Doidge

[http://www.amazon.com/Brain-That-Changes-Itself-Frontiers/dp/0143113100/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1352596355&sr=1-1&keywords=the+brain+that+changes+itself+by+norman+doidge](http://http://www.amazon.com/Brain-That-Changes-Itself-Frontiers/dp/0143113100/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1352596355&sr=1-1&keywords=the+brain+that+changes+itself+by+norman+doidge)

Fetishes are ideal for our "monogamy" that current life dictates. It adds a third leg to the relationship that allows for some seperation of church and state. So it allows a fulfilling sex life that isn't dependent on the relationship and also not a threat to it.

Fetishes are more a problem in the beginning as the prince/princess ideal doesn't allow for someone to be aroused outside of the romance. Once someone can get past that notion, OR join in the fun. . .a much better construct for successful companions and mates.

Just like with Pam though, if the person with said fetish is out there to fill that instead of filling a companion/romantic relationship then it is just creepy.

People with fetish: This should unfold at the exact same rate as all sexual conversations. You don't start drawing maps to your orgasm until well after a relationship and sex start to happen. I think fetish is like that. TOO MUCH TOO SOON!

note for single men: If you let the woman "discover" your fetish, you will have a convert.

### ruby — 2012-11-10T04:29:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63287>

Where does this "carbs are the body's preferred fuel source", "brain and heart prefer carbs as fuel" stuff come from? Anybody knows of any research that shows how we know what the body's 'preferred' fuel source is? Any paper that documents it? This is some kind of official mantra that I keep hearing all around but where are the supporting facts?

### Michael — 2012-11-10T09:30:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63328>

I think because practically speaking it's been found that's what it will use if they're around. This is why people who aim to be in ketosis consume such low levels of carbohydrate (

### ruby — 2012-11-10T15:28:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63364>

Thanks, Michael. I will check out the book. The "preferred" fuel source implies to me that glucose is intrinsically superior as an energy source to ketones (better quality, better/less damaging end products etc) rather than just "preferred" as in terms of a simple economic cost/benefit principle (less energy required to utilize it). What is your take?

### Michael — 2012-11-10T16:19:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63375>

> **ruby;65234 wrote:** What is your take?

I don't think I know enough to have a useful opinion.

I think you're right in suggesting "preferred" could have several senses. Maybe people slip between these sometimes without noticing?

I do wonder if the body seeks to do SOMETHING with glucose in a hurry - burn it for energy, store it as fat, whatever, because the imperative is to get it out the bloodstream before it does too much damage. AFAICT, some of the people loosely connected with the WAPF (Chris Masterjohn, Stephan Guyenet, Chris Kresser) want to play down the role of glucose in damage to bloodvessels etc. and say that it's all more complex than that. I dunno. Maybe that's wishful thinking - and not necessarily for the same reasons in each case.

To be frank, I sometimes think people develop loyalty to someone who points a way out of the mire to them. Chris Masterjohn, who seems a very nice and an exceedingly smart man, obviously reverences Weston Price. Robb Wolf seems to feel the same way about Professor Cordain. Both men had been vegetarians and got very ill from that. Suppose the one had read Cordain at that time and the other Price. Would their opinions have been reversed?

### ruby — 2012-11-11T08:51:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63553>

You seem to be very adept at trawling through info on the net, so if you ever come across a more precise definition of "preferred" in terms of body fuel, please let me know

My simplistic and possibly ill-informed take on carbs seems similar to yours in that the body for all those thousands of years made its own supplies and this is the preferred state of affairs. Now when we consume carbs (pure and loads of them) and flood it with them, it kind of goes into panic to put out inflammation "fires". And after a while it stops succeeding.

I agree with your loyalty theory, like baby duck-style imprinting. Same happened to me

__That sounds right to me. But I guess there are exceptions. That recent New York Times_ article we had here "The Island Where People Forget to Die" would be one:

[http://www.nytimes.com/2012/10/28/magazine/the-island-where-people-forget-to-die.html?pagewanted=all](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2012/10/28/magazine/the-island-where-people-forget-to-die.html?pagewanted=all)

The diet was pretty good: high in fish, fresh produce, olive oil, and local wine (which would be weak but high in antioxidants) - but hardly low-carb, since it also contained a fair amount of bread and pulses.

Perhaps you can get away with that if_ you get the sleep and don't stare at TVs into the night and have a realxed way of life and no central heating.

### ruby — 2012-11-11T11:25:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63561>

Frank Marlowe's "The Hadza hunter-gatherers of Tanzania" gives a good overview of the lifestyle of current tropical hunter-gatherers.

Seasonality rules. Tubers, which are around throughout the year, are fallback foods when preferred things like meat, honey, berries etc are not available. Lifestyle is wake up between 6:30-7:00am and become active gradually. Eat a bit of leftover food and go foraging around 9:00am. Nap at midday. Start cooking around 5:00pm and eat around 7:30pm. Bedtime is 9:00pm. Ocassionally they stay dancing and singing until midnight. They eat:

17.2% berries, 23.5% tubers, 19.2% baobab, 14.2% honey, 19.3% meat (mammals 6.4%, birds 84.4%, reptiles 0.2%, invertebrates 0.1%) and 6.6% other. Average body fat for adults is 20% women, 11% men.

Looks like significant carbs to me ....

### Michael — 2012-11-11T11:59:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63563>

Yeah, they seem to eat a lot of honey at times.

I suppose taking what they currently do as representative would be problematic inasmuch as the land that's left to them is probably marginal. Also, they're subject to game laws. The government aims to preserve the game for tourists on safari to view. I guess that wouldn't cover the birds (84%) but they'd probably have to think twice before killing, say, giraffe.

But they seem to be doing OK on what they are getting, whatever.

seems brain does prefer glucose.

Robert S. Horn of [http://www.medbio.info/](http://http://www.medbio.info/) says:

A stable blood glucose level is absolutely essential for normal brain function. The brain can only use glucose or ketone bodies as its fuel. Ketone bodies (acetyl acetate or ÃŸ-hydroxybutyrate) cannot replace glucose as the brain's energy source on short notice. About 10-14 days are required to increase plasma ketone body levels such that they can provide energy for neural tissues. At most they can provide about 50% of the brain's energy, the rest must come from glucose.

### Michael — 2012-11-16T20:55:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64780>

Nora seems basically to follow Dr. Rosedale in her macronutrient ratios - and, IIRC, credits him explicitly in the back of her book where she gives protein recommenations.

Jack doesn't see quite eye-to-eye with Dr. Rosedale, although he agrees wirh him in some areas. I asked him about Dr. Rosedale's views on the Ask Jack board, and he said he was forgetting how animals stay warm in winter.

AFAIK, protein >>> glucose isn't the only route for gluconeogenesis, although it's the most important one. In any event, Nora's/Dr. Rosedale's prescription provides enough to make as much glucose as is needed as well as to provide for body-building needs.

I think - and how would I know? - that Dr. Rosedale may be over-rating the longevity value of calorie and/or protein restriction. I don't think there's any firm evidence for it in humans.

I think Dr. K would be at ease with a little more protein, and would tell us we don't need to count.

He certainly doesn't believe in year-round ketosis. Carbs are OK when in-season in his book.

Also, I think some of the parameters around what happens on a very low-carb diet CHANGE when that VLC diet is also high in iodine. (Cf the "staying warm in winter" comment.) Dr. Rosedale's diet is not a high-seafood diet.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-16T21:06:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64782>

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-16T21:09:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64783>

This notion that the brain needs exogenous glucose is pure folly.......our liver makes all we need. We do not need any exogenous carbs period. But this does not mean we cant use carbs when they grow. This is where the paleo crew tries to speak for me. #Epicfaileo

### Michael — 2012-11-16T21:38:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64787>

The other thing I remember from Dr. Cunnane's superb book is that iodine is also involved in several steps in ketosis. That I didn't know. I haven't tried to check, although I have the chemistry to do so, partly because I rather dislike chemistry, having been pushed that way against my natural direction at a young age.

I also suspect no account of ketosis would really be complete without an account of energy metabolism in babies. Newborns seem to go into it relatively easily - and that they should be able to seems to be a must. The large human brain is - and this is something people miss - a huge survival problem (although in the long run it may confer some survival advantages on us). If your brain went short of fuel for any length of time it would mean damage and death - hence the baby has to be able to switch between energy substrates easily. I'm indebted again to Dr. Cunnane's book here. I'm not sure quite why it's easy for the baby to get into ketosis: it may be partly the composition of the milk (although that isn't particularly low in sugar), but it seems partly to be linked to the all-over fat layer babies have. Even their wrists are dimpled. The fatty-acid composition of that fat layer is quite unusual. (And there's presumably a connection to post-partum depression here - the baby's robbed out the fatty acids it needs from the mother.

These seem like deep, complex and interesting matters to me. I think trying to approach it all merely on the level of macronutrient ratios, as many seem to be doing, is no good.

I feel very grateful to Jack for opening up a deeper view on these things.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-16T21:43:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64788>

Thanks to you, again, Michael and to Ruby and Dr Kruse. The more times I circle around this knowledge, the better I understand it. I appreciate all the guidance and help!

Interesting re: babies, brains and ketosis. The literature is clear that breastfeeding mothers are partially protected from post natal depression (and that even when babies have mothers who are depressed, if they are fully breastfed they show normal neurological patterns in testing whereas formula fed infants do not). So not only does nature set us up with ketosis, it has a natural mechanism for optimal for the adults supporting these infants, too. The composition of breastmilk also contains leptin, so there is another factor in how this optimal metabolic circuitry is established.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-16T21:51:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64790>

Babies are in ketosis for the first 6 yrs of life.......want to know why? Ketosis is how we myelinate. Think about what really causes MS now?

The nonsense I read from so many paleo book authors is infuriating because they have zero clue about how a human brain is made, born and grows. It is made immature on purpost to fit out the vag and then it myelinates postnatally.......by ketosis......mothers milk is the fuel to do it upon. Once myelinated it can function on a wider range or macro's.......but it is not optimal. Over a life time we marginally lose function when we eat outside the brain's biology. Sustained carbohydrate eating will hurt the physiologic parameters of brain function......it is not arguable unless you are a paleo person who bought the dogma found in their books hook line and sinker. They pride themselves on understanding evolution but few of them understand human brain evolution and this is what sets the standard for what we should eat for our species.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-12-10T19:34:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-67056>

Ron Rosedale's talk at AHS (which I recently watched online for free) discussed this too. Â He concurs with Dr Kruse. Â He also points out that all fibrous veggies break down into fatty acids, so that's eating fat too. Â He totally agrees that we could never have evolved into humans with carbs fuelling the brain and further agrees that our liver makes all the glucose we need. Â He points out that you 'could' argue that instead of the liver making glucose you can feed it to the liver through exogenous carbs. However, he suggests (and this seems reasonable to me) that your body does a much better job of knowing when it needs glucose than you do; so if you eat it, you are dictating when your body has to deal with glucose; if you let your body make it, it will make it at times when it's needed. I think this makes sense. Â And given what he says about fibrous veggies, it stands to reason that one could maintain ketosis throughout the seasons, even while eating seasonal veggies when they are growing. Â I suppose the main difference would be starchy tubers and higher glucose veggies like carrots and beets that I think Wahls promotes.

### ruby — 2012-12-13T06:48:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-67326>

The info is all out there, it's just overlooked or ... You don't need a PhD.

In the most basic, high-school level nutrition course they teach you about essential stuff that your body needs. These are amino and fatty acids (protein and fats). No mention of carbs (cos there's no such thing as essential carbs).

Essential is defined as: body needs to have it and can't make it. So by definition, essential nutrients are exogenous.

Yet, nobody really highlights or registers the significance of the fact that 'essential' and 'carbs' don't go together.

Rather they go on insisting that if you don't get at least 50% of your diet in the carb form you're setting yourself up for deficiencies and worse.Â

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-10T06:32:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-pressure-then-and-now.4762/post-63306>

I have not had any labs done since starting VLC paleo near the first of Spring transitioning to VLC epipaleo however I took my BP at one of the sit-down BP machines at the local K-mart pharmacy. Early in the year my BP was 130 something over 80 something. Two weeks ago I used the same machine and my BP was 114/69. This is after losing a total of 52 pounds since early Spring. I AM considering getting a glucose meter to satisfy my curiosity about the effect of ketones, certain foods, and at different times of the day.

In case someone could use the info, I read today that IV vitamin C can cause false high blood glucose readings. Anecdotally, someone on the Vitamin C Foundation wrote that oral liposomal vitamin C gave him higher glucose readings. Could be another case of a false reading? It made me wonder how the brain utilizes vitamin C when the body is in a ketogenic state since glucose is just one enzyme away from being vitamin C.

Conversely, more than 98 percent of US adults fail to get the recommended 4,700 mg of potassium per day.

> >

As a result, the 2010 DGA identified potassium as a â€œnutrient of concern in American diets.â€￾ Simultaneously meeting sodium and potassium guidelines continues to be a challenge, both for consumers and public health officials. ### JanSz — 2012-11-16T23:43:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-bloating-issues.4764/post-64816>

> **seanb4;65199 wrote:** Hi guys,
>
>
>
> I'm back with some labs that I managed to get from my local GP.
>
>
>
> My problem is bloating after eating or drinking anything, plus lots of other symptoms (constipation, excema, dizziness, and many more). I have tried diets/supplements to treat sibo, candida, dysbiosis, overacidity, underacidity, H pylori, and many more with no success.

Estrogen dominance symptoms include:

Water retention and bloating

Symptoms of Menopause

Weight gain and bloating

Symptoms of Premenstrual Syndrome

Water retention: Bloating, heaviness, weight gain (2-4 Kg), breast soreness and swelling, generalized rheumatic pain, abdominal bloating and heaviness.

///////////////////////////////////////////

Common causes of abdominal bloating are:

Overeating

Gastric distension

Lactose intolerance, fructose intolerance and other food intolerances

Food allergy

Aerophagia (air swallowing, a nervous habit)

Irritable bowel syndrome

Partial bowel obstruction

Gastric dumping syndrome or rapid gastric emptying

Gas-producing foods

Constipation

Visceral fat

Splenic-flexure syndrome

Menstruation, dysmenorrhea

Polycystic ovary syndrome and ovarian cysts

Alvarez' syndrome, hysterical or neurotic abdominal bloating without excess of gas in the digestive tract

Massive infestation with intestinal parasites, such as worms (e.g., Ascaris lumbricoides)

Diverticulosis

Celiac Disease

Certain Medications such as Phentermine

Occurs in some due to Salivary hypersecretion and dehydration.

Intestinal worms.

//

# Thread: ADHD Drugs Impact The Brain's Reward System
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adhd-drugs-impact-the-brains-reward-system.4765/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-10T10:39:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adhd-drugs-impact-the-brains-reward-system.4765/post-63332>

> "It had been discussed for years whether treating ADHD with Ritalin and similar drugs affects the reward system to any significant degree, simply because the dosage given to patients is so low. We are the first to show that some components of the dopamine signaling pathways are extremely sensitive to drugs like Ritalin. We have also developed a unified theory to describe the effect of such drugs on the dopamine signal." ...

[http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/252638.php](http://http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/252638.php)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-10T10:50:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adhd-drugs-impact-the-brains-reward-system.4765/post-63333>

I have never understood why psychiatrists have not understood that the balance of the reward tracts is like a gymnast on a balance beam........about 30 different neural pathways end in this area and all modulate its activity.......Why would not Ritilin be subject to the same effect?

### Michael — 2012-11-10T10:52:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hospital-offers-allergen-free-menu-to-children.4766/post-63334>

On offer at Monash Children's Hospital in Victoria, Australia, will be:

> Six dishes which do not contain milk, eggs, soy, fish, shellfish, peanuts, tree nuts, wheat or sesame
>
>
>
>
> They say:
>
>
>
>
> > "We recognised that as an organisation we are seeing more children with food allergies,"
>
>
>
> ... which kind of begs the question why.
>
>
>
> The news source says:
>
>
>
> An estimated one in 10 children \[in the State of Victoria\] have food allergies.

# Thread: Scottish scientists think they've proved that fat makes you fat ...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scottish-scientists-think-theyve-proved-that-fat-makes-you-fat.4767/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-10T10:58:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scottish-scientists-think-theyve-proved-that-fat-makes-you-fat.4767/post-63335>

I think we saw this one before a couple of months ago in the _Times_ of London.

> SCOTTISH scientists have discovered that ... high-fat foods damage the \[brain\] ...
>
>
>
> The research shed \[sic\] light on how dieters will be able to lose weight without yo-yo dieting and \[will\] help tackle Scotland’s burgeoning childhood obesity crisis. ...

In your dreams ...

[http://www.express.co.uk/posts/view/357225](http://http://www.express.co.uk/posts/view/357225)

The article is illustrated by a picture of a Scotsman eating "high fat" food.

### Stavity — 2012-11-10T14:37:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scottish-scientists-think-theyve-proved-that-fat-makes-you-fat.4767/post-63347>

Sure if it is bad fat, mixed with a bun, American cheese and FF's. I remember those days of eating poisonous fats and what they did to me. I don't miss bloating, my liver enlarging with pain, swollen ankles, neck and shoulder aching, and sleepless 2 hrs at a time with acid reflux.

WHY DO THEY REPORT MEDIOCRE SCIENCE? They really do want to keep people confused.

### Michael — 2012-11-10T15:25:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scottish-scientists-think-theyve-proved-that-fat-makes-you-fat.4767/post-63361>

> **Stavity;65217 wrote:**
>
> WHY DO THEY REPORT MEDIOCRE SCIENCE?

"Mediocre science"? The professor at the University of Aberdeen will already be taking his ancestral Claymore from the wall and asking for your address ...

I guess the papers run these stories, because they have column inches to fill, and "researchers", knowing that and wishing to justify their existence, send them press releases.

I think another reason why such a story might "have legs" is buried in that report: the dietary establishment likes it. It gives them a rationalization for NOT reassessing what they've been saying for the past half-century while accounting for the increasingly obvious - and terribly embarrassing - fact that low-carb diets work. As a dietitian what you claim is that while these "crash" diets work "in the short term", in the long term they fail. No evidence is, of course, necessary for the latter assertion, but you can gesture vaguely in the direction of a rat study, or what the University of Auchtermuchty has said, or might have said, and look knowing.

# Thread: The misuse of neuroscience
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-10T13:22:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/post-63340>

Here's a letter to London's _Daily Telegraph_ signed by several psychologists, neuroscientists, and social scientists that's implicitly critical of the current trend for illustrating newspaper articles with images of brains, implying that neuroscience has demonstrated things it hasn't, and forgetting that we are not merely brains but whole persons and, beyond that, moving in a whole social environment:

> SIR â€“ Failed educational attainment, persistent unemployment, poverty, addiction and crime are problems associated with politics, economics and morality, not neuroscience.
>
>
>
> The picture illustrating Alisdair Palmerâ€™s article showed a large and a small brain, but did not explain that the small brain followed â€œsevere total global neglectâ€￾: an infant caged in a cot for 23 or more hours a day, physically and mentally isolated, malnourished, and often cold and sick.
>
>
>
> In contrast the large brain probably belonged to an infant whose mother provided normal amounts of responsiveness and care to her baby. Parents providing intermittent food, shelter, warmth and comfort, according to the demands of normal life, create children with normal brains.
>
>
>
> Neuroscience has not demonstrated that parenting with heightened sensitivity will resolve social ills. But we understand why some might wish it had. Then there would be no need to restructure the economy, develop good jobs, revitalise blighted areas, and do the other hard work necessary to improve society. Instead we could just blame bad mothers for creating bad people.
>
>
>
> Dr Stuart W G Derbyshire
>
> Reader in Psychology, University of Birmingham
>
>
>
> Dr Ellie Lee
>
> Reader in Social Policy, University of Kent
>
>
>
> Professor Raymond Tallis
>
> Emeritus Professor, University of Manchester
>
>
>
> Dr Caroline Di Bernardi Luft
>
> Research Fellow, Cognitive Neuroimaging Laboratory, University of Birmingham
>
>
>
> Dr Pam Lowe
>
> Senior Lecturer in Sociology, Aston University
>
>
>
> Dr Joe McCleery
>
> Lecturer in Developmental Neuroscience, University of Birmingham
>
>
>
> Dr Jan Macvarish
>
> Research Fellow, Centre for Health Services Studies, University of Kent

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/letters/9652341/Problem-children-soon-reach-an-age-when-they-are-beyond-help.html](http://http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/letters/9652341/Problem-children-soon-reach-an-age-when-they-are-beyond-help.html)

### Thor — 2012-11-13T04:33:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/post-63948>

crazy stuff out there

### nonchalant — 2012-11-13T04:47:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/post-63959>

Maybe THAT's why we are following Jack? Because of the 3-inch brain at the top of this page? Perhaps it's not all the science he reveals, it's just the logo?

### Michael — 2012-11-13T10:26:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/post-63985>

> **Nonchalant;65907 wrote:** Maybe THAT's why we are following Jack? Because of the 3-inch brain at the top of this page? Perhaps it's not all the science he reveals, it's just the logo?

I guess Jack has some title to put one there, since he deals with them professionally. Still, I suppose it's a kind of oblique proof of the iconographic value of the brain. Otherwise, he'd have put a stethoscope or something there instead.

### Michael — 2012-11-10T14:32:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nice-quote.4770/post-63345>

Here's one from one of my favourite poets:

[http://mobile.brainyquote.com/quotes/quotes/w/williambut133313.html](http://http://mobile.brainyquote.com/quotes/quotes/w/williambut133313.html)

### Michael — 2012-11-11T09:55:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nice-quote.4770/post-63558>

There used to be a full-page advertisement for Jameson's Irish whiskey with the line "smoother than the Scotch" accompanied by some particularly spiky and difficult to understand Robbie Burns and Yeats's _The Wild Swans at Coole_ ... "The trees are in their autumn beauty,/ The woodland paths are dry, ..."

Unfair to Burns or Scottish whisky, of course, but quite clever. Amazing how advertising gets into everything.

They've not yet used "He wishes for the cloths of heaven" to sell anything. Maybe a shoe company could make use of that one?

[http://www.online-literature.com/yeats/776/](http://http://www.online-literature.com/yeats/776/)

### Michael — 2012-11-11T12:13:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nice-quote.4770/post-63565>

I'd buy the collected poems. Paperback copies are ridiculously cheap - Amazon UK does them for about the price of a pint of beer:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/gp/aw/d/1853264547](http://http://www.amazon.co.uk/gp/aw/d/1853264547)

Probably the same in Australia.

Some of the poetry is online, but I don't think it's all out of copyright yet. Project Gutenberg may have some. Librivox has some recordings people have made - of variable quality, since it's just amateur offerings by people. About a year ago they did _The Fiddler of Dooney_ which is fun:

[http://librivox.org/the-fiddler-of-dooney-by-william-butler-yeats/](http://http://librivox.org/the-fiddler-of-dooney-by-william-butler-yeats/)

Librivox has also sponsored readings of "When you are old and grey and full of sleep ..." which is a rather beautiful poem.

There must be something by Yeats for every mood.

### LinD — 2012-11-12T04:29:42+0200

### vwaggs — 2012-11-12T16:19:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-my-first-injection-today.4774/post-63814>

I gained 5 lbs LOL! No wonder I feel like crap! Oh I can't wait to feel hungry! Did I really just say that?? Well here goes Day 1 vlcd! Withdrawal from all that crap is going to be painful but its my own fault. Hey Colleen, I asked a question on the HCG forum about docs supplements and wondered if having the rice and veggie cellulose is okay. Have you seen anyone have great results using the supplements vs. not? I spent a lot on them and it was hard to find them without added junk like rice even though it is probably a tiny amount.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-12T16:42:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-my-first-injection-today.4774/post-63817>

Withdrawal from all that crap is going to be painful but its my own fault. Hey Colleen, I asked a question on the HCG forum about docs supplements and wondered if having the rice and veggie cellulose is okay. Have you seen anyone have great results using the supplements vs. not? I spent a lot on them and it was hard to find them without added junk like rice even though it is probably a tiny amount.

Good Luck with VLCD #1, First 3 are the hardest!

### colleencoble — 2012-11-12T17:21:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-my-first-injection-today.4774/post-63824>

I haven't seen anyone actually DO the supplements.  Good luck! You shouldn't have any hunger unless your dose is off.

### vwaggs — 2012-11-13T16:10:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/took-my-first-injection-today.4774/post-64018>

Okay I am just going to do some of them so I don't break the bank. So far so good! I got bit hungry around 3 pm but had some left over apples to munch on and then again around 9:30 pm but it went away. I can't believe the difference in doing the injections! I don't think I ever absorbed anything sublingually. I wanted to chew off my right arm almost the entire round no matter how I changed the dose. This is so awesome! Down 4.1 lbs day 1! All water I am sure but I will take it! I don't even feel withdrawal from carbs (yet). Maybe I will skip that altogether?? I usually get a horrible headache, and exhaustion. This is so easy!

### Thor — 2012-11-30T20:34:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/contemplatin-going-dzugan-need-advice.4776/post-66004>

.

Jack keeps saying MTHFR snp genetic hickup isn't a big deal and it's an exception not rule - I have to say otherwise.Â  I suffer from it and have been treated by the only US based doctor who can deal with it - thank god for Dr Kendal Stewart, MD for saving my life...

Â 

Again MTHFR genetic hickup IS THE ONLY cause for more than 70 diseases/disorders.Â 

Â 

**Nearly 2 in 3 people of some form of the genetic hickup.Â  I have both copies at 677 and 1298 - even worse.

Â 

Guess what's the REAL cause of bad headaches and/or migraines - yep! MTHFR !Â  Treat the methylation issues and no more headaches.Â  I'll lay a million dollars on the table.Â  I could cite more than 5,000 studies and research papers showing clear evidence that it's proof MTHFR is the root of bad headaches/migraines!Â  Nothing else....Â 

Â 

Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase Gene Variant (MTHFR C677T) and Migraine: A Case Control Study and Meta-analysis**

MTHFR C677T polymorphism was associated with migraine with aura (MA) (OR 1.31, 95% CI 1.01-1.70, p = 0.039) that remained significant after adjusting for age, sex and depression status. A meta-analysis of 15 case-control studies showed that T allele homozygosity is significantly associated with MA (OR = 1.42; 95% CI, 1.10-1.82) and total migraine (OR = 1.37; 95% CI, 1.07-1.76), but not migraine without aura (OR = 1.16; 95% CI, 0.36-3.76). In studies of non-Caucasian population, the TT genotype was associated with total migraine (OR= 3.46; 95% CI, 1.22-9.82), whereas in studies of Caucasians this variant was associated with MA only (OR = 1.28; 95% CI, 1.002-1.63).

Â 

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3120667/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3120667/)

### Shijin13 — 2012-12-02T10:08:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/contemplatin-going-dzugan-need-advice.4776/post-66129>

Dzugan (DzLogic) and Rozakis (xR) used to be partners in the Dzugan method... they had some sort of falling out - and Dzugan left to start Dzlogic. Â 

Â 

THe pricing model is similar - though DzLogic is less expensive. Â Initially I started out w/xR not knowing Dzugan wasn't w/them, so I left. Â Rozakis is far more conservative than Dzugan. Â I actually spoke w/ Rozakis initially and he flat out lied to me - he told me Dzugan decided to go back to research. Â Colleen Coble had the 411 - and gave me a heads up - so I jumped ship to DzLogic as fast as possbile...Â 

Â 

IME - DzLogic is a better fit for what we're doing. Â The staff is great - they're working directly w/my PCP even though she's not affiliated - she's using me as a test case for BHRT. The send me my program at the same time they send it to her - so we can discuss the changes, they provide her a pre-written rx for any changes so she doesn't have to make any changes.Â 

they're very responsive, and I'm pretty happy right now. Â We're not yet dialed in fully on the migraines... but we'll get there. Â btwn working on the hormones and mitochondria I'll get there...

The send me my program at the same time they send it to her - so we can discuss the changes, they provide her a pre-written rx for any changes so she doesn't have to make any changes.Â

> they're very responsive, and I'm pretty happy right now. Â We're not yet dialed in fully on the migraines... but we'll get there. Â btwn working on the hormones and mitochondria I'll get there...

I am going to talk to DZLogic tomorrow and get more finite details from xR.Â  I did talk to the xR Dr. Bakke on Friday with great interest.Â  I will also be asking many more questions of xR when she calls me back tomorrow to discuss joining the program.

Either way, one of them has to find me a doc here that will work with their program.Â  That may be the deal maker or breaker.Â  If I can find a new PCP that will work with them, I can petition UHC for In Network benefits for that Doc since there does not seem to be anyone in UHC that deals with BHRT.

I am starting to feel like there is hope.Â  It all makes sense, the timing of the migraines and peri-menopause, and the menstrual migraines I got when I went off BC pills.Â  Ever since my OB changed what I had been on for 20 years, more crap started to hit the fan.Â  I was bad before, but am worse now.

Botox is still not working.Â  I am still dizzy all the time and still have some form of a daily headache/neck ache/back ache.

Also will be providing Mayo with a 24 hour urine sample for Cortisol testing, although Mayo Doc said he saw no signs of elevated cortisol during my exam.Â  Said my Genova saliva test was basically inaccurate and worthless.Â  But also dismissed me when I started to question my other symptoms and if they fell into his realm as he reviewed my bloodwork.Â  His words to me were "We are only here to discuss your Cortisol levels".

Â 

Karen from Arizona

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-02-20T09:20:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/contemplatin-going-dzugan-need-advice.4776/post-156121>

I'm not sure if MTHFR is the whole story when it comes to migraines.
I'm heterozygous for the 1298 SNP. I take methylfolate but I haven't noticed any change.
Plus I have also heard that if your environment sucks, your gut sucks and you are full of heavy metals, you might as well have ALL the SNPS because those issues will block all sorts of pathways. I have candida so I better deal with that first.
I say heal the gut first, deal with circadian rhythms, EMF,

# Thread: gender bias as an evolutionary advantage
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gender-bias-as-an-evolutionary-advantage.4777/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Entelechy — 2012-11-10T21:41:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gender-bias-as-an-evolutionary-advantage.4777/post-63416>

interesting thread ealachan had started and gave me pause for thought.. some random ideas coalescing, but could not post before the thread was closed.

the arguments presented there (other than opinion) assume that homosexuality is evolutionarily disadvantageous.

There are numerous examples of species being able to sway the sex of their offspring based on environmental conditions.

There were undoubtedly periods where resources were limited and too many pregnancies would be disadvantageous, Limiting their numbers would ensure that the fewer pregnancies in the tribe maximized their likelihood survival.

Belonging to a group was essential to survival, and all members of the tribe were invested in each other's success. Perhaps homosexuality could have evolved as a species survival trait, allowing close social bonds to be created by sexual attachment even though those bonds would not result in offspring.

Studies about altered brain gender possibly causing homosexuality have shown that androstendione blocks receptors of the fetus from receiving testosterone. Androstendione is produced during periods of stress, and correlations have been found between maternal stress and homosexuality of their offspring.

thoughts??

# Thread: Trials: supplementation with omega 3s and iron
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-11T09:12:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/post-63557>

They tried this with schoolchildren in South Africa with mixed results. In some cases the omega 3s appeared to **un**helpful. The patchy results could indicate that the samples were too small. However, it has been found in rats deficient in o-3s and Fe that giving either alone "seemed to worsen ... memory performance".

[http://my.chicagotribune.com/#section/-1/article/p2p-73228323/](http://http://my.chicagotribune.com/#section/-1/article/p2p-73228323/)

Seems to me it's likely to be best to eat fish and shellfish and thereby get an abundant supply of o-3s and Fe ... and I, Se, Cu and Zn.

An interesting trial in any event.

### Michael — 2012-11-11T18:58:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/post-63622>

More sources picking that up:

[http://www.emaxhealth.com/11306/iron-and-omega-3-fatty-acid-deficiencies-impacts-childrens-brains](http://http://www.emaxhealth.com/11306/iron-and-omega-3-fatty-acid-deficiencies-impacts-childrens-brains)

[http://www.foxnews.com/health/2012/11/08/iron-omega-3s-tied-to-different-effects-on-kids-brains/](http://http://www.foxnews.com/health/2012/11/08/iron-omega-3s-tied-to-different-effects-on-kids-brains/)

And here's the original resource:

[http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2012/10/23/ajcn.112.041004.abstract?sid=d8a6e39b-a1bf-41e1-bdcc-80be6c1a5719](http://http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2012/10/23/ajcn.112.041004.abstract?sid=d8a6e39b-a1bf-41e1-bdcc-80be6c1a5719)

See also:

[http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01092377](http://http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01092377)

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-11T19:58:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/post-63649>

Wonder what their diets are really like. If they're eating sugar and grains, then taking one little O-3 pill isn't going to help much. The results could actually show to be worse because people can sometimes think, well I took my "healthy little pill", now I can go eat more cake.

### Michael — 2012-11-12T09:08:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/post-63792>

I think they're probably not making decisions about what to eat - just eating whatever the parents give them which will be mostly determined by what's available, what's cheap, and what's culturally normal, and probably never enough. That's probably unchanged whether they take anything or whether they don't.

I think the ineffectiveness in some of the cases probably really is down to these nutrients needing to work synergistically. Why performance can actually get _worse_ I don't know, though - but then I guess no-one does.

It's a little sad.

# Thread: Ribosylation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-11T12:35:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/post-63566>

Is there a concern over possible increased ribosylation end products (AGE) from taking too much D-ribose? There was an in vitro and in vivo mouse study showing even more ribose induced advanced glycation end products in mouse brains than were glucose AGE products in mouse brains. Is it a matter of having all the necessary mitochondrial cofactors and proper dosing to properly utilize D-ribose so that it doesn't float around producing excess AGE?

I understand that fat burning by mitochondria produces less free radicals/less inflamation than mitochondria burning glucose. Is it the same with mitochondria utilizing ribose?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-11T14:07:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/post-63571>

D-Ribose is not physiologically used at all in glycolytic pathways. It is used only in regeneration of ATP and it salvages xanthine and hypoxanthine from AMP in ischemia...........It is vital in RNA and DNA construction too but it comes direct from pentose phosphate pathway exclusively under the control of two allosteric enzymes. It is not uses in the Kreb cycle to burn for energy so the answer is not in humans.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-13T07:38:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/post-63966>

Geez Louise! Why didn't anyone warn me about Ribose Breath?

Does this stink only appear when mixing the powder with water or will it happen if I swallow capsules, too?

The ingredient label says the only ingredient is 100% D-ribose powder. Would ribose smell/taste any different than D-ribose?

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-13T08:05:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/post-63968>

Nevermind. I read that D-ribose is more bioavailable than ribose.

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-11T15:05:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calories-shmalories.4786/post-63576>

"It had been a year, at least, since Ghanshyam last ate meat, eight months since he was able to catch fish in the village river, and six since he had had an egg, he told me.

This diet, heavy in cereals and other carbohydrate-based calories, is what most rural Indians eat. In 2010, 64 percent of the calories consumed by villagers came from cereals, about 9 percent from oils and fats, less than 5 percent each from sugar and pulses like the lentils we ate. Fruit and vegetables, meat, eggs and fish together made up about 2.5 percent, according to studies of meals across rural India by the statistics ministry."

[http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2012-10-22/hunger-stalks-my-father-s-india-long-after-starvation-banished.html#disqus\_thread](http://http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2012-10-22/hunger-stalks-my-father-s-india-long-after-starvation-banished.html#disqus_thread)

# Thread: Mark, I never thought it of you
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-11T19:18:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/post-63624>

I always liked Mark Sisson, but how could he even think of linking this?

> Despite a general trend for larger mammals to have larger brains, humans are the primates with the largest brain and number of neurons, but not the largest body mass. Why are great apes, the largest primates, not also those endowed with the largest brains? Recently, we showed that the energetic cost of the brain is a linear function of its numbers of neurons. Here we show that metabolic limitations that result from the number of hours available for feeding and the low caloric yield of raw foods impose a tradeoff between body size and number of brain neurons ...

[http://www.pnas.org/content/109/45/18571.abstract](http://http://www.pnas.org/content/109/45/18571.abstract)

Put my leaves and twigs on the fire and everything's solved ... or is it?

How about asking a brain chemist?

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;TUpuhXbUX64\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TUpuhXbUX64\[/video\]\[/video\]

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-11T20:26:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/post-63655>

How did we overcome the metabolic constraint? Re read Brain Gut 13.......optimal performance is a function of the DHA quantum effect on increasing energy production 60% at the Mg dependent ATPase.

Simple. You do not want to know this when you pushing eating meat and meat based supplements to your audience.

This is called going perpendicular.......something few in paleo are capable of.

### Michael — 2012-11-11T21:11:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/post-63674>

> **Jack Kruse;65559 wrote:**
>
> Simple. You do not want to know this when you pushing eating meat and meat based supplements to your audience.

I dunno, Jack. I don't think it's calculated; I don't even think he's engaging in self-deception. It just seems to me he's got a pretty loose grip. He's an energetic man and regularly puts out lively and well-written stuff that's he's put thought and research into ... and yet, like the newspapers, he's got space to fill and if someone says something about the Palaeolithic and it makes it into print he'll throw it out there come what may.

But maybe I shouldn't have posted this thread. And now you comment on my post, and there are probably people in Paleo who'll hate you for doing so.

The other day I accidentally stumbled on what seemed to be a paleo hate-site (really) where anyone can post stuff meant to attack other people in the movement. Internal hatred seems to be becoming a disease in the movement. I didn't stay long, but I caught a post from one lady who was complaining about _you on account she said she'd posted some pieces in 2010 saying the movement was overlooking the evolutionary and nutritional importance of seafood, pre-dating your championing of the evidence. Well, good for her. But I guess the upshot is that now we take against people because we agree with them_. Yikes!

I think the movement will have to come round on this one. There's too much evidence. That encephelization is down to cooking just seems barmy to me. I can't see people as clever as Cordain falling for that one.

I stumbled on some interesting material discussing shellfish gathering in Turkey into the Neolithic recently:

[http://www.yesilova.ege.edu.tr/zaferderin-%20makale.pdf](http://http://www.yesilova.ege.edu.tr/zaferderin-%20makale.pdf)

This stuff is cropping up all over the place.

I think if you add the increasing archaeological evidence for the exploitation of water-resources to the increasing evidence for the importance of the nutrients found most abundantly in them - are omega 3s ever out of the news now? - then eventually people have to take notice.

My guess: it may be all liver and bacon now, but within the next two to three years there will be some interesting and useful presentations on water-resouces at AHS.

And if people like Jimmy Moore are half-awake they'll be on the phone to people like Crawford, Cunnane, Kuipers, Broadhurst, Hibbeln, etc., too.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-12T04:39:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/post-63776>

Mike......I could care less what they think. I only care about people who seek their answers. Mr. Moore is not really interested in truth.......he is interested in market share and following the pack. He is a real nice man but not a man of science. Me I like going perpendicular with my thoughts. I do not need to straddle the land mass.......I like going straight out to the open ocean and finding a new world........Where Homo's solution really is. The paleo solution is a step int eh right direction from a SAD........but that is all it is.........and we have a cadre of "nice guys" protecting that franchise.......but none of their info will save our species long term. They are like a band aid on an arterial wound a nice thought that is grossly ineffective.

# Thread: Scary Seafood of the Week....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kathylu — 2012-11-12T06:09:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63784>

I have vowed to try a new seafood item every time I shop at the asian grocery fish counter. This week's entry: flounder roe! Pink, veined, lobular sacs of fishy wonderfulness. I have no doubt that the nutritional value is outstanding, but how to prepare them? Most recipes say to dredge in flour (that's out of course), salt and pepper and saute in butter or bacon fat. I'm having them tomorrow morning for breakfast. Will let you know how they are...with pics!

What are you doing to expand your seafood repetoire?

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-12T16:16:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63813>

Flounder roe! I have been looking for this since I discovered it in a fish market on Long Island last spring. It is delicious and oh so nutritious. All roe is a choline party. It was inexpensive, but seasonal. Where are you located? If the season is on I need to know. It is firm enough to slice into 1/2 in thick medallions which I pan fried and spiced. I have been using cooked frozen lobster roe as a staple ingredient in my fish broths.

### kathylu — 2012-11-12T17:12:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63820>

Ok, here it is! The lobes remind me of a pancreas. Not very appetizing to look at, but they were actually pretty tasty! I sauteed them in bacon fat, seasoned with salt and pepper and squeezed fresh lime over them. Success!

Attached files [ ](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/65761=569-DSCN2050.jpg)[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/65761=570-DSCN2051.jpg)

### kathylu — 2012-11-12T17:15:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63821>

> **Dali Dula;65754 wrote:** Flounder roe! I have been looking for this since I discovered it in a fish market on Long Island last spring. It is delicious and oh so nutritious. All roe is a choline party. It was inexpensive, but seasonal. Where are you located? If the season is on I need to know. It is firm enough to slice into 1/2 in thick medallions which I pan fried and spiced. I have been using cooked frozen lobster roe as a staple ingredient in my fish broths.

Dali, I'm in Maryland. The roe looked quite fresh, but to be honest, I didn't check the origin. I was just so excited to try something new, I forgot to look. So, I suppose they might not have been local.

### shah78 — 2012-11-12T22:25:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63864>

> **kathylu;65761 wrote:** Ok, here it is! The lobes remind me of a pancreas. Not very appetizing to look at, but they were actually pretty tasty! I sauteed them in bacon fat, seasoned with salt and pepper and squeezed fresh lime over them. Success!

Compared to the desocketed-eyeballs I've been eating lately, it looks quiet tame.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-12T22:31:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63865>

Kathylu, it is really good to eat raw, too. If you buy it fresh, just cut it up and eat it. If it's not fresh (as in caught same day), I freeze it in cubes and eat it that way. It is super good for you and when raw tastes of almost nothing. (If it has a fishy smell at all, I freeze it.)

### Michael — 2012-11-12T09:02:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-was-before-sugar.4795/post-63791>

News report:

> The skeletons of three centuries-old Alaska Natives discovered in McGrath last month likely lived and died before Caucasians ever made contact, archaeologists say. ...
>
>
>
> ... recent analysis of charcoal buried just above one of the skeletons has been dated to between 1470 and 1650 A.D., making the bones at least 362 to 542 years old
>
>
>
>
>
>
> I liked this bit:
>
>
>
> ... the nearly complete sets of teeth recovered showed the early Alaskans had good dental health, said Betty Magnuson, first chief of the McGrath Native Village Council. McGrath residents viewing the bones Wednesday were surprised by that, Magnuson said.
>
>
>
> "The young people couldn't believe the condition of their teeth, these beautiful teeth," she said. "Vicki looked at them and said, 'That was before sugar.' "

[http://www.adn.com/2012/11/10/2688770/earky-tests-show-mcgrath-skeletons.html](http://http://www.adn.com/2012/11/10/2688770/earky-tests-show-mcgrath-skeletons.html)

# Thread: Attacked By Toxic Seaweed, Coral Uses Chemicals To Call For Help
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/attacked-by-toxic-seaweed-coral-uses-chemicals-to-call-for-help.4796/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-12T09:18:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/attacked-by-toxic-seaweed-coral-uses-chemicals-to-call-for-help.4796/post-63793>

I thought this was interesting bit of science news, because it shows chemical signalling going on between species:

> When toxic seaweed threatens the health of coral, what does the coral do? It calls for help, of course. A new study, published in Science, reveals that corals under attack send signals to fish â€œbodyguards.â€￾ The fish respond quickly, moving in to trim back the toxic alga that can kill the coral if not removed in a timely manner. ...
>
>
>
> This is the first known example of one species chemically signaling a consumer species to remove the competitors. The relationship between Acacia trees and the mutualistic ants that live in them to receive food and shelter while protecting the tree is similar. ...

[http://www.redorbit.com/news/science/1112729106/coral-reefs-seaweed-attack-110912/](http://http://www.redorbit.com/news/science/1112729106/coral-reefs-seaweed-attack-110912/)

# Thread: Pregnant women warned on phones
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnant-women-warned-on-phones.4797/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-12T10:50:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnant-women-warned-on-phones.4797/post-63795>

Report from the _Daily Mail_ (and quite widely syndicated by the looks):

> Dr. Hugh Taylor, a medical professor and chief of Yale's Department of Obstetrics, Gynecology and Reproductive Sciences \[said\] ... "We had pregnant mice in cages and we just simply put a cellphone on top of the cage. ..."
>
>
>
> Taylor says the study shows there is a "biological basis" to suggest cellphone exposure can impact pregnancies. He says he is encouraging patients to be cautious with devices and recommends pregnant women hold phones away from the body. ...
>
>
> The Mail also has some damning comment from Dr. Devra Davies
>
>
>
>
> > 'While it may be legal for companies to show advertisements of phones being used in ways that are not recommended, **it is not ethical,' she said. 'When they sell phones to people, and they tell them in fine print to not use them next to the body, and then all their advertisements show them using cellphones exactly next to the body ... I think that is really a profound disconnect that people ought to be aware of.'**
>
> **
>
>
> The article continues:
>
>
>
> The world now has almost as many cellphone subscriptions as inhabitants, according to recent numbers published by the UN telecom agency.
>
>
>
> In 2011, the World Health Organization's cancer arm said cellphones could cause cancer and called for more research.**

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-2231498/Warning-pregnant-women-cell-phone-radiation-affects-fetal-development.html#ixzz2BzppVhPT](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-2231498/Warning-pregnant-women-cell-phone-radiation-affects-fetal-development.html#ixzz2BzppVhPT)

# Thread: Gall bladder info
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-11-12T16:19:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63815>

Alright, I need you guys to talk to me about the gall bladder. I woke up about 4am with, what I thought was Terrible menstrual cramps (and they might be), I took some aleve and they settled down and I went back to sleep. Then I woke up at 7am with excruciating pain in my side. When I sat up I got dizzy and broke out in a cold sweat. I was in so much pain I couldn't think straight. After a few minutes the pain subsided. Over the last couple of hours the lower abdominal cramps and the pain in the side have sort of come and gone. Sometimes the pain in my side radiates around to my back too.

Of course I've been consulting Dr Google and it looks like this might be a gall bladder attack. From my cursory perusal of google it doesn't look like there is much that can be done other than taking pain meds and going to the doctor. suggestions?

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-12T17:04:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63818>

Appendix would be my fist thought. Might want to get it checked

### chocolate — 2012-11-12T17:07:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63819>

You could try a dose of milk of magnesia.

### ealachan — 2012-11-12T17:16:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63823>

Agree with Gretchen - gall bladder isn't likely to give you lower abdominal cramps. What you're describing sounds quite a lot like appendix. Are you running a fever at all?

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-11-12T17:22:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63826>

No fever. The intense pain has been in my side right under my ribs. It's come and gone a couple of times. Seems to be settling down right now. Just feeling lower abdominal/pelvic menstrual-like cramps now.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-12T19:42:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63845>

Fwiw DH almost had his appendix burst but didn't have a fever.

### ealachan — 2012-11-12T19:43:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63846>

It could be a whole lot of things. I'd get it checked it, personally.

### chocolate — 2012-11-12T19:46:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63847>

I'd take milk of magnesia and try to poop it out. You're gonna have to to get it looked at anyway.

### caroline — 2012-11-12T23:18:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63873>

I think you should get it checked out ASAP as well. My gall bladder attacks were in my upper back area as I recall and that was very typical apparently. Good luck and keep us posted.

### Thor — 2012-11-13T02:39:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gall-bladder-info.4800/post-63904>

gallbladder attack - you're full of gallstones you'll need to poop out at some point. look into castor oil packs - old time trick that works
