# Thread: New Research - How CT Activates Sarcolipin in Muscles to burn WAT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-research-how-ct-activates-sarcolipin-in-muscles-to-burn-wat.4802/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-12T19:35:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-research-how-ct-activates-sarcolipin-in-muscles-to-burn-wat.4802/post-63844>

..

CT clearly helps burn fat via BAT... However, new research shows muscles play an equally key role in activating BAT during CT sessions. Just released info on Sarcolipin and how it burns fat.

Sarcolipin explains how Bears who hibernate during the winter come out in the spring - ripped with muscles and lean body fat. Another win

**Muscles that do nothing can keep you warm and thin**

09 September 2012 by Andy Coghlan

[http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn22250-muscles-that-do-nothing-can-keep-you-warm-and-thin.html](http://http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn22250-muscles-that-do-nothing-can-keep-you-warm-and-thin.html)

Muscles that burn energy without contracting have yielded new clues about how the body retains a constant temperature â€“ and they may provide new targets for combating obesity.

### freesia — 2012-11-13T01:39:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-research-how-ct-activates-sarcolipin-in-muscles-to-burn-wat.4802/post-63891>

Well this just validates my experience. I never excercised (though I did increase activity as my health improved) but I ice bathed daily this past winter - Sthn hemisphere - and I came out the other side with muscles in places I haven't seen them since before I became unwell.

I didn't lose much weight overall, I didn't have excess to lose, but I lost waist cm's and gained muscle, and a normal body temperature. Sweet. Those things were only the icing on the cake.

I find I can't explain this well to people who haven't yet made friends with (or even realised there is such a potential friend as) their evolutionary biology.

Apart from muscles...how about that Winter induced immunity huh? We're so duped into thinking winter is a time of ills and chills. Too busy bundled up in layers of fleece and lining up for flu shots to feel the real story.

Gah. I could rant about this for the rest of the afternoon. LOL

Instead I'll just wish everyone a Happy Winter I think, and get outside and really enjoy my own Spring sunshine...


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: CT Sessions increase glutathione levels in the body
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-sessions-increase-glutathione-levels-in-the-body.4803/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-12T19:47:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-sessions-increase-glutathione-levels-in-the-body.4803/post-63848>

.

.

Another great reason for CT sessions - Research shows CT sessions increase glutathione levels in your body. Glutathione is critial for health & wellbeing. I'm wondering if the increase in glutathione from CT sessions could help explain why some people (like me) experience a Herx reaction.... Happy to see such insightful research on CT

**Uric acid and glutathione levels during short-term whole body cold exposure.**

Institute of Biochemistry, Medical Faculty (Charite), Humboldt University, Berlin, Germany.

http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8063192?ordinalpos=1&itool=EntrezSystem2.PEntrez.Pubmed.Pubmed\_ResultsPanel.Pubmed\_DiscoveryPanel.Pubmed\_Discovery\_RA&linkpos=1&log$=relatedarticles&logdbfrom=pubmed

1994 Mar;16(3):299-305.

Free Radic Biol Med

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-11-12T23:03:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-sessions-increase-glutathione-levels-in-the-body.4803/post-63870>

Great digging!

Perhaps somebody of the CTers have seen some [improvement related to a higher glutathione level](http://http://jeffreydach.com/2008/04/18/heart-disease-part-two-by-jeffrey-dach-md.aspx) such as decrease in atherosclerotic plaque or COPD improvement? I haven't convinced my father (77 y.o., COPD) yet to embrace the cold.

### freesia — 2012-11-13T00:44:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-sessions-increase-glutathione-levels-in-the-body.4803/post-63881>

> Changes consistent with the suppression of cytoskeletal dynamics provided further evidence that cold-adapted cells are in an energy conserving state. Among the specific changes were increases in the abundance and activity of redox proteins glutathione S-transferase, thioredoxin and thioredoxin reductase, which correlated with a decrease in oxidative stress, an increase in protein glutathionylation, and a recovery of reduced protein thiols during rewarming from 0Â°C. Increases in S-adenosylhomocysteine hydrolase and nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase implicate a central role for the methionine-cysteine transulfuration pathway in increasing glutathione levels and the NAD salvage pathway in increasing the reducing capacity of cold-adapted cells.

From [http://rd.springer.com/article/10.1186/1471-2164-12-630/fulltext.html](http://http://rd.springer.com/article/10.1186/1471-2164-12-630/fulltext.html)

Get your cells cold adapted

### Paradox — 2012-11-13T01:36:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-sessions-increase-glutathione-levels-in-the-body.4803/post-63890>

> **AndrÃ©s;65815 wrote:** Great digging!
>
>
>
> Perhaps somebody of the CTers have seen some [improvement related to a higher glutathione level](http://http://jeffreydach.com/2008/04/18/heart-disease-part-two-by-jeffrey-dach-md.aspx) such as decrease in atherosclerotic plaque or COPD improvement? I haven't convinced my father (77 y.o., COPD) yet to embrace the cold.

Andres- I have a form of COPD and CT has helped me with breathing deeper and better for some reason. It seems counterintuitive as people with breathing and O2 levels also have more trouble in the cold but CT you are not generally breathing cold air but are in cold water and there is a difference on that level.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-11-13T12:10:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-sessions-increase-glutathione-levels-in-the-body.4803/post-63990>

> **Paradox;65835 wrote:** Andres- I have a form of COPD and CT has helped me with breathing deeper and better for some reason. It seems counterintuitive as people with breathing and O2 levels also have more trouble in the cold but CT you are not generally breathing cold air but are in cold water and there is a difference on that level.

Thank you very much Paradox! Others diminished personal experiences as irrelevant but I appreciate them since they are another argument useful in my attempt to be more persuasive with my father . I will try to enroll him next spring... he is even more stubborn than me .

### Paradox — 2012-11-14T18:51:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-sessions-increase-glutathione-levels-in-the-body.4803/post-64280>

> **AndrÃ©s;65943 wrote:** Thank you very much Paradox! Others diminished personal experiences as irrelevant but I appreciate them since they are another argument useful in my attempt to be more persuasive with my father . I will try to enroll him next spring... he is even more stubborn than me .

Andres, I started CT very slow because of all my various difficulties. I began by using spot CT with the 10" x 14" gel cold packs that you put in the freezer and these actually help very much with breathing deeper as well. I would place 3 on the bed and lay down on them from the back of my neck down and then also piled two on my chest and one over my tum. The two on my chest were the clincher for helping with the breathing I believe and perhaps you can get your father to try that first. Going slowly was possibly a key for me as it was not such a shock to my highly compromised body which after the past 9 months and now able to cold bath for 30-40 mins is much stronger and better than I have been for a very long time. Hope your father can at least try the cold packs.

# Thread: Food Allergy progress
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergy-progress.4804/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Adria — 2012-11-12T20:29:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergy-progress.4804/post-63854>

I have Ige medicated allergic reactions since childhood to multiple foods including several types of seafood and fish. Iâ€™ve been to specialists all over the country. Iâ€™ve had every type of treatment, from immunotherapy (allergy shots) to slow desensization (drops) to the latest drugs like anti-IgE. All treatments had to be discontinued due to anaphylaxis. Due to my history, allergists would only prescribe medication.

As they say.. "Warning.. do not try this at home"....

Determined to begin epi-paleo, I made a personal decision to experiment with rapid desensitization. By myself, on myself. This is where very small food proteins are introduced at specific intervals and increasing proportions. Recently this method has become popular treatment in food allergy, especially w/ children. I studied it until I was comfortable to begin.

I wanted to try shimp. I cannot remember the last time I purposely ate shrimp. But about 10yr ago I went out to lunch at a place where they stir fry your order on a grill. Shrimp must have been on the grill prior as I could faintly taste it, then my mouth began to tingle and swell. That was my latest taste of shrimp.

So I began at the kitchen table along with my trusted epi-pen and my trusted husband and stared down at a shrimp. I looked at it for a long time before I touched it to my tongue. Each day I increased contact and noted any reaction.

Now it has been about 6wks and Iâ€™m up to eating 5 shrimp per day!

I believe living optimal is the cure.

### gizmeaux@pacbell.net — 2012-11-12T20:45:39+0200

What's next?

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-13T03:24:15+0200

Congrats!!! That is awesome.

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-13T03:37:32+0200

Yes, you are very brave! This scares the \*\*\*\* out of me, I'm anaphylactic to peanuts.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-13T04:02:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergy-progress.4804/post-63939>

You are one determined woman, Adria! Congrats on taking control over your life!

I have a penecillin allergy but I found after 25 years of avoiding it, that I could take it again. (But now I know better, and I'm avoiding it again.)

And a relative with a peanut allergy is now able to eat peanuts, after avoiding them for years as well. But he is careful to not eat many.

### bigknitwit — 2012-11-13T15:35:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergy-progress.4804/post-64012>

That is wonderful news.

I have a friend whose son is highly peanut allergic, as well as allergic to tree nuts, eggs, and lots of environmentals. She enrolled him in a peanut desensitization trial about 2 years ago. The goal of the trial was to take people who were so sensitive that even cross contact was a risk to their lives (ie. airplane travel, etc). Gradually, every 2 weeks, they increased the dose of peanut, starting at 3mg/day, all the way to 3 peanuts/day. Ironically, her son is the only one in the trial who just couldn't make it to 3 peanuts (too much reactivity), but he has made it to 2 peanuts. He has to eat them very day (his "dose") to maintain the desensitization. If he is sick with a fever he needs to avoid the dose, and if he misses for more than a few days, they have to cut back severely and work back up again. He ahs to be careful to not move around much after his dose, or even get too excited (I hypothesize that both lead to an increase in cortisol, which in turn leads to a leakier gut... temporarily, I think?). They have had times when they have not followed the rules, and he ends up with light reactions - pink eyes, stomach aches, etc.

It has been a mixed blessing for him. On one hand, it allows a little wiggle room in their lives. On the other though, is the constant worry about the chronic exposure to his allergen, and what else that might be doing to him that is not seen on the outside. I wonder if over time and regular exposure, if the IgE numbers will gradually drop. I wonder if inflammation from the exposure could be measured using an HS-CRP. None of these things are in place in this trial.

Actually, it's crossed my mind that I should look into dong something like this for myself for some of the foods I can no longer tolerate. I may experiment with it once I feel my thyroid labs and overal hormone profile are improved. Please keep updating!

### CarolMorris — 2012-11-13T23:09:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergy-progress.4804/post-64125>

Congrats Adria...your progress is so encouraging!

You say...I believe living optimal is the cure. This has crossed my mind and I am so hoping you are correct. I was thinking of starting with the egg that I do not have the anaphalactic reaction instead of the shrimp, mollusks and swordfish that I have the anaphalaxis reaction.

Can't wait for more updates!

### caroline — 2012-11-14T08:02:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergy-progress.4804/post-64182>

Hugs to Dr. K. you do so much for all of us. There are so many success stories here - you should be so proud.

### caroline — 2012-11-14T08:08:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-allergy-progress.4804/post-64183>

Congratulations river - I can only imagine how much courage it took to post your before picture. You look so happy and alive and glowing now and the benefit to your kids is immeasurable. You are all gorgeous. Good luck with the fish thing - it sounds like that is going to be hard work for you .....and I guess you won't be having a MHS anytime soon!

# Thread: The Kelp Highway Hypothesis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-kelp-highway-hypothesis.4805/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-12T20:50:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-kelp-highway-hypothesis.4805/post-63858>

This is new to me.

Apparently, anthropologist Jon Erlandson of the University of Oregon has long suggested that the peopling of the Americas may have taken place via

> a nearly continuous "kelp highway" \[that\] stretches from Japan, up along Siberia, across the Bering Strait to Alaska, and down again along the California coastline
>
>
> He notes that:
>
>
>
>
> > Kelp forests are some of the world's richest ecosystems. They are homes to seals, sea otters, hundreds of species of fish, sea urchins and abalone, all of which would have been important food and material sources for maritime people.
> >
> >
> > The article goes on to posit a possible island-hopping route to Australia:
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > > Although the coastal migration theory has yet to be proven with hard evidence, it is known that seafaring peoples lived in the Ryukyu Islands near Japan during the height of the last glacial period, about 35,000 to 15,000 years ago. These peoples may have traveled 90 or more miles at a time between islands.
> > >
> > >
> > >
> > > Some scientists believe that maritime people boated from Japan to Alaska along the Aleutian and Kurile Islands around 16,000 years ago. Before that, people may have island-hopped their way to Australia 50,000 to 60,000 years ago.
> > >
> > >
> > >
> > > Scientists have discovered settlements 11,500 to 9,000 years old along the coasts of some of these Pacific islands, which also have ecologically-rich kelp forests nearby that Erlandson believes existed when people were island hopping. The remains of kelp resources have been discovered in a settlement in Daisy Cave in the Channel Islands off southern California, dated to about 9,800 years ago.
> >
> >
> >
> > This article is from 2006.
> >
> >
> >
> > [http://www.livescience.com/7042-ancient-people-kelp-highway-america-researcher.html](http://http://www.livescience.com/7042-ancient-people-kelp-highway-america-researcher.html)
> >
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > Here's notice of a recent lecture by the same academic on the topic:
> >
> >
> >
> > In this illustrated lecture, University of Oregon Professor Jon Erlandson will explore the role of maritime adaptations and coastal migrations in human evolution and the spread of anatomically modern humans around the world. He will discuss the Kelp Highway Hypothesis, which proposes that North Pacific kelp forests facilitated a coastal migration of Upper Paleolithic peoples from Northeast Asia to the Americas roughly 15,000 years ago. Included in his presentation will be the latest findings from his research on California's Channel Islands.
>
>
>
> [http://events.kptv.com/The\_Kelp\_Highway\_Hypothesis/200210285.html](http://http://events.kptv.com/The_Kelp_Highway_Hypothesis/200210285.html)
>
>
>
> This I suppose would be some of the work on California's Channel Islands:
>
>
>
> Three archaeological sites on California's Channel Islands show that Paleoindians relied heavily on marine resources. The Paleocoastal sites, dated between ~12,200 and 11,200 years ago, contain numerous stemmed projectile points and crescents associated with a variety of marine and aquatic faunal remains. At site CA-SRI-512 on Santa Rosa Island, Paleocoastal peoples used such tools to capture geese, cormorants, and other birds, along with marine mammals and finfish. At Cardwell Bluffs on San Miguel Island, Paleocoastal peoples collected local chert cobbles, worked them into bifaces and projectile points, and discarded thousands of marine shells. With bifacial technologies similar to those seen in Western Pluvial Lakes Tradition assemblages of western North America, the sites provide evidence for seafaring and island colonization by Paleoindians with a diversified maritime economy.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21385713](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21385713)

### caroline — 2012-11-12T23:14:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-kelp-highway-hypothesis.4805/post-63872>

Thanks for all the interesting stuff that you post Michael

### freesia — 2012-11-13T00:33:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-kelp-highway-hypothesis.4805/post-63880>

> **caroline;65817 wrote:** Thanks for all the interesting stuff that you post Michael

I second this!

# Thread: Ducking Google in Search Engines
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ducking-google-in-search-engines.4806/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-12T21:58:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ducking-google-in-search-engines.4806/post-63862>

DuckDuckGo, a search service that doesn't believe in tracking users, takes on the might of Google:

> A start-up taking on Google in search is much like a raft taking on a cruise ship as a vacation option. But Weinberg is not delusional. With money lining his pockets from selling a start-up for $10 million, Weinberg bet there was a place in the market for a product capitalizing on users’ emerging annoyances with **Google — its search results gamed by marketers; its pages cluttered with ads; every query tracked, logged and personalized to the point of creepiness.
>
>
>
> He called his little search engine project DuckDuckGo, after the children’s game Duck, Duck, Goose. (Instead of “Just Google it,” think “Just Duck it.”)
>
>
>
> “My thesis for the company was, what can we do that other search engines, because they’re big, can’t do easily?” Weinberg said. “Because what’s good for Google business is bad for Google users**.”

[http://www.washingtonpost.com/business/ducking-google-in-search-engines/2012/11/09/6cf3af10-2842-11e2-bab2-eda299503684\_story.html](http://http://www.washingtonpost.com/business/ducking-google-in-search-engines/2012/11/09/6cf3af10-2842-11e2-bab2-eda299503684_story.html)

_Via_ the Daring Fireball blog

It's possible of course to simply open the site from a bookmark and search from there:

[http://duckduckgo.com](http://http://duckduckgo.com)

But, if you've got a technical bent, you can make DuckDuckGo the default search engine for searching from the address bar in browsers such as Safari by modifying the Hosts file:

[http://help.duckduckgo.com/customer/portal/articles/255650](http://http://help.duckduckgo.com/customer/portal/articles/255650)

### nonchalant — 2012-11-13T03:10:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ducking-google-in-search-engines.4806/post-63923>

Oh, I really like DuckDuckGo. I've been using it for a while now. And StartPage also.

Hi Everyone,

Was hoping someone could chime in here on my labs. I'll be 49 in a few weeks.

I got a full pael done last week on day 21 of cycle, fasted both food and water. I'm on bioidenticals for past 15 months...biest, progesterone (vag. creams) and test. (exterior cream). I was off E2 for 3 days prior to testing because cycle had started on day 18 and I thought it may be too high. I'm way off and I can feel it. # Thread: are humans getting dumber?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-13T01:16:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-63886>

I have also wondered for quite a long while ....

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-2231924/Are-getting-stupid-Researchers-claim-longer-need-intelligence-survive.html](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-2231924/Are-getting-stupid-Researchers-claim-longer-need-intelligence-survive.html)

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-13T02:10:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-63895>

Yes. Weee rrrr dmbr.

We no longer have to think. Cashiers don't even have to count out change anymore. Ever watch someone try to count your change when their automatic counter is broken? It's excruciating.

Our school systems are failing; this "Everyone gets an award!!!" mentality just steams me.

When my niece was a small child, I corrected her as she added 8+8 = 15. Her mother (my sister-in-law) said, "Pam, we don't correct her, and we don't appreciate it when you do". Umkay, so this child can believe 8+8=15 till she's an old lady. Gotcha.

Flipping through these unbelievably stupid t.v. shows (Honey Boo-Boo? I refuse to watch) actually scares me. Yes, literally, I have fear for our future.

Is it too much technology? Our light cycles completely screwed up? Unbelievably poor nutrition? Too much control over our environment (heated Escalade seats)? Not enough DHA (pasta and potatoes for dinner again!!)? Too many ABX's? Or a combination?

I don't expect an answer; I can only fix me.

### Thor — 2012-11-13T02:22:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-63898>

Hucked on fonics wurked 4 me

### ruby — 2012-11-13T02:35:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-63901>

Pam, couldn't agree more.

Seems to me that just like preference for sugar is hardwired because there's so little of it in nature, so is preference for periods of intellectual inertia  which there should be little of in nature.

Heard many times from farmers in China abandoning fields and switching to factory work - "of course I prefer factory, income is guaranteed and hours set. So what that I'm not my own boss. At the farm nothing is predictable, you never know what problems you are going to face on any given day. Weird weather, crops fail, animals get sick, tools and machinery break, you need to be alert and inventive all the time. You need to improvise quickly. It's stressful."

Because most of this problem solving is done "in-house" because of limited services, this requires a lot of cerebral energy but also provides a good workout for the brain.

### ruby — 2012-11-13T06:17:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-63962>

but there's hope, at least in the UK

"Schools are reintroducing chess lessons in an attempt to boost children’s brainpower.

Three decades after it was virtually wiped out in state schools, the game is making a dramatic comeback.

In just two years, 175 primary schools across England and Wales have introduced formal teaching in chess."

Read more: [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-2232014/Chess-returns-schools-boost-childrens-brainpower.html#ixzz2C4a6t500](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-2232014/Chess-returns-schools-boost-childrens-brainpower.html#ixzz2C4a6t500)

### Michael — 2012-11-13T08:39:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-63971>

> **ruby;65831 wrote:** I have also wondered for quite a long while ....
>
> [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-2231924/Are-getting-stupid-Researchers-claim-longer-need-intelligence-survive.html](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-2231924/Are-getting-stupid-Researchers-claim-longer-need-intelligence-survive.html)
>
>
> Now, in a provocative theory, a team from Stanford University claim we are losing our intellectual and emotional capabilities because the intricate web of genes which endows us with our brain power is particularly vulnerable to mutations - and these mutations are not being selected against our modern society because we no longer need intelligence to survive.

I don't think they've found this. I think they've expressed what they believe and what fits in with their theories.

For a start I don't think it's so much "provocative" as incredibly tendentious to think of intelligence in "survival" terms. Intelligence takes you out of the flow of unconscious life and therefore makes the response to a survival situation slower and more problematic. Once you have concepts and are able to stand back and make judgments and decisions you can also make mistakes. I could make some long-term plans that might assist my survival, but that's not necessarily a lot to throw in the scale. The rest of Nature survives all right without. Intelligence as a tool for survival? It's less than that - but also far more. I don't think they see this, because I don't think they really think about it. They make assumptions - one of which is that everything is to be seen in survival terms.

### ruby — 2012-11-13T09:10:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-63973>

to get closer to settling this, I guess, we have to define intelligence. For me the hallmark of it is resourcefulness based on a balanced interplay between the gut feeling and conscious input. what's yours?

### Michael — 2012-11-13T11:11:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-63989>

I suppose that it requires consciousness and not just consciousness but self-consciousness.

And even consciousness, incidentally, isn't known to science. Neither does it know anything of tenses - there's no past, present, future in physics. Yet we spend our days reasoning about what might be and regretting what has been.

We live in this world of concepts and meanings and don't even notice how strange that is, because that's normal to us. (Well, philosophers do, but they're something of an exception.) There's probably nothing we do, however apparently simple, that isn't saturated by meaning.

If you encounter another person, don't you experience that there is a "self" there? Yet how can selves exist in a world of matter ruled by immutable laws of nature? There's something profoundly mysterious there. How can love exist? And yet it does.

And all this is to be "explained" by metaphors drawn from information technology (which doesn't, in any case, use the term "information" in the normal sense of the word) and a lot of hand-waving and evoking of a handful of concepts from biology, such as "survival value".

There's a profound disconnect here, that seems to have gone unnoticed in this report, inasmuch as intelligence places _huge_ energetic demands. That's something Jack covered in his series. It's not clear that's an advantage in survival terms at all.

### ruby — 2012-11-14T05:39:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-64170>

the brain is a limiting tool in itself. it only allows you to see and process that much (with some individual variations ;-). I think the article refers to the "that much", not to the objective quantum consciousness that is out there.

Some brains see more than conventional brains are meant to see. My favourite is Grigori Perelman who solved the Poincare Conjecture a few years ago. His brain is out of this world but nobody would probably hear about it if not for the fact that he declined a US$1 million prize. He is currently jobless and lives in a small old apartment in St Petersburg. And he refused US$1 million - how dumb can he be?

(In August 2006, Perelman was awarded the Fields Medal for "his contributions to geometry and his revolutionary insights into the analytical and geometric structure of the Ricci flow." Perelman declined to accept the award or to appear at the congress, stating: "I'm not interested in money or fame." On 22 December 2006, the journal Science recognized Perelman's proof of the PoincarÃ© conjecture as the scientific "Breakthrough of the Year", the first such recognition in the area of mathematics. On 18 March 2010, it was announced that he had met the criteria to receive the first Clay Millennium Prize for resolution of the PoincarÃ© conjecture. On 1 July 2010, he turned down the prize").

### Michael — 2012-11-14T17:13:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-humans-getting-dumber.4808/post-64256>

> **ruby;66129 wrote:** ... not to the objective quantum consciousness that is out there.

I'm not sure what that (objective quantum consciousness) would mean. AFAIK, some people have thought they could explain consciousness by invoking quantum physics. However, it seems that quantum physics requires consciousness rather than the other way round, inasmuch as quantum effects are what you get when there's an _observer. In effect, it seems to be a backdoor way of smuggling in consciousness without noticing that you've done so. What consciouness is and how it arises is still a problem, much as some people don't like to admit it.

What I didn't like about the report is the glib invoking of survival value with regards to intelligence when it's not all obvious that it's pertinent. I think it's really an assumption, something that they bring with them rather than find.

There are some indications that there are massive_ cultural wars coming over understandings of our minds, whether they can be simply identified with our brains, how our brains are to be understood, and what the status of culture is. One astute philosopher points out that previously the opposition has been, or seemed to be, between science and religion. Now there's a brewing conflict between science (or what's presented as such) and enlightenment liberalism. However, the problem isn't really as narrow as that and affects the ordinary understanding of ourselves that persons of all political view or none have as well.

[http://www.prospectmagazine.co.uk/magazine/nature-nurture-and-liberal-values-roger-scruton-jesse-prinz-david-eagleman-neuroscience/](http://http://www.prospectmagazine.co.uk/magazine/nature-nurture-and-liberal-values-roger-scruton-jesse-prinz-david-eagleman-neuroscience/)

There have already been a few minor skirmishes on the paleo forums, like MDA, over these kinds of issues (mostly conducted at a pretty low intellectual level) - acrimonious disputes over the role of women in society and so forth. I'd expect many more to come.

# Thread: Limiting carbs to dinner-time increases satiety, reduces risk for diabetes and cardio
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/limiting-carbs-to-dinner-time-increases-satiety-reduces-risk-for-diabetes-and-cardio.4809/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-13T02:03:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/limiting-carbs-to-dinner-time-increases-satiety-reduces-risk-for-diabetes-and-cardio.4809/post-63893>

"The innovative dietary manipulation led to changes in daylight hormonal profiles. The researchers examined the experimental diet's effect on the secretion of three hormones: **leptin, considered to be the satiety hormone, whose level in the blood is usually low during the day and high during the night; **ghrelin**, considered the hunger hormone, whose level in the blood is usually high during the day and low during the night; and adiponectin**, considered the link between obesity, insulin resistance and the metabolic syndrome, whose curve is low and flat in obese people. The findings suggest there is an advantage in concentrating carbohydrate intake in the evening"

[http://www.huji.ac.il/cgi-bin/dovrut/dovrut\_search\_eng.pl?mesge135262755005872560](http://http://www.huji.ac.il/cgi-bin/dovrut/dovrut_search_eng.pl?mesge135262755005872560)

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-13T02:11:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/limiting-carbs-to-dinner-time-increases-satiety-reduces-risk-for-diabetes-and-cardio.4809/post-63896>

Hmmmm... maybe it had more to do with them eating larger amounts of protein first thing in the morning since they weren't eating carbs

### ruby — 2012-11-13T06:22:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/limiting-carbs-to-dinner-time-increases-satiety-reduces-risk-for-diabetes-and-cardio.4809/post-63963>

> **RiverNeighbor;65842 wrote: Hmmmm... maybe it had more to do with them eating larger amounts of protein first thing in the morning since they weren't eating carbs**

**

hmmm, were they eating protein at dawn?

meal **timing** and composition** - synergistic or independent benefits? that is the question

Thanks!

I actually stopped pushing; stopped talking about my diet totally.

### bigknitwit — 2012-11-13T19:36:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-64074>

> **Nonchalant;65998 wrote:** It's hard for the baby of the family to take the lead, sometimes.

My older brother still knows everything. So much that there's no point discussing anything since all he can say are conversation ending remarks... My little sister on the other hand, calls all the time for advice! Crazy how sibling order continues to play a role in how we see each other, even into ages when it no longer matters. Good for your sister for giving up wheat! I always tell people it is a great time to go gluten free, because it's such an exploding niche diet. So many things are being labeled gluten-free, and products are even being reformulated to be gluten free (like Rice Crispies - how hard was it to remove to malt and replace with a different sweetener?). It makes the transition easier anyway.

### fitness@home — 2014-10-22T22:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-144747>

I just had a wonderful realization...let me sit back and enjoy it just a while longer

Not everything I talk about (regarding health-related subjects) goes in one ear and out the other! Every once in a while there are glimmers of hope that more will make it through.

DH just called me and asked, if I were him, would I get a Flu vaccine and the Hepatitis B Series? I decline the flu shot every year. Both are offered at his work. I told him in the end it is his decision, but he should look into ingredients before deciding. Sent him a couple of websites, one being:

[http://vaccines.procon.org/view.resource.php?resourceID=005206#header\_hepb](http://vaccines.procon.org/view.resource.php?resourceID=005206#header_hepb)
I realize vaccines are a volatile subject with many different opinions. The number of vaccines that I received as a child is a small amount compared to current standard practices. It has increased substantially.

**The real point of this is that I _actually had an impact._** It made him stop, think and look for more information and opinions before making a decision.

### nicld — 2014-10-22T22:42:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-144750>

^^^ I skipped the flu shot this year too. We will see.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-10-23T02:55:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-144777>

I went to a funeral today for a partner who died in a wreck............had people sitting around me during the service listening..........as I was walking out several asked me about health.......it turns out my partner who died read Tensegrity 7 on my laptop before I posted it and was talking to the nurse about it........apparently it made an impact. Now people who were deaf to my science are interested. It took sitting in a funeral to move them to action. Sometimes you can never predict what will do it.........they key is you must not stop producing the content of your knowledge like you're on a conference call...........you never know who is hearing vs listening.

### nicld — 2014-10-23T05:04:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-144797>

^^^^^ My condolences.

### kposs — 2012-11-13T11:00:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-tyrosine-ok-with-autoimmune.4817/post-63988>

I'm trying to figure out if L-Tyrosine is safe to use if you have autoimmune disease.

My husband has autoimmune thyroiditis (but no longer has a thyroid so no problem with producing additional thryoid hormones) and symptoms of low dopamine, so I'd like him to try L-Tyrosine, but I can't confirm whether there are any contraindications for autoimmune in general.

thanks.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-13T19:15:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-tyrosine-ok-with-autoimmune.4817/post-64065>

You could look at Dan Kalish's info for this sort of stuff. He has various podcasts and free training, too. From memory, you don't want to address the dopamine side of things without also addressing the serotonin side.

# Thread: Fish going up in popularity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-going-up-in-popularity.4820/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-13T13:07:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-going-up-in-popularity.4820/post-63993>

... in Ireland at any rate.

> Irish shoppers are increasing their purchasing of fish with retail sales rising by one per cent to €189 million in the year ending May 2012 according to Kantar worldpanel, reports Teresa Brophy, Ireland Market Manager, Bord Bia.
>
>
>
> Around 95 per cent of households now buy fish every two to three weeks with the frequency of purchase continuing to rise. ...

[http://www.thefishsite.com/fishnews/18755/irish-consumers-buy-more-fish](http://http://www.thefishsite.com/fishnews/18755/irish-consumers-buy-more-fish)

Anyway, the popularity of seafood seems to have been falling across the Western world for many years, so it's good to see that turning round.

### Stavity — 2012-11-13T15:05:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-going-up-in-popularity.4820/post-64005>

The Japanese are buying up our Alaskan salmon. Not only has it gone up in price but we have a shortage because of it.

Thank goodness, I can still buy 2011 salmon and I am stocking up big time.

### Michael — 2012-11-13T15:35:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-going-up-in-popularity.4820/post-64011>

Yeah, that's interesting, too.

Of course, people going long distances in search of fish is nothing new. It's been said since at least the 1950s that Basque, French and English fishermen were almost certainly picking up cod off Newfoundland a long time before Columbus sailed. Unlike him, they didn't publish, though. You tend to keep quiet about a food-resource as good as that if your livelihood depends on it.

The more one thinks about it, the stranger the relative neglect of water-resources in the paleo movement is. Presumably, people are just in thrall to the picture of "cavemen" chucking spears at four-footed animals and don't stop to consider how limited that picture might be.

Marine foods seem to have been particularly important in the Mesolithic. This, coincidentally from Ireland again, is telling:

> So what did they eat? As stated above, fish, and lots of it. At least this was their main "animal" food. **At two of the sites examined, one at Mount Sandel at the mouth of the Bann river and the other at Lough Gur in Limerick Co., from 70 - 80 % of the faunal remains were of fish, 15 - 23% of mammal and 4 - 8% of birds. Mount Sandel's proximity to the sea and the river introduced Atlantic Salmon in to the diet, along with Eel and Brown Trout, some Sea Bass and Flounder. This site shows signs of what could be a drying rack, a double line of post holes, so it is possible that by this or other means they dried or smoked fish for storing. Lough Gur is an inland lake with no river to the sea, so we don't find Atlantic Salmon, the available fish were mostly Brown Trout and Eel. Crustacean shells have been found among the fish bones. Depending on the location, river, sea or lake side, these include, Crab, Limpets, Whelks, Cockles, Scallops, Periwinkles, Oysters and Mussels**. Though no Irish fishing tools have yet been found, concurrent evidence from Britain and the Continent shows that spears and harpoons with barbs of bone and conical traps made of twisted willow rods and willow fibers nets were being used to catch fish either from the shore or in deeper waters from dugout canoes.

[http://www.danann.org/library/arch/pantry2.html](http://http://www.danann.org/library/arch/pantry2.html)

(It's incidentally interesting that there are all these faunal remains but no tools. If you were using tool finds as a marker of what people were eating you'd be out by miles.)

It would be a similar story for some British or Danish sites.

# Thread: ground beef sauce?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-11-13T17:23:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64027>

Anyone have a good sauce or something that makes bison or ground beef taste good to kids? We can't have tomato which is such a bummer because my daughter loves the beef with tomato paste and stevia. I really need easy and good tasting bab ideas. Thanks!

### ealachan — 2012-11-13T17:47:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64032>

Have you tried maybe adding chili or taco type spices to it?

For chili, I would use cocoa, chili powder, cumin, coriander, salt, black pepper, garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, and a little cinnamon.

For taco seasoning, I usually use all of the above, minus the cocoa, cinnamon and oregano.

Alternatively, perhaps try some allspice, fennel, garlic powder and salt, to make kind of a sausage-y tasting ground meat? A little allspice goes a long way, so start with a little and taste-test.

When I use ground bison, I usually cook it in bacon fat and/or add bacon fat to it, because it's super lean and gets kind of dry (the kind I can get, anyway).

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-13T17:52:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64036>

A non spicy green chili sauce - around here there are some premade ones that are fairly optimal. The mild ones don't have spicyness

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-13T17:56:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64038>

I grind up some pastured bacon to mix with the beef/bison and my little ones like that better than any spices I have tried

### ealachan — 2012-11-13T18:16:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64049>

Oooh, or your could do a curry: coconut milk, curry spices, ground meat, some veg. We have this for dinner fairly regularly and it's very tasty.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-13T18:18:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64050>

We spice up the coconut milk with a variety of ethnic flavors. Works with mex and pesto too!

### ColdBren — 2012-11-13T18:45:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64054>

Thanks guys! Tell me more about the coconut milk ideas....can i go sweeter rather than spicy? ideas on how to make this work?

### ColdBren — 2012-11-13T18:46:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64055>

river- will try the bacon too! thanks!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-13T19:18:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64068>

We use coconut milk sauces, too... add ginger, garlic, tamari or coconut aminos, cilantro, etc. It's very yummy and my kids call it a 'Thai' sauce.

We also do a 'Greek' sauce for ground meat... LOTS of mint and dill, with lemon juice, garlic, salt and pepper. I sometimes add grated cauli if I want a rice within it (we have a grape vine and I often make this mix and stuff it in grape leaves, but you could use cabbage leaves etc). If your children can eat dairy, it's also really nice with goat or sheep feta.

### ColdBren — 2012-11-13T20:32:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64082>

I love it! Thank you all!! We can't do dairy either.....we can't do anything really! I believe my daughter's tics are from autoimmune issues so it eliminates most everything, poor thing. She literally almost stops all ticcing when she just has ground bison and rice based things....and Dr. Kruse said he would eliminate the rice.....but I have almost nothing left. I have really high hopes for the coconut milk, but I have salicylate intolerance and am just praying she doesn't so she can expand her diet.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-13T20:33:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64083>

Chinese 5 spice is more of a sweet taste

### bigknitwit — 2012-11-13T21:37:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64103>

This sounds like a great idea I haven't tried yet! Thanks!

> **KiwiLauren;66024 wrote:** We also do a 'Greek' sauce for ground meat... LOTS of mint and dill, with lemon juice, garlic, salt and pepper. I sometimes add grated cauli if I want a rice within it (we have a grape vine and I often make this mix and stuff it in grape leaves, but you could use cabbage leaves etc). If your children can eat dairy, it's also really nice with goat or sheep feta.

My kids will eat ground beef with lots of melted butter and sea salt. If there's bacon, that's also well received (but I don't make it much because I react).

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-13T22:53:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64117>

My kids love anything with turmeric, cumin, cinnamon, garlic, ginger. Can she do mushrooms? if so - what about a beef stroganoff using coconut milk over cauliflower rice

also, what about ground beef/buffalo w/ chopped/ground bacon, with Chinese 5 spice - served in blanched cabbage leaves. this is always a BIG HIT in our house

### Susan M. — 2012-11-13T23:05:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64121>

I've made meatballs and cooked them in sauce made from a small amount of olive oil/butter, coconut milk, a little curry, a little tiny bit spice like cayenne and a small spoonful of honey. For my husband I will also put in a small spoon of peanut butter. We almost never eat legumes, so I will bend on this recipe once in a while.

### Susan M. — 2012-11-13T23:07:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64122>

The reason for the peanut butter is to thicken the sauce up, which it does rather quickly.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-14T00:56:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64140>

I just chopped up a sweet potato and added it to some browned ground beef. Cooked til sweet potato was just tender and seasoned w Chinese 5 spice. It got a rating of yumaso, which is a word my daughter made up from yummy and awesome.

### BJK77 — 2012-11-14T01:17:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64144>

I make a "chili" for the kids and DH that is dairy/nightshade free. I use ground beef and add veggies (spinach, zucchini, carrots, etc) to replace the beans. Instead of using pepper-based spices, I put in cumin, turmeric, coriander and a dash of cloves. It is a HUGE hit at my house with the kids.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-14T18:33:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64278>

There's Hollandaise type sauces made with egg yolks and butter or your fat of choice. Spices could probably change the flavor quite a bit for adding to ground beef. There's a Japanese egg yolk sauce in which you might be able to substitute another savory flavor for the miso paste. I found a few pumpkin meat sauces, but they all used tomato sauce to varying degrees. For example, I found a recipe for pumkpin sloppy joes using ground beef. What about a meat casserole with pumpkin and sweet potato sauce? Coconut might be a flavor that would go well with it. What about coconut cream/ground beef stew? Or ground beef and sweet potato/pumpkin enchiladas using coconut wraps instead of tortillas? Do you like avocado and beef? Try ground beef/guacamole enchiladas or as a taco bowl. Cumin is a classy upgrade for guacamole.

Here's a tomatoless recipe for a spaghetti type sauce that includes carrots, beets, bacon, and chicken stock among other things.

[http://www.againstallgrain.com/2012/09/08/tomatoless-meat-sauce/](http://http://www.againstallgrain.com/2012/09/08/tomatoless-meat-sauce/)

### nonchalant — 2012-11-14T18:52:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64281>

> **Glen PDQ;66270 wrote:**
>
> [http://www.againstallgrain.com/2012/09/08/tomatoless-meat-sauce/](http://http://www.againstallgrain.com/2012/09/08/tomatoless-meat-sauce/)

Nice website, thanks for the link, Glen. It has some Thanksgiving ideas I'd like to try.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-14T22:10:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-64320>

Today I did an experiment with persimmon... I took beef Bone broth and brought just to a boil and dropped in mini meatballs seasoned with garlic, salt, and , pepper .... Like how you would for Italian wedding soup. I took out the cooked meat balls and added seaweed to the broth and let it simmer for a while. I took out the seaweed and ate it, then added mashed up persimmon to the broth(I took the skin off). I simmered it down and seasoned it and poured

Over the meatballs in the crockpot and put it on warm..... I thought it tasted good. Kids are sick and sleeping so the real test is when they wake up.....

It sorta reminds me of those grape jelly meatballs/weenies people bring to potlucks

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-19T06:22:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-65276>

Are those the wild American persimmons or the muscle-Hulk type big lobed Asian persimmons? Those wild persimmons will send up clonal shoots all over the yard so that you will eventually have a grove of them unless you mow or have goats, etc.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-19T17:24:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-65353>

They are persimmons that came from the organic farmer we do a CSA subscription with locally. So.... Not sure of the variety. Lots of people grow them here though....

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-25T11:38:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ground-beef-sauce.4823/post-65555>

> The native persimmon is the size of a grape, and the asian ones are the size of peaches.Â  We had geese at one time and they hung out under the native persimmon tree scrambling for them when the fruit fell.

# Thread: Olive Oil (PUFA) Reduces DNA Strand Breaks - T2D Patients
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/olive-oil-pufa-reduces-dna-strand-breaks-t2d-patients.4826/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-13T18:52:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/olive-oil-pufa-reduces-dna-strand-breaks-t2d-patients.4826/post-64057>

.

Article on the use of Olive Oil (PUFA) to help T2D with DNA Strand Breaks....

Type 2 diabetes is a multifactorial disease associated with increased oxidative stress, which may lead to increased DNA damage. The aim of this study was to investigate the effect of a healthy diet on DNA oxidation in diabetics and nondiabetics

**Molecular Nutrition & Food Research**

12 NOV 2012

University of Vienna, Austria

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/mnfr.201200343/abstract](http://http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/mnfr.201200343/abstract)

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-11-13T20:23:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/olive-oil-pufa-reduces-dna-strand-breaks-t2d-patients.4826/post-64081>

Olive oil is a MUFA. Now bacon fat...

Can't tell much from this summary, but it is concerning that they seemed to have combined 2 seperate issues (PUFA's and vegetables) to create an imaginary "healthy diet", thereby muddling where the actual benefit is derived.

### Thor — 2012-11-13T20:49:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/olive-oil-pufa-reduces-dna-strand-breaks-t2d-patients.4826/post-64091>

It gets a bit more confusing in that Olive Oil contains 10 percent PUFAs.

# Thread: Cape Town
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cape-town.4830/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-13T19:31:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cape-town.4830/post-64073>

Andreas Eenfeldt reporting from South Africa. No wonder they have problems there. He says it's bad enough back home in Sweden, but he's never seen so much sugar in his life as in South Africa:

# Thread: Sublingual immunotherapy for food allergy/sensitivity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sublingual-immunotherapy-for-food-allergy-sensitivity.4833/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### CarolMorris — 2012-11-13T21:27:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sublingual-immunotherapy-for-food-allergy-sensitivity.4833/post-64099>

I would like to know if anyone has had success with this method. As I understand, it is the same meds as the allergy shots, but using it sublingually you can do it at home after the initial visit. It takes 2-3 years everyday depending on the allergen.

I am lucky enough ( to have anaphalactic allergy to shellfish, mollusks and swordfish. I also have sensitivities to eggs and dairy. The first hs-CRP test I had was 3.69, the only change I made was to eliminate eggs and my hs-CRP went down to 1.91--in 2 months!

Any opinions, comments or info is very welcome...thanks!...Carol

Excuse the sad face in the title, I couldn't figure out how to remove it. My daily struggles navigating a forum--at least I now know the difference between a blog and a forum!

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-13T22:34:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sublingual-immunotherapy-for-food-allergy-sensitivity.4833/post-64115>

Carol Check out the following thread: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?5485-Food-Allergy-progress](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?5485-Food-Allergy-progress)

### CarolMorris — 2012-11-13T22:55:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sublingual-immunotherapy-for-food-allergy-sensitivity.4833/post-64118>

from the link above:

Men in committed relationships choose to keep a greater distance between themselves and an unknown woman they find attractive when given the hormone oxytocin, according to new research in the November 14 issue of The Journal of Neuroscience. 90% of men have a penis under 7 inches,huh? LOL....for some reason I am always choosing those in the 10% range or else that stat is not correct. I thought 6-7 inches was a norm in my personal experience.

These were mouse studies in the article. According to the article: It's been established that vasopressin is released into the bloodstream during sexual arousal in men—but whether that corresponds with higher uptake in the brain is unclear. Although receptors for both vasopressin and oxytocin are found in the human brain, the distribution does not resemble that seen in either the monogamous prairie vole or the promiscuous montane vole.

Penis size is not related to cheating behavior. Like Dr. K, I know someone who had a teeny penis and he was a huge horn dog and had an open marriage. He slept around constantly. He was married, loved his wife, but they had an agreement they could both have sex with others outside the marriage and they did. So this isn't really cheating behavior but it is not typical pair bonding.

vasopressin is a powerful drug with intense and dangerous side effects. I am used to administering it in an ICU setting by IV, but it can be given also subcutaneously, intramuscularly, and I think by inhaling. I don't think it will ever be ok'd for at home use. [http://www.drugs.com/mtm/vasopressin.html](http://http://www.drugs.com/mtm/vasopressin.html)

I enjoyed this book.

[Sex at Dawn: How we Mate, Why we stray and what it means.](http://http://www.amazon.com/Sex-Dawn-Stray-Modern-Relationships/dp/0061707813) Sex paleo style. "For the husband and wife team Christopher Ryan and Cacilda JethÃ¡ in their new book Sex At Dawn, this example is one of many that suggests the human species did not evolve in monogamous, nuclear families but rather in small, intimate groups where "most mature individuals would have had several ongoing sexual relationships at any given time." We are the descendants of these multimale-multifemale mating groups and, even though we've constructed a radically different society from our hunter-gatherer forebears, the behavioral and psychological traits our species evolved in the distant past still manifest themselves today. Ryan, a psychologist, and JethÃ¡, a psychiatrist, argue that understanding human sexual evolution this way helps to explain our species' unique creativity inside (as well as outside) the marriage bed. It may also shed light on why fidelity has been such a persistent problem for both men and women throughout recorded history."

The female bonobo monkey chooses a mate, drags the male out into the jungle and has hot monkey sex with him for about two weeks before bringing him back into the pack, spent and exhausted. ### John Dalton — 2015-04-16T11:20:21+0300

>

> Vasopressin is also known as "anti-diuretic hormone". It doesn't make you pee, it stops you peeing. It is not affected by caffeine but it's production is suppressed by alcohol.

# Thread: Commonalities in Human and Chimp Gut Flora found
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/commonalities-in-human-and-chimp-gut-flora-found.4836/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-11-14T02:39:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/commonalities-in-human-and-chimp-gut-flora-found.4836/post-64151>

[http://phys.org/news/2012-11-gut-bacteria-similar-humans-chimps.html](http://http://phys.org/news/2012-11-gut-bacteria-similar-humans-chimps.html)

"This shared \[human and chimpanzee\] organization of the gut microbial community is millions of years old and the findings attest to their functional importance," Howard Ochman, an author of the study and director of the Microbial Diversity Institute, said in a Yale news release.

"Now that we know enterotypes have been maintained over evolutionary timescales, our goal is to determine their functions and how they might be important to the health of their hosts," Ochman said.

The study was published Nov. 13 in the journal Nature Communications.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Fat Cell Clock/Obesity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cell-clock-obesity.4837/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-11-14T03:12:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cell-clock-obesity.4837/post-64153>

I'm reading this rather boring article [http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-11-it-not-just-what-you.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-11-it-not-just-what-you.html) about stuff Jack has been saying for years, then this pops up in the middle of the article:

"The Penn team found that only a handful of genes were altered when the clock was broken in fat cells and these governed how unsaturated fatty acids, such as eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) were released into the blood stream. Interestingly, these are the same fatty acids that are typically associated with fish oils. Sure enough, levels of EPA and DHA were low in both plasma and in the hypothalamus at the time of inappropriate feeding. "To our amazement, we were able to rescue the entire phenotype - inappropriate fatty acid oscillation and gene expression in the hypothalamus, feeding pattern and obesity - by supplementing EPA and DHA to the knock-out animals," notes Paschos.

### shilohman — 2012-11-14T04:00:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cell-clock-obesity.4837/post-64156>

> **AKMan;66110 wrote:** I'm reading this rather boring article [http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-11-it-not-just-what-you.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-11-it-not-just-what-you.html) about stuff Jack has been saying for years, then this pops up in the middle of the article:
>
>
>
> "The Penn team found that only a handful of genes were altered when the clock was broken in fat cells and these governed how unsaturated fatty acids, such as eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) were released into the blood stream. Interestingly, these are the same fatty acids that are typically associated with fish oils. Sure enough, levels of EPA and DHA were low in both plasma and in the hypothalamus at the time of inappropriate feeding. "To our amazement, we were able to rescue the entire phenotype - inappropriate fatty acid oscillation and gene expression in the hypothalamus, feeding pattern and obesity - by supplementing EPA and DHA to the knock-out animals," notes Paschos.

Just another thumbs-up for Epi-Paleo diet. Remeber you first read it here on this site.

# Thread: Mechanism of brain plasticity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mechanism-of-brain-plasticity.4838/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-14T08:36:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mechanism-of-brain-plasticity.4838/post-64186>

Animals that are socially isolated for prolonged periods make less myelin in the region of the brain responsible for complex emotional and cognitive behavior, researchers at the University at Buffalo and Mt. Sinai School of Medicine report in Nature Neuroscience online. The research shows that brain plasticity, or ability to adapt to environmental changes, is not restricted to neurons but actively occurs in glial cells such as oligodendrocytes, which produce myelin and that stress of social isolation disrupts myelin formation. "This research provides the first explanation of the mechanism behind how this brain plasticity occurs," says Karen Dietz, PhD, research scientist in the Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology in the UB School of Medicine and Biomedical Sciences.

[http://www.buffalo.edu/news/13797](http://http://www.buffalo.edu/news/13797)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: BBC's advice for heart health
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bbcs-advice-for-heart-health.4839/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-14T08:39:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bbcs-advice-for-heart-health.4839/post-64187>

Reduce fat in your diet, cut salt, eat wholegrains with every meal, and eat soya daily.

They say:

> Swap saturated fats such as butter for unsaturated oils such as **sunflower, rapeseed or olive oil
>
>
> And:
>
>
>
>
> > Studies of large groups of people in the US have shown that diets rich in wholegrain food can reduce the risk of CVD by up to 30 per cent. **You can include wholegrain food in every meal** by choosing wholemeal bread and wholegrain varieties of pasta and rice.
>
>
>
> On soya - well it sounds like a commercial:
>
>
>
> A diet that includes at least 25g of soya per day** has been associated with reductions in LDL cholesterol and CVD. Soya isoflavones in particular have been shown to reduce CVD risk as they inhibit the growth of cells that form artery-clogging plaque. Soya protein is also an excellent substitute for meat and is available in a convenient and tasty form in many ready-made meals. Another good source of soya protein is soya milk and yoghurt.

When did _health_ advice start to claim that foods are "convenient" or "tasty"?

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/health/treatments/healthy\_living/nutrition/dietary\_cvd.shtml](http://http://www.bbc.co.uk/health/treatments/healthy_living/nutrition/dietary_cvd.shtml)

What a load of really bad advice! Furthermore, I can't help wondering if someone at the BBC was paid by a third-party to plug soya in that offensively gushing manner.

I've half a mind to look for a contact page and complain.

### caroline — 2012-11-14T09:18:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bbcs-advice-for-heart-health.4839/post-64190>

This is pretty scary stuff and it is what all my friends and family firmly believe. They basically think I am a little - maybe a lot - nuts. They mostly just humour me now.

### Michael — 2012-11-14T10:13:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bbcs-advice-for-heart-health.4839/post-64199>

LOL. I love that, Caroline.

All that ... And yet it's a castle built on sand. You don't even have to go back 40,000 years as Cordain would try to do. You've only got to go back to aboriginal peoples of the 1930s, like Price did.

Where's the healthy aboriginal population, whether mostly hunting, gathering, fishing, herding, or farming eating like that?

Excessive quantities of modern hybrid dwarf wheat with ALL the bran (which is a GI irritant) left in eaten in half-cooked form as "muesli" or as quick-rise bread, using baking technology not available before the 1960s (Chorleywood Process), limited animal fats, sunflower and rapeseed oil, low salt, soya convenience foods, and all washed down with soy "milk".

NO historical population ate like that.

What it amounts to is an attempt at a huge population-wide experiment on people on the basis of hunches but no real evidence and what suits large commercial interests.

And it involves closing one's eyes and one's mind to so much. It's unconsionable that what is supposed to be a "public service" broadcaster pushes this.

### Linz — 2012-11-14T10:50:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bbcs-advice-for-heart-health.4839/post-64201>

It's not just wheat in the bread. Virtually every loaf contains vegetable oil and soya as well.

And it's not just sugar in the yoghurt. Since the fat has been removed you have to look a long way to find yoghurt without added starch.

### caroline — 2012-11-14T11:02:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bbcs-advice-for-heart-health.4839/post-64203>

Michael - there kind of is evidence tho - don't you think? Populations all over the world are becoming more and more obese on this diet they have been recommending for years now. I have been to China a number of times the last few years and I am overwhelmed by how fat they are becoming. I think it is also tied to fast food swamping this country. I have read that big US companies set up R&D and can then turn on a dime and develop products to suit their \[Chinese\] tastes. I was just in Uzbekistan and Kyrgyzstan and there is no fast food there. they seem to eat locally and seasonally. I fervently hope that doesn't change but I guess it is bound to.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-14T23:26:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bbcs-advice-for-heart-health.4839/post-64338>

Destiny, it will take most doctors an average of 20 years before they begin using updated information from the latest studies and research in their daily practice.

A number of my coworkers have asked me how I lost 50 pounds and I tell them I eat lots of low-polyunsaturated fats, eggs, grassfed meat/seafood and no potatoes, bread, noodles, or grains (vlc). After my answer a few have gone on to ask if I exercise, probably because they can see no connection between eating fat and losing weight. I tell them that I do not exercise.

One of my co-workers and his wife have been dieting as long as I have except they are on an atkins style diet and use those sugarfree Mio flavorings instead of drinking diet sodas. I see him eating atkins bars, fried pork skins out of a bag, and drinking Mio for lunch. He has lost 20 pounds and his wife has lost 60 pounds or more. According to him, after work she eats and then goes to bed around 6 pm so that tells me she is getting less artificial light and more sleep as well as restricting food consumption. He works until 11pm intermittently during the week under artificial lights.

A lady coworker who is very nice encourages me on my weight loss and is inquisitive about what I am eating and my lifestyle changes, so I share what I am eating and doing. I found out she had her gallbladder removed and let her know that eating the way I do might be a problem for her.

A young, female, former coworker's mother had a failing heart 2 years ago. It was a dire situation. I figured the doctors probably had the mother on statins so I told the coworker that alpha lipoic acid and CoQ10 might assist her mother to improve as well as explain that statins can damage the heart by limiting production of CoQ10. I never knew if she tried to utilize the info or not. I suspect she just listened politely to me and never thought about it again, probably trusting the doctors to do what was right. Her mother passed away. Nowadays I would still offer the same advice except I would additionally suggest PQQ and Acetyl-L-Carnitine along with the ALA, the reduced form of CoQ10 and D-ribose.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-14T23:46:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bbcs-advice-for-heart-health.4839/post-64342>

# Thread: Cave of the lone woman
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cave-of-the-lone-woman.4842/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-14T11:06:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cave-of-the-lone-woman.4842/post-64205>

Archaeologists might have finally found the cave of a native woman who lived alone off the coast of California:

> One of the most famous people associated with the Channel Islands, the Lone Woman belonged to the Nicoleno, a Native American tribe who lived on the remote wind-blasted island of San Nicolas off the Southern California coast. ...
>
>
>
> The tribe was decimated in 1814 by sea otter hunters from Alaska. By 1835, less than a dozen Nicolenos lived on the island. At that time, the Santa Barbara Mission arranged a rescue operation which brought to the mainland all NicoleÃ±os but the Lone Woman. ...
>
>
>
>
> She got missed somehow and lived there alone till 1853.
>
>
>
> Two redwood boxes \[on the island\] ... contained more than 200 objects, including shells, bone tools, harpoon points, bone fishhooks and even a smoking pipe.
>
>
>
> It may never be known just who left the boxes, but "it's at least a reasonable hypothesis" that it was the Lone Woman ...
>
>
>
> Although the Lone Woman managed to live alone for 18 years on the wild, tiny island, she did not last long when she came back to "civilization" at a possible age of 50.
>
>
>
> She died from dysentery only seven weeks after she arrived to the Santa Barbara Mission, unable to communicate, but totally fascinated by the new life she was discovering.
>
>
>
> Indeed, her great fondness for food such as green corn, vegetables and fresh fruit, was probably the cause for her fatal illness.

[http://news.discovery.com/history/juana-maria-cave-121101.html](http://http://news.discovery.com/history/juana-maria-cave-121101.html)

### nonchalant — 2012-11-14T17:57:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cave-of-the-lone-woman.4842/post-64275>

> Indeed, her great fondness for food such as green corn, vegetables and fresh fruit, was probably the cause for her fatal illness.

I needed to wait on the counter for my butter because the pharmacist needed to ghange my note to the butter and then she got so impressed about the huge butter brick that she wanted her colleague to picture her with a camera holding the butter... When we dream we don't talk change, we begin to live it........it helps mandate change exists in your current life. I have learned to embrace change because nothing diminishes anxiety faster than action. I came to realize each person’s task in life is to improve our former selves. Not all of us get this text message in time. All of our dreams can come true if we have the courage to just pursue them at some level. Our life does not get better by chance; it gets better by accepting change.

### Michael — 2012-11-15T16:20:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dreamed-about-jack.4843/post-64473>

> **Inger;66467 wrote:** @ Michael... I am now thinking I got it all wrong with the trolls.. lol. You see, in my imagination they are not evil at all. But I like to see the beauty in everything so I got to wonder if I might have made my own trolls in my mind that has nothing to do with the real ones.. at least in that way that they are evil? hmm..

I don't know. I'm not a folklorist (and not a lot of other things). I don't think they were supposed to be seen as harmless, but they may have morphed over time. Look what happend to fairies! If you're at all interested in literary history and you ever see a copy of C. S. Lewis's _The Discarded Image have a look at the range of beings medieval people believed in and how they saw them. Fairies could definitely be dodgy and tended to be morally "neutral" at best. They could bewitch you and carry you off, make you forget who you were. I guess Keats's Belle dame sans merci_ is the last hint of that - the main character in the poem is sexually captivated by the dame, who is obviously a fairy. Not like the tiny sentimental gossamer-winged Victorian fairies - like effete butterflies sitting on flowers - at all. Powerful and ambiguous beings.

There's still an expression in Ireland "he's away with the fairies" giving some hint of the former conception.

EDIT

Elves were believed to wound people. The Anglo-Saxons, finding microliths - tiny flint bladelets - from the Mesolithic in the fields, and not knowing what they were, had it that they were elvish weapons. If someone, or some farm animal, got ill and they didn't know why, they said maybe he had been hit with "elf shot".

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-16T01:04:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dreamed-about-jack.4843/post-64595>

> **Cavemam;66559 wrote:** She sings technically. Vibrato too contrived for my taste. Her runs seem lifeless and forte is breathy.

Ah Cavemam, you're a singer? Yes I can see that this singer is more technical and there could certainly be more fluidity overall. Perhaps a little more support from the diaphragm and more relaxed throat would produce that extra umph. I think she's pretty decent though and for sure I've heard WAY worse. It was still pleasant to listen to.

Btw, this thread was about dreams. If I have a 'bad dream' I now am afraid for my immediate health. I have noticed in the last year that any time I had a bad dream it was only followed by an acute illness or just general badness all around. I include that as one of my health markers in addition to any other more typical parameters for good health.

So, whenever I have a good dream or even just a neutral dream and I can recall it well, then I can expect only positive things. If I don't dream and/or don't recall them, then it's more 'neutral' but of course I stay vigilant just to make sure things are going in an upward trend.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-16T01:08:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dreamed-about-jack.4843/post-64597>

Haha...so if I look to last November (before I knew of this website lol)...the rate of actual nightmares was pretty much every night or every few nights. The following winter was ....not that good to say the least. The actual month of November was horrendous and I do not want to experience that EVER again!

Anyways, at least in January I found this site and from then only positive things have been happening. (Well I mean overall not to say there weren't any bumps or roadblocks). I'm very glad for this site for more than just the physical health aspects I've gained, but for my mental and emotional well being- that is priceless.

If you feel a blog in your fingers do not think just let the fingers flow. Get the mind out of the way and let everything be zen. When you paint let the fingers feel the brush to get the picture out. The ink will touch the brush and know what the fingers want to accomplish. When your in your natural state you just automatically get outof your own way. Meditation brings you to places you can get out of your own way easily. Some of my best artwork and blogs are written when i feel the words in my fingers.
