# Thread: Research Shows Body Fat Fuels Cancer Growth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-body-fat-fuels-cancer-growth.4848/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-14T20:01:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-body-fat-fuels-cancer-growth.4848/post-64302>

.

Interesting article on body fat fueling cancer growth

[https://www.worldhealth.net/news/fat-may-fuel-cancer/](http://https://www.worldhealth.net/news/fat-may-fuel-cancer/)

The World Health Organization reports that in 2008 there were more than 1.4 billion obese adults in the world and that cancer claimed the lives of 7.6 million that year. Whereas a number of previous studies have demonstrated a link between obesity and certain cancer, the physiological causes have not been identified. Mikhail Kolonin, from, The University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston (Texas, USA), and colleagues completed a study in which adipose stromal cells were transplanted into mice; they observed these cells to become recruited by tumors, where they incorporate into blood vessels and become fat cells. The researchers also observed that cancer induces a six-fold increase of adipose stromal cell frequency in the systemic circulation â€“ where they are incorporated into blood vessels as pericytes and they can differentiate into adipocytes in an obesity-dependent manner.

The study authors conclude that: â€œour results suggest that \[adipose stromal cells\] recruited from endogenous adipose tissue can be recruited by tumors to potentiate the supportive properties of the tumor microenvironment."


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: New Nordic Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-14T21:36:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64314>

This movement looks quite interesting - seems to have been around for a while but I only just heard of it today.

> Move over cavemen and Mediterraneans, Vikings are the new diet gurus.
>
>
>
> Claus Meyer, co-founder of the Danish restaurant Noma, twice named the best in the world, has been collaborating with nutrition experts to combat the obesity epidemic. ...
>
>
> [http://shine.yahoo.com/healthy-living/the-new-nordic-diet-the-next-big-weight-loss-trend-2607925.html](http://http://shine.yahoo.com/healthy-living/the-new-nordic-diet-the-next-big-weight-loss-trend-2607925.html)
>
>
>
> The cuisine seems to be based on plenty of fish, shellfish, free-range meat, nuts, vegetables and fruit, and seaweed. Sounds good to me.
>
>
>
> There's a hint of "eat up your bread" and of low fat about it, but this is light years ahead of the dreadful diet the BBC was pushing at its audience that I linked this morning.
>
>
>
> Unlike the BBC, Claus Meyer does take a swipe at:
>
>
>
>
> > processed starches in our pantry and produce shipped from across the globe
> >
> >
> > says that everything should be home-cooked and says to eat the food grown in your region.
> >
> >
> >
> > The BBC, readers of this board will recall was pushing non-home-cooked made from processed ingredients to people silly enough to take its advice:
> >
> >
> >
> > **Soya protein is also an excellent substitute for meat and is available in a convenient and tasty form **in** many ready-made meals**.
>
>
>
> And people do seem to be getting weight-loss results with the Nordic Diet - not surprising I guess as it's real food.
>
>
>
> Add in some more fat and take the cereal grains out and it would pretty much be Epipaleo.
>
>
>
> Looking at the guy's site, the bread he's making himself is from [heirloom varieties of grain, mostly rye](http://http://www.clausmeyer.dk/en/grupe___meyer_flour.html), unbleached and unadulterated four, and baked properly. I think this stuff is probably better out the diet, but if you're going to have it in at all, _this_ is the way to do it.
>
>
>
> While on the surface this diet is closer to the mainstream that what we do, look closer and it's a long, long way from the toxic industrlalized diet people like the BBC are pushing.
>
>
>
> I love the chef's manifesto, too:
>
>
>
> 1\. To express the purity, freshness, simplicity and ethics we wish to associate with our region.
>
>
>
> 2\. To reflect the changing of the seasons in the meals we make.
>
>
>
> 3\. To base our cooking on ingredients and produce whose characteristics are particularly excellent in our climates, landscapes and waters.
>
>
>
> 4\. To combine the demand for good taste with modern knowledge of health and well-being.
>
>
>
> 5\. To promote Nordic products and the variety of Nordic producers – and to spread the word about their underlying cultures.
>
>
>
> 6\. To promote animal welfare and a sound production process in our seas, on our farmland and in the wild.
>
>
>
> 7\. To develop potentially new applications of traditional Nordic food products.
>
>
>
> 8\. To combine the best in Nordic cookery and culinary traditions with impulses from abroad.
>
>
>
> 9\. To combine local self-sufficiency with regional sharing of high-quality products.
>
>
>
> 10\. To join forces with consumer representatives, other cooking craftsmen, agriculture, the fishing, food , retail and wholesale industries, researchers, teachers, politicians and authorities on this project for the benefit and advantage of everyone in the Nordic countries.

[http://www.clausmeyer.dk/en/the\_new\_nordic\_cuisine\_/manifesto\_.html](http://http://www.clausmeyer.dk/en/the_new_nordic_cuisine_/manifesto_.html)

Seasonality, good taste, tradition, ethics, animal welfare, localism, social responsibility. This is what food should be about: not just fuelling up.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-14T21:48:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64318>

If bread were made like that, and eaten seasonally (autumn), and you were healthy to begin with, and not too much bread was eaten per day.... it would probably be just fine.

### caroline — 2012-11-14T22:38:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64327>

About 2 years ago I saw Rene Redzeppi at the Opera House here in Sydney. He was involved with Noma. He is an amazing young man and very humble - even tho his restaurant had just been named #1 in the world. It was the most fascinating and electric evening. He talked about all the stuff you mention. Later there was about a half dozen other people - food critics, chefs etc talking about all this. One Chef from Melbourne had brought various and assorted sea weeds/vegetables that he had picked at the beach that morning before coming to Sydney. I wish I could remember more of that evening - I just loved every second of it. They even placed some sea vegetables on everyone's seat! I often think of that evening when I am down at the beach and wish I could remember and identify the stuff they showed us. He is a magic young man. A friend of mine has been to Noma a couple of times. It really pushes the boundaries. For some reason I think Noma has closed - I could be wrong. I have his cook book - I am going to go and get it out. Thanks for reminding me of that wonderful evening.

### Michael — 2012-11-15T09:24:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64427>

That sounds like an amazing evening, Caroline.

I guess this is the book?

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Noma-Time-Place-Nordic-Cuisine/dp/0714859036/](http://http://www.amazon.co.uk/Noma-Time-Place-Nordic-Cuisine/dp/0714859036/)

So he's the co-founder of Noma with the other bloke. I see as well as an emphasis on seasonality he's interested in foraging food from the wild.

Over here Hugh Fearnley-Whittingstall (River Cottage) used to be big on that. Not just nettles and such with Hugh either. He once got death threats against his baby son after he was filmed climbing a tree to gather rooks from their nests.

I found Pubmed has some articles on the diet:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=new%20nordic%20diet](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=new%20nordic%20diet)

It's interesting to see from pubmed that they're trialling offering it in schools - shades of Jamie Oliver there. Pubmed was how I found out about the New Nordic Diet, actually. I happened to be looking for something to do with shellfish and up it popped.

I had found this on Amazon last night and ordered a copy:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Nordic-Diet-Organic-Promote-Lifestyle/dp/1616081899/](http://http://www.amazon.co.uk/Nordic-Diet-Organic-Promote-Lifestyle/dp/1616081899/)

I'm not much of a cook, but I do enjoy trying sometimes. I like looking through cookbooks, too, specially if they've got good photographs.

### caroline — 2012-11-15T12:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64435>

It was an amazing evening Michael. That is the cookbook that I have. I have 45 boxes of cookbooks in the garage from when I moved. It doesn't seem to be in my apartment so I will look for it tomorrow. I had forgotten about it. It has so much information on scavenging for stuff. I just love seaweed now so I would like to be able to identify some of these things. Do you know the website ......Nom Nom paleo. ?

She has lots of good recipes and lists other paleo cookbooks. What do you like to eat/cook? Do you do MHS? That actually sounds kind of erotic - will blame Inger for that! Have your dreams improved/moved towards butter and banknotes and kisses?

### Michael — 2012-11-15T13:35:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64446>

45 boxes! That is impressive.

I heard the name of that site, but haven't seen it.

I used to like the Elizabeth David books, because they weren't just recipe books but brought a whole world to life as well - what it was like to be in that part of France (or wherever), how you shopped there, all the history and culture around it.

I don't recall dreaming anything particularly interesting last night. I'd have been hugely amused to have had the dream OptimalBound had - Jack sitting in a floodlit cave at midnight, eating a banana and laughing. That's hilarious!

### caroline — 2012-11-15T13:49:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64451>

Have you seen any books by Patricia Wells? She was a food writer in Paris. Great insight into Paris and a lot of fun stuff about markets. You are lucky to live so close - do you travel often? I always brought local cook books home when we travelled (hence why I have so many) you get so much local color/history/insight into people's lives from their cooking. I have spent a great deal of time over the years reading cookbooks. Great memories actually.

### Michael — 2012-11-15T15:04:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64461>

My sister wants to go to Paris for her birthday next year. Her husband has this crazy idea that the whole of the extended family should accompany them. So I guess we will. Perhaps I should get her that book to get her in the mood.

This thread could get expensive. You've already made me think that I should probably get the _Noma_ book!

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T18:04:25+0200

# Thread: This is exactly what happens...(cortisol)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-exactly-what-happens-cortisol.4850/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### freesia — 2012-11-14T21:38:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-exactly-what-happens-cortisol.4850/post-64315>

This is Dr Kruse's answer to a question about how Cold Thermogenisis works towards normalizing cortisol.

> CT lowers inflammation. Inflammation alter mitochondrial signaling to make ATP from foods. When mitochondria do not work well you get a lowered hormone response and this is better known in these parts as LR. This implies that inflammation disrupts the normal efficiency of making ATP in mitochondria and this correlates directly with out ability to make hormones. This is exactly what happens.

My experience follows that exactly. My recent cortisol tests are now all in normal range after years of high at night and low in the am. Thought re-posting Jacks answer might help others.

x

### nonchalant — 2012-11-14T21:49:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-exactly-what-happens-cortisol.4850/post-64319>

That is fabulous, freesia!!!

And I like that quote. Thinking of putting part of it in my sig line, but I'm too fond of the "Quelson everything" one. ;-)

Could I ask how long it took for your cortisol to normalize? Thanks.

# Thread: When something is difficult, be thankful
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2012-11-14T22:30:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/post-64325>

This is a really thought provoking article on how things that are difficult actually make us more productive, more creative while creating a deeper and longer lasting understanding.

I see this in reading through Jack's posts. They certainly aren't "easy" for me - but because I work at it and go back and re-read, I value what I get out of them more. And they really make me think, unlike these pop culture magazine ads of "do this and get this".

> Our brains respond better to difficulty than we imagine. In schools, teachers and pupils alike often assume that if a concept has been easy to learn, then the lesson has been successful. But numerous studies have now found that when classroom material is made harder to absorb, pupils retain more of it over the long term, and understand it on a deeper level.
>
>
>
> Also think this is a great read for those of us who think that getting our health back is "hard". Because if it's hard - we're going to be more creative about problem solving, and it will be more likely to be lifelong!
>
>
>
>
> > The researchers also found that when people are forced to cope with unexpected obstacles they react by increasing their "perceptual scope"â€”taking a mental step back to see the bigger picture.

[http://moreintelligentlife.com/content/ideas/ian-leslie/uses-difficulty?buffer\_share=7da8f](http://http://moreintelligentlife.com/content/ideas/ian-leslie/uses-difficulty?buffer_share=7da8f)

### caroline — 2012-11-14T22:46:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/post-64329>

> **diane;66319 wrote:** This is a really thought provoking article on how things that are difficult actually make us more productive, more creative while creating a deeper and longer lasting understanding.
>
>
>
> I see this in reading through Jack's posts. They certainly aren't "easy" for me - but because I work at it and go back and re-read, I value what I get out of them more. And they really make me think, unlike these pop culture magazine ads of "do this and get this".
>
>
>
> [http://moreintelligentlife.com/content/ideas/ian-leslie/uses-difficulty?buffer\_share=7da8f](http://http://moreintelligentlife.com/content/ideas/ian-leslie/uses-difficulty?buffer_share=7da8f)

Thanks Diane for posting that. I, like you struggle to understand Dr. K's blogs at times but I keep rereading and it is getting easier - I just wish I could explain them but I guess in time that will come

### nonchalant — 2012-11-14T22:52:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/post-64330>

Interesting article, Diane.

I still remember a science lesson from ... 6th grade? The teacher presented a practical problem, and absolutely refused to tell us if we had the correct answer or even if we were on the right track. This was not for a test, or homework. She never answered that question, even after the school year. It was quite intriguing to us, and we discussed/debated the problem amongst ourselves.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-14T23:00:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/post-64331>

> As a poet, Ted Hughes had an acute sensitivity to the way in which constraints on self-expression, like the disciplines of metre and rhyme, spur creative thought.

Yes! I've experienced this.

I'll write something out in prose, and then convert it to a traditional poem, and I'm delighted at the results. I'm forced to use different words or phrases, because they just won't work in the poem, and I end up with something much better. More concise, and descriptive.

# Thread: Egg Free, Dairy Free, Grain Free, Soy Free Cookie
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-15T00:31:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64351>

Well, I had been making egg muffins for the kids in the morning but with DD's enterolab results I had to set out in search of something that they would enjoy having with some meat. Veggies for breakfast aren't at the top of their list... yet. While we work on all that I thought I'd try to whip up something not too bad for them. After looking at several recipes on the internet I blended them and here's what I came up with:

9T Coconut Flour

6T Coconut Oil (you could use butter or ghee here just fine I think)

2T Honey or Maple Syrup

1/4 t Celtic Sea Salt

3T dried wild berries mixed with soaked nuts

1T chopped dark chocolate bits

Mix all together, drop onto cookie sheet (I used a silpat liner, not sure how they'd do straight on the pan), pat down a bit, and bake at 365 degrees for 8 to 10 minutes or until browned on bottom. Let them cool all the way after you take them out of the oven or they'll crumble.

If you don't like salty sweet reduce the amount of sea salt...

Here's a close-up

[](http://http://imageshack.us/photo/my-images/835/cookiehk.jpg/)

If anyone else has good ideas that are free from the items in the subject line I'd love if you shared! Thanks!

Edit: This makes 12 cookies... One baking sheet full

### nonchalant — 2012-11-15T01:00:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64356>

Sounds yummy, thanks River. With the holidays coming, I'm looking at baking again. It's not something I do anymore, but for a special occasion I can make an exception.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-15T01:49:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64367>

If it weren't for the little ones I wouldn't even think about something like this - probably even for the holidays. But, yank milk and eggs away from someone who doesn't really fully understand what's happening (because they're 3) and put something that looks like dinner in front of them for breakfast with a glass of water and it's a shock, and they're in a fasted state, and well... they're 3 so it seems like a BFD when you're used to having a glass of milk first thing in the morning. Every one of their favorite meals has been impacted so I am being a little flexible on breakfast so I can be hard on the other meals. Some 3 year olds love trying new things and experimenting with food but mine are adventurous in other ways... not so much with food.

### bigknitwit — 2012-11-15T02:27:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64371>

These look like a great transition food - thanks for the idea. I could use something like this to pack for dd's snack for church (why the 3 year old class needs to have snack during their brief sunday school meeting is beyond me, but I digress...)

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-15T06:10:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64419>

bigknitwit,

They need to have a snack because even at 3 they can't go a couple hours without their carb fix to stave off the hypoglycemia.....

I mean who hasn't seen a toddler these days go into full meltdown because they've got the shakes and need their juice!

Okay, maybe I'm being dramatic.... and cynical.... but I'm in a mood and I'm claiming perogative. I realized this afternoon that there is some sort of grieving/coping process when dealing with food issues and children.

### bigknitwit — 2012-11-15T14:00:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64454>

> **RiverNeighbor;66416 wrote:** bigknitwit,
>
>
>
> They need to have a snack because even at 3 they can't go a couple hours without their carb fix to stave off the hypoglycemia.....
>
>
>
> I mean who hasn't seen a toddler these days go into full meltdown because they've got the shakes and need their juice!
>
>
>
> Okay, maybe I'm being dramatic.... and cynical.... but I'm in a mood and I'm claiming perogative. I realized this afternoon that there is some sort of grieving/coping process when dealing with food issues and children.

And in the nursery (for the children under 3), where I volunteer every few weeks, we feed the babies goldfish and apple juice. We never feed them anything different because of allergy issues. Goldfish and Apple juice. I've had to pack my dd her own snack since she could start eating solid foods (and not scream in the nursery). The way we handle food with our children in sickening, IMO. It's even more sickening though, as a mother, when we realize just how our child is being damaged by these practises, and how they no longer have the option to participate, even once in a while. There is some definite grieving to be done here, I agree. Last week, 20 minutes after snack, one of the 14 month old little boys asked for his juice filled bottle...

Sorry I'm completely pulling away from your great recipe idea again.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-16T06:12:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64658>

Not pulling away at all, bigknitwit

Cookies were a big HELP today, but not a big hit. I'd say between the two of them they ate 1 cookie. Having it as an option really made breakfast go more smoothly. Son asked me first thing this morning if he couldn't have milk could he have an extra big cup of water. He's having a harder time than daughter because he likes fewer meats and vegetables and he was more into the milk/yogurt/cheese and egg. They're adjusting and this is turning out not to be as hard as I thought it was going to be.

Daughter stood at the grocery store this afternoon and asked me why so many things have things in it that hurt people.... at first I thought she was saying it because she was sad she couldn't have the foods that looked appealing. Turns out she was concerned that other people were buying it and getting hurt. It was sweet.

### vkiernan — 2012-11-17T15:46:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64931>

> **RiverNeighbor;66657 wrote:** Not pulling away at all, bigknitwit
>
>
>
> Cookies were a big HELP today, but not a big hit. I'd say between the two of them they ate 1 cookie. Having it as an option really made breakfast go more smoothly. Son asked me first thing this morning if he couldn't have milk could he have an extra big cup of water. He's having a harder time than daughter because he likes fewer meats and vegetables and he was more into the milk/yogurt/cheese and egg. They're adjusting and this is turning out not to be as hard as I thought it was going to be.
>
> Daughter stood at the grocery store this afternoon and asked me why so many things have things in it that hurt people.... at first I thought she was saying it because she was sad she couldn't have the foods that looked appealing. Turns out she was concerned that other people were buying it and getting hurt. It was sweet.

You are apparently doing a great job with this! I couldn't imagine being in your shoes.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-19T03:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-65257>

It is really interesting to watch the family adapt....

DD is feeling so good. She jumped up and down and yelled "I feel great" into the phone when my MIL asked her how she was feeling. Her behavior has improved dramatically (she had been having some strange behavior).

Tonight she ate both a sardine and some seaweed with her dinner.

DS went from doing well for several days into meltdown mode today and refused to eat anything after breakfast. Tomorrow's a new day. I'm sure he'll eat when he's hungry enough.

# Thread: The Potato Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 35
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-11-15T02:10:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64369>

I think the paleo blogosphere is onto something, but they don't know what. The so-called "Potato Diet" is lighting up all the forums and blogs from PaleoHacks to Free The Animal to Mark's Daily Apple.

This diet was started by Ray Cronise as a simple trick to prove that carbs weren't the cause of weight problems and in fact could be used to lose fat.

On his blog, Ray propositioned overweight folks to eat only potatoes, as plain as possible, for a period of 7-14 days. Everyone that did this lost 1/2 to 1 pound per day. It's as he says "predictable and repeatable".

I'm thinking the timing of this led to it's success. Fall is starchy tuber time.

Maybe this is one of those ancestral pathways Jack talks about, like the Leptin Reset. Our ancestors would have surely faced times of starvation when all that was available was starchy tubers, since these grow underground and are impervious to drought, frost, insects, and animals. During times of starvation, and when eating only starchy tubers, it would have been prudent to go into fat-burning/muscle sparing mode. The insulin surges from eating starchy tubers without any additional fat or protein maybe create a signalling pathway to burn fat.

So what do you all think? A seasonal way to lose a few pounds of fat or just straight-up calories in-calories out?

### Huck — 2012-11-15T02:33:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64374>

The potato diet was 1st done by Chris Voigt, head of the Washington State Potato Commission, as a protest against a government proposal to restrict potatoes in some government food programs.

In 60 days of eating just potatoes, he lost 21 lbs, blood glucose dropped 10 points to 94, cholesterol dropped 67 points to 147, and triglycerides dropped 60 points to 75. His BP at the end of the diet was 112/70.

[http://20potatoesaday.com/index.html](http://http://20potatoesaday.com/index.html)

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T02:55:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64382>

So maybe while there are no 'safe starches', the potato gives us an ancestral inroad to fatloss with no hunger--like HCG. Not for long-term use, but as a quick fat loss strategy to get our fat levels down to where they are manageable with leptin, sunlight, epi-paleo and all that jazz.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-15T02:59:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64383>

Say what you want, yes it's tuber season, but my hip joints really don't like potatoes. They complain every time I indulge. And they complain at night. Quite effectively.

### hellojtm — 2012-11-15T03:15:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64389>

Hey AKMan,

I have to say that I think Ray is a nutritionist. I.e. a person that subscribes to nutritionism. One who believes that we know what's in food and can therefore decide what we need to eat. As such I have a distaste for where he is headed. I think that once he abandons that then he will become a great resource. OK, that caveat aside, I kind of followed the potato diet thing. From my understanding he's doing it to foster the growth of a specific gut flora ecosystem. My question is this, we've seen the potato diet before, we've seen the banana diet, we've seen the raw meat diet, etc... but those are all just paleo diets. \*MY\* opinion, starchy tubers are not an evolutionarily novel food. As such your body knows what to do with them when they are a staple. Eliminate \*all\* the other bull\*\*\*\* and just have a staple of paleo food, in this case a monocrop of potatoes, and your body will know what to do with it. Do it with broccoli. Do it with blackberries. Sure, some might be better than others, like in this case his nutritionism mind tells him that a potato has aminos in it so it's better than other fruits or what not. But I think my point stands. Eat a paleo diet and you will get the benefits of a body and a meal that are in sync and you will be on your way to optimal. Although I don't subscribe to the religion of nutritionism I have a gut feel that these potato dieters are incurring deficiencies, probably not ones that become clinical in months but they would in years.

jesse

### hellojtm — 2012-11-15T03:17:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64390>

Also, to address the tuber season point, as we know most people's bodies have no clue what season it is so I don't buy that argument for the general population.

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T03:25:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64395>

> **hellojtm;66386 wrote:** Also, to address the tuber season point, as we know most people's bodies have no clue what season it is so I don't buy that argument for the general population.

Thanks for those two thoughtful posts. Very astute observations.

It seems to me this is being used a as very short-term, 1-2 week, way to lose 5-10lbs of fat without hunger. I would definitely agree with you that much longer than that would lead to deficiencies. I always get excited when I see people losing weight without hunger, like hcg users, and not resorting to surgery. The gut flora comment intrigues me also. What if a short term diet of starchy tuber clarifies the gut flora creating a signalling situation as we saw in the BG series that leads to quicker than normal fat loss?

The folks I see talking about the potato diet are saying 5-10 lbs in 1-2 weeks, that's better than any other fat-loss hack I've seen people trying like IF, CT, spot-icing, etc... Granted those aren't supposed to be quick-fix fat-loss tools, but so many people would love to have a way to lose 5-10lbs in a couple weeks. Just kinda hoping this is a good way to do it.

### Huck — 2012-11-15T03:31:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64398>

Many, many, many, many years ago I did the Diet Center diet, which was a high protein, low fat, low carb diet. The 1st 2 days, you ate a baked potato with dinner. If I remember correctly, it was supposed to help stabilize blood sugar. Maybe the idea was to keep from cutting out all sugars and starches cold turkey and eat a specific amount of starch during the 1st 2 days.

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T04:05:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64402>

> **Huck;66394 wrote:** Many, many, many, many years ago I did the Diet Center diet, which was a high protein, low fat, low carb diet. The 1st 2 days, you ate a baked potato with dinner. If I remember correctly, it was supposed to help stabilize blood sugar. Maybe the idea was to keep from cutting out all sugars and starches cold turkey and eat a specific amount of starch during the 1st 2 days.

[http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2012/10/protons-zero-fat\_3.html](http://http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2012/10/protons-zero-fat_3.html)

Peter at Hyperlipid said this of an all-starch diet:

"But the physiology of weight loss on ultra low fat diets is basically comprehensible, especially once you look at lipids and superoxide at the ETC level, and what the body needs to function effectively. Running your metabolism on pure glucose would induce, theoretically, an infinite glucose sensitivity and low fasting insulin. If we do reductio ad absurdum you would end up with no fat stores and experience death from hypoglycaemia if you ever depleted your glycogen stores. Mitochondria like (saturated) fatty acids. Fatty acids keep them in control."

In the comments, someone we know and love said this:

"Jack Kruse said...

I know that sounds sounds counterintuitive to some, but it is precisely how the brain is wired in humans to couple food and light into our metabolism and sleep. The body collects that data from the eye (SCN) and skin for the brain and the brain senses and correlates that data to set the the metabolic rate and growth cycle by going up or down with dopamine levels at the HPA. Dopamine interacts with the leptin receptor and its pathways directly as mentioned above and this is why women and men are different at a central Nervous system level. This is why sleep, metabolism and the cell cycle are all linked to the dopamine pathways tied to light and not just food. Food reward is a good idea not looked into deep enough by those who buy it 100%. I love your series Peter. I feel like you are digging the same hole I am from different ends of the earth and soon we will meet. Our only difference is our perspective and our digging tools."

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-15T04:42:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64403>

OK, AKMan, I am now intrigued. Can you please translate the exchange between Peter and Jack for me? I've read it but I don't fully understand. How is what Peter suggests true? And how did Jack go from what Peter wrote to discussing light?

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T05:40:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64410>

> **KiwiLauren;66399 wrote:** OK, AKMan, I am now intrigued. Can you please translate the exchange between Peter and Jack for me? I've read it but I don't fully understand. How is what Peter suggests true? And how did Jack go from what Peter wrote to discussing light?

Maybe this is the portion of the article Jack was referring to, more specifically:

"How about scaling this up to a massive dose of potato induced insulin and limiting dietary fat? Severely limiting dietary fat. And never mind pussy footing around at 40g of mixed carbs and protein. There is a limit to how low FFAs can be driven, and it seems safe to assume that a baked potato or three might just inhibit lipolysis maximally and keep it that low for rather a long time. But if you deprive beta cells of free fatty acids you blunt their ability to secrete insulin. Very, very high carbohydrate diets really ought to be able to inhibit lipolysis to the point where the knock on effect is the inhibition of insulin secretion, provided you don't supply exogenous fat. Look at the nicotinic acid treated rats...

Once you get FFA levels low enough to inhibit insulin secretion you will start to move in to the sort of territory where insulin secretion might be blunted enough to allow hyperglycaemia. But the feedback effect of reduced insulin levels is also the re commencement of lipolysis. This will restore enough FFAs to maintain functional insulin secretion and so avoid potential hyperglycaemia, which the body tries to avoid. Of course you have to throw in the increased insulin sensitivity of muscles deprived of exogenously supplied FFAs too.

So is it possible to eat an ad lib, calorie unrestricted diet based on near pure carbohydrate and lose weight? Working from the premise that lowered insulin is a pre requisite for hunger free weight loss, as I always do, the answer is possibly yes. We all remember Chris Voight on his all potato diet (plus 20ml of olive oil, low in palmitate, per day) who lost a great deal of weight over a few weeks, the rate of weight loss accelerating as the weeks progressed? I had a think about it here, well before I had any inkling as to what might be happening in the electron transport chain."

OK, AKMan here, again - What I think Peter is saying, is that a massive dose of carbs, like potatoes only for 2 weeks, would greatly effect insulin and hunger. This isn't the 'safe starch' line of thinking that rice is OK every day, but a deeper biologic response to a massive dose of starch and nothing else. Since the body NEEDS fat, it will strip it from fat stores in the body. Since potatoes are so satiating, it is nearly impossible to eat enough to slow the stripping of fat from our fat supply. I think I am looking at this from a 3000 ft view, not 30,000 as Jack is, but maybe not 30 like most people talking about potato diets today.

### Owl — 2012-11-15T05:58:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64416>

I tried the potato diet, and posted about it on MDA. The first day of eating nothing but potatoes and salt, I did pretty well. Wasn't hungry in between meals.

The next morning, I decided to measure blood glucose, with breakfast of about 1 lb potatoes with salt.

Fasting 87

1/2 hr. 136

1 hr. 181 !!!

2 hr. 94

That was too high for me, I stopped the experiment. I lost nothing from the one full day of the diet.

I normally eat about 75-100gm carbs/day, so I'm not super low carb. From what I've read, BG over 140 can damage nerves and organs. I don't want to do that to lose a little weight.

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T17:58:34+0200

It is all-starch, calorie restricting, (possibly) seasonal and ketogenic. Yes, ketogenic.

Cold Thermogenesis got the same treatment in the paleosphere: Get cold, burn calories, lose weight! But upon closer examination, we know there is way more to it than that.

Much like shortened daylight and cold cues a hibernation response, maybe a complete lack of exogenous fat and animal protein with ample starch triggers an 'anti-starvation' response that results in hyper-insulin sensitivity and release of ffa from adipose stores.

### AKMan — 2012-11-16T07:00:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64661>

My "primal senses" tell me there's something going on in the areas of leptin, T3, SCN, and BG signalling...

Another gem from Peter at Hyperlipid:

"Without fat, bulk calories are stored as glycogen, excepting that there is a little de novo fat synthesis from glucose in the liver. Hepatic glycogen does not cause hepatic insulin resistance. In the near absence of FFA supply the liver maintains insulin sensitivity and the ability to degrade insulin. Nothing like as much insulin reaches the periphery as is produced by the pancreas in response to 2-3000kcal of potatoes.

The...effect of shutting down free fatty acid supply from adipocytes and diet is the loss of fatty acid intermediates in muscle. Insulin sensitivity increases, the amount of insulin needed to facilitate glucose uptake by muscles decreases. Insulin secretion from the pancreas will then decrease but hepatic extraction of insulin continues while ever carbohydrate adaption continues.

The ultimate determinant of weight loss is fasting insulin. This determines how much lipolysis occurs during the period before the next meal. No one expects to lose weight during the 4 hours immediately after any meal. The following 8 hours, especially overnight, is when weight loss occurs."

### Stavity — 2012-11-17T14:44:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64921>

> **Nonchalant;66861 wrote:** Mee too, Stavity. And now you're making me wonder about my left ankle. It has given me big problems in the past, and occasional twinges now. I'll see if there is a correlation.

My left ankle seems to be the worst and the first one to swell. Please do look into it and you will never eat a white/purple potato again. NOt sure about anyone else, but one food I did abuse is the potato. I was the potato chip kid--oh and FF's, twice baked, mashed, cheesy, must I go on. Potatoes were the hardest food I ever had to give up. I still crave them, even knowing what I do today.

But, now I can walk without excruciating pain, and I do not have to feel embarrassed by limping. I am probably older than you, and being totally crippled was just around the corner for me because it just worsens with age. Since giving them up there is, absolutely, no swelling or pain. YOu know, if I relied on doctors, they would have had me on pain pills, and steroid shots or worse surgery that would have complicated things more because I still would have eaten potatoes.

We have an advantage over those who rely on drs and medicine to try and fix us. Instead we are determined to fix ourselves and take responsibility for learning our own bodies and what is good for us. Epi-paleo is for me and I notice good things happening all the time--have a long way to go because I didn't get to the shape I am in overnight.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-18T18:47:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-65160>

After X amount of days in ketosis, the muscles switch to using fat as fuel, and the brain switches from glucose to using ketones as fuel. On a potato only diet do the brain and muscles switch back to burning glucose as fuel? Or due to the available potato starches, does the brain switch back to burning glucose and the muscles begin using ketones? Do glycogen stores in the liver increase on a potato diet?

I remember about the Potato Council guy eating only potatoes. According to his statements it seemed his biggest problem was food boredom, not hunger.

### AKMan — 2012-11-19T05:51:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-65271>

> **Glen PDQ;67165 wrote:** After X amount of days in ketosis, the muscles switch to using fat as fuel, and the brain switches from glucose to using ketones as fuel. On a potato only diet do the brain and muscles switch back to burning glucose as fuel? Or due to the available potato starches, does the brain switch back to burning glucose and the muscles begin using ketones? Do glycogen stores in the liver increase on a potato diet?
>
>
>
> I remember about the Potato Council guy eating only potatoes. According to his statements it seemed his biggest problem was food boredom, not hunger.

My impression is, if you are in ketosis to begin with, and you start eating nothing but potatoes, you'd remain in ketosis due to the calorie restricted nature of the diet. The glucose is quickly cleared by your supremely insulin sensitive liver and muscles and you return to burning free fatty acids and using ketones for brain fuel. If you totally pigged out on potatoes, you would probably revert back to using glucose for fuel, but from what I've seen, it is nearly impossible to eat potatoes at more than 50% your normal calorie intake.

If you are a sugar burner to begin with, an all-potato diet should put you in ketosis in a few days as you use up available glycogen from liver and muscles. Again, assuming you are eating at a huge calorie deficit, burning ffa as primary fuel.

All speculation on my part, based on minimal available research...

I'm still confused about how an all-potato diet would be ketotic. I thought the only way to get into ketosis was to cut carbs way down? How would eating nothing but high-carb potatoes achieve this? Sorry, this is probably like Paleo 101 and I was making spitballs in class that day or something...:

# Thread: Research Shows Carbs Fuel Brain Diseases  Fat Helps Heal Brain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-carbs-fuel-brain-diseases-fat-helps-heal-brain.4858/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-15T02:13:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-carbs-fuel-brain-diseases-fat-helps-heal-brain.4858/post-64370>

.

Yet another reason not to eat carbs - research shows carbs cause brain disease:

**Carbs & Sugars Raise Risk of Alzheimerâ€™s Disease**

Nov. 14, 2012

[https://www.worldhealth.net/news/carbs-sugars-raise-risk-alzheimers-disease/](http://https://www.worldhealth.net/news/carbs-sugars-raise-risk-alzheimers-disease/)

Previously, studies suggest that high caloric intake associates with an increased risk of cognitive impairment. Rosebud O. Roberts, from Mayo Clinic (Minnesota, USA), and colleagues analyzed data collected on 1,230 men and women, ages 70 to 89 years, who were surveyed on their dietary patterns of the previous year. At that time, their cognitive function was evaluated: only the roughly 940 who showed no signs of cognitive impairment were asked to return for follow-up evaluations of their cognitive function. About four years into the study, 200 of those 940 were beginning to show mild cognitive impairment, problems with memory, language, thinking and judgment that are greater than normal age-related changes. The subjects who reported the highest carbohydrate intake at the beginning of the study were 1.9 times likelier to develop mild cognitive impairment, as compared to those with the lowest intake of carbohydrates. Participants with the highest sugar intake were 1.5 times likelier to experience mild cognitive impairment , as compared those with the lowest levels.

The team also determined that the subjects whose diets were highest in fat -- compared to the lowest -- were 42% less likely to face cognitive impairment, and those who had the highest intake of protein had a reduced risk of 21%. When total fat and protein intake were taken into account, people with the highest carbohydrate intake were 3.6 times likelier to develop mild cognitive impairment. The study authors urge that: â€œA dietary pattern with relatively high caloric intake from carbohydrates and low caloric intake from fat and proteins may increase the risk of \[mild cognitive impairment\] or dementia in elderly persons.â€￾

Go Fat  woooo another win.....

Participants with the highest sugar intake were 1.5 times likelier to experience mild cognitive impairment , as compared those with the lowest levels.

### chocolate — 2012-11-23T15:35:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-carbs-fuel-brain-diseases-fat-helps-heal-brain.4858/post-65454>

http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/research-shows-diabetes-drug-improves-memory-15120
Quote:The molecule, called extracellular signal-regulated kinase (ERK), becomes hyperactive both in the brains of Alzheimerâ€™s patients and in the mice at a disease stage corresponding to mild cognitive impairment in human Alzheimerâ€™s. This excessive activity leads to improper synaptic transmission between neurons, interfering with learning and memory.

Rosiglitazone brings ERK back into line by activating whatâ€™s known as the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma (PPARÎ³) pathway, which interacts with genes that respond to both PPARÎ³ and ERK.

â€œUsing this drug appears to restore the neuronal signaling required for proper cognitive function,â€ said UTMB professor Larry Denner, the lead author of a paper describing this work now online in the Journal of Neuroscience. â€œIt gives us an opportunity to test several FDA-approved drugs to normalize insulin resistance in Alzheimerâ€™s patients and possibly also enhance memory, and it also gives us a remarkable tool to use in animal models to understand the molecular mechanisms that underlie cognitive issues in Alzheimerâ€™s.â€

ERK dysfunction in the Alzheimerâ€™s mouse model was discovered several years ago by UTMB associate professor Kelly Dineley, senior author of the Journal of Neuroscience paper. But putting together the protein, gene and memory pieces of the puzzle required a multidisciplinary translational research team including animal cognitive neuroscientists, biochemists, molecular biologists, mass spectrometrists, statisticians and bioinformaticists.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rosiglitazone
PPARs are expressed in fat cells, cells of the liver, muscle, heart, and inner wall (endothelium) and smooth muscle of blood vessels. PPARÎ³ is expressed mainly in fat tissue, where it regulates genes involved in fat cell (adipocyte) differentiation, fatty acid uptake and storage, and glucose uptake. It is also found in pancreatic beta cells, vascular endothelium, and macrophages.\[9\] Rosiglitazone is a selective ligand of PPARÎ³ and has no PPARÎ±-binding action. Other drugs bind to PPARÎ±.

Rosiglitazone also appears to have an anti-inflammatory effect in addition to its effect on insulin resistance. Nuclear factor kappa-B (NF-ÎºB), a signaling molecule, stimulates the inflammatory pathways. NF-ÎºB inhibitor (IÎºB) downregulates the inflammatory pathways. When patients take rosiglitazone, NF-ÎºB levels fall and IÎºB levels increase.\[10\]

Rosiglitazone may also benefit patients with Alzheimer's disease who do not express the ApoE4 allele.\[11\] This is the subject of a clinical trial currently underway.\[12\]

Rosiglitazone may also treat mild to moderate ulcerative colitis, due to its anti-inflammatory properties as a PPAR ligand.\[13\]

A clinical trial has suggested these agents may be of use in treating malaria.\[14\]

â€œWe were extraordinarily lucky to have this diverse group of experts right here on our campus at UTMB that could coalesce to bring such different ways of thinking to bear on a common problem,â€ Denner said. â€œIt was quite a challenge to get all of these experts communicating in a common scientific language. But now that we have this team working, we can move on to even more detailed and difficult questions.â€

this article seems to fit here. the drug uses fat cell stuff

GEEKS: The implications of Brain Gut 4 mean that to develop a brain seafood is required part of the program to maintain it. Nutrients and many other environmental factors have also been found to influence epigenetic programing of our DNA either directly or indirectly via metabolic sensors. Peroxisomal proliferator-activated receptors (PPARâ€™s), the vitamin D receptors, and the retinoid X receptors (RXR), and the retinoic acid receptors (RAR) are all examples of the nuclear receptors that interact with the brain cell membranes to control inflammation and metabolism all over our bodies. It turns out that PPARâ€™s are the receptors that are at the crossroads of where inflammation and metabolism actually cross. # Thread: The Love of your life........say something about it/them
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 81
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T05:00:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64404>

You know you're found amazing love when you can't fall asleep next to her, because your own reality is finally better than anything ever found in your dreams before. And in the memories of those dreams, you recollect desires just to be the air that enters her quietly, while it sustains her for just that moment, but it becomes the air that fuels you. In that wonder, your goal is to be unnoticed by her, but simultaneously necessary to sustain her. In turn, she exceeds your imagination, your forgetting, your opiate, your complete oblivion..........

Make love your remedy.

### caroline — 2012-11-15T05:47:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64412>

Love to me ......the song -" You Lift me up" I cried the first time I heard it and I have cried every time since. For me it says it all. I cry just thinking about the words.

### Inger — 2012-11-15T12:58:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64436>

Jack, that was so beautiful what you wrote. Very beautiful. This is love.. amazing love..

He was the sun and moon and the stars.. all in one...

We were intimate.. and I closed my eyes to fully enjoy the amazingness feeling him inside me.. as I opened my eyes again he looked straight into my eyes.. He's eyes and how he looked at me got forever burned into my mind. It is almost 5 years ago now.. but I can recall it anytime as alive as yesterday. These eyes and what they express are what I often see in front of me releasing oxytocin alone. It needs nothing else... the most beautiful love from a spirit so tender and full of beauty.. but at the same time so strong, the most diciplined human beeing I ever met. He had and was everything I was not.. and he still looked at me in a way that goes so much deeper then adoring...

The beautiful memories he gave me the whole year I got to see him almost every week, have carried me through the hardest times since, they keep me sane and raise me up when I think I have lost it

### nonchalant — 2012-11-15T17:37:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64497>

My husband and I are empty-nesters. Once the hustle and bustle and conflict over how to raise the kids was all over between us, we were left in a sort of no-man's land. We were going on inertia. But then we created a goal for our lives, and we have both thrown quite a bit of effort and thought and creativity into that dream. We have enjoyed making plans and decisions, and working to realize those plans. Our love and trust has bloomed anew in the process, and we both feel like teenagers again, in our relationship. Actually, it's better than when we were teenagers. We have decades of experience with each other now, and we appreciate each other more, know each other better, and we have confidence in each other.

The only concern that I had, in our dream, was the possibility (probability) that illness would bring an early end to it. We would have to move close to a hospital somewhere, and we would be apart from each other for long periods of time. We would still have each other, but we wouldn't be living our dream. That concern has vanished, thanks to the good doctor. I feel like I know, now, how to live in a manner that is healthy for our bodies. And yes, it meshes beautifully with our goal.

Dr. K, you have made our dream a long-term possibility.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64499>

I am no dream maker for anyone else........I am only a dream innovator for others........I show you bits and pieces of what makes us......us and allows you to see you differently in a new reality.......then you dream and create it.

### ColdBren — 2012-11-15T18:55:35+0200

### Meg — 2012-11-15T20:26:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64543>

There are so many threads that I feel inept to respond to but not this one. Been married to the same guy for twenty five years and have four kids.

My husband has and does give me many things but one of his greatest gifts is the gift of words. I was raised in a home were there was not much communication. You had to guess whether you were "pleasing" mom & dad and constantly take the temperature in the air to know how they were feeling. Kind of stressful, cause I was a pleaser. With my husband there are NO guessing games. What you see is what you get and you always know what he's thinking and where things stand. Can you say FREEDOM! Now, with all that talking, (because being inside a man's head all the time can be a bit overwhleming) comes constant affirmation that he is devoted to me and the kids and is not going anywhere, ever.

The combination of these two things gave me life! And the freedom to explore places inside of me that I never would have been able to otherwise. I do give him alot as well. I put up with his insanity because he is one of a kind. We both have sacrificed alot for each other and for the family but it has been worth it. Like good health, good relationships take alot of work and usually require making decisions that go against the grain to many. But, as someone we all know says (well kinda) when you know better, you choose better!

### zoebarry@yahoo.com — 2012-11-15T21:21:07+0200

there was one guy that I dated after my divorce.....he was the love of my life, but he decided after a year and a half that marriage was not in the immediate future, and he wanted to pursue some personal goals. We broke up six years ago and I have never felt that way about anyone else, and frankly, I'm afraid that I never willl.......I guess I'm gonna be one of those old ladies with a bunch of cats!!! hahahah!

my own experience is the same. It wasn't until I decided I was worth my own effort did I meet my husband. He is my heart, my soul, my bestfriend and lover. Every day he makes me a better person. Even when we're have a rough time of things - he's focused on making us better as a family. there are times he disappoints me and it does break my heart. but since beginning the journey to optimal - I now can let him know this w/o anger or resentment - just honest open feelings from which we can continue to build and shape our journey in life together. ### BJK77 — 2012-11-15T22:06:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64563>

Love . . . one of my favorite topics. I’m blessed to be married to my high school sweetheart (even though I broke his heart a few times in high school). We had so many people tell us it would never last, that it was just puppy love. We’ve now been married for 14 years and it’s truly been amazing. Y’know how when you first get married everyone kind of warns you that there will be trials and tribulations and you’ll fight over money or whatever?!?! We’ve never had that.

From the moment we said “I do,” it has just felt absolutely right. There was never a doubt in my mind that this was the man for me. I’ve never taken compliments well, always been harder on myself than anyone else would ever be and really can just be a PITA! That has never once stopped my husband from telling me every day that I’m beautiful and he loves me and he feels so lucky to have me in his life. To this day, I still make a joke out of his compliments. I don’t know why, but I struggle to accept the gifts he gives so generously. No matter, he still gives and gives . . . .

I’m here on this forum because of him and my amazing kids. Of course I want to be better for me, but more importantly I want it for them. They’ve all given me so much and I want to return it all tenfold. When I was at my sickest and could barely walk up and down the stairs due to exhaustion and pain, my husband would have carried me without a second thought. My pride kept this from happening, but he was an unbelievably solid rock that I always knew I could count on. He never questioned me, never thought I was just being lazy or was a hypochondriac. He always believed in me, always supported and encouraged me and always, always loved me. When I got upset over spending so much $$ on myself for labs and doctors, he’d tell me over and over again the money meant nothing if he didn’t have me. When I couldn’t go outside and play with the kids because I literally felt like I was dying, he’d come up with activities we could do inside – even lying in bed if that’s all I could do.

I could go on and on. I am blessed beyond belief.

### Inger — 2012-11-15T22:27:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64565>

Wow.. I love this thread.. So much beautiful stuff to read...! BKK7, Gretchen.. all.. thank you for sharing.

> **Jack Kruse;66556 wrote:** I do not want this to be taken wrong.....my intent is to help: Maybe it is not them.......maybe it is the person in the mirror. Why do I point this out? I've been diviorced and I can say that I first had to begin to like then love me.......before I could ever find the love of my life. I firmly believe if I met my wife before I did I would have ruined it because I was a shadow of what I later became. I began sculpting me.........and when I chipped off all the crap........then I had a chance to love. My wife became the exercise to further fix that neural network........and yes I believe my inability to love was caused by the things in my environment both that I could not and could control........when I realized it I changed them and in turn changed me........

This is why I am in no hurry to get a partner. I feel deeply I need to heal first. At times it can be hard to be alone for sure, but it is only temporarely, just for a few hours or a day. I know when I heal first everything else will come. I just know it. I need to sculpt me first into something beautiful as Jack says..lol because I know it is there it just need some work! Last spring I thought one year would be enough, but now I think about 4 years would be just perfect time to heal...lol Then I would be 40 yo. Ha.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T22:30:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64568>

Inger the real truth is there is no time limit.......when youre ready things move in the direction you do not expect.

### Inger — 2012-11-15T22:43:41+0200

There is no way life will ever get boring..

(I still wish me a more boring life.. there is so much I need to figure out! For that I need silence and darkness and some cold too. But I do all I can to make it like that, sometimes I do fail but I am getting better. IDK I figure it must mean I have so much to heal still)

### ealachan — 2012-11-15T23:14:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64574>

> **Jack Kruse;66556 wrote:** I do not want this to be taken wrong.....my intent is to help: Maybe it is not them.......maybe it is the person in the mirror. Why do I point this out? I've been diviorced and I can say that I first had to begin to like then love me.......before I could ever find the love of my life. I firmly believe if I met my wife before I did I would have ruined it because I was a shadow of what I later became. I began sculpting me.........and when I chipped off all the crap........then I had a chance to love. My wife became the exercise to further fix that neural network........and yes I believe my inability to love was caused by the things in my environment both that I could not and could control........when I realized it I changed them and in turn changed me........

I had a similar experience. I actually "met" my husband online in 2001. We talked via email and phone for 5 years before we ever met in person. I changed a LOT as a person in those five years, as did he. I honestly believe that if we had met each other in person in 2001 instead of getting to know each other and learning from each other for those five years, we wouldn't be together now. He said something to me that I specifically remember stopping me in my tracks and really making me look around at how I was living my life at the time. He said "You make me want to be a better person". At the time, I remember my self-absorbed drunk-ass 22 year old self thinking "well what the hell does THAT mean?" but the more I looked around at my life and the people in it, the more I thought about it...the more I realized that HE made ME want to be a better person, too. I started to make those changes slowly, one foot in front of the other, and I have him to thank for that.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T23:31:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64577>

That is a true love story.........Love is a major part of Optimal. It begins in the mirror before it shows up in your partner or on your body.

Oxytocin connects us to other people; oxytocin makes us feel what other people feel. Oxytocin connects our brain back to the giant pharmacy in our own brain. Moreover, it’s easy to cause people’s brains to release oxytocin. Let me show you. Go hug somebody.......

### Destiny — 2012-11-16T03:07:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64614>

When we met, I was 18 and my husband was 21. We fell in love like crazy, he proposed after 3 months and were married in 7 months. I know it was quick but we could not wait and knew we were meant to be together. There were also these funny signs like we were born same day, my mom and his brother were born same day and his mom and my brother were born same day. Coincidence? I don't thinks so!

All of a sudden, we have been married for 32 years. We are still crazy about each other, about our kids and grand kids. Life is good !

### caroline — 2012-11-16T04:02:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64632>

> **Inger;66433 wrote:** Jack, that was so beautiful what you wrote. Very beautiful. This is love.. amazing love..
>
>
>
> He was the sun and moon and the stars.. all in one...
>
> We were intimate.. and I closed my eyes to fully enjoy the amazingness feeling him inside me.. as I opened my eyes again he looked straight into my eyes.. He's eyes and how he looked at me got forever burned into my mind. It is almost 5 years ago now.. but I can recall it anytime as alive as yesterday. These eyes and what they express are what I often see in front of me releasing oxytocin alone. It needs nothing else... the most beautiful love from a spirit so tender and full of beauty.. but at the same time so strong, the most diciplined human beeing I ever met. He had and was everything I was not.. and he still looked at me in a way that goes so much deeper then adoring...
>
> The beautiful memories he gave me the whole year I got to see him almost every week, have carried me through the hardest times since, they keep me sane and raise me up when I think I have lost it

Inger - that is so lovely - it gives me goose bumps. Actually every story here gives me goose bumps and brings tears to my eyes.

### caroline — 2012-11-16T04:14:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64633>

> **Inger;66479 wrote:** @ Caroline,
>
> I love this song too...
>
>
>
> @ Elachan, that is amazing... wow. You are one blessed and happy woman for sure.

That song is so important to me and I can't believe I wrote the title down wrong - the song is - You raise me up. The first time I heard it - Josh Groban sang it and I still remember it so vividly.

The words - you raise me up to more than I can be. And - I am strong when I am on your shoulders - in fact all the words in that song are how I feel about my husband. I loved that man with all my heart and soul and it will never change. He will be in my heart forever.

### caroline — 2012-11-16T04:25:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64637>

> **ealachan;66503 wrote:** I grew up under the impression that I wasn't worth anybody's time. It's a hard habit to break, but little by little, I'm getting there. The older I get, the less of a crap I seem to give about what people think of me, which is really helping. By the time I'm 60, I should have the world by the ass.

I grew up that way too and yes, because of my husbands overwhelming love and devotion to me - I take less crap these days too etc. but it is pretty hard to ignore those growing up years when we should have had love and didn't. But a husband's/wife's endless love gives us such confidence to be all we can be.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-16T05:48:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64656>

This afternoon at the grocery I bought 2 Kevita's. One for me, one for my husband. I opened mine to have before my dinner. Hubby thought we were sharing..... he started reaching for it.... and I told him that his was in the fridge. He looked hurt. I wanted to take it back. Even if I drank all mine and didn't share I know that when he opened his he would let me drink as much as I want. I told him that, and that I knew that. I could tell he was mustering all his manliness not to shed a tear....

It sounds like just a thing about a drink but it was a bigger deal than that.

I love hearing everyone's love stories.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T02:37:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65059>

...mirror neurons allow us to learn by mimicry, experiencing the state of another person, to feel what they are feeling.

when you are bonded with someone,,, when they experience pleasure, you feel their pleasure as your own.

They in turn, feel what you are feeling, creating a feed back loop of ever increasing intensity.

like an optical cavity in a laser gain medium being pumped to produce a population inversion, until...

spontaneous emission, superluminescent light, a crescendo of coherent photons spring forth, released forever from the bonds of their metastable orbits...

_erotic poetry for geeks._

### diane — 2012-11-18T04:04:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65081>

After our first date, I described it as the Seinfeld episode where Jerry meets Janine Garafalo and they were so much alike - "it was like I was dating myself!". There was something about him, and I definitely wanted to know more. When I was leaving, we found out we had the same birthday, which explained some of the similarities. When we were talking, it was like I was looking in the mirror - same sense of humour, same hand movements.

That was 2004, and since then, there are still a lot of ways we're similar, and a lot of ways we're different. I appreciate the differences as much as the similarities. He often balances me out. And drives me crazy - which tells me that the spark is still there, because when they don't drive you crazy, it's usually gone.

The last few months he has had a job where he's gone during the week and home on the weekend. It's been an adjustment, and I really miss him. But then sometimes am worried that I'm getting used to being on my own - and like that I can keep the house cold and dark!  But when he's home it feels right that we're together.

I've been working a lot on myself lately - not just physically but mentally. I know I've changed a lot in the last 6 months, and my perspective on things is much healthier. And I know that he's going through some things lately, so I'm trying to figure out the best way for me to be supportive so he can get to where he needs to.

He has introduced me to so many things that I didn't know before him. He lets me be who I need to be, and do the things I need to do. I can be an individual, and be a couple at the same time. But a little too much individual lately, I think. We're definitely not the sappy type - he makes me laugh, he makes me hot. And that's what's important to me.

### caroline — 2012-11-18T04:10:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65082>

Beautiful Diane - thanks for sharing your story. I was kind of worried that you only had dogs!

### diane — 2012-11-18T04:17:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65084>

Hahaha! My dogs are definitely important to me! They are a passion of mine - which is why I have opened my own dog business. But I definitely have other interests too.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-18T04:22:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65085>

I didn't even know I was looking for the love of my life until I found him. I'd been in a bad, dead-end relationship that I had recently ended and started therapy to deal with it (but I managed to dump the bad guy before therapy - good on me!). My DH and I met when we were both 47, never married, no kids. He had left his job and life in California to move here to find somebody. A mutual friend suggested we meet and within a few weeks, I knew I had met my match. We are compatible in so many ways and share the same values. I had basically given up on meeting someone and the idea of dating was just plain frightening. It's just as well we met when we did; neither of us was really mature enough or ready when we were younger. I like to think it was fate. There were so many little things that had to happen in my life just at the right time and place to put me where I was to meet him. Good things do come to those who wait.

### caroline — 2012-11-18T05:09:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65099>

I don't think we have talked about this.....Does anyone believe in love at first sight?

I can start......I emphatically do - well at second site

The first time - I wasn't paying any attention at all - other things going on \[hockey game/Canada - what can I say\] .......BUT

The second time - I saw him across the street \[He came to find me} and I will never forget that feeling - it was overwhelming. I have forgotten a lot things - but not that!

### Inger — 2012-12-25T03:01:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-68352>

if love also have a lot to do with seeing/recognizing the other(s), that is what I think at least... then it is understandable how it matters huge in what shape our brain is too. ### DarleenMB — 2012-12-25T14:53:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-68391>

It's the little things he does. Â He made me cry today because of this:

It is engraved with both our names and our anniversary date. Â He knows I have a soft spot for Disney's Cinderella. Â Short story ... when I was little I had a Cinderella watch with a pink grosgrain band that came in a plastic "glass slipper." Â I also had a windup toy of Cinderella and the Prince in dance pose ... you wound it up and it played the music from the movie as they spun around the floor. Â Sure wish I still had them.

Even though I ceased believing in "happily ever after" I still get silly when I see "Cinderella" at DisneyWorld. Â so over the years he has given me 4 (now) Cinderella snow globes and a mug. Â He spoils me silly. Â And I never feel like I really deserve it but I'm working on that.

Inger

I totally agree and love what you sayâ€“- a healthier brain allows us to listen better, relate more, open up wider without the need for drama or conflict.Â  Itâ€™s only recently that I understand the wisdom of your words.Â  Â Â 

But please tell me what you make of this.Â  For the last five years I did a lot of internal spiritual and personal growth work; I mean a lot. After my last drama-filled relationship in 2006, I made a commitment that I would never do things the â€œold wayâ€ again.Â  If that meant I had to be â€œaloneâ€ so be it.Â  I decided I would be alone for however long it took to learn a â€œnew wayâ€ of being in relationship. Â For part of this time, I dated.Â  I met scores of men.Â  Many were interested in knowing me better, yet no one seemed quite right, and I would move on quickly.Â  Looking back, I realize I had some pretty severe un-diagnosed adrenal fatigue that I didn'tÂ identify until I discovered Dr. Kruse.Â 

So the connection Iâ€™m making is that no matter how well or how much work I was doing psychologically or spiritually, I needed to heal my brain first and let love flow from there.Â  Iâ€™m not saying I was standing still waiting for Dr. K., just that all the internal work I had done didnâ€™t really begin to sprout until I began to heal my brain.

> Inger
