# Thread: kidneys
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-11-18T16:50:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/post-65132>

[http://www.hindawi.com/journals/ijn/2011/315879/](http://http://www.hindawi.com/journals/ijn/2011/315879/)

The Impact of Fructose on Renal Function and Blood Pressure

> Fructose intake can also induce a central leptin resistance in **rats, leading to increased food intake and the development of visceral obesity \[35\]. Interestingly, rats on a high-fructose diet may not show an increase in overall body weight unless it is associated with diets high in fat \[36\]
>
>
>
> > 2.4. Hypertension and Vascular EffectsFructose intake from added sugars is also associated with elevated blood pressure in humans \[37\], and diets low in added sugar have been reported to lower blood pressure \[38\]. Furthermore, the acute ingestion of fructose (60 g) can increase systolic blood pressure in humans, and this is not seen in subjects given the same dose as glucose \[39\]. In addition, in one study, overweight men were administered 200 g fructose daily for two weeks and sustained a significant increase in ambulatory blood pressure \[26\]. Studies in experimental animals have confirmed that fructose can raise blood pressure. **Interestingly, the increase in blood pressure in response to fructose is better observed by tail cuff measurement as opposed to intra-aortic telemetry \[40, 41\]. However, blood pressure rises in response to sucrose or fructose diet by telemetry during the first hours of feeding \[12, 42\]. The mechanism of hypertension in response to fructose is complex but appears to be mediated by increased sodium absorption in the intestine, by inhibition of systemic endothelial function, and by stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system \[14, 43, 44\] (Figure 1). In addition, some of the effect of fructose to increase blood pressure may be the consequence of fructose-induced increases in intracellular and serum uric acid. First, fructose-induced hypertension in rats is largely ameliorated by lowering uric acid levels \[40\]. Second, in one study in overweight men, the rise in ambulatory blood pressure in response to 200 g of oral fructose per day for two weeks was blocked in those subjects concomitantly administered allopurinol \[26\].
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > > The administration of fructose to rats with reduced renal function (the remnant kidney model) can accelerate the progression of renal disease, resulting in worse proteinuria, glomerulosclerosis, and tubulointerstitial fibrosis \[47\]. Fructose intake also impairs calcium absorption and reduces 25-OH Vitamin D and 1,25-dihydroxy Vitamin D levels in this model
> >
> > **
> >
> > > \[48\]. Furthermore, the intake of sugary soft drinks in humans is associated with increased prevalence of albuminuria \[49\]. Our group has also recently administered a low-fructose diet to subjects with stable chronic kidney disease for a period of 6 weeks. While we observed no effect on renal function during the period of the study, we did observe a reduction in inflammatory markers and a fall in blood pressure in subjects with the “dipper” physiology (i.e., those subjects whose blood pressure spontaneously falls at night during sleep)**
>
> > > \[49\]. Clearly further studies are needed to determine if limiting added sugars may benefit subjects with kidney disease.
> >
> >
> >
> > 2.6. Role of Natural FruitsWhile much work has focused on fructose as driving obesity, insulin resistance, and cardiorenal disease, not all fructose sources may be the same. Thus, natural fruits also are rich in antioxidants, ascorbate, polyphenols, potassium, and fiber that may counter the effects of fructose \[13, 50\]. Indeed, Forman et al. \[51\] reported that fructose intake did not correlate with elevated blood pressure in a population in which much of the fructose intake was from fruit, whereas Jalal et al. \[37\] found a strong association of fructose intake with blood pressure when the fructose content from natural fruits was excluded.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Fructose effects are also potentiated by glucose \[61, 62\], and most studies have only examined fructose alone. Most studies also show, both in animals and humans, that the effects of fructose are greater on postprandial lipids, fatty liver, and insulin resistance rather than on weight gain per se. Indeed, the effects of fructose may be more on inducing leptin resistance, and it may require the addition of high-fat diet to show the weight gain

### chocolate — 2012-11-18T17:16:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/post-65136>

> Dietary fructose, salt absorption and hypertension in metabolic syndrome: towards a new paradigm.
>
> Soleimani M.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Center on Genetics of Transport and Epithelial Biology, Department of Medicine, University of Cincinnati, OH 45267-0585, USA. [manoocher.soleimani@uc.edu](mailto:manoocher.soleimani@uc.edu)
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The worldwide increase in the incidence of metabolic syndrome correlates with marked increase in total fructose intake in the form of high-fructose corn syrup, beverage and table sugar. **Increased dietary fructose intake in rodents has been shown to recapitulate many aspects of metabolic syndrome by causing hypertension, insulin resistance and hyperlipidaemia. Recent studies demonstrated that increased dietary fructose intake stimulates salt absorption in the small intestine and kidney tubules, resulting in a state of salt overload and thus causing hypertension. The absorption of salt (sodium and chloride) in the small intestine is predominantly mediated via the chloride/base exchangers DRA (Down Regulated in Adenoma) (SLC26A3) and PAT1 (Putative Anion Transporter 1) (SLC26A6), and the Na(+) /H(+) exchanger NHE3 (Sodium Hydrogen Exchanger3) (SLC9A3). PAT1 and NHE3 also co-localize on the apical membrane of kidney proximal tubule. Luminal fructose stimulated salt absorption in the jejunum and kidney tubules, responses that were significantly diminished in PAT1 null mice. These studies further demonstrated that Glut5 (SLC2A5) is the major fructose-absorbing transporter in the small intestine (and kidney proximal tubule) and plays an essential role in the systemic homeostasis of fructose. Increased dietary fructose intake for several weeks upregulated the expression of NHE3, PAT1 and Glut5 in the intestine and resulted in hypertension in wild-type mice, a response that was almost abolished in PAT1 null mice and abrogated in Glut5 null mice. This article will discuss the interaction of Glut5 with salt-absorbing transporters and review the role of dietary fructose in enhanced salt absorption in intestine and kidney as it relates to the pathogenesis of hypertension in metabolic syndrome.**

pretty interesting

### chocolate — 2012-11-18T17:25:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/post-65138>

> Fructose consumption and the risk of kidney stones.
>
> Taylor EN, Curhan GC.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Renal Division and Channing Laboratory, Department of Medicine, Brigham and Women's Hospital, Harvard Medical School, Boston, Massachusetts 02115, USA. [entaylor@partners.org](mailto:entaylor@partners.org)
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Fructose consumption has markedly increased over the past decades. This intake may increase the urinary excretion of calcium, oxalate, uric acid, and other factors associated with kidney stone risk. We prospectively examined the relationship between fructose intake and incident kidney stones in the Nurses' Health Study I (NHS I) (93,730 older women), the Nurses' Health Study II (NHS II) (101,824 younger women), and the Health Professionals Follow-up Study (45,984 men). Food frequency questionnaires were used to assess free fructose and sucrose intake every 4 years. Total-fructose intake was calculated as free fructose plus half the intake of sucrose, and expressed as percentage of total energy. Cox proportional hazard regressions were adjusted for age, body mass index (BMI), thiazide use, caloric intake, and other dietary factors. We documented 4902 incident kidney stones during a combined 48 years of follow-up. **The multivariate relative risks of kidney stones significantly increased for participants in the highest compared to the lowest quintile of total-fructose intake for all three study groups. Free-fructose intake was also associated with increased risk. Non-fructose carbohydrates were not associated with increased risk in any cohort. Our study suggests that fructose intake is independently associated with an increased risk of incident kidney stones.**

Fructose is just not so good. I had to quit eating apples.

### chocolate — 2012-11-18T17:30:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/post-65139>

> Characteristic clinical and biochemical profile of recurrent calcium-oxalate nephrolithiasis in patients with metabolic syndrome.
>
> Rendina D, De Filippo G, Zampa G, Muscariello R, Mossetti G, Strazzullo P.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Departament of Radiology, Hospital Universitario Insular de Gran Canaria, Spain. [domenico.rendina@unina.it](mailto:domenico.rendina@unina.it)
>
> Abstract
>
> BACKGROUND:
>
>
>
> Metabolic syndrome is a risk factor for nephrolithiasis. This study was performed to evaluate the clinical and biochemical profile of calcium-oxalate nephrolithiasis in stone formers with metabolic syndrome.
>
> METHODS:
>
>
>
> A total of 526 recurrent stone formers, 184 of them with metabolic syndrome, and 214 controls were examined on a free diet and after a sodium-restricted diet (sodium intake < 100 mmol/24 h).
>
> RESULTS:
>
>
>
> On free diet, stone formers with metabolic syndrome showed higher sodium excretion \[mean (95% confidence interval), 196 (176-218) vs 160 (150-168) mmol/24 h; P < 0.01\] and lower citrate excretion \[2.23 (1.99-2.58) vs 2.84 (2.51-3.17) mmol/24 h; P < 0.01\] compared to controls, whereas stone formers without metabolic syndrome showed higher calcium and oxalate excretion \[5.43 (5.01-5.82) vs 3.58 (2.84-4.19) and 0.34 (0.32-0.36) vs 0.26 (0.20-0.31)m mmol/24 h for calcium and oxalate, respectively; P < 0.01\] and lower citrate excretion \[2.18 (1.98-2.38) vs 2.84 (2.51-3.17) mmol/24 h; P < 0.01\] compared to controls. The ion activity product of urinary calcium-oxalate salts was similar between stone formers with and without metabolic syndrome \[1.41 (1.31-1.59) vs 1.40 (1.35-1.45); P > 0.05\]. After the test diet, this index was lower in diet-compliant stone formers with metabolic syndrome compared to diet-compliant stone formers without metabolic syndrome \[1.15 (1.10-1.21) vs 1.39 (1.31-1.45); P < 0.01\].
>
> CONCLUSIONS:
>
>
>
> **The biochemical profiles and responses to the sodium-restricted diet were significantly different between stone formers with metabolic syndrome and those without. Dietary habits play a central role in the pathogenesis of nephrolithiasis in stone formers with metabolic syndrome.**

Here, they are blaming the salt instead of the fructose.

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-18T17:01:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-without-twinkies.4923/post-65133>

Is anyone else bothered by the amount of media attention this is getting? My FB page has been blowing up all week. Executive chefs are posting Twinkie recipes. The news stations seem to have a story every newscast about empty shelves; sellers on Ebay are getting $100 a box.

This was a business. The union said no to compromise and the company said, well fine, then we have to close. Don't want to negotiate? Then you don't have a job, 'cause we just can't afford it anymore.

I think the bigger issue is why....did their healthcare costs rise? Did their manufacturing costs rise? Did they anticipate higher costs and realized they just couldn't keep up? What does this say about unions and the power they have over American companies?

No one seems to care about the reasons WHY this business failed.

All they want is their d\*mn Twinkies. I never liked 'em anyway. Now Ding Dongs, that's a whole 'nother story.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-18T18:09:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-without-twinkies.4923/post-65150>

Definitely the nostalgia thing. Those of us who grew up in the 60s and 70s found Hostess products in our lunch boxes quite often. I was more fond of Suzy Q's rather than twinkies. I had a twinkie a few years ago when I saw them in a convenience store at the end of a long ride. I was disappointed - it didn't taste anywhere near as good as I had remembered. Kind of stale and icky. Either my tastes changed or quality went downhill (or maybe both?).

Much of the commentary I've heard about the decline of the business focused on their inability to adapt to changing consumer tastes & preferences. I think they could have capitalized on parents' fond memories while innovating and come up with a product that was perceived to be more healthy. Instead, they were stuck in a time warp, making the same old product for years on end.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-19T02:15:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-without-twinkies.4923/post-65241>

I copied the following post from another forum I sometimes check in on. I figured if I put in the link no one would bother to read it:

> Hostess Brands/Interstate Brands Business Practices from a person who lived it (My mom)
>
>
>
> From 2001 to 2004, my mom worked for Interstate Brands (aka Hostess Brands) at their facility on Route 23 in Wayne, NJ. My mom like many of her co-workers were used as part-time on call employees. She was promised regular hours and full time status but never received such. Instead my dad, my brother, or I would take my mom to work at 2 am or 4 pm depending on when they called her in.
>
>
>
> My mother explained that she and her fellow employees were expected to transfer hot cakes as they came out on assembly line from the ovens with gloved hands onto other smaller lines where the cakes were to be shrink-wrapped by automatic machines. Bad cakes were to be discarded and a high quality product was to be wrapped up and delivered to happy consumers. Such was not the case.
>
>
>
> My mom, having worked in quality control position before, knew something was amiss the first week she began working there. She shook her head and realized this company would not last, not with what they were doing on the production lines.
>
>
>
> Her and her group working a specific line (be it Twinkies, Yodels, Apple Pies, or HoHos) could transfer 300-600 cakes a minute onto the packing lines, but the baking machines were set to 1,000 cakes a minute leading to incredible waste. My mom and her workers tried hard as possible to keep up with his hectic pace but to no avail, 100's of cakes were lost a minute. You would think that someone would find an optimal speed to let the ovens produce just enough that workers can quality check the product and package at a comfortable pace. But not here.
>
>
>
> My mom described at the end of her shift, she would find 10 large bins (5'x10'x5') packed full with cakes on her line. Employees were encourage to consume as much as possible at work but not to take any home.
>
>
>
> Management looked the other way when it came to waste and when my mom and fellow union workers complained about aches and pains resulting from this rapid, ridiculous pace, the machines were slowed down for the union inspection and then ramped up several days later.
>
>
>
> Production mattered more than anything, including employee well being, waste of raw material, and quality of product. The more the managers produced and packed, never mind the waste or employee injuries/burns, the more managers were rewarded.
>
>
>
> My mom would often come home and consume Tylenol and painkillers to ease the pain. She was also injured twice on the job, a severe high ankle sprain and tearing of the muscles on her right hand and thumb which still bother her to this day and limit mobility.
>
>
>
> In my opinion, this corporation knowingly and willfully drove this company into the ground on the backs of their workers and in spite of worker protest. Having set the building ablaze the robber-barons/arsonists are grabbing as many valuables as they can, all the while screaming fire, fire and pointing fingers, as they run out of building first leaving others to fend for themselves.

Again, this is not MY post, I simply copied it from another forum I belong to to give another perspective.

### chocolate — 2012-11-19T04:28:22+0200

> Her and her group working a specific line (be it Twinkies, Yodels, Apple Pies, or HoHos) could transfer 300-600 cakes a minute onto the packing lines, but the baking machines were set to 1,000 cakes a minute leading to incredible waste.

I used to work at a pizza parlor and we would make a thousand dollars worth of pizza with 9 dollars worth of dough. Wasting dough was no big deal. I don't know if the spent cakes were fed to pigs to finish them or not, but that's what happens with the bread pulls at grocery stores. (It makes them sick to raise them on it. They start peeing weird and get stiff joints and can hardly walk)

I also worked as a cake decorator and would do pulls while the first batch was in the oven in a small grocery store and the donut fryer was working. The bread was to go into a locked dumpster but I handed it off to a well recognized character actor that fed street people and birds with it years ago. The stuff is garbage and we all know it. You can feed broilers the stuff, also. They are cruddy quality meat, but... you are what you eat.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T21:32:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/us-imports-radioactive-food-unfit-to-eat.4925/post-65199>

just some perspective on the numbers, as North America normally uses REM instead of Bq, both measurements are derived to represent actual ionization damage, instead of total 'energy'. The idea being you only care about the energy that gets deposited in the body.

A Becquerel is a very tiny measurement. One banana has about 15 Bq due mostly from potassium40,

Brazil nuts are higher due to radium, at about 200Bq per pound.

What isn't fully taken into account with Bq or REM is the 'biological half-life" or the human body's constant homeostasis, any excess absorbed compensated by the elimination of an equal amount. The faster turn over in the body, the reduced Biological Half life, and reduced dose.

From Fukashima, the greatest risk would be in radioactive Iodine, for two reasons. One, iodine is a major fission by product, and makes up a large component of the total releases from the fuel failures, secondly.. as most people are deficient in iodine,it tends to be readily absorbed and concentrated in the thyroid.

This is why the KI (potassium Iodide) pills where in such high demand at the time. the idea is to top yourself up with 'clean' iodine, to reduce the uptake of potentially contaminated environmental iodine.

### Thor — 2012-11-19T00:22:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/us-imports-radioactive-food-unfit-to-eat.4925/post-65225>

.

Actually this is serious - several 100 fish caught in August 2012 bore the highest levels of radioactive particles seen to date—25,000 Becquerels per kilogram! that's scary....

Radioactive cesium (what's in the fish) decays by emitting what's known as a beta radiation, a negatively charged particle that is easily blocked by metal, plastic or wood—but not skin. In particular, ingesting beta-emitting radioactive elements is "a concern," according to the NRC. "Beta particles released directly to living tissue can cause damage at the molecular level, which can disrupt cell function." Plus, beta particles are small enough to travel far in the body, causing damage far and wide.

Radioactive cesium Inhibits potassium current in myocardial cells, increases intracellular pH may affect erythrocytes, liver, and central nervous system.

Symptoms of Cesium Toxicity:

Fatigue, muscle weakness, palpitations and arrhythmia

_Therapeutic Drug Monitoring. 2003 Feb;25(1):114-6_

# Thread: Prostate - PSA and Cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2012-11-18T21:52:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/post-65203>

I'm one of those guys whose age and family history makes me a likely candidate to get prostate cancer. I had a biopsy at age 53 that was negative, but have had some additional spikes in PSA the last few years, plus BPH with resulting urinary problems. In all cases, treatment with antibiotics relieved BPH symptoms and lowered PSA.

But I need a better alternative to antibiotics because when I take them, they kill off gut flora and I start having cravings again and weight loss stops. I tried colloidal silver instead of antibiotics and have been gaining weight.

I have listened to Jack's Prostate Webinar which was very informative. I was already taking at least half of the recommended supplements and ordered several additional ones mentioned in the webinar. Maybe that will be enough, maybe not.

I do still have reservations about not getting a PSA exam. I don't trust a government sponsored committee (USPSTF) to make medical decisions for me. As I understand the argument, even if an older man has prostate cancer, something else will most likely kill him before the cancer does. At 63, I probably have about 20 years still left in me. If cancer is detected when I'm 65, I don't want the government telling me that they no longer pay for treatment, which is what I think this recommendation by USPSTF is leading up to.

The American Urological Association (AUA) has rejected the USPSTF recommendation against PSA testing. Here's the statement from AUA Presdent Suhil S. Lacy, MD.

The AUA (American Urological Association) is outraged at the USPSTFâ€™s failure to amend its recommendations on prostate cancer testing to more adequately reflect the benefits of the prostate- specific antigen (PSA) test in the diagnosis of prostate cancer.

It is inappropriate and irresponsible to issue a blanket statement against PSA testing, particularly for at-risk

populations, such as African American men. Men who are in good health and have more than a 10-15 year life expectancy should have the choice to be tested and not discouraged from doing so.

There is strong evidence that PSA testing saves lives. The randomized trials used by the USPSTF do, in fact, show a benefit to patients. The PLCO Trial, imperfect by the pre-screening contamination of the control arm, nonetheless showed that, in a group of young men with no comorbidities, there was a significant reduction of prostate cancer death rates after a median follow- up of seven years (JCO 2011;29:355-361).

Additionally, the Göteborg Trial also showed a substantial 44 percent relative risk reduction in prostate cancer mortality occurring in men 50-64 years of age after a median of 14 years.

Importantly, the risk reduction occurred in a setting where many of the patients were not aggressively treated for prostate cancer, indicating that the harms of PSA-based screening can, in fact, be minimized by good clinical practice (Lancet Oncol 2010;11:725-732).

Furthermore, we have seen a 40 percent reduction in prostate cancer- specific mortality in the United States over the most recent 20 years of PSA-based screening. This has occurred without substantial change in how men with prostate cancer were treated (primarily with surgery and radiation therapy).

Models have suggested that more than 50 percent of this reduction is due to early detection (Cancer Cases Control 2008;19:175-181). Additionally, updated data from the European Randomized Study for the Screening of Prostate Cancer (ERSPC) has demonstrated that there is a 21 percent risk reduction in prostate cancer related death associated with screening (up to 29 percent after accounting for non-compliance). The number of cancers that would need to be detected to prevent one death has now dropped to 37.

Rather than instruct primary care physicians to discourage men from having a PSA test, the Task Force should instead focus on how best to educate primary care physicians regarding targeted screening and how to counsel patients about their prostate cancer risk.

The PSA test has allowed us to move beyond a time when men presented with high-grade, metastatic disease for which there were little or no treatment options other than palliative care.

In its earliest stages, most prostate cancers cause no symptoms; to say that only men with symptoms of prostate cancer should be tested will potentially result in a return to such a time.

Disparaging the PSA test when newer tests and diagnostics are not yet widely available does a great disservice to American men.[http://epubs.democratprinting.com/publication/?i=116007](http://http://epubs.democratprinting.com/publication/?i=116007)

The majority of the (Summer/Fall, 2012) QUEST, published by [Dr William J Catalona](http://http://www.drcatalona.com/) and the Urological Research Foundation, addresses the problems with the USPSTF recommendation. The article on p 8 discusses the use of PSA, % free PSA, and Phi score for detecting prostate cancer. According to this article, the diagnostic accuracy of Total PSA is 55%, % Free PSA is 65% and Phi is about 75%. Phi hasn't been approved by FDA in US, but is approved in Europe.

I found the % Free PSA lab earlier this year when I was looking for the best price for a PSA lab. It wasn't that much more expensive so I bought it and was glad I did. My PSA was elevated, but my % Free PSA indicated I had less than a 10% chance of cancer and the high PSA was most likely from inflammation.

So I'm questioning everything. The AUA makes a good case for dismissing the findings of the USPSTF. On the other hand, I guess the income of some members of the AUA would be effected if the PSA test is not used as a diagnostic tool.

For me, I will continue to have a yearly PSA including %Free and Phi when it becomes available. Even a biopsy, although not a fun day at the beach, is not that bad. When I had mine done, the doc used Novocain to dull the pain. He did it in his office and I drove home about a half hour after it was over. So PSA and biopsy if necessary.

The problem for me is choosing what to do if biopsy shows cancer. My preferred treatment, if it should become necessary, is proton beam treatment, something rarely presented as an option. Quite expensive, but it is painless and seems to have the smallest incidence of side effects. More info on proton beam can be found [here](http://http://www.protonbob.com/proton-treatment-homepage.asp).

Thoughts? Any other "older" guys going through this? If so, what is your plan? If you already met the robot, what were the side effects?

### Thor — 2012-11-19T00:24:42+0200

### colleencoble — 2012-11-19T04:37:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/post-65260>

A routine PSA caught the huge spike in my husband's PSA (from 1.5 to 9) and a biopsy showed VERY aggressive prostate cancer. By the time he had surgery it had moved from the prostate to the seminal vesicles. Right now he's cancer free but there is a 30% chance it could come back. I tell every guy I know to make sure they have a baseline and get tested. It could save their life. Yes, it can go up for something other than cancer just like the CA-125 can go up for things other than ovarian cancer. But it's the best screening tool we have right now. At least you can get tested further to find out what is the cause. And please, the biopsy is not that bad. I was with Dave in the room when he got his and he didn't even wince. We girls get poked, prodded and squashed every year.

I will never ever quit urging guys to get checked. I lost a dear friend to prostate cancer the same year Dave was diagnosed. When it strikes younger men, it is deadly.

### Thor — 2012-11-19T07:28:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/post-65280>

.

Colleencoble - did you read my post above? PSA test has nothing to do with cancer. ugh  sad that doctors brain wash people.....

Even if you have cancer there is only a 25% or less chance the biopsy will find it and if the needle does catch cancer cells...there is something you should ask your urologist about:

**"NEEDLE TRACK SEEDING".**

It will be a VERY uncomfortable subject for the doc...since there is a considerable chance the biopsy needle will drag cancer cells out of the prostatic capsule and "seed" them into the rest of your body. Consider the information that follows:

"In The New England Journal of medicine Dr. Fritz Schroder and Dr. Ries Krause from the Erasmus Medical Center in Rotterdam, Netherlands were asked if prostate biopsies really save lives. Their answer was, "No."

Only 1 out 4 of these biopsies was able to even detect cancer. The reason for this is: The way a prostate biospy is performed is a needle is inserted into the prostate gland. Then, very small sections of prostate tissue are extracted from 6 - 45 different areas within the prostate gland.

But, the needle may not even go where a cancer exists. Some call it the "needle in a haystack search".

> **colleencoble;67267 wrote:** A routine PSA caught the huge spike in my husband's PSA (from 1.5 to 9) and a biopsy showed VERY aggressive prostate cancer. By the time he had surgery it had moved from the prostate to the seminal vesicles. Right now he's cancer free but there is a 30% chance it could come back. I tell every guy I know to make sure they have a baseline and get tested. It could save their life. Yes, it can go up for something other than cancer just like the CA-125 can go up for things other than ovarian cancer. But it's the best screening tool we have right now. At least you can get tested further to find out what is the cause. And please, the biopsy is not that bad. I was with Dave in the room when he got his and he didn't even wince. We girls get poked, prodded and squashed every year.

Did his doctors mention proton beam as a treatment option? My guess is no since they don't get to do surgery.

### colleencoble — 2012-11-19T17:12:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/post-65346>

Dave's biopsy showed cancer in ALL quadrants. And we never would have known it was there if not for the PSA.

Yes we discussed proton beam. We have one of those here in Indiana. But Dave's cancer was very aggressive. We just wanted it OUT and gone.

### Thor — 2012-11-19T19:22:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/post-65382>

.

Removal of the prostate gland (prostatectomy) means you're impotent and will never have sex again - thats a bit extreme for treatment

if it moved into the testicles then he must have needed to be castrated too? That's really a sex change operation in the end.

that's scary

### colleencoble — 2012-11-19T19:35:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/post-65384>

It wasn't nearly as scary as him dying! And the shots work as he's regaining some of that. So the no sex thing is not true.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T23:44:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-the-state-pays-for-your-healthcare.4933/post-65213>

[Should Canada introduce a smoker's licence](http://http://www.besthealthmag.ca/blog/post/debate-should-canada-introduce-a-smokers-license)

Chapman's recommendation is for a type of smart card licence which would be designed to limit access to tobacco products. Proposed details include:

\- an annual cost (depending on the number of cigarettes smoked),

\- reissue each year,

\- daily limits for the number of cigarettes bought, and

\- completion of a test of knowledge about the health risks of smoking.

for those with no sympathy for smokers... replace tobacco with your personal lifestyle choices.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-19T05:41:19+0200

> for those with no sympathy for smokers... replace tobacco with your personal lifestyle choices.

I have never used tobacco, but I think the medical industry is blaming it for a lot more than it is actually guilty of.

Although this is my very first post, I've been visiting this site daily for one year today. It's been the most wonderful, healthy, hopeful year! I read every post and comment, and implemented each protocol as it was introduced--Leptin Reset, supplements, CT, EpiPaleo. I have lost 100 pounds, resolved pain in my hips and knees that had almost immobilized me, improved my memory and concentration, and regained my joie de vivre. # Thread: What Doctor Remembers This ??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-doctor-remembers-this.4937/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-19T07:47:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-doctor-remembers-this.4937/post-65283>

.

I wonder how many Doctors remember or even heard of "banana babies?" Sensitivity to gluten based food was first noticed during the 1930's. A bright Doctor thought gluten based foods must be the source of certain sick children showing up for office visits. Children were taken off all food & given only Bananas and Buttermilk. Interesting history in medicine from so long ago. Doctors started to see carbs could make kids sick.

**Banana Babies**

The parents were asked to leave their child at the hospital for six months. (Yes, 6 months!) While at the hospital, the children were fed nothing but bananas. Thatâ€™s right. Bananas and only bananas for six months. Some of them got better, others didnâ€™t make it. They became known as the banana babies.

How the Gluten-Free Diet Began

It was a Greek scientist who first described this disease of the gut and gave it a name, but it wasnâ€™t until after World War II that the cause of the problem was found. A pediatrician in the Netherlands noticed the mortality rate among children affected by celiac disease dropped from more than 35% to almost 0% during the war. At the time, there was a shortage of wheat so corn flour was used instead. When the war was over, and wheat was used once again, the doctor observed that the mortality rate rose to its previous height. It was later discovered that gluten was the offending part of the grain and was also found in rye and barley.

### Michael — 2012-11-19T09:01:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-doctor-remembers-this.4937/post-65289>

> **Thor;67290 wrote:** .
>
> I wonder how many Doctors remember or even heard of "banana babies?" Sensitivity to gluten based food was first noticed during the 1930's. A bright Doctor thought gluten based foods must be the source of certain sick children showing up for office visits. Children were taken off all food & given only Bananas and Buttermilk. Interesting history in medicine from so long ago. Doctors started to see carbs could make kids sick. ...

I wonder if this would be Dr. Sidney Haas, upon whose work the Specific Carbohydrate Diet (SCD) was based, Thor.

[http://scdiet.com/haas.html](http://http://scdiet.com/haas.html)

The date would be about right, and he certainly did use bananas, although I don't think that was all everyone had.

My understanding is that, before Haas found what he did, the only treatment for coeliacs was to put them on a ketogenic diet. (Just as this was the treatment for diabetes before insulin, and for epilepsy before anti-seizure medications.)

I think in those days low carb and ketogenic diets were not thought of as anything very strange or dangerous at all and the kind of thing a doctor might have tried with a patient. They obviously found coeliacs did better on them. I think Haas observed almost by accident, but obviously because he was keeping his eyes open, that people (possibly babies) who would be ill with bread seemed OK with bananas. After this, he began experimenting with different foods. What I believe Haas thought was that although a ketogenic diet would work, it must be a bit boring for people; so what he would do was find the carbohydrates they _could handle.

His conclusion was that the problem was some people had difficulty, at least temporarily, in digesting disaccharides and polysaccharides. This was why he thought the bananas were OK - because most of the sugar in fruit is in the form of monosaccharides (glucose and fructose). The bananas had, of course, to be fully ripe.

At this time no-one knew anything about gluten as a cause.

Interestingly, Elaine Gottschall, who developed and popularised the SCD, based on Haas's work, felt that people fixing on gluten as the problem was actually mistaken, and although it was often part of the problem it wasn't the sole factor. She felt that Haas was right to conclude that some people are better off just off _all_ starchy foods and all table sugar (sucrose) for extended periods.

I'm inclined to think there might be something in what she says. I understand that large numbers of people who are diagnosed as coeliac and put on "gluten-free" diets don't get much healing and relief of their symptoms, although some do. Maybe for people with a damaged gut large amounts of rice and tortillas and all those cakes and biscuits made from mixed gluten-free flours and lashings of sugar really isn't the best thing.

The GAPS Diet is based on the SCD and is similar, but adds a lot of bone-broth and fermented foods and other foods thought to have gut-healing properties.

Interestingly, and totally independently of the SCD/GAPS, but getting to rather the same place, a lady from New Zealand came up with a low-starch diet that's now used with IBS patients and for arthritic pain:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-IBS-Low-Starch-Diet-hazardous/dp/0091912865/](http://http://www.amazon.com/The-IBS-Low-Starch-Diet-hazardous/dp/0091912865/)

It's difficult not_ to believe that carbohydrate, particularly starch, isn't problematic for many people. Not to say that everyone should avoid them at all times and in places, but do carbohydrates really deserve the halo they've been given in very recent times?

I think part of the problem is the demonization of fat that began with the broken diet-heart hypothesis. (And this is why Gary Taubes puts that whole story at the front of his book.) Once you start looking askance at fat, what's left to eat? There's only three macronutrients, and most people (outside low carb circles) don't eat more than ten to fifteen per cent of calories as protein. That really only leaves carbohydrate as your major source of calories.

### Optimalbound — 2012-11-19T11:57:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-doctor-remembers-this.4937/post-65293>

> **Michael;67296 wrote:**
>
> It's difficult _not_ to believe that carbohydrate, particularly starch, isn't problematic for many people.

It's difficult not to believe that there isn't a negation too many in that sentence.

# Thread: Scotland's oldest house
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-19T11:17:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/post-65290>

A new discovery - what's probably the oldest dwelling in Scotland from 10,000 years ago.

This would be what "the movement" would call Paleolithic, but archaeologists Mesolithic. IOW, it pre-dates farming, but is also characterized (among other things) by particular lithic material used for smaller, fleeter game. (They would have the bow and arrow.)

Plenty of charred hazelnut shells and, people will not be surprised to learn:

> Proximity to the rivers would have allowed its occupants to exploit the abundant aquatic life.

The dwelling was probably occupied seasonally.

[http://www.scotsman.com/news/environment/archaeologists-unearth-stone-age-dwelling-on-the-banks-the-of-new-forth-crossing-1-2643154](http://http://www.scotsman.com/news/environment/archaeologists-unearth-stone-age-dwelling-on-the-banks-the-of-new-forth-crossing-1-2643154)

### caroline — 2012-11-19T11:52:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/post-65292>

so interesting - thanks Michael. I have always wanted to go to Scotland - It is so close to you - I suppose you have gone many times. My Husband was born in Nova Scotia and his ancestors were from Scotland.

### Michael — 2012-11-19T13:58:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/post-65298>

I've only been to Scotland once, and then no farther north than Edinburgh.

Some of the scenery is spectacularly beautiful. I sometimes think that I would like to spend a long stretch of time living somewhere quite remote. The southern part of England has its charm - Sussex is actually one of the most wooded counties and has some beautiful flint buildings - but it's just too crowded. You notice this driving north in England - the further you go, the more everything opens out. And you can be sitting in an urban conurbation in the North Country and still be in somewhere like the Peak District in minutes.

Here you're never far off the beaten track. Go to cross a country lane, and there's probably a car coming along.

I don't think it's so much a natural/man-made distinction, as the sheer shoddy quality of the way we build and the way we live in the landscape nowadays. That and the sheer overtcrowding.

I think the west coast of Ireland is probably as remote, unspoiled, and spectacular as Scotland.

A woman said to me once that she wished that she could just be in a cottage and go for long walks and be with me, which I suppose was a nice thing to say to someone. I don't think it was a thought that lasted very long, though.

# Thread: The hand
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-19T12:21:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65294>

Here's someone else who isn't really known in the paleo movement, but whose work, it seems to me, deserves attention: Raymond Tallis. I did see him mentioned once at MDA, but that would have been a rare encounter.

Tallis, a former professor of medicine, now retired to become a full-time writer, is a useful corrective to the "evolutionary psychology" which is quite popular in the movement. This often seems to offer some very simplistic, and occasionally ludicrous, explanations of phenomena in human life. Part of the problem seems to be that people fail to understand the importance of self-consciousness and misdescribe human life in terms that would be equally applicable to animal life. Tallis says this is not Darwinism but Darwinitis - and something the great man himself was generally free of. For example, Darwin only says that facial expressions have their _origin in those observable in animals. And, of course, everything is not simply equal to its origin - or there would be no change in life.

Tallis has an interesting explanation for self-consciousness, which whether it be right or not is worth reading. He thinks the human hand is key here. He says that a large brain is a necessary but not a sufficient criterion for human mental life. What is needed is a particular mode of consciousness - namely self-consciousness. Some primates can pass into this state episodically, but they don't live in it. The human hand, Tallis thinks, enables that barrier to be crossed, because it creates a kind of reflexive awareness. He goes into quite a lot of anatomical detail, noting that humans are the only creatures with a _fully_ opposable thumb, also that the fingers can move independently (not totally unique but important), and much else. Furthermore, once you have an upright posture, the hand can come into relation with the eyes. Also, with the head up from ground smell becomes less important, so it has less chance to overwhelm awareness of the hand-eye relation.

He thinks pointing_ is of particular importance.

This is obviously a ridiculously quick and bare sketch of some important ideas, but if anyone thinks any of this may be of interest to them, one place to start might be looking at the Amazon reviews of his book Michelangelo's Finger:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Michelangelos-Finger-Exploration-Everyday-Transcendence/dp/1848871201](http://http://www.amazon.co.uk/Michelangelos-Finger-Exploration-Everyday-Transcendence/dp/1848871201)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65301>

Interesting topic...

I would posit that consciousness would have to stem more from the reaction to stimulus, rather than the stimulus itself.

Wouldn't proprioception, pain, hearing, or any other sense have done this? why the emphasis on vision feedback awareness?

Also, lets not forget the importance of head balance... Uprightness came first, then the sophisticated use of the hand.

What allows for the brain chemistry? (I think we have a pretty good idea).

Were we upright before or after we went to water for food. Or more likely that would have been the transition. Just like the other swamp/aquatic primates.

Can bonobos get into this limited awareness more frequently than less upright primates?

Wish I had more time to look into this today...

### Michael — 2012-11-19T15:03:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65309>

> **prAna303;67303 wrote:** Come to think about this post... [http://blog.exuberantanimal.com/smart-hand/](http://http://blog.exuberantanimal.com/smart-hand/)

Oh, yeah - Frank Forencich's site. He would be the sort of person to discuss that sort of thing. I do get his RSS feed; but I haven't seen this. Must have a look.

### Michael — 2012-11-19T15:17:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65315>

> **Tom;67318 wrote:** Wouldn't proprioception ...

It's feedback through touch that he sees as important. But specifically through the hand, which with us allows different grips and has a lot of nerves in. The toes, for example, might be "aware" of the sides of each other, but it seems to him that there might be relational hierarchies within the hand - thumb to fingers and so on. I think he thinks the relation of the eyes to the hand, which is possible in an upright posture, reinforces that, but that it begins with the hand itself. But it's a quite complex discussion and I'm only part-way through and could be introducing misunderstandings of my own.

He doesn't think animals _really_ have tool-use, despite the way people sometimes speak, because they give no sign that they have any grasp of underlying concepts related to tool use, don't re-purpose an object used in one context into another, and while they can learn seem not to teach. He thinks the stick or stone or whatever is totally embedded in its context and probably to them more like an extension of their own body than a tool. He also makes the interesting suggestion that since a tool proper has this separate and rather abstract nature it is itself something like a "sign". Hence we may have one of the roots of language here.

# Thread: Low fruit, and bones to gnaw on
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2012-11-19T17:23:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65352>

I've been reading (upon my wife's request) Joe Dispenza's book, "Breaking the Habits of Being Yourself" and it has some good stuff. I have really seen myself and some long-held habits that I engage in with my thinking.

This book, along with Bruce Lipton's, "Biology of Belief", left me with the impression (and especially my wife and her friends) that my thoughts and beliefs control ALL my bodily processes - hormones, peptides, etc.) and say next to nothing about all the dozens of life forms that take up residence inside us and wreak havoc, or the poor dietary decisions we humans make.

But those authors kinda leave a lot of meat on the bone when it comes to esoteric hormones, neuropeptides, etc. It addresses low-hanging fruit like cortisol and oxytocin (stress and sex, both big book sellers) and does not talk much about all the other body processes that are affected by the environment and not so directly by your thoughts.

Having thoughts of a lion chasing you is easy to overcome (relatively) and thus one can lower their cortisol. TGF-beta? Not so much.

She and her friends are all about controlling their bodies with their minds and think they can do things that are perhaps outside the biological parameters of humans. I see my woman in her seventh year of chronic nasal drippage and the constant coughing to clear it. And her Pe/E ratio? Terrible. Thyroid/Cortisol? Trashed. The few tests I've seen of her's are all low, and in the bad way. I want to help so bad it hurts. I've offered but she announced that she is going to "walk her talk" and I totally respect that. I hope she makes it.

But what about a spider or tick bite, or a toxin from a reef fish? Or, what about leptin, gherlin, et al? And what are affects when we eat a lousy Western diet? Or, if we stay up and stare into a computer screen, can one overcome the effects light has on a eutherian mammal with their mind and it won't bother them? Do some people have that power?

Did my thoughts cause the reactions I've been experiencing on and off my whole life, or was is a neurotoxin or a lousy genetic download from my sick mother? A mold or bacteria has just as much right to live as my a$$ does, so I have no resentment building up cortisol wackiness and the resulting hormonal imbalances.

And what about genetic abnormalities passed down to us? Sometimes I think that people think we are born perfect and vibrant and it's our "decision" to get bodily wonky. I have had so many people point out my "responsibility" by the way I think but nothing about what other influences the world out there offers up in the way of incompatibility.

So where am I going with is?

I guess I'd like some thoughts on how the world works and how I can best optimize myself. Over and over this group that my wife hangs with offers up the phrase brought on by the quantum physics experiment where the,"observer influences the experiment" referring to the particle/wave experiments where whatever the scientists were looking for is what the experiment resulted in. I hear it often from Chopra, Braden, and others who are writing about this.

Any thoughts from you quantum eggheads?

Martin

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-19T17:46:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65360>

Martin, obviously your wife's thoughts are keeping her from actions that can help her.

Blue light from a computer, viruses and bacteria spread to us from insects, mold genetic code are all part of the world that is deterministic. Our freedom from determinism lies in our thoughts.... Because thoughts lead to solutions and decisions. Positive thinking no doubt helps our biology, but saying that a thought changes the laws of nature is just .... Wishing.

We have to use our thoughts to navigate through a world of laws (physics, chemistry, biology,etc.) that we can't change.

### Martin — 2012-11-19T17:55:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65364>

Well said. Thanks J!

I have tons more thoughts on this so stay tuned.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-19T18:53:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65376>

> **Martin;67369 wrote:**
>
> I guess I'd like some thoughts on how the world works and how I can best optimize myself. Over and over this group that my wife hangs with offers up the phrase brought on by the quantum physics experiment where the,"observer influences the experiment" referring to the particle/wave experiments where whatever the scientists were looking for is what the experiment resulted in. I hear it often from Chopra, Braden, and others who are writing about this.
>
>
>
> Any thoughts from you quantum eggheads?
>
>
>
> Martin

some excellent thoughts here Martin.

I agree with Riverneighbours sentiments about thoughts affecting internal vs consensual realities.

the 'observer effect' is one of the most badly misrepresentated discoveries of quantum mechanic theory. It was one of the first discoveries, and looking back one can find it's roots in Maxwells equations. (google Maxwells demon)

in it's simplest form, the concept is not hard to grasp.

If you are are measuring the location of a particle, it must interact with another particle to do so. If you measure the temperature of a cup of coffee, the temperature of the thermometer is going to affect the temperature of the coffee. Using a smaller thermometer with less thermal mass is going to change it less. Using an optical thermometer seems like the perfect solution, yet..We see the coffee by bouncing photons off of it and receptors detect the photons. The act of intercepting the photons changes the actual energy balance and hence the "temperature" of the coffee. Nowhere in physics does it say the outcome will be what the experiments were looking for. Although this is a common problem in science, and the origin of the double blind.

QM does get deeper and 'weirder' than this, with particles theoretically entangled and existing in two places at the same time, but this is the start of the woo woo Chopra et al start talking.. They tell us that quantum physics proves that we change particles just by observing them. While it is based on a true sentence, it misapplies the physics definition of observe, the intention of the statement, and is disingenuous application of the field of quantum physics. Can internal reality be changed by a thought? sure. of course. Can that change induce one to physically affect and change the external/consensual reality of course. No "woo woo Volcano God, vibrational crystal energy" required. All these pundits do is say "See? it's too complicated to understand! therefore, it's majic, praise (whatever spiritual universal force/secret/ancient wisdom/Midi-Chlorian particles) and follow me, for I know the way! Notice none of these people have any background in physics, let alone QM? They posture, and puff, but have no content behind them, just feigning expertise in the subject.

thems my thoughts.

Cheers

### Michael — 2012-11-19T19:39:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65386>

> **Martin;67369 wrote:** ... how I can best optimize myself.

I think probably like this: eat how Jack tells you; get enough sleep; allow yourself to get cold and dark (as Jack also says); don't hold cellphones to your head; do some exercise (leaning towards the sort of thing that Erwan le Corre and the Exuberant Animal people do); meditate; and perhaps do some bodywork and/or neurofeedback.

I think our great problem is that we have transformed our environment so comprehensively and so fast, mostly on the basis of short-term thinking, and certainly without thinking about how what we're doing impacts on ourselves.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-19T21:15:17+0200

My plan for optimal bets on the gene machine

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-19T21:24:00+0200

All you can do by trying to rip that from her is either alienate her and damage the bond, or break her and leave her with a big empty space that she obviously needed to satisfy.

I hate the way this sounds judgemental, I don't mean it meanly, but it does kind of apply. "Ignorance IS bliss". There are many thing in life I wish I never knew - I'm sure I would be a happier person for it.

### mtdalling@gmail.com — 2012-11-21T19:42:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65433>

Dawkins says he's a machine. I wouldn't know. Maybe he is. \*shrug\*

He drones on about "memes", but almost everyone who's bandied this concept around in print seems to mean something different by it, and it's not clear that we need it anyway. **Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65531>

MT,Â  I wasn't trying to set up a Dawkins/Lipton opposition....Â Â  as I said I haven't even read Liption and I only pulled one idea of Dawkins.Â  And,Â  I've only followed the idea of memes in the philosophical writings so I think we're probably talking about completely different ideas that happen to use the same word.Â 

Selfish Gene as a whole may be obsolete but I still like the quote.Â  Even now that we know about epi genetics and don't have to feel doomed to genetic determinism we still cannot escape the fact that those switches behave based on a wide variety of inputs.Â  Affecting change for one's self means knowing more and more about the inputs that throw the switches in the gene machine.Â  Though we can change our own genes expression by acting on thoughts the genes are still a rules based system even though the inputs may be varied and complex.Â  No matter how hard I've thought about it, I still have not been able to think myself into becoming a mermaid ....Â Â Â Â there are still constraints that are determined by biologic laws that we cannot escape.Â  This part of the world still is determined for us, even if there are a lot of choices that we can make within it's constraints.

### Martin — 2012-11-25T12:20:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65560>

All interesting comments.Â 

Â 

I think people feed and follow what they have egoically developed in their lives. If they are all about "magic" and the supernatural, that feeds their imagination along those lines in which they are interested. Same with anything, I suppose.Â 

Â 

But when I have followed a religion/philosophy/movement, I tended to ignore the challenges from another viewpoint because it came up against my ego. I want to be right and have the "truth". But when something else showed up and shot holes in that viewpoint, I had to take a hard look at my considerations and usually made some changes. After years of being "fooled" by the latest and greatest info on Life to come down the pike, I guess I got a little jaded. Numerous discussions on the nature of the universe among my family and friends always left me with more questions than answers.Â 

Â 

The biggest thing in my life has been a quest for health. I have struggled, had few wins, but the chronic pain and lethargy always put me back in the game of finding a way out. I had an epiphany one day when I realized that if I compare my measurements - labs, body comp, etc. - with a healthy human individual, I could perhaps see where I was not doing well. Where to find those statistics?

Â 

That question turned out to be one of the most pivotal moments in my life. My "statue of David" moment might be overstating it a bit, but it's late and I'm going' with it. Asking that question lead me to this site and Doc's 30k foot view. And, most importantly, I realized that I can look to evolutionary biology as my guide and inspiration.

Â 

Nature, not some man-made corp/gov diet or a spiritual guru selling books and lecture packages, has become my compass.

"7. A selfish gene simply seeks its own maintenance. â€˜Selfish genesâ€™ have no phenotype or strategy associated with them and hence no direct consequence to host competition or evolution. I have asked for a long time what is an optimum human diet, one that is free from politics, faddishness, and corp/gov influence. I have come closer to that here than anyplace else.Â 
>
> "Who out of everyone who's responded in the thread is asking you to buy a diet or a book or a lecture package?"
>
> I'm not sure how you got this from what I'm bringing up, but I'm nowhere near that statement. That's your interpretation. I meant out there in the world I've been in, not here right now on this forum entry.Â 
>
> Â " 'flatulent" -Â pompously or portentously overblown "
>
> Perhaps, but my intention was not to be pompous, but to state that after many failed pathways with many gurus, books, and leaders, I am finding that when we observe Nature and see where it is doing best, we can learn something and get our hubris out of the way and thrive.
>
> Â 
>
> I guess we may have a different definition of Nature as well. Now that would cause some problems! Mine is this: the natural world as it is when balance is striven for without a human ego trying to change the rules to suite their own agenda.
>
> Â 
>
>
> "Nature" simply means the whole - everything there is. How can the \*whole\* be a compass - something that points somewhere? It can't.

The "whole" denotes reality to me, Nature, to me, is the biochemical, rhythmical, dance that strives for balance on into the future. A difference in definitions, I guess.

# Thread: Greenland's viking settlers gorged on seals

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### Michael — 2012-11-19T20:19:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greenlands-viking-settlers-gorged-on-seals.4950/post-65391>

A nice Epipaleo news story:

> "Our analysis shows that the Norse in Greenland ate lots of food from the sea, especially seals," says Jan Heinemeier, Institute of Physics and Astronomy, Aarhus University.
>
>
>
> "Even though the Norse are traditionally thought of as farmers, they adapted quickly to the Arctic environment and the unique hunting opportunities. During the period they were in Greenland, the Norse ate gradually more seals. By the 14th century, seals made up between 50 and 80 per cent of their diet." ...

[http://news.ku.dk/all\_news/2012/2012.11/greenland\_norse\_gorged\_on\_seals/](http://http://news.ku.dk/all_news/2012/2012.11/greenland_norse_gorged_on_seals/)

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-19T21:38:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greenlands-viking-settlers-gorged-on-seals.4950/post-65415>

Scholarly stuff never leaves room for sheer chance and serendipity. Perhaps a significant portion of the young women were won in a battle that a majority of Norse participated in. The women were drug off as slaves/concubines etc and assimilated into other cultures and perhaps one bad winter dwindled the rest.

Could be there was a great battle and through "an act of god" all the ships sank, etc.

Sometimes \*\*\*\* just happens.

I have read some really interesting observations about the Norse/Viking reproduction habits. I was noticing one day that going back on the fraternal side of my family (my clearly a viking overloard uncle has a passion for this) would not reproduce much. Only every 3rd or 4rth person would reproduce, and almost exclusively single children not multiple.

Seems when you notice these trends over multiple generations, there may be some reason that is material.

If this "trait" went in the way-back machine, perhaps a combo of happenstance and low reproduction just left them too small to rise up again.

# Thread: Have some humans retained vit. C enzyme
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RubyKittany — 2012-11-22T14:35:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-65436>

In the body of a Vitamin C making mammal, the Vitamin C molecule is made from a few small modifications to the glucose molecule. While glucose is in abundant supply in humans and animals, there are four enzymes that are required to convert glucose into Vitamin C. Humans, have only three of these enzymes having lost the ability to make the fourth L-gulonolactone Oxidase somewhere in evolution. Some serious research should be undertaken to find out if indeed all humans are lacking in this critical liver enzyme. To answer the question as to why humans lost the ability to make their own Vitamin C one can only assume that because their diets at the time were abundant in Vitamin C. Humans are the only species to eat meat and be unable to produce their own Vitamin C.
In mammals which retained the ability to make Vitamin C, it is made in response to all sorts of stress, especially the stress of infection. Under stress, the normal daily amount can be quadrupled.
http://www.vitaminc.co.nz/introduction-to-vitamin-c.htm

Nishikimi and co-workers observed that the gene that codes for gulonolactone oxidase is actually present in humans, but is not active due to the accumulation of several mutations that turned it into a non-functional pseudogene (Nishikimi & Yagi 1991). Not only all humans, but also gorillas, chimps, orangutans, and some monkeys have this inborn genetic flaw, meaning that the loss of vitamin C biosynthesis must have occurred first in one of our primate ancestors. But how can something so crucial for survival be eliminated through the course of evolution?
Inclusion of vitamin C in the human diet explains why our non-synthesizing ancestors did not become extinct, as they found this an effective compensation for the mutations in the gulonolactone oxidase gene. However, biochemists speculate that there may have been some concurrent advantage of this mutation. For instance, since one of the products of the reaction catalysed by gulonolactone oxidase is hydrogen peroxide, Halliwell suggested in 2001 that the loss of biosynthesis balanced the "cost" of production, since the advantage of producing one vitamin C molecule would be lost by the production of this reactive oxygen species (Halliwell 2001).

More recently, Grano and De Tullio proposed another hypothesis, based on the studies by Knowles et al. In 2003, Knowles et al. demonstrated that vitamin C regulates a key stress-induced transcription factor called Hypoxia Inducible Factor 1Î± (HIF1Î±), a protein that, when activated, regulates the expression of hundreds of stress-related genes. Notably, the activation of HIF1Î± occurs in the absence of adequate oxygen or vitamin C supply. Grano and De Tullio therefore proposed that organisms that have lost vitamin C biosynthesis have an advantage: they can finely regulate HIF1Î± activation on the basis of the dietary intake of vitamin C (Grano & De Tullio 2007). When vitamin C supply is sufficient, the HIF transcription factor is less active than in conditions of vitamin C deficiency. In other words, the lack of vitamin C biosynthesis allows our bodies to know more about our nutritional status and consequently set the proper baseline of HIF1Î± expression. It is like a sensitive titration system.

There is a third yet still unexplored possibility. We know from other studies that pseudogenes are not inert, but can have a significant role in epigenetic control of gene expression (Poliseno et al. 2010). Could this also apply to the human gulonolactone oxidase pseudogene? Time (and much research) will tell.
http://www.nature.com/scitable/topicpage/the-mystery-of-vitamin-c-14167861

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-23T13:44:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-65443>

It is believed that Vitamin C is in active trait loss because of how iodine is now used in biochemical reactions. Vitamin C has its place but it become more important the more DHA and iodine deficient one is........say for example in an AI or an Adrenal Fatigue case.

### GlenPDQ — 2012-11-24T13:51:19+0200

>
> It is believed that Vitamin C is in active trait loss because of how iodine is now used in biochemical reactions. Vitamin C has its place but it become more important the more DHA and iodine deficient one is........say for example in an AI or an Adrenal Fatigue case.

I'm going to try out the new form here since the question asking if any humans still retain the ability to produce vitamin C has been on my mind for awhile.

It almost sounds that when we are sufficient in DHA and iodine, then also getting adequate vitamin C should assist in a powerful process when all 3 nutrients are abundant in the body. What more important things would the body rather be creating or doing when iodine, DHA, and Vitamin C are all adequate? Are there other processes which open up?

### ruby — 2012-11-26T05:51:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-65612>

seems like we still need it:

Â Vitamin C plays a central role in the antioxidant defense system and exists in two major forms. The charged form, ascorbate, is taken up into cells via sodium-dependent facilitated transport. The uncharged form, dehydroascorbate, enters cells via glucose transporter and is then converted back to ascorbate within these cells. Because dehydroascorbate and glucose compete for glucose transporters, hyperglycemia will exclude vitamin C from the cell and result in a decreased antioxidant capacity in some cell type that is dehydroascorbate dependent.

Tubular epithelial cells are dehydroascorbate dependent. Vitamin C exclusion from tubular epithelial cells through competition of glucose and dehydroascorbate for common transport mechanism in diabetes will deprive the cells of antioxidant ability and could lead to reactive oxygen species accumulation.

Â 

http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16118484

### Glen PDQ — 2012-12-01T14:57:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-66047>

Imagine the chagrin of the diabetic cell after it uptakes a phosphatidyl choline nanosphere (liposome), unwraps it, and finds vitamin C inside!

Â 

Also, the proteolytic enzymes like bromelain from pineapple, actinidain (from kiwi), and papain (from papaya) have interesting effects like making vitamin C and some other substances more bioavailable.Â

### ruby — 2012-12-01T21:28:50+0200

# Thread: Can progesterone trigger Hashi's?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-progesterone-trigger-hashis.4958/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-11-23T14:50:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-progesterone-trigger-hashis.4958/post-65450>

I was just reading an article by a doctor saying progesterone triggers or worsens Hashimotos. Should people with Hashi's be concerned with taking progesterone?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-23T14:54:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-progesterone-trigger-hashis.4958/post-65451>

No progesterone wont trigger hashi's but it could cause an issue if the Hashi's is so bad the liver is involved and your are using the oral route. Thankfully this is not common. Most people use the topical or vaginal route unless sleep is a big problem.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-26T16:43:20+0200

They might be ugly but where I live when it drops to -20 and the daily high temp manages to get to ZERO they are wonderful to own. Â I've had my pair for 15 years and they are all I wear once the snow flies. Â and when there's nothing on the streets and sidewalks but black ice they are what you want on your feet.

A fashion statement they are not. Warm and snuggly and sturdy ... yes. Â Oh and I may go barefoot indoors (and outdoors in the summer) but not when it's hip deep snow and the temps are in the basement.

# Thread: Havoc likes raw oysters!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/havoc-likes-raw-oysters.4963/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### gebronson@gmail.com — 2012-11-23T20:13:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/havoc-likes-raw-oysters.4963/post-65468>

Chaos isn't so sure. But she does like smoked oysters. And octopus & squid are still he favorite seafood!!!

### NaturalHealthForLife — 2013-08-07T06:15:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/havoc-likes-raw-oysters.4963/post-91725>

I am getting my autistic 5 y.o. son to swallow 1 raw oyster per day for lunch. I don't know if he "likes" it, but he is so used to taking a variety of meds, that he didn't put up a fuss getting these down. I do it with him. I'm wondering if I should try to get him to take more than 1/day, but not pushing it for now...

Oyster shots!

Love it!

Thanks Dr. K!

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-11-27T18:57:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-65729>

I do not like losing the 'Ask Jack' forum. Nearly $600 is a dear price to read the posts and ask a few questions. Especially when you are having a time of it financially.

I understand Jack gives some very expensive knowledge (it was expensive to him to become schooled in some of the medical knowledge he lets out). But some of answers were mere sugestions as to which blog post to read to get the knowledge, so then after re-reading it, you may still be in the dark or have more and new questions.

### freesia — 2012-11-27T21:42:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-65733>

> **ljbprrfmof said**
> I do not like losing the 'Ask Jack' forum. Nearly $600 is a dear price to read the posts and ask a few questions. Especially when you are having a time of it financially.
>
> I understand Jack gives some very expensive knowledge (it was expensive to him to become schooled in some of the medical knowledge he lets out). But some of answers were mere sugestions as to which blog post to read to get the knowledge, so then after re-reading it, you may still be in the dark or have more and new questions.

Â 

It's hardÂ to lose that feature. especially because you can see the questions but not the answers which makes BronzeÂ a bit like a view from the "cheap seats".
But, I did notice on the old forum many times people would register and their first post would be to Ask Jack. No sense of community evident - just straight in for the free advice. As often as not it would be a question that could either be answered by reading the blog, the blog comments (not to be underestimated!), by googling - especially questions about various properties of foods etc (!)Â -Â or by asking the starfish.

It would be good to have aÂ thread started on each blog. Starfish could help newbies there, if they're so inclined, and Jack (cos he's just a good guy like this) would probablyÂ come in from time to time and set everyone straight if they were going _too far_ off course...but then again he may not.....we may just be all part of some experiment...

### caroline — 2012-11-28T05:57:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-65752>

### Huck — 2012-11-29T21:32:25+0200

### youthfuliz — 2012-11-29T22:52:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-65913>

OK here are my comments on the new forum: (obviously there're a lot of good things about the new forum, but I guess you know those already, so I'll jump to the drawbacks directly if you don't mind)

1\. the text color is too light...why don't leave it black? I think quite a few people have complained about this. I have very good eyes, but it's hard to read.
2\. the background color is too bright... a light grey/blue would do a better job - that's gonna be much easier on our eyes
3\. space on the top of the forum is not used efficiently... I use a 17" laptop, when I open this forum I don't see anything but the banner, you're logged in as... and advanced search. You want to put as much information as possible at the top so you attract random viewers. I scroll down to see what's there, but I definitely would like it better if the space is used more efficiently.

I know it's a lot of work upgrading a forum and user feedback is what you need to improve...so I contributed. Hope this helps. And keep up the good work!

by the way, won't hurt to have more smileys!

### Optimalbound — 2012-12-09T10:10:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-66925>

I would really like to see a better search function. There is so much gold in many of the old threads, but the output from the current search function is merely a list of entire threads rather than individual postings. For the long threads, this is almost completely useless, and the result is that the same questions keep being asked again and again while the good answers that already exist, are left buried and forgotten in the long, old threads.

### endless — 2012-12-09T11:40:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-66932>

Would somebody PLEASE post a VERY detailed description of how to quote text when posting....I know I am not the only one who can't figure this out because I see it all over the forum and it makes it really bloody hard to follow threads. I don't always want to quote the entire post, either. Help? Anyone?

I also cannot figure out how to access the row of smileys...they just sit there mocking me. Any advice there appreciated as well!

### Inger — 2012-12-09T12:06:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-66935>

Endless..

It should work when pushing the quote button and then you just go a bit under the quoted text and write what you want and it should work. And if you want to quote only a part of the text, just remove what you do not want.

But.

It does not always work that way. For me at last. This is when the quoted text shows in a smaller window, not the broad one with all the extra buttons and stuff. So what I do then.. is;
I just add \[quote \] manuallly, (you have to type the letters) before the text begins that I want to quote and then I add \[/quote \] at the end. Take the space away for sure, I had to add it or it would quote what I write right here..lol This ALWAYS works.

Remove the text you do not want to quote. Or copy directly from the post the part of the text you want to quote and copy it into your reply (quoted manually).

Hope this helps.. I am not good in explaining such stuff at all..

### Inger — 2012-12-09T12:14:50+0200
