# Thread: hs CRP vs wide range wr CRP
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hs-crp-vs-wide-range-wr-crp.5047/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JoeBranca — 2012-12-04T14:54:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hs-crp-vs-wide-range-wr-crp.5047/post-66316>

My hs-CRP result from August was 1.84 mg/L (through Directlabs).

Last week I requested an hs-CRP test from Kaiser. The result (0.8 mg/L) came back specified instead as Wide Range CRP (wr-CRP) measurement. I had not heard of this, but just found this paper:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16018882](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16018882)

Â 

The paper claims little difference in accuracy, but I was wondering if anyone has used this measurement method? Do I have two data points I can use or should I retest with hs-CRP?

Â 

thanks

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-04T20:28:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hs-crp-vs-wide-range-wr-crp.5047/post-66359>

one test alone is never something to base treatment on but a battery of test that all point the same direction is actionable just sayin'

### JoeBranca — 2012-12-04T20:52:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hs-crp-vs-wide-range-wr-crp.5047/post-66375>

Yep, I certainly intend to follow on with further tests. I'm just trying to determine if this recent wr-CRP result can be used to compare with the previous hs-CRP result from August to begin to show a trend, or do I throw it out since a different method of measurement was used? I'm done with Kaiser after 2012, presumably future tests will again be hs-CRP.

# Thread: Few more questions about Leptin Reset

**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 21

### furface — 2012-12-04T16:30:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66318>

I have a few more questions regarding the Leptin Reset.

Â 

1) If I'm already obese, won't eating all of this extra fat, even in lieu of carbs still make me gain weight/bodyfat until my body becomes Leptin sensitive, and begin to lose weight/fat? (I don't want my clothes to stop fitting before I begin to lose weight.)

Â 

2) How long before our stomachs/system gets used to eating all of this extra fat? (May not always have a washroom handy.)

Â 

3) How does one know if they have a "leaky gut"?

Â 

4) Is type 2 diabetes an AI disease, and should tomatoes, bell peppers, and "hot" spices such as cayenne, chili powder etc be avoided?

Â 

Thanks again.

### furface — 2012-12-05T02:10:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66416>

I guess no one knows the answers, or else I've asked in the wrong forum.

Â

### Destiny — 2012-12-05T07:51:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66426>

Furnace,

I'll give it a try.

Â 

You might gain some weight at the beginning but if you stay at it, you will lose. I think the change is a change and it doesn't happen overnight.

Â 

The stomach/system issue is very individual. I had a lot of problems for almost 8 months but I have no gallbladder. What helped me was Ox bile and Digestive enzymes.

Â 

For a leaky gut, I would go back to the older blogs from Dr. Kruse. It is not an explanation on a couple of lines but you could start with the labs.

Â 

I don't know about the diabetes/AI question.

Â 

Good luck!

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-05T09:34:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66434>

Welcome, you wil most likely have no issues as you start to eat lots of fat. If you eat lot's of fat, which I recommend, make sure you reduce carbs dramatically or you will have issues. Eating lots of fat is a challenge because you ave been told it's bad. Eat fatty meat, eat seafood, drown your green veg (the only carbs you should eat now) in fat; butter, olive oil, bacon fat, lard. You will feel full and satisfied and as the carbs leave your system you will be calmer with less gas and bloating. As with many things addictive I find the cold turkey approach to carbs the best. The sooner you have it out of your system the easier it is. Try to find some raw cold pressed coconut oil and start using it liberally. Many people have constipation issues initially. The remedy is.....more fat. Don't just fill up on lean protein like steak or chicken. Do not eat anything labeled lo-fat. Read food labels sugar is in almost every food product.

I am T2D with Hashimoto's Thyroiditis. I don't think T2D is AI, but Hashi's is for sure. If you are T2D you may unfortunately find out you have other issues. This disease rarely travels alone. Thyroid issues are common. You will need further testing to determine if the solanacea family( tomatoes,peppers, potatoes) are a problem for you.

Start reading the Quilt and the blogs. It's all in there and the learning is important. When YOU learn it YOU own it. That's where the power to make these changes come from. Good luck.

### Linz — 2012-12-05T11:53:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66444>

Furface,

I'm not sure I can give you the best answers either but don't worry about eating fat at all. Just make sure you use the right ones.

I was only a bit overweight when I changed but had quite severe digestive problems and I had too much weight loss and very up and down digestive symptoms for several months, certainly not constipation! I think it does take some time to adapt to fat burning. I would change the fats and eliminate gluten and all grains immediately as well as sugar and sweet starches but perhaps keep some of the starchy vegetables for a while - just not too many. Replace starches with extra green vegetables in the beginning and eat lots of fish and shellfish.

After a lifetime of hearing fat is bad it is not an easy change but add fat to all your green veg as Dali says, don't skim soup stocks and pour the bacon fat onto your vegetables. Hindsight is the best way to learn for your own circumstances as we are all so different. It's so much easier to look back and see the changes andÂ  once you start to see real differences for yourself there is an incredible amount of knowledge here to interpret them.

I think most who have lived a lifetime of SAD have gut problems to sort out.

Best of luck

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-05T12:00:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66447>

great advice right there........

### freesia — 2012-12-05T16:09:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66479>

I just want to echo what above posters said.
...Becoming fat adapted painlessly seems to be a naturalÂ process asÂ you cut carbs. Coming into winter where you are this should suit your body well { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### furface — 2012-12-05T18:45:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66496>

Thanks everyone for your wonderful and very helpful answers.

Â 

I know it's hard toÂ  "erase" what we've been taught for all these years about fat being bad for us, and carbs being the best source of energy etc.

Â 

I love coconut oil, and just bought myself a jar of Nutiva. (It was only a small jar...that's all they had in stock, but that should suffice me for a couple of weeks until I get paid.)

Â 

I've severely reduced my carb intake, to where my only carbs have been either a few spears of asparagus, some brocolli, a few pieces of 99% Dark Chocolate...not bad actually, and some baby bok choy which I had for dinner last night along with some bacon, and a grass-fed ground beef burger. The baby bok choy in the bacon grease was delicious!

Â 

I've even begun taking cold water showers after my regular shower in the morning...a great waker upper. So I'm beginning to get more and more "into" this lifestyle. I already have a lot more energy, and I hope it just continues.

### caroline — 2012-12-05T19:35:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66503>

Everyone has given you so much great advice. I would just add - once you adapt to all the fat etc. your cravings completely go away and your appetite decreases. Now I use food strictly as fuel for my body and I want to use the best fuel possible. It has been such a revelation to not always be thinking of food. I only eat when hungry and then only the best stuff. Really liberating actually! I do sometimes think I would like an ice cream cone but I doubt I could eat one!

### furface — 2012-12-05T19:44:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66505>

Good points, Caroline. I think I'm already beginning to feel the "feeling full" effects of eating "extra" fats.

As far as the ice cream cone goes, I don't think one cone would hurt you, and in fact, you...or people who have their LS in tact, and their Paleo/Epi-Paleo diet down pat, could probably enjoy it WITHOUT any "fear" or "regrets" afterward (i.e. "Damn I shouldn't have done that!") as a "reward" to themselves BECAUSE they have control of their diet. IOW, YOU/THEY control their food...food DOESN"T control them.

Â 

Just a thought.

Â 

Nothing wrong with enjoying something, or "treating" one's self once in a while.

### caroline — 2012-12-05T20:21:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66507>

Good points, Caroline. I think I'm already beginning to feel the "feeling full" effects of eating "extra" fats.

As far as the ice cream cone goes, I don't think one cone would hurt you, and in fact, you...or people who have their LS in tact, and their Paleo/Epi-Paleo diet down pat, could probably enjoy it WITHOUT any "fear" or "regrets" afterward (i.e. "Damn I shouldn't have done that!") as a "reward" to themselves BECAUSE they have control of their diet. IOW, YOU/THEY control their food...food DOESN"T control them.

Â 

Just a thought.

Â 

Nothing wrong with enjoying something, or "treating" one's self once in a while.

I definitely don't think there isn't anything wrong with enjoying yourself - but my thinking has changed. I wouldn't feel guilty about having an ice cream cone but I don't think I would really like it now. It would be way to sweet etc. In time your thinking will change too. It isn't that you can't have something - it is more that if you do you will feel physically not well and not enjoy it either. I am sure all of us have been there and tried something we used to love and the attraction just isn't there anymore. Now I love dark chocolate and a glass of wine and some really great blue cheese. You are thinking - I think - that we are all suffering and missing the pleasures of life. That is so not the case. I feel fantastic .....and I a not giving that up for an ice cream cone!!

### caroline — 2012-12-05T20:30:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66510>

It will be interesting for me to see - on Christmas day I join a bunch of friends and family at a friend's house. Â A friend who is an amazing professional baker always makes me an enormous Pavlova with all kinds of fresh cream and fresh fruit. Â This has always been my favorite and I usually eat huge amounts this one time - at Christmas. Â I think I might just eat the cream and fruit this year!!

### furface — 2012-12-05T20:55:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66516>

Good points again.

Â 

I just don't think we should let food, or ANY type of eating plan "control" us. We should still be the one in control, and not think or feel that having something we've been told is "bad" for us once in a blue moon is going to make us feel awful, or ruin all of our progress so we become "scared" or even "brainwashed" to think that we can never have it, or if we do we "won't" enjoy it because we've been "programmed" to ONLY enjoy eating this "new" way. Not saying that there's anything wrong with eating this way...I'm fully committed, but that doesn't, or better yet, SHOULDN'T mean that we shouldn't be allowed to enjoy something that we used to enjoy, once in a blue moon. IOW I still want to be in control of my life, and my enjoyment. I still want to be in control of myself...not some diet plan. I'm still a human, NOT a robot.

Â 

Besides, I don't think ONE ice cream cone once in a blue moon would KILL anyone. (Unless of course they have specific health issues related to the ingredients.)

Â 

But hey, in a few months, I may feel exactly the same way that you do.

Â 

I just HATE to be told WHAT TO DO, and have a proverbial GUN held to my head, and think I don't have any say in the matter.

Â 

I realize that this may be overreacting, and again, not trying to knock anything, just expressing my current opinion. I love my freedom. I LOVE my life, and I want to be able to enjoy it the way I want to enjoy it.

### furface — 2012-12-06T00:22:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66531>

I completely concur with the fact that eating extra fat will make you feel fuller.

Â 

For dinner I only had 2 slices of bacon, a small boneless pork chop, and 6 thin spears of asparagus cooked in the bacon fat, along with one tablespoon of Nutiva coconut oil, and that was nearly 3 hours ago, and I still feel SO FULL!!

Â 

I can see how this way of eating would clearly lower your total food intake on a daily basis.

### caroline — 2012-12-06T03:50:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66536>

Good points again.

Â 

I just don't think we should let food, or ANY type of eating plan "control" us. We should still be the one in control, and not think or feel that having something we've been told is "bad" for us once in a blue moon is going to make us feel awful, or ruin all of our progress so we become "scared" or even "brainwashed" to think that we can never have it, or if we do we "won't" enjoy it because we've been "programmed" to ONLY enjoy eating this "new" way. Not saying that there's anything wrong with eating this way...I'm fully committed, but that doesn't, or better yet, SHOULDN'T mean that we shouldn't be allowed to enjoy something that we used to enjoy, once in a blue moon. IOW I still want to be in control of my life, and my enjoyment. I still want to be in control of myself...not some diet plan. I'm still a human, NOT a robot.

Â 

Besides, I don't think ONE ice cream cone once in a blue moon would KILL anyone. (Unless of course they have specific health issues related to the ingredients.)

Â 

But hey, in a few months, I may feel exactly the same way that you do.

Â 

I just HATE to be told WHAT TO DO, and have a proverbial GUN held to my head, and think I don't have any say in the matter.

Â 

I realize that this may be overreacting, and again, not trying to knock anything, just expressing my current opinion. I love my freedom. I LOVE my life, and I want to be able to enjoy it the way I want to enjoy it.

Maybe I will try a different approach - Dr. Kruse has said many, many times that he is just shining a light on stuff. This is my road to travel and I want to be as healthy as possible. No one is holding a gun to my head and no one is making me do this. I love my life too and that is why I am doing it. Everyone's journey is very different.
He also has said many times that no one cares about our health as much as we do - so it is up to us to read and learn and ask questions and do whatever we need to - to get where we want to go. A lot of us here have been on an incredible ride with Dr. K the last eighteen months. It hasn't been easy especially for me - I don't have a scientific bone in my body and so I find a lot of the language difficult but I keep trying and there are so many really smart, helpful, caring people on this forum. Dr. Kruse wants us to be excited about our life and live every second to the fullest and he is giving us the tools to do it if we so choose. I know - for me- I keep reading and trying different things and finally a lot of things have come together - a light bulb has finally gone on.
For me the most important piece in the puzzle was my circadian rhythm and getting the proper testing. That has made the biggest impact.
sorry about the soapbox!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-06T09:30:30+0200

### kzimm — 2012-12-06T13:14:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/few-more-questions-about-leptin-reset.5048/post-66556>

> **Jack Kruse said**
> That post is likely going to help more people than anything I write. Â We need more people to pour their words into this community to create more starfish. Â 

Since October 2011, I've been here at the school of Jack.Â  I haven't posted, as Caroline saidÂ Â  "I don't have a scienticfic bone in my body".Â  I am, however, able to take the information and make it actionable.

I've watered it down to a 3 prong approach.Â  One - Live within circadian/seasonal cycles - be it food or exposure to light.Â  Two - eat paleo with an emphasis on seafood, sea veggies, fat - veggies cooked and meats as raw as possible.Â  (Under the food umbrella, timing is key.Â  No eating 4-5 hours before sleep, etc.)Â  Three - your cells need to experience cold.

Â 

All of the posts interweave information that you need to read.Â  We are all different.

Paraphrasing Jack - years of living in a modern world eating a SAD diet are not undone overnight.

You may have to start with labs so you know what to address first.Â  It takes time.Â  It's a choice to change.Â  Keep a journal if that helps you.

Â 

My own experience has been a gradual,Â plotted course.Â  It's been drilled here over and over - food is information for your hormones not metabolic fuel.Â  Once one wraps one's head around the fact "just because you eat it, doesn't mean it's food"Â  it starts to fall into place.Â  This is coming from someone who used Ben and Jerry as counselors.

### shilohman — 2012-12-04T22:10:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-members-i-need-your-input.5049/post-66386>

Podcasts - Yes, they add a different perspective to your answers sometimes. Â I have noticed that on the podcasts you are limited in time with your answers so you tend to be more concise and simplified with your answers, very helpful.

Evolution - For me, I have a hard enough time trying to understand the technical science stuff, the evolution stuff just gets in the way.

Philosophy - Yes, this is good. Â Often times you have to put a phiosophical view point to explain alot of what is covered in this forum.

Bio Hack - Yes, Yes, Yes. Â I find this very interesting. Â Even without the data, would like a better understanding of why the biohack. For example the biohack you did early this year with the liposuction and then MRSA injections, why was the liposuction necessary? Â Was there a reason why you picked a bacteria (MRSA) and not a virus? Â  Â 

Q&A worth it - Personally, I have gotten a lot out the Q&As. Â Have only attended one live, but I always go through the recorded ones. Â The Q&As ask more specific and directed questions than the podcasts. Â The podcast questions are more general.

One thing that I would like to see added is a monthly case study/hypothetical consult. Â For example, I think it is in the T3 forum Destiny discusses how she felt that she had cleared the rT3 stored in the cells of her body only to find that she after doing so, she feels worse now than before. Â So how about look at applying Destiny's situation to a hypothetical consult where you cover things like: Â How to determine that your body has truly cleared rT3 from your cells (testing?). Â What type things can happen once the rT3 is cleared to increase rT3 and what things can be done with the diet, supplements etc. to overcome this type of problem. Â In other words, present a specific situation and then discuss resolutions along with implications of fixing the leaky gut, balancing the HPA axis, getting the omega 6/3 ratio optimal, that kind of thing.

That's my two cents.

### nonchalant — 2012-12-04T22:15:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-members-i-need-your-input.5049/post-66387>

Great comments.Â  ++Podcasts!

I would like someone to make some short videos explaining, for the general public, some of the concepts you have taught us.

Like the part of the Hormone101 blog post explaining the conversion of cholesterol to steroid hormones.Â  Or perhaps the circadian cycle of hormones.Â  Or maybe avoiding light at night and seeking it in daytime.

They could cover the subject lightly, to peak curiosity, then refer people to this website for more info.

Maybe a contest for best video idea?

### Awainer1 — 2012-12-04T22:26:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-members-i-need-your-input.5049/post-66390>

Yes on the biohacks. Like the wbinar over podcast. There are too many podcasts out there as it is and I look forward to the webinar every month. More personal than a podcast. Dont really feel the Q & A is all that necessary as it gets sidetracked with other questions and there is so much info in the webinars that its enough to take in. Really excited and a little bit nervous to hear about the recent biohack. But when you know better you do better. Love the evolution and philosophy would like to hear more about lifestyle and what to do with all this info. Would love to hear your views on how to apply this to where you live/type of work/daily stressors. A lot of people dont have a way to make passive income and spend time living perfectly optimal. Since getting into paleo I quit my job in an office and work outside at a golf course making less money but living minimally and with much less acute stress. Would like to hear you expand on stuff like that.

### SeaHorse — 2012-12-04T22:31:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-members-i-need-your-input.5049/post-66391>

Hi Dr. K

Â 

Here are some of my thoughts...the webinars have been great. Podcasts would be great too. I like listening to the information. It compliments reading the blogs. The Q and A's have been a mixed bag. When there aren't a lot of people involved it seems to go off track pretty easily...then becomes more of a social gathering. That's not a bad thing, but it may not be worth your time and effort if there are only a few people. Perhaps something to commit to, or sign up for, maybe schedule one every two months? Not sure what the answer is.Â 

Â 

I like a separation between evolutionary mind-blowing biology and your philosophical musings in the blogs. The education we are receiving is fantastic. I really appreciate the evolution stuff when it is written and edited carefully. It makes it so much easier to study and retain. When it comes to your philosophy...well, let 'er rip. I'd rather read the stream of conscious writing that comes straight from your heart...who cares about the editing then! I think the balance between the two has been good... I like it.

Â 

All the bio-hack stuff is just really interesting to know...and even without data, the information will be welcome.

Â 

I think the site has improved consistently. Certainly an opportunity to examine how we all deal with change! I'm curious as to how the different levels of membership have changed the numbers of people reading and using the site.Â 

Â 

As I said awhile ago...I've stopped posting for the most part and just check in to read and keep up with new stuff...but that is because I'm feeling SO MUCH BETTER!!! I finally have some energy (manufactured by me, not outside stimulants) Â to go out in the world and do things....it's an amazing feeling. I'm still not where I want to be, but I'm on my way. Without the support and information you and this forum provide, this wouldn't have happened for me.Â 

Â 

Hope all the changes in your life lead to great things too. SeaHorse

I grew up in Northern Colorado (for those of you who missed it). Â Summers in my most of my childhood were spent up in the Rockies at a cabin my Mom and Grandmom bought just outside Allenspark. Â I slept wonderfully there, was more relaxed, enjoyed the quiet and the sound of the wind in the pines, the scents, everything about it. Â 

Since most of my ancestry is Northern European (and hazarding a guess here until I can spring for the 23andme test ... a healthy dollop of Asian) I am not at all surprised that I love the woods and the altitude. Â There is a peace there that I simply can't define. Â And just let me add it has been ruined. Â The last outing we took to the woods the morons who were there were playing their boomboxes at an ear splitting volume, there were campers with satellite dishes on them, no one was walking anywhere they were tearing up the terrain with their atv's. Â god I sometimes really hate "civilization."

Moving North to Wyoming put us at a slightly lower altitude which took me a long time to adjust to. Â Whenever I would return from a visit to Fort Collins I would feel a sense of dread steal over me the closer I got to Sheridan. Â Some people claim it's because we're located in close proximity to an energy center (the Medicine Wheel up on the mountain) but I'm not really a crystals and chanting kinda gal so I never fully bought into that. Â The change in elevation is only about 1500 feet but it apparently is enough of a difference.

### Aussie Nana — 2013-01-19T18:56:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forum-members-i-need-your-input.5049/post-71673>

Thor, obviously you feel very strongly about this, and for good reason. When I was a health researcher working in a medical school I had a year's sabbatical that gave me time to look at published papers outside my own field of expertise. What I discovered horrified me and eventually meant I could no longer work at a medical school. It was distressing to find that much of what my colleagues were saying just was not based on evidence despite their insistance that they were practicing evidence based medicine - those 3 words are emblematic of their belief and its routinely reduced to EBM and is the supposed holy grail of what they claim to be teaching the medical students. But when I went looking at the evidence I found that it wasn't often there.

I didn't spend much time looking at the evidence in relation to vaccines, just enough to discourage my daughter from allowing GD to get the Gardasil at 13, though she did get the tetanus and whatever went with it. Anyway when I went asking for the key literature to support vaccinations noone knew it and I was told to use google scholar because there were "tens of thousands" of papers which proved efficacy and safety. The funny thing was that when I investigated the very few papers that I did find none of them supported either efficacy or safety once I put them through a thorough analysis. It was downright scary. What was even scarier was that when I went back to colleagues about this most of them got very belligerant. The anger was palpable. I was being difficult. I was wasting not only my time, but their time when it was so self evident that vaccines were both safe and they worked. Not being able to find the evidence was evidence of my incompetence as a researcher and so why was I being paid and so on.

I talked about this with a younger non-medical colleague with children. He had to take his children to the doctor and the doctor insisted that they be immunised against influenza. He asked to see the information insert that went with the vaccine and was left alone to read it. The insert had a clearly written statement on it saying "there is no evidence that this vaccine works". He was not at all impressed and left with his children, only to be chased by the nurse who insisted it was "better to be safe than sorry, and the children must have the vaccine".

# Thread: Found a source for Grass-Fed Beef, Bison,  Lamb in Vancouver, B.C. area.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-a-source-for-grass-fed-beef-bison-lamb-in-vancouver-b-c-area.5053/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2012-12-04T22:57:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-a-source-for-grass-fed-beef-bison-lamb-in-vancouver-b-c-area.5053/post-66393>

Just to let anyone in the Vancouver, B.C./Lower Mainland area now, that there is a good source for Grass-Fed & Finished Beef, Bison, Lamb, as well as Wild Duck and other "Wild" game in Coquitlam. It's Hills Foods on Glacier St. They also have a website. http://www.hillsfoods.com/

Â 

They even have 3 different PrePackaged "Paleo" diet packs, which feature Grass-Fed Bison.

Â 

Just thought I'd pass it along in case there are others in the Lower Mainland trying to find Grass-Fed meats.

### endless — 2012-12-08T16:28:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-a-source-for-grass-fed-beef-bison-lamb-in-vancouver-b-c-area.5053/post-66837>

Thanks for the link! I'm in the Kootenays so hopefully they ship. I can at least stock up when I get down there for visits.

### furface — 2012-12-08T17:15:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/found-a-source-for-grass-fed-beef-bison-lamb-in-vancouver-b-c-area.5053/post-66848>

> **endless said**
> Thanks for the link! I'm in the Kootenays so hopefully they ship. I can at least stock up when I get down there for visits.

No prob. I've tried quite a few things from them...grass-fed bison steaks, lamb, bison, elk burgers, plus a few other things that I can't remember off hand lol. Just a note, everything they sell is frozen.

Â 

Also, if you're ever in Vancouver, Market Meats on West 4th Avenue, does sell fresh Grass-Fed/Finished Beef, lamb, bison, etc. Can't wait to go shopping there in a week and a half!

# Thread: Which cuts of Grass-Fed steak do you eat?

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2012-12-04T23:05:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-cuts-of-grass-fed-steak-do-you-eat.5054/post-66397>

Just curious as to which cuts of Grass-Fed Beef/Bison do you eat?

Â 

I myself love Ribeye steak, even though it's higher in fat, but isn't beef fat supposed to be good anyway? (Grass fed that is)

Also found out that "regular" Grass-Fed ground beef is higher in Omega 3, and CLA than Lean, or Extra-Lean.

### diane — 2012-12-04T23:22:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-cuts-of-grass-fed-steak-do-you-eat.5054/post-66404>

I get a side of beef, so all kinds of cuts. Sirloin is one of my favourites, and have found that the blade roast is amazing in the slow cooker. Plus I get bones and make bone broth. Oh, so many possibilities!Â  { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### furface — 2012-12-04T23:25:16+0200

Ooooh the blade roast in the slow cooker sounds delicious! As does the broth. Yeah a side, or even a hind/quarter sounds like the best idea. Hopefully one day I'll be able to afford one. (and get a bigger freezer LOL)

### diane — 2012-12-04T23:51:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-cuts-of-grass-fed-steak-do-you-eat.5054/post-66406>

I got my freezer on kijiji for $50. One of the best buys I've made!

Â 

Overall the 1/4 beef saves me money - between what we eat, and my dogs eat raw, so overall it saves me money. Plus so much better quality!

### furface — 2012-12-05T00:20:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-cuts-of-grass-fed-steak-do-you-eat.5054/post-66413>

### Rachelle — 2012-12-05T02:58:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-cuts-of-grass-fed-steak-do-you-eat.5054/post-66419>

As poor as I am, I buy, mostly, tenderloin from Wellness. I love it seared off or tartare. My only other choice is the ground beef for stews and such. Â Thank goodness I only eat beef twice a month, the rest of the month is seafood.

# Thread: Your Experience With D-Ribose
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 258
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheKid — 2012-12-05T04:07:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66424>

So since there has been some discussion of D-Ribose in Ask Jack and elsewhere, I thought maybe we could share some experiences, for those of us who are using it.

Â 

As I have noted in my last post, I have a number of symptoms that could point to a range of co-morbidities.Â  Jack, however, thought I should look into D-Ribose, and I have done that.Â  My Life Extension shipment just came.Â 

Â 

First I am curious what people are using it for specifically.Â  I was not away of its impact on Adrenal Fatigue or the mechanism by which it would have an effect. But I assume that anything that improves energy production at the cellular level would have a positive impact on adrenal health.

Secondly, I am curious about dosing.Â  I have a recommendation to do a 2-week loading phase where I would consume 2.5g 4 times per day, for a total of 10g per day.Â  However, I have seen a lot written on the internet about using much higher doses during the loading phase.Â And the serving size itself for the Life Extension product is 5g.Â  So I am wondering what other people are doing in terms of loading.

Â 

Lastly, I am interested in any negative side effects.Â  Do you know of any real risks of loading with higher amounts, say 20g - 30g per day (divided doses)?Â  On this issue, I will report that yesterday, I made the mistake of taking 20g all at once.Â  I basically read the label wrong, thinking that one scoop had 500mg, when in reality it was 5000mg (5g).Â  I know that it can cause blood sugar to drop, but I can report that I did not feel unwell in any way whatsoever after that 20g dose.Â  I will not be trying that again, though.

### Shijin13 — 2012-12-05T08:44:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66429>

I'm using the stuff. Have been for almost a week. I'm using the LEF brand. 1scoop 3x/day in addition to some ubiquinol, acetal l-caratine, increased mg, based on a consult I had w/Jack. no adverse effects from it yet. I have 2cups of BPC in the am & 1cup of BP green tea when I get home from work that I put my scoop in. It gives the drink a bit of sweetness.

I jumped right in It seems that's what I always do. The positive so far I've felt like I was going to have a migraine for the past 6 days but it never fully showed up.

One of the benefits from what I've read it can help w/BG issues.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-05T10:23:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66437>

That is why you began the Rx gretch.......hopefully to kill those migraines. Â D ribose has a massive therapeutic index so higher doses are tolerated. Â Severe Grade 4 Heart failure patients take 25 grams a day.

### Shijin13 — 2012-12-05T11:19:51+0200

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-12-05T11:44:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66442>

I began usingÂ  D Ribose 3 weeks ago. The main benefit I have noticed is the elimination of heart palpitations ... within several days they were noticeably less frequent, and after several weeks, they are gone.

My dose has been 5g / serving 3 times a day.Â 

I first learned about D Ribose in the book 'From Fatigued to Fantastic' by Jacob Teitelbaum, MD.

I found the best price on D Ribose at Swanson Vitamins.

### DarleenMB — 2012-12-05T12:57:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66449>

I''m using it because when I started the cytomel I began getting really trembly ... so bad my left hand would shake. Â I started the D-ribose and it stopped. Â Immediately.

I've been dosing 1 heaping tsp with each dose of cytomel and any more when I started shaking again. Â Â 

I'm up to 1/2 tab of cytomel twice a day now and yesterday only needed one dose of the d-riobose.

### kathylu — 2012-12-05T16:55:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66486>

Jack suggested the "mitochondrial Rx" for me too. Interestingly, everything he suggested I already had in the house! Kinda gives me a clue about how long I have been looking for a treatment for my fatigue and fogginess and how I was doing it piecemeal instead of in an organized fashion. Started ubiquinol, acetyl-L-carnitine and d-ribose about 2 weeks ago. I'm taking 5 g d-ribose 2X per day. I am noticing a slow but steady increase in energy and mental clarity. No side effects (none!). Liking this a lot.

I think you and I are on the same schedule w/Jack... gotta fix those mitochondria!

### TheKid — 2012-12-07T08:31:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66656>

I see some of you are also taking care of your mitochondria.Â  In addition to D-Ribose, I am also taking Acetyl L-Carinitine, PQQ, Magnesium, and CoQ10.Â  My understanding though is that it's the D-Ribose that is the real game changer.Â  I am now on I think my 5th day. No noticable differences just yet.

Â 

I am trying to not get too excited, because I know there is no silver bullet.Â  BUt I am hopeful that it will at least help me with exercise and CT tolerance.Â  I actually had to discontinue most of my CT because I would just get wiped out at night by it.Â  I am going to CT today a short while and see if there is any difference.

Â 

What I have noticed is that there has been no improvement in the adrenal symptoms I have been experiencing; and that is one thing I want to sort out: if my HPA axis issue is the main issue for me or if some underlying mitochondrial dysfunction is driving what I feel.Â  It is a bit confusing, because although my 4x salivary cortisol was bad, a bunch of more recent adrenal testing was pretty normal.Â  My endo said that some people can be at an earlier stage of adrenal fatigure but have symptoms that would make one think it was a later stage.Â  So I was thinking that a mitochondrial issue layered on top of an HPA issue, my be what's making me feel so badly.

Â 

For me, if the D-Ribose does not result in some improvement, then I will need to start hydrocortisone.Â

### Jude — 2012-12-07T18:28:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66728>

Have no experience with D Ribose but do you have a set time limit in mind with regard to assessing D-B's effectiveness? It may take you a bit longer to experience any noticeable Â improvement............patience with healing, especially if slow, is very frustrating.

As an aside, have found it's taken Â 4mths to experience Â improvement in gut flora. Started Resveratrol 2G daily back then and only now can finally notice that magnesium tolerance has become almost 98%. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### TheKid — 2012-12-08T03:11:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66785>

@Jude,

Â 

You are right -- patience is a must.Â  However, as I know, if D-Ribose is going to do something for you, it should show fairly quickly.Â  With some cardiac related issues,Â JackÂ mentioned to me that 2 days worth of dosing can show some improvements.Â  I would imagine that the worse off a person is, the more noticeable the change, if indeed there is an improvement. So for many of us, who are dealing with a range of sub-clinical disease manifestations, I would imagine remaining patient is great advice.

Â 

I am happy to report that yesterday's CT went well.Â  I had discontinued it for almost a month due to the way it would wipe me out.Â  If I would CT in the evening, it would destroy my sleep and I would often have all-over body fatigue by morning.Â  Yesterday, however, I CT for 30 minutes starting at 6:30 pm.Â  After a month off, and after nearly 3 weeks of an autoimmune flare + cortisol issues, I feared what the night and subsequent morning would bring.Â  I did wake in the early AM, earlier than usual.Â  I also did have a hint of fatigue; however, it was ever so slight -- almost unnoticeable.Â  So for me, I am really happy with that.

Â 

Can I directly attribute my tolerance of CT to D-Ribose?Â  Hard to say. Time will tell as I CT more consistently.Â  If I start to get wiped out again, then I would assume the D-Ribose is not impacting that.

Â 

On a related topic, I would like to say that CT is indeed awesome when i can tolerate it.Â  When Jack says it "crushes" cytokines, I am apt to say he absolutely correct.Â  I have suffered through what I believe has been an extended, intense autoimmune flare.Â  I could feel it coming on again yesterday which prompted me to hit the CT.Â  After 30 minutes, the pain I was experiencing was G-O-N-E. So at least the CT is working.Â

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-08T15:38:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-66826>

D ribose fixes energy salvage.......not efficiency. Â That is why there are other parts of the Rx. Â The worse off you are the more efficiency one needs to make traction. Â It is not just a D ribose story.

### TheKid — 2012-12-19T07:50:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-67927>

So I am done with the loading period for D-Ribose and am into maintenance now.Â  I was doing 5 grams, 3 times daily with meals.Â  Now I am into 5 grams at breakfast and dinner.

Â 

Although I hoped it would show some demonstrable gains, I have to report that I have not seen much resolution of the problems I was thinking it might address.Â  This is actually a good thing though.

Â 

I have been trying to discern which of my health issues are primary and which ones are secondary or derrivatives of the main ones.Â  Now that I have done the D-Ribose, and continued testing, I am certain that my main issue is hypothalamic adrenal fatigue.Â  Could my mitochondria also be non-optimal?Â  Sure.Â  And I am happy to support them any way I can, and I will continue with the D-Ribose.

Â 

But I can report that the problems I was having have remained, thus pointing me directly to cortisol related problems.Â  I get horrible reactive hypoglycemia which includes frightening chest pains.Â  That didn't go away.Â  Nor did any of my other symptoms.

Â 

So at least D-Ribose allowed me to rule out one thing.Â  I haven't exercised while using it, so it will be interesting to see how it would impact recovery time and general tolerance for exertion.

### bigknitwit — 2013-01-14T19:47:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-70755>

I started d-ribose and acetyl-l-carnitine after catching wind about ribose on this forum, and reading more about it in the book, "Fatigued to Fantastic." He recemmends both supplements, as well as ubiqunol and others. I started the ribose about 3 weeks ago, and started noticing a difference about 1.5 weeks ago. However, this was also around the time that I really increased my acetyl carnitine dose based on LEF dosing guidelines (now I take 2000mg/day). My head feels much better... much more stable. Everything feels more stable, for lack of a better description. Not perfect by any means, but I feel like I am pushing away from the bottom now, instead of trying to slow the sinking. I'm going to increase ubiquinol and start into Mg full tilt, and see where that gets me. I can't tell for sure whether it's the ribose, or the carnitine, or both, but I'm staying the course!

### diane — 2013-01-22T14:36:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-72327>

Hi Jena. Do you time your meals so that they're 4 hours before bed? I have (had?) adrenal fatigue as diagnosed by an ASI test, and I found the biggest difference for me was changing meal timing, blue blockers and timing of sleep. I now don't feel like I have AF symptoms other than getting out of the bed in the morning (although haven't done another ASI test). My energy throughout the day is great, and then I'm tired in the evening. My sleep quality has improved so much - even if I wake during the night, I feel the quality of my sleep has gotten better.

Of course everyone is different - and I'm sure some take longer than others to feel the difference.

### jenafraserrmt@gmail.com — 2013-01-22T14:56:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-72332>

Hi Diane,
I eat BAB within the 30 mins of waking (anywhere between 5:30 am and 7am) ... on the days I work, 10- 6pm, my lunch is 2-3pm and then I get home about 6:45 and am not very hungry but try to have something before 7pm, then lights out by 7:30pm, I read (and spot CT) by candle light until about 9pm. But then here's my conundrum, my naturopath has me taking my isocort at noon with a small snack so i'm not sure if i should change my work schedule to have a proper meal at noon, with maybe dinner as I leave work or as a snack at 5pm (i'm a massage therapist so i can't eat whenever since i'm pretty hands on with my patients - of course my electric table and my electric heat pad on the table are surely contributing to loss of electrons!!! rrrr)
My sleep quality right now is better but not great, if i wake up more than once in the night then i can't get out of bed very well in the morning, but if i only wake once at 2pm, i'm tired but up early, if i wake once at 4 i'm up and raring to go, and crashing about 10am, right when i start work.

I think overall I've had some small improvements in energy levels during the day, but i'm just frustrated with the slow improvements.

### Glen PDQ — 2013-01-22T23:35:58+0200

> > > > bigknitwit said
>

>
> > > > I started d-ribose and acetyl-l-carnitine after catching wind about ribose on this forum, and reading more about it in the book, "Fatigued to Fantastic." He recemmends both supplements, as well as ubiqunol and others. I started the ribose about 3 weeks ago, and started noticing a difference about 1.5 weeks ago. However, this was also around the time that I really increased my acetyl carnitine dose based on LEF dosing guidelines (now I take 2000mg/day). My head feels much better... much more stable. Everything feels more stable, for lack of a better description. Not perfect by any means, but I feel like I am pushing away from the bottom now, instead of trying to slow the sinking. I'm going to increase ubiquinol and start into Mg full tilt, and see where that gets me. I can't tell for sure whether it's the ribose, or the carnitine, or both, but I'm staying the course!
> > >
> > > I would suggest to you to make sure you are only using the reduced form of CoQ10. It is supposed to be many many times more bioavailable. Let's say you switched from 100 mg of plain CoQ10 to the reduced CoQ10. By making the switch you have just INCREASED your dosage of CoQ10 even though the mg are the same! Good journey to you.
> >
> > How do you tell the difference between the plain and the reduced CoQ10,
> >
> > please?
>
> The reduced is ubiquinol, the other is ubiquinone, or also I think often just labeled as CoQ10.

Unfortunately, the reduced form is more expensive, so that's one more way to tell.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-06T21:08:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-74473>

Okay, my experience with d-ribose...

First day I took it, I was still having major cravings, and d-rib fed those. I had somewhere between 7 & 10gm. I white-knuckled the cravings and they started to die down, as they usually do if I can manage a full day ignoring them with all my might.

Second day I took 5gm. No change in anything. Not too much of a problem with them feeding cravings that day, since I was no longer mega-primed.

Third day 5gm. Still nothing. Still dreadfully sleepy (it's 2pm, "that" time of day for me).

I'll keep ya posted.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-08T16:12:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-74848>

I'm making plans again!!!!!
Oh, this D-Ribose is wonderful for me. This is the fourth day since I've been taking it by the scoopful, and it's just what I needed, it seems. I first took just one scoop, with lemon and water as Lyndra suggested, and I did notice reduced cravings, fairly quickly. The next 2 days I took two scoops, at mid-morning and afternoon. Yesterday morning before I got out of bed, I found myself making plans. Nothing big, just like "take in the tiller for repair" and "ground the roost in the chicken coop." (And I did those things yesterday too! ) This morning I was making more plans, "like clean the fridge", and "prune the roses".

Sorry, but this is a Big Thing for me. Planning, unless for some big event, was just something I didn't do. It took too much energy to think, and I probably wouldn't feel like doing it anyway, and I would be disappointed in myself, and .... well .... I'm just a spontaneous-type girl. Yeah, that's the ticket.

So I would do things as I had the energy and time, but often I had neither. It was not depression, I've been there before, but more a conservation of energy type thing. Even when DH would start vocalizing plans for some far-off vacation, going into excruciating detail about what we would do first, and second, etc... I just had to tune him out. I couldn't see the point in using all that effort, to keep track of where he was going. Sad, really, when you can't spare the energy to even listen to someone's dreams.

I think I feel a bit of the zest for life returning.
I'm trying 3 scoops today.

I'd love to suggest this for my sister, but she is early in the journey. She just decided to give up gluten late last year. I doubt her mitochondria are ready for D-Ribose yet. Thanks, everyone, for relating your experiences!

### Glamazon — 2013-02-09T06:20:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-74990>

> [Patty Cakes said:](/goto/post?id=74906)
>
> I've used both Jarrow and Doctors Best brands that are pure powder. I have also used the tablets from Jarrow, just for the convenience when traveling. Don't like those as well.

I use this: [http://www.amazon.com/Now-Foods-D-Ribose-Powder-8-Ounce/dp/B000MLAO4Y](http://www.amazon.com/Now-Foods-D-Ribose-Powder-8-Ounce/dp/B000MLAO4Y) It warms me up, keeps me from nodding off in the morning and in the afternoon.

I don't know why anyone would rave about the taste. Yes, it has a sweetness. Yes, it mixes well in water but it makes me dry heave if I smell it. It's weird because the taste isn't really that strong. I am going to try it with lemon tomorrow.

I take 10 gms about 9 am and then another 10 at 1 pm. I am going to try and just take 20 tomorrow and see if that does it. I don't like carrying the bottle to work. I can smell it in my bag under the desk even though I keep it in a plastic bag too. (I have a sensitive nose.)

### Glamazon — 2013-02-09T06:29:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-74991>

I also want to add that I don't feel like going to bed as soon as I get home from work. It takes me through that slump. I also notice I walk a lot faster. It definitely has increased my energy level. I do notice a little headachy feeling an hour after I take it like I feel when my BP is high. I get a headache about once every couple of years if that so it is really noticeable when I do. A couple of aspirin and I am fine. The first time I took it I really wanted to throw up and started dry heaving about an hour later...at work no less in my super's office. Sigh. But the nausea eventually passed and I don't get it anymore. I have only been taking it since Monday. I started with the 5 gm dose 2x a day and now 10 gm 2x a day. I missed one morning and holy face plant on the desk! I could not keep my eyes awake. Fortunately, I had brought the powder with me to work and mixed it up in about a half a cup of water. In about an hour I was OK again.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-09T23:54:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-75086>

I caved and bought some d-ribose w/ extra money I got from a gift card sale. Sweet! Anyways I had some @ 2 pm in a coffee. (Don't mention about having a coffee @ 2pm lol)

I didn't have lunch today so I don't know if it was from that or the d-ribose but I did feel hungry an hour and a half later. So instead I made some tea and put it in that since I was figuring I might have felt hungry b/c my cells were liking this d-ribose and wanted some more. lol My first encounter with it I like it so far. My hands warmed up at 2 and I did not feel any headache or heaviness from having coffee too late. \*Note my hands don't normally warm up from coffee anyway.

I am hoping it is not a honey moon effect and I will see how it continues.

Btw I took 10 g @ 2 and then another 10g @ 3:30pm. LOL I don't know if that's too much in a short span of time, but I figure you can't really OD on this stuff. Also I figure that once my body/cells get more d-ribose it will be longer and longer before I feel the desire for it and less and less of a dose (so the theory goes).

+1 to the smell being likened to burnt marshmallow. Yum. +1 for it providing the right amount of sweet to whatever beverage you're drinking. But I think after the coffee and tea I like it best in coffee (whereas for something like honey I prefer honey in tea...you don't need as much and you taste it more). I think I will use d-ribose as my new 'sugar'.

### Lyndra — 2013-02-10T01:53:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-75116>

I"ve been taking both Q10 (ubiquinol) and d-ribose for the last few weeks. Late this morning I went to the gym for a strength workout and then immediately walked the dog when I came home. I had a surprising amount of energy! Usually I can barely keep pace with her (when she's not stopped sniffing at something) but today it was no problem. Lunch was delayed for about half an hour after we got home but I wasn't especially hungry when I did eat. Something seems to be working ... plus I am feeling warm most of the time (but not in a hot flashy kind of way).

### diane — 2013-02-10T01:56:20+0200

Funny coincidence today....

Went to the place where I buy the raw food for my dogs. They sell supplements there as well. I look on the shelf - and what do you know, D-rhibose. So I ask them what they use it with dogs for - and she wasn't really sure yet, but had read studies on how it helps performance. So I bought the bottle (cheaper than the health food store), but after reading EMF-4 yesterday, am going to put the dogs on it as well. She even had Vitamin D3 drops there too. And kelp. I've been putting a CoQ10 pill in their ground beef every day. Now to figure out how to deliver the d-rhibose. I'll either mush it into their ground beef, or maybe put it in some bone broth (I will give them the end of mine if I don't eat it all - and they love it! Just enough to lick the bottom of the bowl).

From EMF-4 it sounds like all mammals need d-rhibose.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-11T00:21:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-75294>

Last night after taking 10 g d-ribose @ 2 pm and again 10g @3:3pm and then 5g @7:30 (Btw I HATE eating so freaking late but I'm staying with my parents for next few/several months...so I don't know what to do about this, since it is polite to eat with family and that is when they eat. They are being kind by helping me out...so I don't know what to do ) (Also when I eat late I now go to bed later too, to allow at least/best as possible 4 hrs between food and bed).

Anyways, update:

Went to bed @ 11:30pm. Fell asleep the same. Woke up at 'whatever' time and didn't look at clock to see what time. Fell back asleep pretty quickly. \*This is the same pattern for last few nights so this seems independent of d-ribose.

Possible difference for d-ribose. I naturally started to wake a bit before 7 sometime but don't know what time for sure. Lazed in bed until 7:18AM.

I cannot report on my feelings today since I have some confounding variables from another experiment I stupidly decided to try. UGH! My brain feels frizzed and I feel drugged. But weird, I clear and can type....but I feel like I can't walk properly (or straight). I feel like my speech is slurry too....\*Note this is not d-ribose effects this is from another failed and now ABANDONED experiment!!

\*smashheadondeskyoustupidretardwhatmadeyouthinktoevenTRY\* I also almost feel like a dry heaving.

...

I drank more green tea and took extra Mg maybe that helps. My brain feels better now, phew. I just feel a bit tired (no d-ribose this PM). My hands are nice and warm right now though which I like. I feel normal temperature and not excessively sweating in some areas and cold in other areas.

I'm getting a bit bummed as to my TOTM for Feb. I don't think it's going to arrive... ### freesia — 2013-02-15T02:11:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-75932>

I think  I understand it's about supporting the efficiency of the PPP and may be needed at least short term why we make the shift there? Longer term depending on circumstances.

It may be needed especially for those who've been solidly burning the PC pathway too hard too long - like fibromyalgia sufferers for example?

As far as I can tell, lepin-melanocortin and PPP both supported will provide a certain level of "terrain" protection against environmental stressors.

My information is based on my experience and my best attempt (so far) at understanding it. Warning.Take it FWIW!

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T04:41:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-76073>

My report: taking d-rib at 5, then 10, then 15gm/day for two weeks, and results:

1 - bloating & water retention won't go down no matter what I do
2 - subtle but solid increase in physical energy
3 - mood meltdowns disappeared then got worse than ever
4 - sugar binge

In that order.

I think #1 could be from very low carb intake on a few days (berries). But the swelling is drastically out of proportion to that. Or it could be that my new ox bile sucks. I kind of suspect the latter, b/c cramps & runs are back here and there.

I think #2 could be the d-rib or continued increase in iodine or continued thyroid improvement or continued improvement from circadian T3. Not sure.

I think #3 could be b/c being on the slopes made all better, and within a day of being back at home, I was falling apart mid-afternoon again, in more dramatic fashion than before. I am doing things I never in my life would have imagined, like crying because I have to leave the house. I used to be one who disdained those who were affected by their emotions. Now my emotions are running amok.

I think #4 could be the d-rib, too. It may not be a sugar, but it TASTES like it, and my taste buds know, just like they know when you eat Splenda or xylitol or aspartame that things taste sweet. I had a disaster tonight after nearly a week clean.

I'm out of d-rib now. Anyone know where to buy it in bulk for a good cost? That little LEF container lasted two measly weeks. I was planning to go without a week or two to see if energy runs low again, but then was frightened by the thought of losing the energy, and now am even more frightened by the binge. Dunno...

### freesia — 2013-02-16T10:42:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-76091>

Low NADPH is very common in ME/CFS, probably Autism spectrum disorders too. Other neuroinflammatory conditions? Maybe?

Here is a quote from the late Rich Van Konynenburg [http://forums.phoenixrising.me/inde...fs-nadph-measurement-at-riordan-clinic.15641/](http://forums.phoenixrising.me/index.php?threads/new-biomarker-test-for-me-cfs-nadph-measurement-at-riordan-clinic.15641/) ....after a test detecting low NADPH was patented as a diagnostic test for ME/CFS last year. Some of it seems relevant here.

> Note that low NADPH has a lot of ramifications: NADPH is normally used to convert folic acid to THF. It is also used to reduce oxidized glutathione. It also supports Phase I detox by the CYP450 enzymes. It does other good things, also.
>
> I think that the reason it is low in ME/CFS is that the main source of it is the pentose phosphate shunt on the glycolysis pathway. When the mitochondria become dysfunctional in ME/CFS, the cells are forced to ramp up glycolysis to try to make up the shortfall in ATP production. This is done primarily by the enzyme phosphofructokinase, which is upregulated by low ATP and high AMP. When this occurs, more of the flow goes through the main glycolysis chain, and less is available to enter the pentose phosphate shunt, so less NADP+ gets reduced to NADPH, and therefore, NADPH goes down.

For me (with ME/CFS) it seems as if getting more PPP action is a good way to go. Perhaps anyone who needs to improve their ATP AMP ratio.

Re what it burns for fuel... glucose 6 phosphate: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glucose\_6-phosphate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glucose_6-phosphate) Anna I'm not sure if that's sugars from dietary sources exactly. It's too complex for me to understand...it seems to be related to DNA and RNA...I'm way out of my league here!

OK, so **how** does this - supporting the PPP - tie in with the cold pathway? I need that part explaining to me like I'm a ten year old. Preferably with pictures.
Too many questions.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-16T14:38:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-76097>

> [Anna K. said:](/goto/post?id=76087)
>
> If it the pathway that recycles left over ATP, then why do you need to feed it d-ribose? Recycle means re-use...not get new stuff to use...

A recycling facility takes in my old paper and turns it into new paper, but it needs machines to do that. Those machines need fuel, oil, replacement parts when they get old. If I keep using the same machines for years and years and it runs out of fuel or parts need replacing, and I don't do that, I can't recycle anymore.

Recycling requires not just the old parts, but the equipment and the fuel for the equipment needed to do the actual recycling.

### freesia — 2013-02-22T05:15:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-76988>

In it's first stage (the hexose monophosphate shunt) the PPP generates NADPH as the reducing agent...and you end up with plenty of reduced glutathione in the cells - which is always a good thing.
_Reduction is the gain of electrons, or a decrease in oxidation state._ I guess if you don't have enough of this you end up with toxicity, inflammation...poor genetic expression.

### Destiny — 2013-02-27T14:14:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-77988>

I stopped using the D-ribose a couple of days ago. My FBS started steadily climbing up. The day I stopped it was 105. This was after many months of being in 70's -80's. My hearth rate went over 100 upon rising and the palpitations were truly uncomfortable all day long and during the night. I also could feel that my cortisol was in overdrive. I started feeling very much off and my night sleep was taking a hit too. This morning my FBG was 88, heart rate 94 and palpitations are weaker and going away.

I actually also stopped not only d-ribose but also the nasal oxytocin, decreased COQ10 since those were initiated at the same time with d-ribose. So for what I know, it might have been the effect of oxytocin. Not sure. I guess it is never a good idea to start many new things all at once. I really need to grow some patience!

> Okay, holey moley. I took 10-15g d-ribose an hour ago. I am shaky & weak now. Just took my blood sugar. 49.
>
>
> !!!!
>
> What is going on?!
>
> ETA I'm not the only one. [http://www.livestrong.com/article/496655-d-ribose-safety/](http://www.livestrong.com/article/496655-d-ribose-safety/) Taking more than 10gm has a known side effect of hypoglycemia. Problem is, I can't take just 5gm. I want more and more and more.
>
> It does not look like this is a good idea for me.
>
> ETA - it looks like much of d-ribose is made from corn sugar. I can't have corn!!! How do I find out which d-ribose isn't made from corn?!

The maximum I have ever taken at once of d-ribose is 7.5 mg and I have not had it make me hypoglycemic. I have been taking it for over a year. I also eat ketogenic and take 2500 mg of metformin per day. Also, you and Gretchen must have guts of steel if you can take 10-15 mg at once without having diarrhea! I have to take it just after a meal for that same reason.

Where did you read that it is made from corn? Are you buying some with added maltodextrin, or did you read that the d-ribose itself is made from corn?

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-12T01:13:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-80121>

Yes, I'm sure this is some major clue that others, like Gretchen, tolerate 20g well, and I don't.

I wasn't saying last week that it was raising blood sugar, but that it made my head feel woozy like I had too much sugar. When I had that feeling in the past, I always assumed it was increased blood sugar, but then I started testing and realized it wasn't. That's what made me test last night. I wanted to prove to myself that it wasn't raised, and indeed, it was shockingly low.

I'm not sure that my EMF issues are worse than Gretchen's, however, we all respond differently to unnatural stressors. I think that how I respond negatively to EMF vs how she responds (like the migraines) will tell us something, somehow about our issues.

Just one example of the corn issue when I google "d-ribose corn": [http://www.livestrong.com/article/496655-d-ribose-safety/](http://www.livestrong.com/article/496655-d-ribose-safety/)

However, if you don't have that deficiency, it may work. You would have to try it to see._

MG, I'm not talking about you behind your back! Just my opinion, but if you are having that intense a reaction to d-ribose, you should consider only taking it with food, or not at all. If your BG drops to 49 (!!!) after 10 grams of ribose, I'm not surprised you would be a trembling, dizzy, hungry, craving maniac. Low blood sugar does exactly this to you...signals you to EAT NOW.

You should probably assess the cost/benefit ratio of d-ribose. You may not need it anyway. Also, if sugar cravings are a known problem for you, I would think the yummy, sugary taste of d-ribose could certainly set those cravings into high gear again.

### Martin — 2013-08-08T22:45:51+0300

### persistence — 2014-03-14T08:48:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-115327>

I'm starting to experiment with d-ribose and have just started to look through the literature on it as well. The positive effects have been reported here and elsewhere, but I was looking for studies that might point out possible adverse effects. One study I found suggested that d-ribose would form advanced glycation products with proteins more rapidly than glucose:

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0024623](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024623)

I do realize nothing in this study suggests any specific level at which ribose might create significant AGEs in humans. Ribose might be quite safe up to a significant percentage of caloric intake. But the study suggests the germ of an idea that at \*some\* intake level ribose might become toxic by this mechanism. The devil is in the details, and I have no details. But still the study makes me think you wouldn't want to go hog wild on d-ribose (e.g., 1000 calories a day) without studying this more.

### persistence — 2014-03-14T09:23:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-115334>

I have only taken two small doses of d-ribose so far (5 gm after walking and 10 gm before dinner). That's probably not enough to assess any impact on my muscle recovery from exercise or ability to regenerate glycogen. But both times I have noted an incredible mental calmness, about 30 minutes after taking it, which allows me to focus and work a bit better.

I have a strange neurological issue I have had since about 18 where my left eye is always wanting to close and go to sleep. Right eye stays wide awake. No one would ever notice this in conversation with me. It's something I can easily control in interactions with people, but the internal feeling of left-side sleepiness is constant and annoying and it is there all the time even when I get up from a night of restful sleep. After taking d-ribose, I am no longer aware of that neurological symptom. I don't feel the left-side sleepiness at all. So that's a fairly profound and noticeable thing for me.

Does d-ribose cross the blood brain barrier and directly provide energy to brain cells? I read one paper that suggested d-ribose does not cross the blood brain barrier in significant amounts. I wonder what that would suggest that this neurological symptom appears when glucose is the energy source but disappears when d-ribose is available in surplus? Any neurologists in the house?

The whole effect with mental calmness only lasts about 30 minutes for me.

### persistence — 2014-03-14T10:18:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-115337>

Just to have it on file here, this is a nice overview of different theories of how d-ribose temporarily suppresses glucose production in the liver:

[http://www.bioenergy.com/pdf/mf/sl/ribose & blood sugar/ribose\_blood sugar\_science 070303.pdf](http://www.bioenergy.com/pdf/mf/sl/ribose%20&%20blood%20sugar/ribose_blood%20sugar_science%20070303.pdf)

There is some increase in insulin when d-ribose is taken, but that alone is not enough to explain all of the glucose-lowering effects. Several researchers think d-ribose is interfering with an enzyme needed by the liver to convert glycogen to glucose.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-14T16:08:09+0200

Interesting, what you have written in the last couple posts, Pone. I'm not sure the body actually uses d-ribose for energy. Jack hinted about this some time ago.
Perhaps d-ribose is like DHA from seafood. Too precious to be used for ordinary energy. Maybe it is used for infrastructure instead? DNA and RNA?

My experience with d-ribose is that larger amounts aren't necessarily better. 10 g would probably be my max. I did try baking a cheesecake using d-ribose, but I didn't like the results. Texture was off.

It definitely gives me a lot of energy, but I wonder now if I am beginning to get too dependent on it for that... I noticed that my energy was significantly lacking when I would forget to use it....

### persistence — 2014-03-14T22:03:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-115451>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=115366)
>
> Interesting, what you have written in the last couple posts, Pone. I'm not sure the body actually uses d-ribose for energy. Jack hinted about this some time ago.
> Perhaps d-ribose is like DHA from seafood. Too precious to be used for ordinary energy. Maybe it is used for infrastructure instead? DNA and RNA?
>
> My experience with d-ribose is that larger amounts aren't necessarily better. 10 g would probably be my max. I did try baking a cheesecake using d-ribose, but I didn't like the results. Texture was off.

Hello nonchalant. If you reference that link I put up on the insulin and glucose effects of d-ribose, and start browsing the literature, what you will see is that there are many studies that do an analysis of mannitol, ribose and fructose and compare their effects against glucose. So - right or wrong - the researchers treat ribose as a sugar, but a sugar with a different metabolic pathway than glucose.

I'm not qualified to even guess at how d-ribose is metabolized. And - by the way - you get the feeling reading the science papers that the researchers don't fully understand ribose either. You read a lot of comments in those papers about their observing some effect and not fully being able to explain why it is happening. I look forward to reading Jack's stuff and reading more widely about ribose metabolism. If d-ribose is capable of glycating proteins as harmful AGEs, then I'm not sure it matters if they produce energy or simply facilitate other biochemical processes. The end result of glycation of a protein is still a bad thing?

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced\_glycation\_end-product](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_glycation_end-product)

Anyway, let's be clear that there are no free rides. You eat glucose and you get some AGEs too. In fact the uncontrolled diabetic is glycating his body away internally and doing huge damage. You slow roast meats in the oven at high temperature, and you just turned that meat into a glycation factory and you are serving yourself a large amount of AGEs. There are some papers out there showing AGEs in common foods using common cooking techniques, and it's clear AGEs are a big problem just in common foods cooked at high, dry heat. The AGE link I put up was just a warning flag that maybe you wouldn't want to drink many hundreds of grams of ribose every day without really studying it.

In my case I'm looking at difficult trade offs. I'm having problems keeping glycogen in my muscle. I can't use glucose to replenish that because I'm prediabetic and my insulin resistance means I won't process whatever glucose I present to my body efficiently. If I were able to use ribose post-workout and it managed to build back glycogen for me, while simultaneously keeping my glucose low, that might be a very good thing. I'm at the very beginning of the journey in understanding ribose. As long as I'm doing work to see what others have found about it, I am just sharing that research with an interested audience that might help me put all of those data points into a framework.

### persistence — 2014-03-14T22:54:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-115462>

> [persistence said:](/goto/post?id=115327)
>
> I'm starting to experiment with d-ribose and have just started to look through the literature on it as well. The positive effects have been reported here and elsewhere, but I was looking for studies that might point out possible adverse effects. One study I found suggested that d-ribose would form advanced glycation products with proteins more rapidly than glucose:
>
> [http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024623](http://www.biomedcentral.com/1471-2121/10/10/abstract)
>
> I do realize nothing in this study suggests any specific level at which ribose might create significant AGEs in humans. Ribose might be quite safe up to a significant percentage of caloric intake. But the study suggests the germ of an idea that at \*some\* intake level ribose might become toxic by this mechanism. The devil is in the details, and I have no details. But still the study makes me think you wouldn't want to go hog wild on d-ribose (e.g., 1000 calories a day) without studying this more.

Oh, really important point here: these researchers may have been loading up the mice with way more ribose than they could safely metabolize. In other words, they may have deliberately overloaded their systems and left a lot of ribose hanging around in the blood. That excess of ribose is what would allow the harmful glycations they measured. That's a totally different scenario than just taking the amount of ribose your body can metabolize quickly.

I didn't read the paper critically enough to determine if the doses they were giving were megadoses or not.
