### persistence — 2014-03-22T05:27:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117082>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=80006)
>
> After two weeks on, two weeks off, and a week back on d-ribose, all I can say definitively is that it \*does\* slightly increase my cravings, I do definitely crave it, and when I eat a bunch, say 10-15gm in one sitting, it makes my head spin the same way too much sugar or fruit will do.
>
> I must be totally broken. LOL.

In the literature, 10 grams or more of D-Ribose has a well-known effect of inducing slight hypoglycemia after 45 to 60 minutes. So, \*if\* you had low blood sugar to start, ingested 15 grams of D-Ribose, then no surprise at all that you had the reading of glucose = 55 a while later.

The studies also agree with your observation that five grams of D-Ribose does NOT induce much hypoglycemia.

You might want to use a glucometer to measure your blood sugar prior to ingesting the D-Ribose. If blood sugar is low, you might want to have a small amount of starchy carb at same time as D-Ribose to offset the hypoglycemia. You should be able to balance it out with experiment.

### Josh — 2014-03-22T05:39:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117083>

In my experience, it will destabilize things without enough Ubiquinol and Magnesium. The magnesium seems to be the most important, think ATP production and proper hydration for protein synthesis and unfolding perhaps....

### persistence — 2014-03-22T05:40:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117084>

> [MamaGrok said:](/goto/post?id=80256)
>
> Yes, 5gm 3x/day does work fine for me, but 10 is too much, and I can't stop at 5. Gar!
>
> I haven't seen any benefit from it yet, but I was hoping to. I will just stick with all the other mito rx stuff for now, and keep d-rib in mind for a future when I don't have any risk of cravings getting out of control again.

MamaGrok, D-Ribose isn't stimulating your cravings. It's the steep and rapid drop in glucose that stimulates hunger. Control the rapid drop in glucose and you will probably stop the cravings. I'm sure that is tricky because too much glucose and you then get a steep rise in glucose and then rebound hypoglycemia, which just keeps you on a cravings treadmill.

Wonder if you should think about installing a Dexcom 24x7 glucose monitor and start to learn in real detail what level of starchy carb intake would relieve your hypoglycemia without causing glucose to rise abnormally. You might want to keep a bowl of sliced yams around and learn to weigh out just enough to give you 5 grams of starchy carbs. Start to experiment with what ultra-small starchy carb meals do to your glucose levels and how you rebound from that. My guess is your hypoglycemia can be relieved by 5 to 10 grams of starchy carbs and you are probably ingesting 20 grams or more.

### Josh — 2014-03-22T05:59:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117086>

> [persistence said:](/goto/post?id=115462)
>
> Oh, really important point here: these researchers may have been loading up the mice with way more ribose than they could safely metabolize. In other words, they may have deliberately overloaded their systems and left a lot of ribose hanging around in the blood. That excess of ribose is what would allow the harmful glycations they measured. That's a totally different scenario than just taking the amount of ribose your body can metabolize quickly.
>
> I didn't read the paper critically enough to determine if the doses they were giving were megadoses or not.

I did not read it that way. I understood that D-Ribose causes glycation with the bovine albumin more rapidly than any other sugar in vitro. Furthermore it was observed to damage nerve cells in vitro at concentrations lower than those found in the blood. However there is no evidence that in vivo it causes these issues making it unclear as to what other mechanisms and factors may counter this propensity in vivo. They identify several areas where things are "unclear" . I do not know enough to figure this out. I can guess that as they readily cause glycation and misfolding, the redox potential and hydration will play a significant role in their interactions with proteins.

### persistence — 2014-03-22T06:05:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117088>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117086)

> I did not read it that way. I understood that D-Ribose causes glycation with the bovine albumin more rapidly than any other sugar in vitro. Furthermore it was observed to damage nerve cells in vitro at concentrations lower than those found in the blood. However there is no evidence that in vivo it causes these issues making it unclear as to what other mechanisms and factors may counter this propensity in vivo. They identify several areas where things are "unclear" . I do not know enough to figure this out. I can guess that as they readily cause glycation and misfolding, the redox potential and hydration will play a significant role in their interactions with proteins.

I am completely freaked out here. I don't understand what is going on but I am posting a link to an animal study and when you click on it the browser is opening to a totally different study, on bovine serum. That is the WRONG study. Let me try here again.

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0024623](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024623)

Okay, the above link now works. Wow, that is frustrating.

Look at the biopsy brain slices they did on those animals at the ends of the experiment. Their brains are completely glycated from excess Ribose. It is NOT a serum study!!!

The more I think about this study, the less I worry about it. It only pertains if you take large doses of Ribose beyond what your body can quickly metabolize. I think the person taking five grams has nothing to worry about. I take 15 grams after exercise and I think the muscles are so starved in that state they have no problems metabolizing 15 grams of Ribose.

The guy or gal I worry about is someone who is insulin resistant and takes 50+ grams of Ribose all at once, without benefit of exercise. That \*might\* be a situation where a lot of unmetabolized Ribose sits around in the blood for hours, and some of that can glycate proteins.

### persistence — 2014-03-22T06:14:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117090>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117087)
>
> Have you ever questioned how LR and IR you may be and whether vigorous exercise is your friend at this stage of the journey?

I am starting to read through the Leptin Rx, and I did cut back on the amount of exercise I do.

I am frustrated by my attempts to discover how LR and IR I may be because:

1) My Endo refuses to let me get the tests that Jack wants to help diagnose LR. I'll get them anyway, but more delay, more hassle.

2) My Endo refuses to let me get the glucose-insulin tolerance test that would show insulin resistance until I increase my carb intake (which is a whole can of worms we don't need to discuss here...I'm defining the obstacle not agreeing with it).

There is a really simple test they can give you in fasting state to quantify insulin resistance known as the "Insulin Tolerance Test". They inject small amounts of insulin and watch glucose response for just 15 minutes. It turns out that calibrates with high accuracy to the clamp tests they do in research studies. Yet almost no lab gives this test. Almost no Endo even knows it exists. When I told a Stanford Endo about it she said "Oh, that's cool. No, we don't do it."

So even defining the condition, in order to start attacking it, is not a simple thing sometimes. There are no excuses for not getting to the bottom line and getting the information you need. But I'm learning you shouldn't expect everyone to support you.

I'm already going in this direction Inger. And if I have many more doctor visits like this last one, I'll soon be walking around insane, naked and tanning.

But, I think it is important to solve problems you really have, and don't waste time solving ones you don't have. If my pancreas is weak and doesn't produce enough insulin, that's one case. If the pancreas is strong and produces a lot of insulin, but my body doesn't respond to it, that's a different case. If my body responds to insulin well, but my liver just overproduces glucose between meals, that's a third case. You can't blame me for wanting to know which problem I actually have? I mean treat the actual disease, not some theory about what the disease might be.

I'm doing the Leptin Rx in any case.

### persistence — 2014-03-23T22:52:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117465>

Dr Rosedale has a short blip on his web site claiming that he doesn't think dietary Ribose will enter the cell:
[https://drrosedale.tenderapp.com/kb...oken=c7a3586be5dd30e86b9014d93930ec362d87c1a8](https://drrosedale.tenderapp.com/kb/supplements/d-ribose-does-it-effect-insulin-or-leptin?anon_token=c7a3586be5dd30e86b9014d93930ec362d87c1a8)

His claim is that it is better to take Benfotiamine which will convert to Ribose.

Any comment on any of the above?

It seems to me you would never find Benfotiamine in anything other than pill form, and it is pretty hard to make that cost effective for a metabolically significant amount of energy supplementation (e.g., the bio equivalent of 30 grams of D-Ribose per day)?

I am definitely seeing faster recovery after exercise using D-Ribose, and how is that possible if it is not entering the cell and being used to make ATP? I also see a lot of research has been done on cardiac muscle benefitting from dietary D-Ribose, and again how would that be possible if there was not some of it getting into the cell? Exercise physiologists have demonstrated muscle recoveries post-exercise with Ribose > 300%. I don't understand how that is possible if Ribose is not entering the cell?

It is still an interesting question how much of dietary Ribose goes into the cell for energy production, and how much of it just sits around in the blood and is disposed of by glycation with proteins or other means?

### persistence — 2014-03-23T23:22:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117473>

Another study on D-Ribose glycating proteins more rapidly than Glucose:
[http://www.researchgate.net/publication/221774391\_D-ribose\_in\_glycation\_and\_protein\_aggregation](http://www.researchgate.net/publication/221774391_D-ribose_in_glycation_and_protein_aggregation)
Full text link is on the right side.

This study claims that when you take enough Ribose to induce hypoglycemia that they can measure increased glycation of serum proteins at the same time. This is what I hate about nutrition. Every study you can find that says a substance can save your life, someone else publishes a study saying it will kill you.

The researchers recommend that you if you are ingesting Ribose for any therapeutic reasons, that you should track your glycated serum proteins over time. I found a Labcorp test 001693 CPT Code 83036 for this here:
[https://www.labcorp.com/wps/portal/...BJUzNPNE4yTjY2ODAvdmlld1Rlc3Q!/?testId=407963](https://www.labcorp.com/wps/portal/!ut/p/c1/04_SB8K8xLLM9MSSzPy8xBz9CP0os_hACzO_QCM_IwMLXyM3AyNjMycDU2dXQwN3M6B8JG55AwMCuv088nNT9SP1o8zjQ11Ngg09LY0N_N2DjQw8g439TfyM_MzMLAz0Q_QjnYCKIvEqKsiNKDfUDVQEAOrk-dE!/dl2/d1/L0lJWmltbUEhL3dQRUJGUUFoTlFBaERhQUVBWEtHL1lJNXlsdyEhLzdfVUU0UzFJOTMwT0dTMjBJUzNPNE4yTjY2ODAvdmlld1Rlc3Q!/?testId=407963)

I'm not clear on the difference between A1C and what the above test shows. I guess A1C shows glucose attaching to hemoglobin, whereas the test above shows glucose attaching to protein.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-24T19:44:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117637>

Benfotiamine requires concomitant use of high dose B vitamins especially B 1. to get D ribose to work. I dont buy Rosedale's argument. many studies have shown D Ribose gets into the cytosol and into mitochondria in the cardiac literature on heart failure cases. Mushrooms are the best B vitamin supplement out there. In T2D with neuropathy and nephropathy high-dose thiamine and benfotiamine therapy increased transketolase expression in renal glomeruli, increased the conversion of triosephosphates to ribose-5-phosphate, and strongly inhibited the development of microalbuminuria.

### persistence — 2014-03-24T20:56:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117658>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=117599)
>
> I like your line of inquiry here. How might we deal with each of the three scenarios?

1) If my pancreas is weak, then I suppose I just need to avoid things that spike glucose. I kind of doubt that this is my issue, but you would think the Endo would at least want to know that?!

2) If I am insulin resistant, then I have to work on supplements that raise insulin sensitivity, as well as lower carbs.

3) If I only overproduce glucose between meals (and definitely in the morning I am getting above 120 glucose without eating), then maybe I have to look for what is overstimulating glucagon release and treat that. I might also look at glucagon suppressors of some kind?

Regarding 3), I have noticed that if I take a short nap my glucose falls from 95-to-100 into the 78-to-80 range. Within one hour of waking I am back to 95 to 100. Any ideas on what this might suggest? Is this cortisol? I feel like the Endo should have more interest in following through on clues like that, and combine with tests to at least understand what the ultimate causes are for these symptoms.

If you don't separate things out, you end up just throwing buckets of water in the direction of heat, instead of right onto the flame.

### persistence — 2014-03-25T11:19:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117825>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=117599)
>
> I like your line of inquiry here. How might we deal with each of the three scenarios?

Dali, you will enjoy this article, which is by far the best explanation I have ever read for how insulin behaves, and what insulin resistance actually means:

[http://bja.oxfordjournals.org/content/85/1/69.long](http://bja.oxfordjournals.org/content/85/1/69.long)

One of the main points being made there is that most of diabetes is about the liver overproducing glucose and NOT about tissues failing to absorb glucose. He gives example that muscles absorb glucose through GLUT4 rather than insulin alone. By implication, what insulin resistance actually represents is a failure of the system to downregulate glucagon when presented with higher and higher levels of insulin. That also leaves open that many people with normal insulin levels - or lower-than-normal insulin levels - might also see high fasting glucose simply because their endogenous insulin fails to regulate glucose production in the liver.

Assuming you do not have high persistent levels of insulin, and insulin resistance in the liver, what might cause glucagon production to be overstimulated? I'm wondering if it might make sense at taking things that downregulate glucagon.

P.S., Not sure this belongs in a D-Ribose thread. If you know of a better place for it I can move it.

### Dali Dula — 2014-03-25T15:55:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117859>

> [persistence said:](/goto/post?id=117825)
>
> Dali, you will enjoy this article, which is by far the best explanation I have ever read for how insulin behaves, and what insulin resistance actually means:
>
> [http://bja.oxfordjournals.org/content/85/1/69.long](http://bja.oxfordjournals.org/content/85/1/69.long)
>
> One of the main points being made there is that most of diabetes is about the liver overproducing glucose and NOT about tissues failing to absorb glucose. He gives example that muscles absorb glucose through GLUT4 rather than insulin alone. By implication, what insulin resistance actually represents is a failure of the system to downregulate glucagon when presented with higher and higher levels of insulin. That also leaves open that many people with normal insulin levels - or lower-than-normal insulin levels - might also see high fasting glucose simply because their endogenous insulin fails to regulate glucose production in the liver.
>
> Assuming you do not have high persistent levels of insulin, and insulin resistance in the liver, what might cause glucagon production to be overstimulated? I'm wondering if it might make sense at taking things that downregulate glucagon.
>
> P.S., Not sure this belongs in a D-Ribose thread. If you know of a better place for it I can move it.

Thank for this. I did enjoy the article. So is there a difference between over production of glucose by the liver and insulin resistance at the liver? Interesting that fasting hyperglycemia is not due to tissue IR. What methods and or treatment other than exogenous insulin are available to us to reduce glucose production by the liver. Please keep digging. My a1C and FBG have been creeping up for the last 6-8 months after 2 years of excellent control. Not sure why.

### persistence — 2014-03-25T20:47:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117890>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=117859)
>
> Thank for this. I did enjoy the article. So is there a difference between over production of glucose by the liver and insulin resistance at the liver? Interesting that fasting hyperglycemia is not due to tissue IR. What methods and or treatment other than exogenous insulin are available to us to reduce glucose production by the liver. Please keep digging. My a1C and FBG have been creeping up for the last 6-8 months after 2 years of excellent control. Not sure why.

That article really throws my understanding of things into a dark well. I thought I was starting to understand insulin resistance, and now it is morphing into something else.

It looks like the primary disorder is the rate of glucose produced by the liver. Is this because the liver does not pay attention to insulin, or is it that the pancreas fails to downregulate glucagon when insulin is high? It's not clear to me. Even more frustrating to me is that the Endocrinologists never try to characterize the disease in the actual patient. Why don't they try to measure endogenous insulin patterns and dietary insulin responses more carefully? Don't they care that a given patient overproduces glucagon and has normal insulin, versus a different patient with high insulin resistance?

The article seems to be saying that when your glucose jumps high, your muscles and liver are still uptaking glucose at a steady rate. In fact, the implication of one of his graphs is that at higher glucose levels your muscles are uptaking \*more\* glucose not less.

If that is the case, when we take things to make us more insulin sensitive, like alpha lipoic acid, all we are doing is increasing the amount of glucose our muscles take out of circulation:
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8922368](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8922368)

We aren't actually doing anything to adjust the basic imbalanced level of glucose production in the liver. So, like you, I am wondering what substances will directly lower glucose production in the liver? I remember reading somewhere that green coffee extract does that. Others?

### Dali Dula — 2014-03-25T21:08:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-117893>

In seeking actionable measures I try to not only think of substances. I think that this is a signaling issue. Where is communication broken and how do we address it. I suspect it is related to cortisol. I have been looking at cortisol research lately and it is deep.
Your Endo thinks their job is to maintain lab values. From info you have posted I am sure you Endo thinks you are fine
If you want to swim in deeper water get the 23andme test.

### persistence — 2014-05-17T00:05:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-127270>

> [persistence said:](/goto/post?id=117088)
>
> I am completely freaked out here. I don't understand what is going on but I am posting a link to an animal study and when you click on it the browser is opening to a totally different study, on bovine serum. That is the WRONG study. Let me try here again.
>
> [http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0024623](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024623)
>
> Okay, the above link now works. Wow, that is frustrating.
>
> Look at the biopsy brain slices they did on those animals at the ends of the experiment. Their brains are completely glycated from excess Ribose. It is NOT a serum study!!!
>
> The more I think about this study, the less I worry about it. It only pertains if you take large doses of Ribose beyond what your body can quickly metabolize. I think the person taking five grams has nothing to worry about. I take 15 grams after exercise and I think the muscles are so starved in that state they have no problems metabolizing 15 grams of Ribose.
>
> The guy or gal I worry about is someone who is insulin resistant and takes 50+ grams of Ribose all at once, without benefit of exercise. That \*might\* be a situation where a lot of unmetabolized Ribose sits around in the blood for hours, and some of that can glycate proteins.

I read today that fructose is seven times more likely than glucose to glycate with proteins and cause harmful AGE glycation products. So Ribose is apparently not unique in its tendency to glycate proteins.

That gives some sense to the Perfect Health Diet recommendation to greatly limit fructose intake, and probably one could apply their guidelines to D-Ribose intake levels as well.

### WereBear — 2015-07-08T18:02:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-experience-with-d-ribose.5058/post-171395>

> [Cpt.Tired said:](/goto/post?id=168889)
>
> Good for you for quitting coffee! I need that inspiration

I dropped coffee and got some sleep improvement, so it was the right thing to do. But I still longed for the warm hearty beverage, and herbal tea wasn't cutting it. But I found what I wanted in roasted, ground, cocoa beans. The theobromine in it is very gentle, did not disturb my sleep, and the flavonoids heal glucocorticoid receptors, according to one paper I read.

And it tastes fantastic!

I like d-ribose; I've only been taking it for a couple of months, but I do feel it helps. Strange thing though -- it's so SWEET. And I no longer like such!

# Thread: Birthday party success!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/birthday-party-success.5059/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### amber_tyrrell@hotmail.com — 2012-12-05T05:25:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/birthday-party-success.5059/post-66425>

I just wanted to share with you all that my daughter had a birthday 'sleepover' with 4 of her friends at our house last friday night... I usually HATE birthday parties, and I had quite a revelation the next day after I had taken all of her little friends home - I really enjoyed it because the food was all paleo! Â Normally, you see, I would eat some of the sweets, and have some of the birthday cake (which would have been wheat), and then I would feel like absolute rubbish, absolutely exhausted and I just would want all the little darlings to go home NOW!! Â This time I made a paleo chocolate cake with coconut and almond flour and coconut sugar, and I made paleo pizza, we made some fruit kebabs (strawberries are in season now - yay), and my energy/moods levels were fantastic because I didn't have any rubbish food! Â So the revelation for me was that I don't actually hate birthday parties at all - its just that in the past the food made me feel terrible and I associated that with birthday parties being the biggest hassle ever! Â I enjoyed every moment of the little darlings being here. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### Thor — 2012-12-05T21:37:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-fry-oysters-without-breading-them.5066/post-66520>

.

Are you asking about **Rocky Mountain Oysters**? { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Â  LoLÂ Â Â 

Â 

I"m blessed to live in the Pacific Northwest - home to some of the best seafood.Â  As an Executive Chef I'm a kid in a candy store....

Â 

You could fry the oysters with a non-bread safe paleo coating...Â Â  use raw organic almonds & place them in a food processor - try to get the nuts to almost flour like - still a bit crunchy.Â  Don't over do it.Â Â  Use the almond pieces like you would Panko and egg wash.Â  Extra yummy.Â Â 

Â 

The best way aside from raw is to steam the oysters.

Â 

Use a steamer kettle/pot

-

    1 Add about 2 cups of water or a wee bit wine, or enough to be 3/4 inch deep in theÂ  steamer. Boil on high heat for about 5 minutes or until the steam rolls out whenÂ  the lid is opened slightly.

-   2Â  Â AddÂ oystersÂ  and an additional cup of water and cover on medium heat.Â 
    3Â  Steam oysters about 5 to 10 minutes, or until the majority of the oystersÂ have opened. Do not overcook.
    4Â Â  Serve hot, with melted butter

### furface — 2012-12-05T22:12:16+0200

A spritz of lemon sounds yummy...but no to the horseradish sauce for me. LOL

Â 

Great idea Thor about the raw organic almonds. I'll have to try that. I might even try ground up macadamia nuts.

Â 

Thanks for the responses.

# Thread: Question about elevated SHBG and EOS even after Leptin Reset and Paleo
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-elevated-shbg-and-eos-even-after-leptin-reset-and-paleo.5067/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chad@compasshealthcare.com — 2012-12-06T00:23:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-elevated-shbg-and-eos-even-after-leptin-reset-and-paleo.5067/post-66532>

Hi everyone,

> I have been lurking here for a while, but I am in need of some feedback following my recent lab results. I did a leptin reset a few months ago and have been paleo since and was hoping for some good news when I got my labs done recently-I did not. Â My doctor is stumped and I am concerned about our course of treatment. After my lab results showed an elevated SHBG (and consequently low free testosterone) I have started taking danazol 100mg x 2 daily. Today is day 2. Seems reasonable enough I suppose, but I think we need to find what is causing the elevation. The symptoms I am having are all the typical low-T symptoms. My numbers are listed below:
>
> Testosterone 496 Range 292-867
> SHBG 87 Range 16-94
> Calc Free Test 5.1 Range 4.8-25.0
> Estradiol 27 Range <=63
>
> Below are the elevated lab values
> Albumin 5.1 Range 2.9-5.0
> Calculated Globulin 2.0 Range 2.0-3.8
> Calculated A/G ratio 2.6 Range 0.9-2.5
> Eosinophils 9 Range 0-7
>
> Noteworthy is that my Eosinophils seem to always be elevated-as long as I have been having labs done. Sometimes my liver enzymes are also high. I have a history of heavy alcohol abuse for 20 years, but have not consumed alcohol for 4 years. I did have a fatty liver disease diagnosis during my drinking days. I am 6'3" and 200 lbs and approximately 16% BF. My body fat wont seem to budge. My diet is very clean, low carb, and mostly organic. I avoid wheat completely and avoid most grains 90% of the time. Diet has been like this for months. I was 30 lbs heavier in the past and had similar labs.
>
> I am looking for some feedback as to what might be the root cause of my elevated SHBG and other abnormal labs. When these labs were done last time my Test level was higher (732) and my SHBG was 62.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-12-06T01:29:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-elevated-shbg-and-eos-even-after-leptin-reset-and-paleo.5067/post-66533>

From Dr Kruse in Ask Jack back in September:

"SHBG tells us about Hypo or hyperthyroidism, estrogen collection., inflammation, osteopenia, or porosis, and LR. IT has to be placed in context but it gives us other things to look at that maybe going south."

### NittDan — 2012-12-06T07:36:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-elevated-shbg-and-eos-even-after-leptin-reset-and-paleo.5067/post-66539>

How are your lipids TRENDING?

### chad@compasshealthcare.com — 2012-12-06T22:42:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-elevated-shbg-and-eos-even-after-leptin-reset-and-paleo.5067/post-66631>

> **NittDan said**
> How are your lipids TRENDING?

Iron, Serum: 168 HIGH (40-155 Normal range) \*I love grass fed beef\*

Total Cholesterol: 250 HIGH (100-199 Normal range)
Triglycerides: 68 (0-149 Normal range)
HDL: 61 (>39Normal range)
VLDL Cholesterol Calc: 14 (5-40 Normal Range)
LDL Cholesterol Calc: 175 High (0-99 Normal Range)
T. Chol/HDL Ratio: 4.1 (0.0-5.0 Normal Range)
Estimated CHD Risk: 0.7 (0.0-1.0 Normal Range)

EOS: 8 HIGH (0-7 Normal Range)

# Thread: Do Testosterone and Estradiol levels have an effect on the ability to lose bodyfat?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-testosterone-and-estradiol-levels-have-an-effect-on-the-ability-to-lose-bodyfat.5068/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2012-12-06T02:59:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-testosterone-and-estradiol-levels-have-an-effect-on-the-ability-to-lose-bodyfat.5068/post-66535>

Just curious as to whether or not Testosterone, and Estradiol levels have any effect on the ability to lose bodyfat?

Â 

I'm a 53 year old male on TRT since February...120 mg of Testosterone-Cypionate weekly via SubQ injections per Dr. John Crisler's protocol, and after starting with a Total Testosterone level of 41 ng/dl from a range of 241 - 827 ng/dl, my TT is currently at 412.1 ng/dl, but my Estradiol (E2) level is 92.1 pg/ml from a range of < 42.5 pg/ml!!

Â 

I've been trying to lose weight since forever, as I know that bodyfat...esp around the belly produces the Aromatase Enzyme, which converts testosterone into estrogen, but so far I haven't had any luck.

Â 

I've also recently began taking .5mg of Arimidex 24 hours after east Test-Cyp injection to hopefully help lower my E2, but so far, all it's done is lower my Total Testosterone! Although it's only been 2 weeks since I began the Arimidex.

Â 

But I'm just wondering if my exorbitantly elevated Estradiol levels have any effect on my inability to burn/lose bodyfat?

Â 

Hopefully the Leptin Rx will help me lose the weight, and get my hormones back in line.

Â 

I was also diagnosed with severe sleep apnea a few months ago, where my breathing was interrupted 72 times per hour, but began CPAP therapy, and now my breathing is only being interrupted 2.5 times per hour, and I'm averaging 6.3 hours sleep per night, and more often than not, I sleep for at least 7-9 hours.

Â 

I apologize for the long winded post, but I wanted to give as much detail as I could.

Â 

It is so frustrating when things that are supposed to work, don't. It almost makes a person throw in the towel, and give up.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-06T09:33:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-testosterone-and-estradiol-levels-have-an-effect-on-the-ability-to-lose-bodyfat.5068/post-66543>

They have a huge effect thru an enzyme called hormone sensitive lipase. You need optimal hormones to maximally lose weight. The best way to regain those is to eat Epi-paleo Rx consistently and when exercise is safe, IE: when your a good fat burner/LS, then you go pedal to the metal with kettlebells, sprints or you sign up for MovNat. Erwan LeCorre work in movement is the best in the paleosphere in my view. He gets it.

### furface — 2012-12-06T10:50:36+0200

Awesome, thanks Jack. Even though I am obese, I LOVE working out. I used to go to the gym six days per week...3 days cardio, 3 days weights with majority of those, heavy, basic, compound lift days. I LOVE SQUATTING!!, Deadlifts, benchpress, etc. I also love doing H.I.I.T. cardio, and also Tabata (I think that's what it's called.) I also love using the kettlebells...or even dumbbells in lieu, if KBs are not available. I've also used the heavy bag as part of my cardio routine, along with jumping rope.

Â 

I'll continue to work on getting my hormones in sync, and have never let any type of setback, keep me from reaching my goals...no matter how long it takes.

I'm on my 5th day of LeptinRx, and am getting more and more adapted/used to an Epi-Paleo way of eating. I already have far more energy in the morning than I've had in several years...even when I was going to the gym at 5:30 A.M. every morning. But I now realize that working out at night is the optimum time.

Â 

Thanks again for the answer, Dr., and I look forward to continuing on my journey to becoming the optimum ME!!

# Thread: Question about how to consume the extra fat.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 14

### furface — 2012-12-06T14:45:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66569>

Just wondering how everyone consumes all of the extra fat that is in/on the Leptin RX, or Epi-Paleo eating plan?

Â 

What I mean is if you add a tablespoon or more of coconut oil to the pan while cooking whatever meal you are having, and if part of the meal already includes a few strips of bacon etc, or some beef tallow, etc. Even if you have some veggies with the meal, and they absorb some of the fat/grease that's in the pan, there's usually quite a bit left on the plate after you finish the meal.

Â 

So do most/all of you just "drink" the remaining fat/grease/"juice" that's left on the plate?

Â 

I know it seems so tempting to grab a nice, soft, warm, bun, and sop up all of that tasty liquid, but I know that's a big NO NO!!

Â 

Just curious.

>
> Â 
>

>
> Â 

Um. yeah. Â Lunch today was shrimp seared in bacon fat, garlic and salt. Â I ate all the shirmp and then drank the left over fat in the bowl ~ 1/4c. oh so yummie.

I have been known to spoon out ghee, coconut oil, bacon fat, and duck fat and just eat plain while Â making a meal. Â my kids eat copious amounts of ghee straight off the spoon...Â

### PrimalPam — 2012-12-06T16:51:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66578>

Morning:Â  1-2 tbsp. of coconut oil in coffee.Â  I also add coconut milk to my coffee because I like the creaminess, plus it adds more coconut fat.Â  Make sure you buy the regular version, not the lowfat kind.Â  Also, 2 eggs with bacon, all cooked in butter.Â 

Â 

Lunchtime:Â  a couple of spoonfuls of coconut manna and a square of dark chocolate.

Â 

Dinner:Â  Shrimp salad with olive oil and avocado and bacon.Â 

Â 

Also, learn to eat your bacon not so crispy.Â  Cook it a little less, so there's more fat on it.Â

### furface — 2012-12-06T16:59:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66579>

Thanks, PrimalPam.

Â 

The coconut oil in coffee sounds like a great idea, and I'd try that if coffee didn't literally make me sick. I LOVE the aroma of it, but my stomach just can't handle it's consumption.

Â 

Lunch sounds very tasty...I've recently bought some 99% Lindt dark chocolate.

Â 

Dinner sounds very appetizing as well.

Â 

I actually prefer my bacon cooked not so crisp...takes too long to crispen it up, and I usually can't wait that long. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### PrimalPam — 2012-12-06T17:01:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66580>

If you don't like coffee, then just take a spoonful of coconut oil by itself.Â  It's ok if you don't love the taste or texture; think of it as medicine.Â  Hey, it tastes better than cough syrup.Â

### Patty Cakes — 2012-12-06T17:05:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66581>

You can also put the CO or the ghee in green tea and blend that up.Â  I personally eat a lot of bone broth this time of year.Â  Usually a cup w/breakfast that has a Tblsp each of CO and ghee.Â  Then that same concoction is often my lunch...keeps me full and is satisfying my fat requirement!

### furface — 2012-12-06T17:36:24+0200

I actually don't mind consuming coconut oil by itself when it's in the solid form, but "drinking" it "neat" sort of upsets my stomach. I wish the coconut oil wouldn't keep "melting" in my cupboard.

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-06T17:51:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66591>

figuring out how to get enough fat can be a challenge. I basically make everything with fat, reheat things with more fat and por fat over everything. CO in coffe and bone broth as mentioned above. Cook bacon in oven @ 260 deg or so use bacon fat to cook eggs and mushrooms eat bacon put away excess bacon fat to cook eggs in tomorow. I just made venison liverwurst from deer liver 50%and pork fat 50%. Mushroom will absorb lots of fat when sauteed. Butter in coffee. Real whipped cream you can eat tons if not AI. Sour cream. Drink the juice lick the plate. Fat party.Â  I eat like this everyday and my weight has been a solid 165 for many months on my 5' 9" frame. Body fat % somewhere around 10. This time of year I eat the highest fat.

### furface — 2012-12-06T18:01:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66592>

> Body fat % somewhere around 10

Â 

OMG that is fantastic!! I'd love to have a BF % that low one day. (My egotistical dream is to one day see my abs.)

Â 

Those all sound like delicious ideas/recipes. I'll have to try some.

Â 

Another question...is Type 2 Diabetes an AI? I tried searching on google, but they never EVER answer your direct question. All the results that they did give were basically a mixed message. (Why can't they ever just answer your direct question? LOL)

### PrimalPam — 2012-12-06T19:35:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66599>

I don't think T2D is definitely AI, but a lot of T2D's are AI.Â  You'd have to get tested for antibodies to see.Â  If you are hypothyroid, chances are upwards of 80% that you are AI, and since many T2D's are hypothyroid, it's a good chance.

Â 

Re:Â  Eating coconut oil in solid form.Â  Just refrigerate it, and then you can scoop it out.Â  I love watching the video Dr. K. made, walking through Whole Foods, where he shows a (16 oz?) jar of Coconut Oil, and says, "This is a weekend pack at my house".Â  I go through about 1/2 gallon a month by myself.

Â 

Another favorite dinner of mine (which I purchased today for dinner, thanks to your bringing up this issue, it reminded meÂ of it!Â  thanks!) is what I call my Fat Salad.Â  A huge bowl (I use a serving bowl!) of kale, spinach, arugula, etc.Â with mushrooms, cucumbers, onions, etc.Â  I add 1 whole avocado cut up, some shrimp or salmon, bacon, and drizzle olive oil all over it.Â  Tons of fat; I have no idea how much, but so yummy.

### furface — 2012-12-06T20:01:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66605>

Glad to help with your dinner.

Â 

Thanks for the info on the A.I. I'll try and find out about my situation.

Â 

Good suggestion about keeping the CO in the fridge.

Â 

Yeah, I love that whole video. I think we actually have a Whole Foods here in Vancouver.

### Chrysanthemama — 2012-12-06T22:07:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66624>

> **furface said**
>
> I actually prefer my bacon cooked not so crisp...takes too long to crispen it up, and I usually can't wait that long.

I have become quite adept at baking bacon in a large casserole dish in the oven.Â  Depending on how much you're cooking, and how closely packed in the dish it is, it might take 30 to 40 minutes, and you can control the crispiness of it pretty well.Â  I long ago tired of frying up bacon in pan.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-12-06T22:16:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-how-to-consume-the-extra-fat.5069/post-66628>

# Thread: Does anyone make sauces out of heavy/whipping cream?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-sauces-out-of-heavy-whipping-cream.5070/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2012-12-06T17:40:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-sauces-out-of-heavy-whipping-cream.5070/post-66589>

Just curious as to whether or not anyone makes sauces out of heavy/whipping cream, and then add some spices, to pour on top of fish, or veggies, or meat, just as another way to consume fat? Or just for a change of pace.

Â 

If so, do you use anything to thicken it up?

Â 

Perhaps even some sort of creamy clam, shrimp, or lobster chowder...without the spuds of course. Or maybe with a few portions of yams, or sweet potatoes.

Â 

Or would this all be taboo?

### Rachelle — 2012-12-06T19:41:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-sauces-out-of-heavy-whipping-cream.5070/post-66601>

I try to stay away from cream because I am not all together sure my immune system is not compromised. My ANA tests say negative, but I still am not sure.

Â 

I make sauces out of coconut milk. Here is a really good fish recipe for Brazilian fish stew.Â http://www.simplyrecipes.com/recipes/moqueca\_-\_brazilian\_fish\_stew/

Â 

It is very yummy. You can make it with shrimp or anything fish really. You can add or subtract anything you like to this. I use nothing to thicken this up.

Â 

Bacon wrapped sweet potato bites are really wonderful too.

### furface — 2012-12-06T19:55:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-sauces-out-of-heavy-whipping-cream.5070/post-66603>

Thanks, Rachelle...that all sounds so yummers!!

### kathylu — 2012-12-06T22:09:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-sauces-out-of-heavy-whipping-cream.5070/post-66625>

If you can tolerate dairy, a really easy way to make a sauce is to pour heavy whipping cream into a saucepan and let it reduce by 50-70% over medium heat. Add spices, herbs, wine, etc and away you go! It thickens on its own, no need to add a thickener.

### furface — 2012-12-06T23:04:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-sauces-out-of-heavy-whipping-cream.5070/post-66634>

> **kathylu said**
> If you can tolerate dairy, a really easy way to make a sauce is to pour heavy whipping cream into a saucepan and let it reduce by 50-70% over medium heat. Add spices, herbs, wine, etc and away you go! It thickens on its own, no need to add a thickener.

Thanks Kathy.Â  I'll try that.

### Charlotte — 2012-12-07T14:52:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-sauces-out-of-heavy-whipping-cream.5070/post-66709>

I use Mascarpone to make sauces and desserts. Mascarpone is the thickest Italian cream you can get and is not liquid anymore it is so thick. Italians make Tiramisu with it. I just add it to meat, fish or anything else I can think of. Very delicious as a dessert is melting dark chocolate (90 - 99 %) in a pan over boiling water, add some almond milk and stir in the Mascarpone. Just add vanilla or any other spice you like and/or Stevia. Put it in the fridge and you have the best mousse au chocolat. Sometime I put in raspberries or blueberries, or I mix Mascarpone with strawberries to make a strawberry cream.

### furface — 2012-12-07T14:56:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-sauces-out-of-heavy-whipping-cream.5070/post-66710>

> **c.adler@colostrum.de said**
> I use Mascarpone to make sauces and desserts. Mascarpone is the thickest Italian cream you can get and is not liquid anymore it is so thick. Italians make Tiramisu with it. I just add it to meat, fish or anything else I can think of. Very delicious as a dessert is melting dark chocolate (90 - 99 %) in a pan over boiling water, add some almond milk and stir in the Mascarpone. Just add vanilla or any other spice you like and/or Stevia. Put it in the fridge and you have the best mousse au chocolat. Sometime I put in raspberries or blueberries, or I mix Mascarpone with strawberries to make a strawberry cream.

OMG that all sounds so delicious!!

### Charlotte — 2012-12-08T04:00:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-sauces-out-of-heavy-whipping-cream.5070/post-66787>

yes, it is definitely delicious, and you know what is the best part? # Thread: Nutrition resources for high schoolers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-resources-for-high-schoolers.5074/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-12-06T22:03:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-resources-for-high-schoolers.5074/post-66623>

I teach a high school Biology class for home schooled students. We only meet once a week for an hour for class (they have an hour with another teacher for lab), but we use moodle during the week to communicate and I give them assignments etc this way. Anyhow, weâ€™ve gotten to the â€œNutrition and Digestive Systemâ€ chapter. EEK! Itâ€™s making me crazy. Itâ€™s based on the food pyramid and extols the virtues of canola oil, corn oil, and a grain based diet. Because I have such a limited time, I really wonâ€™t be able to dispel all of this nonsense. Last week (while talking about the nervous system) we already got into a conversation about some of this as I was talking about how essential good fats were for proper nervous system functioning â€“ some of the girls were freaking out about the â€œsaturated fatsâ€ I was talking about. I was also explaining about how sugar can cause problems with the nervous system and one very athletic male student said heâ€™s prediabetic and has a very fast metabolism so has to eat sugar and carbs because of his metabolism. I tried to explain that he could avoid the prediabetes progressing to type2 diabetes with dietary changes. Anyhow, all of this to say I want these kids to learn more about nutrition than what I can pack into next weeks lecture, most of which needs to cover the digestive system.

Do you know of any resources, articles or online movies/clips, on good nutrition that would be appropriate for a high schooler? I'd like to have some resources to put up on moodle for them to be looking at over the next several weeks while we are on Christmas break. Any ideas?

### kathylu — 2012-12-06T22:14:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-resources-for-high-schoolers.5074/post-66627>

I'd start with www.westonaprice.org. Lots of great articles about nutrition there. I don't really agree with their stance on grains (ok, if prepared in a "traditional" manner), but it's good place to start directing people gently away from the SAD. They are right on the money about a lot of topics.

### Shijin13 — 2012-12-06T22:43:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-resources-for-high-schoolers.5074/post-66632>

Fat head the movie. And the perfect health diet movie.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-12-07T10:14:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-resources-for-high-schoolers.5074/post-66671>

Fat head is fun, I could see high schoolers enjoying it. Is the other movie, "Fresh"? I haven't seen that one, wish there was easier access to it.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-12-14T13:41:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-resources-for-high-schoolers.5074/post-67507>

Any other great articles that anyone could recommend for high school level? Blog posts?Â

### Shijin13 — 2012-12-14T16:37:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-resources-for-high-schoolers.5074/post-67533>

Why not have them read a blog by Mark Sission and some other paleo bloggers, as well as JAck's Epi-Paleo Rx. Â And have them discuss the topics, then the next week have them do one on SAD, and the following week one on the Meditrarian diet. Â then the final week give them discussion topics Â in which they have to defend a particular way of eating.

HAve them watch Terri Whals TedX talk too!Â 

Have them answer - the following questions: Â What nutrients does the brain need most to run optimally? Â Which way of eating provides the most of the nutrient?Â 

What foods did humans evolve eating? Why are these foods critical to humans today?Â 

Â 

Ask them critical questions - that get them thinking, that make them what to learn more. Â  take this opportunity to build the foundation... ask them to question the status quo of the USDA and the food pyramid. Â Ask them to research who funds the research USDA does to come up w/the food pyramid/plate? Â Have them look into genetically modified foods - and defend why they're ok to eat, or not ok to eat. Â 

Â 

shine the flashlight... and let them discover their own ability to read, comprehend, and determine whats total BS vs truth.Â

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-12-16T12:26:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-resources-for-high-schoolers.5074/post-67635>

Thanks for the ideas, I had some similar ideas, but was looking for specific resources. I hadn't thought of Terri Whals TedX talk, but that would be a great one. I've already uploaded it and a few more blog posts/videos I've found onto moodle! I also gave them the opportunity to earn extra credit by writing a paper after watching Fat Head and checking out the other resources.Â 

Â 

It's fun to be able to expose the kids to some new (for them) ideas. I'll be interested to hear what they think after Christmas break!

Â

# Thread: When starting out on the Leptin Rx is it MANDATORY to have lots of fat every meal?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2012-12-07T00:25:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66639>

Not sure if this should be in the Leptin Rx forum, or the Optimal Nutrition forum so if it should be moved, please feel free to do so and I apologize for posting it in the wrong forum.

Â 

Just wondering when a person is just starting out on the Leptin Reset, is it MANDATORY to have plenty of fat with EVERY meal? I realize that this is one of the major keys/components to making this all work, but would it be truly detrimental if a person were to have one or two meals a week if they wanted, where they didn't have a large amount of fat with the meal? Just to give their stomach/system a little break?

Â 

Then once they've become more adapted to eating this way, they can eat plenty of fat with every meal.

Â 

I'm speaking mainly of the "added" fat such as bacon grease/fat, added coconut oil etc. Not the fat that is included in the actual food that they are eating. Even if cooking bacon, instead of eating all the fat drippings during that meal for instance, could they put it in a container, and use it later?

### vkiernan — 2012-12-07T08:59:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66658>

> **furface said**
> Not sure if this should be in the Leptin Rx forum, or the Optimal Nutrition forum so if it should be moved, please feel free to do so and I apologize for posting it in the wrong forum.
>
> Just wondering when a person is just starting out on the Leptin Reset, is it MANDATORY to have plenty of fat with EVERY meal? I realize that this is one of the major keys/components to making this all work, but would it be truly detrimental if a person were to have one or two meals a week if they wanted, where they didn't have a large amount of fat with the meal? Just to give their stomach/system a little break?
>
> Then once they've become more adapted to eating this way, they can eat plenty of fat with every meal.

I'm speaking mainly of the "added" fat such as bacon grease/fat, added coconut oil etc. Not the fat that is included in the actual food that they are eating. Even if cooking bacon, instead of eating all the fat drippings during that meal for instance, could they put it in a container, and use it later?

You don't need added fat at every meal and not quite sure where you got that from.Â  And you certainly don't need to eat all the bacon fat when cooking it.Â  I always saved my bacon grease for cooking in.Â  Fat helps satiate.Â  So if you get hungry before your next meal, then eat more fat to help tide you over.Â  Otherwise, eat the right things and don't worry about it.Â  With the Leptin Rx, the main idea is the BAB with at least 50 gr of protein.Â  Then of course eat as much epi-paleo as you can.Â  You'll be fine.Â  Throw any questions out here that you like!Â  We will all help you however needed.

### furface — 2012-12-07T11:24:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66681>

Thanks vkiernan. That's good to know about the fat. I do feel so much fuller when I do consume a lot of the fat, but at times it makes me feel "too full." Which I guess is the idea behind it. The fuller you feel, the less you eat. DUH!! lol

### PrimalPam — 2012-12-08T10:47:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66801>

> I do eat fat with every meal, but I don't believe it's a "requirement".Â  Yes, the fat makes you feel very full, which is a great thing!Â  Remember, your brain is >60% fat, so you need fat to help your brain make these improvements.Â  So the more fat you eat, actually, the better!Â  But if you eat a certain meal that doesn't contain any fat and you just don't feel like it, well don't worry about it.Â 
>
> A key component is the BABÂ plus the fact that you will only eat once or twice more each day.Â  We are a society so accustomed to snacking every 2-3 hours to "stoke" our metabolism, which is just so false.Â 
>
>
>
> The funniest thing I've noticed ever since going on the LR in Jan. 2012 is watching how much, and how often, other people eat.Â  I've been at airports waiting for a flight, and about 80% of the people can't seem to wait 1 hour for a flight without eating something.Â  They are ALL snacking.Â  Once, the crunching and the sounds of the plastic bags they were eating from annoyed me so much I had to change to another waiting area.Â  It occurred to me that that used to be me:Â  I'd be first in line at those little airport convenience stores, stocking up on snacks for the waiting area + the flight.Â  Heaven forbid I go 4-5 hours without food.Â  These days, it doesn't even OCCUR to me to eat for 5-7 hours after the last time I ate.
>
> Furface, it sounds like you're doing great here and asking all these questions is terrific.Â  We've ALL been there, just finding Dr. K., and we ALL know how overwhelming it can be.Â  So please, ask away.
>
> Â 
>
> Â 
>
> Â 
>
> Â

### furface — 2012-12-08T11:09:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66804>

Thanks Pam, very much appreciated. I also appreciate the encouragement. I think the biggest obstacle to overcome is being able to break "bad" habits, and getting out of an old routine, and starting a new, much healthier one.

Â 

As far as asking so many questions, I'm sure a lot of the answers are in the blogs, but to be totally honest, a lot of the techno babble jargon can get confusing, esp if the answer a person is looking for is somewhere in the middle of all of that scientific mumbo jumbo. I've always been a straight to the point, drive or get off the road, no lollygagging type of person, so while at times knowing how, or why something works is interesting, or something that I'd like to know, more often than not, I just want to know WHAT works.

Â 

It's like if you've ever seen the old TV show, "All In The Family", I'm sure you'll remember scenes when Edith would be telling Archie a story, she'd be going on, and on, and on, putting in endless, mind numbing, at times totally irrelevant details, and Archie would be sitting there twirling his hand for Edith to "hurry up and get to the point", while rolling his eyes, sometimes even pretending to put a bullet in a gun, spin the cylinder, aim it at his head, pretend to pull the trigger, "miss", spin the cylinder again, aim, fire, then shake his head as if he'd been shot...only to have Edith still not have gotten to the point. That's how I feel at times when I'm reading through all of that techno jargon trying to find an answer to my question.

So at times I just think it's faster asking it on the forum.

Â 

No offense meant to anyone whatsoever about the long, drawn out explanations, or techno jargon, it is very impressive, and does show just how intelligent whoever wrote it is, but as I said, most often I, and I'm sure a few others would just like a simple answer to their question if it is at all possible.

Â 

As far as eating only 2-3 times per day, with no snacking in between, I think I'm already getting used to that, and the BAB is definitely key. And I've been one of those who have been on the "eat 5-6 small meals, one every 2-3 hours each day" eating regimen for a while. Esp when I was going to the gym 6 days per week.

Â 

So thanks again for the help, I apologize for all the rambling, and look forward to a new, happy, healthier, me.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-08T21:04:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66896>

furnace you would do well to listen to the Dec Webinar when it goes live to explain to you why the Leptin Rx is critical..........

### furface — 2012-12-08T22:44:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66906>

> **Jack Kruse said**
> furnace you would do well to listen to the Dec Webinar when it goes live to explain to you why the Leptin Rx is critical..........

Thanks Dr. Kruse, I will.

Â 

And again, I meant no disrespect whatsoever with all the "techno babble" talk. It's just that reading it all can get confusing, and like I said, I'm a "get to the point" type of person. So I apologize if I've offended you, or anyone else, in any way whatsoever.

Â 

I've been on the Leptin Rx since Monday, and I'm doing my utmost to follow it as best I can. I haven't "cheated" on my diet, I've eaten as much fat as I can...healthy fats of course, and have not snacked once between meals. I realize it's a long process, but I am determined to change my life, and change my health.

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-09T09:36:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66921>

You really don't need the techno babble. Some of us need it because we need to unerstand why. If you don't care about why here is some simple: There are three macronutrients -Fat, protein and carbs. If humans have access to animal fat and protein they do not need carbs. so assuming you have eliminated carbs that leaves protein and fat. You should eat more fat than protein. That's the basic program. As you put this in practice it gets easier with each passing day. I have been doing this for 18 mo. with great results, but I am still changing and adapting. It takes time, it gets better every day.

### PrimalPam — 2012-12-09T10:22:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66927>

Re:Â  The "techno-babble".Â  Furface, I have to read every blog post like, 4 times.Â  First, I skim it, then I go back and read thoroughly, then I wait for some comments to come in, because they help me further understand.Â  Then about a month or so later, I read again.Â  Hey, I have a business degree from a party school; I'm not a scientist.Â  That frog we dissected in HS Biology was as far as I got.Â  Here's something I do regularly though, that I think could help: Go to the archives and pick a blog.Â  Any blog, doesn't matter the subject.Â  Read that blog completely, thoroughly, until your brain literally hurts.Â  Google words you don't understand.Â  Really spend time on the comments.Â  Print it out if you have to.Â  Do this about once a week, and they will start falling together like puzzle pieces.Â Â What starts happening is you start saying, oh....he said that same thing, in a different way, in that blog, months ago.Â  And here's another puzzle piece...wow.Â  And it starts making sense in a big way.Â  Of course, that could be also because I've dramatically upped my seafood content (and thereby my DHA, which I now understand! LOL) which makes my brain work better.

Also, regarding the fats, are you eating seafood every day, or almost daily?Â  Remember, seafood (esp. shellfish) contains a ton of DHA, which is a fat.Â  So it's not like you have to "add" fats.Â  In fact, Dr. K. recommends not adding sat. fat to your seafood meals, as it doesn't allow as much of the DHA uptake.Â

### furface — 2012-12-09T12:40:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66937>

Very helpful information, Pam. Both about the blogs, and the seafood and fat.

I might start to reread the blogs, and as far as seafood goes, right now all I have in my fridge/freezer/cupboards are about 3 pieces of wild salmon, 3 pieces of tilapia, 30 prawns, and 8 cans of tuna. I don't get paid for another 11 days when I plan on jamming my fridge, and freezer with all types of seafood.

Â 

Still have to acquire a taste for oysters...tried a can of smoked oysters last week, and to be totally honest, they tasted like dirt, very gritty. But I finished them. But I'll try some fresh ones, along with some fresh clams (Afraid to try canned ones after my canned oysters experience.), some more wild salmon, tilapia, basa, shrimp/prawns, maybe even some dungeness crab and lobster if I can afford it. Also some grass-fed beef, and grass-fed lamb. Oh and of course bacon and free run pastured eggs.

Â 

That's good to know that you don't need extra added fat with seafood because I think that's where I have my biggest adjustment...consuming the extra fat on it's own.

Will also be adding some avocado, continue with the coconut oil, will try and find some coconut manna, possibly some heavy whipping cream from Jersey cows (so it's A2), as well as pasture butter, and hopefully things will be in full swing.

> **Coriander said**
> Hi Furface, be careful with the dairy. Just because it's a Jersey, doesn't mean it's A2.

Oh I will. I'm not going to go way overboard, but I'm also not going to be so overly paranoid and worry whether or not it is A1 or A2 over 1/2 a cup of cream once or twice a week...if that. At times I think we can OVER think things at times, and become TOO cautious, and over worry when it's not really necessary. At times we must let common sense prevail. JMHO.

### Martin — 2012-12-10T09:07:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-starting-out-on-the-leptin-rx-is-it-mandatory-to-have-lots-of-fat-every-meal.5075/post-66998>

> **PrimalPam said**
> Re:Â  The "techno-babble".Â  Furface, I have to read every blog post like, 4 times.Â  First, I skim it, then I go back and read thoroughly, then I wait for some comments to come in, because they help me further understand.Â  Then about a month or so later, I read again.Â  Hey, I have a business degree from a party school; I'm not a scientist.Â  That frog we dissected in HS Biology was as far as I got.Â  Here's something I do regularly though, that I think could help: Go to the archives and pick a blog.Â  Any blog, doesn't matter the subject.Â  Read that blog completely, thoroughly, until your brain literally hurts.Â  Google words you don't understand.Â  Really spend time on the comments.Â  Print it out if you have to.Â  Do this about once a week, and they will start falling together like puzzle pieces.Â Â What starts happening is you start saying, oh....he said that same thing, in a different way, in that blog, months ago.Â  And here's another puzzle piece...wow.Â  And it starts making sense in a big way.Â  Of course, that could be also because I've dramatically upped my seafood content (and thereby my DHA, which I now understand! LOL) which makes my brain work better.
>
> Also, regarding the fats, are you eating seafood every day, or almost daily?Â  Remember, seafood (esp. shellfish) contains a ton of DHA, which is a fat.Â  So it's not like you have to "add" fats.Â  In fact, Dr. K. recommends not adding sat. fat to your seafood meals, as it doesn't allow as much of the DHA uptake.Â 
>
> Â 
>
> Â 
>
> PrimalPam, you have "channeled" my way of absorbing this information. It really does sink in and one can start to get the gist after a while. I remember seeing some of the first blogs and was overwhumped by the complexity and would have to walk away... but not for long. They start to coalesce after a while. Love it. Changed my life for the better.

# Thread: 50-55 degree temp basis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-55-degree-temp-basis.5076/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-12-07T02:28:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-55-degree-temp-basis.5076/post-66641>

Is this 50-55 degree temperature basis partly because this is part of adapting?Â  As you adapt to the cold you can stay in this range longer?Â  I was in the pool for 35 minutes and my temperature would not go below 55 degrees.Â  I was thinking about waiting till it went to 50...but it wasn't going down so I went ahead and stopped at 35 minutes.Â  A minute after getting out of the pool and the temp was already up to 58.

Â 

The temp of the water was 53.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-07T09:06:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-55-degree-temp-basis.5076/post-66660>

No.....its due to how the cold receptors in mammals are set and wired into their central and peripheral nervous system. CT 4-6 has that info.

# Thread: Eczema and leaky gut
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eczema-and-leaky-gut.5080/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 6

### neil@princeglobal.net — 2012-12-07T06:48:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eczema-and-leaky-gut.5080/post-66650>

I have had eczema since I was 5 (now 42) and it has changed over the years. It currently manifests itself as extremely dry and flaky skin especially on the face (beard line is worse again). Itchiness is also associated with the dryness and for the better part of this year, my face has been red and blotchy. This varies on torso, legs and arms as well.

Had some bloods done in May and October and discussed with a naturopath this week. Highlights are:

May 2012:

ThyroglobulinÂ Â Â Â  9 IU/mLÂ Â  (range <4)

ImmunoglobulinÂ Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â  H 2019 kIU/LÂ Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â  (This was 5587 in Jul 2011)

Oct 2012:

hsCRPÂ Â Â  Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â  0.4 mg/L

HbA1c FractionÂ Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â  5.3%Â  (in SI units: 35 mmol/mol)

HomocysteineÂ Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â  10.0 umol/LÂ Â Â Â Â Â Â  (naturopath wants this down to 6-8 â€“ I donâ€™t really understand what this is yetâ€¦.)

25-hydroxy Vitamin D3Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â  80 nmol/LÂ Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â  (naturopath wants this up in 100-120)

I have also done a leaky gut test this month (6 hour urine collection after taking Lactulose and Mannitol). Lactulose recovery was high at 0.46% (range is 0 to 0.3%) suggestive of GI mucosal hyperpermeability.

After discussions with naturopath, it would appear I have adrenal fatigue as well (no testing â€“ just from questionnaires).

I have been epi-paleo for about 3 weeks now.

Now to my questions:

When my October bloods were taken, I was on 50mg prednisone daily â€“ will this affect my hsCRP reading and should I get it retested once I am fully weaned off prednisone (about another week or so)?

My naturopath says it can take 2 years to heal a leaky gut. If I follow the leaky gut prescription in your blogs would this be correct? Is there any way I can speed up the healing eg cold thermogenesis? Will CT reduce my inflammation? Should I be following the GAPS diet you reference in your leaky gut blogs?

My naturopath wants me to do things similar to the leaky gut prescription, then do a detox for parasites, funghi etc followed by a liver cleanse. Does this make sense if I am following the leaky gut prescription and epi-paleo?

Should I get tested for my adrenals? What should I do to improve them?

### Destiny — 2012-12-07T08:01:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eczema-and-leaky-gut.5080/post-66652>

Neil,

To your question about a leaky gut. My husband had Crohn's most of his life. He has been eating right an doing CT twice a day since Feb 2012.

He is symptoms free. His labs are still wonky but he feels great.

# Thread: Fire breathing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2012-12-07T09:56:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/post-66668>

I have been doing fire breathing while fully immersed in <40 deg . This is rapid deep belly breathing through the mouth. You have to try this . At colder temps when i breathe like this it fires up all my skin nerves with each breath. The sensation is amazing. It helps pass the time too.

### Shijin13 — 2012-12-07T10:09:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/post-66670>

do you have a good description of how to do this.... I'm interested.....Â

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-07T10:33:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/post-66674>

this is a technique we use in Ashtanga yoga usually at the end of class in a lifted seated pose. Breath from the low abdomen rapidly and forcefully in and out like a steam train through the mouth. I do sets of like 100, Do as many sets as you like. I don't know what's going on with the sensation and it only happens in deep cold, but it's like with each breath my entire epidermal nerve web fires up. Way cool. I am fully immersed to my lips I am blowing out right across the water which is fun .Â  I am outdoors in a pond. I face the sun so there is great visual.

### nonchalant — 2012-12-07T10:56:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/post-66676>

> **Dali Dula said**
> I don't know what's going on with the sensation and it only happens in deep cold, but it's like with each breath my entire epidermal nerve web fires up.Â  I am fully immersed to my lips I am blowing out right across the water which is fun .Â  I am outdoors in a pond. I face the sun so there is great visual.

Â 

Wow, Jack will give you an A+ for all that!Â  Sounds like you are doing it right!

Interesting effect.Â  Guess it isn't the vagus nerve.Â  I'd like to try it, but I haven't gone deep cold yet.

### SeaHorse — 2012-12-07T11:38:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/post-66683>

That sounds really intense and wonderful!

The way I was instructed to do "breath of fire" was to only think about blowing breath out...you naturally take the in- breath...having the breath start from the bottom of your belly engages your core muscles rather than shallow upper chest breathing. I'm going to try this too. I haven't been able to get through the ice in our pond for the last week, but I'm going to try for my bath and shower CT.

### endless — 2012-12-07T23:16:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/post-66775>

It's important to do it properly....the out breath is forceful and accompanied by a tightening of the diaphragm and core muscles. The in breath is kind of passive. It might be helpful to look up examples on YouTube.

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-08T10:44:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/post-66800>

> **endless said**
> It's important to do it properly....the out breath is forceful and accompanied by a tightening of the diaphragm and core muscles. The in breath is kind of passive. It might be helpful to look up examples on YouTube.

I don't know about important and properly. I breathe rapidly and forcefully in and out from the belly and my neural network explodes. I fool around with all types of breathing during CT it is all incredible. This would not be proper as it is properly done in Ashtanga through the nose while engaged in ujayi breathing. I have found any breathing you do mindfully has benefits. Play people play!

### kathylu — 2012-12-08T11:34:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/post-66806>

"Breath of Fire" is also a great technique for dispelling pain and fatigue. Increases tissue oxygenation and helps detoxify the body.

### Shijin13 — 2012-12-08T13:17:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fire-breathing.5081/post-66815>

I'm wondering if its similar to the breathing techniques I employed while in labor. Especially the breaths I used during the most challenging contractions. I focused on tucking my chin and breathing out and focusing on opening with each breath.

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-07T11:04:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-bo.5082/post-66677>

18 months paleo and on board with allÂ  of Jack's protocols for the last year I am now incredibly stink free. I wasn't trusting my own observations because you can't always smell yourself. Yesterday I wore a tee shirt for 14+ hours. I smell the pit area. Nothing. I shove the pit area of the shirt under my wife's nose this morning and ask "Do you smell anything?" "like what?" she says." Like BO?" "No" WTF The kicker is I haven't used soap for months. I shower everyday with just water and i have no stink. I don't use deodorant. WOW!

Otherwise.. no smell at all. I am guessing chocolate and eggs gives me inflammation because Jack said somewhere BO = inflammation..
I use no soap eather (excpt if I had gone overboard with eggs and chocolate..lol).

Â  Â  coumadin, then find another doctor. Â You need to start Vitamin K2 supplementation once you get your blood thinned.

5\. Scary lipid panel - you need to tightly control you carb intake

If a person can tolerate this, would it be an approved meal/snack for an occasional "treat"?...

Â 

1/2 cup heavy creamÂ  (or 1/2 cup cold water if you don't want the cream) or 1/2 cup coconut milk

1-2 Tbsp Coconut Manna

2 Squares 90-99% Dark Chocolate Melted

Stevia to taste if desired

1 scoop Kaizen Naturals Whey Protein (Made from milk from 100 % Grass-Fed New Zealand Cows...24 gr Protein, 2 gr Carbs, 1 gr fat)

### Glen PDQ — 2012-12-08T01:53:58+0200

### furface — 2012-12-08T02:13:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-this-be-an-approved-meal-snack-for-an-ocassional-treat.5085/post-66783>

> **Glen PDQ said**
> Are you having hunger pangs or food cravings? I don't expect there are any approved snacks/treats on epi-paleo rx.Â  Frequenting this website I've learned if one is still Leptin Resistant then snacks certainly aren't optimal since one should allow 4-5 hours between meals so digestive system can recover.
>
> As for it being an acceptable meal replacement maybe someone else will chime in.Â  Doesn't protein powder cause a larger insulin spike than meat?

I didn't necessarily mean as a "snack" or "treat" between meals, I meant instead of having a "regular" seafood/beef/fish etc full meal. Yes I'm having the occasional hunger pang, but that's just something I'll have to get used to. Esp between dinner and the time I go to bed.

Â 

As far as this particular protein powder goes, I performed an experiment upon myself this afternoon...the data etc is in the Leptin Rx forum, and my blood glucose never rose higher than 9 points over a two hour period, and I tested it every 20 minutes during the first hour, and every 15 minutes during the 2nd hour. So the insulin spike for me wasn't too bad at all. I mixed one scoop in 6 ounces of water. Drank it on an empty stomach. Didn't consume anything during the testing.

I am also a type 2 diabetic.
