# Thread: Low HDL (33) reading a result of pnemonia, or of leaky gut?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-hdl-33-reading-a-result-of-pnemonia-or-of-leaky-gut.5177/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mra_jesusfreak@yahoo.com — 2012-12-15T00:32:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-hdl-33-reading-a-result-of-pnemonia-or-of-leaky-gut.5177/post-67564>

This past July, I got a bad bronchitis infection, that turned into Pneumonia.Â  After 3 trips to the doctor, I finally got diagnosed correctly, as having pneumonia.Â  The following day, I got a metabolic panel done. Â 

Â 

HDL Cholesterol (flagged as Low) 33 \[40-59 MG/DL\]

CHO/HDL Ratio 3.4 \[0-4.4\]

Â \[Edit: 24 yrs. old, 135 lb. at the time. Female\]

None of this meant much to me.Â  I googled HDL, and found it it was the "good cholesterol" and needed to be higher, but that was it...

Â 

Until I found out about Jack Kruse a couple mo. ago, and read that he feels low HDL is a huge indicator of leaky gut syndrome.Â  Which was a huge ligh tbulb moment for me, b/c I have Hashimoto's thyroiditis, and have been wondering if I have leaky gut, and if that's the root cause of my Hashi's. Â 

Â 

So now I'm wondering if that low HDL reading was a direct result of my testing while I had pneumonia and hadn't eaten much in the two weeks prior to taking the blood work (no appetite due to pneumonia and virus), or if having pneumonia wouldn't have affected it at all, and if the low HDL reading is a sign of something bigger (like leaky gut).Â Â 

Â 

This is my first time posting in this forum, not sure if this was the right section to post in, might repost in one of the lab threads, as well.Â  Thanks. /wp-includes/images/smilies/icon\_smile.gif Â


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Low HDL (33) reading a result of pnemonia, or of leaky gut?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-hdl-33-reading-a-result-of-pnemonia-or-of-leaky-gut.5178/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4

### mra_jesusfreak@yahoo.com — 2012-12-15T00:37:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-hdl-33-reading-a-result-of-pnemonia-or-of-leaky-gut.5178/post-67566>

This past July, I got a bad bronchitis infection, that turned into Pneumonia.Â  After 3 trips to the doctor, I finally got diagnosed correctly, as having pneumonia.Â  The following day, I got a metabolic panel done. Â 
Â 

HDL Cholesterol (flagged as Low) 33 \[40-59 MG/DL\]

CHO/HDL Ratio 3.4 \[0-4.4\]

Â \[Edit: Female. 24 yrs. 135 lbs. at the time\]

None of this meant much to me.Â  I googled HDL, and found it it was the "good cholesterol" and needed to be higher, but that was itâ€¦

Â 

Until I found out about Jack Kruse a couple mo. ago, and read that he feels low HDL is a huge indicator of leaky gut syndrome.Â  Which was a huge ligh tbulb moment for me, b/c I have Hashimoto's thyroiditis, and have been wondering if I have leaky gut, and if that's the root cause of my Hashi's. Â 

Â 

So now I'm wondering if that low HDL reading was a direct result of my testing while I had pneumonia and hadn't eaten much in the two weeks prior to taking the blood work (no appetite due to pneumonia and virus), or if having pneumonia wouldn't have affected it at all, and if the low HDL reading is a sign of something bigger (like leaky gut).Â Â 

Â 

This is my first time posting in this forum, not sure if this was the right section to post in, might repost in one of the lab threads, as well.Â  Thanks. /wp-includes/images/smilies/icon\_smile.gif Â

### nonchalant — 2012-12-16T19:21:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-hdl-33-reading-a-result-of-pnemonia-or-of-leaky-gut.5178/post-67657>

Hi fearless, welcome.Â  Do you eat much animal fat?

### mra_jesusfreak@yahoo.com — 2012-12-16T19:30:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-hdl-33-reading-a-result-of-pnemonia-or-of-leaky-gut.5178/post-67658>

> **Nonchalant said**
> Hi fearless, welcome.Â  Do you eat much animal fat?

Hi, thanks for the welcome!Â  At that point, when that lab was taken, I wasn't really going out of my way to, no.Â  Since then, though, I've been frying bacon every morning, ate a 1/2 quart jar of lard (in cooking over a month or so), eating pork cracklings, and eating tons of coconut oil.Â  When the lab was drawn, I hadn't eaten stable meals in the week or so leading up to it, as I was sick, and had no appetite.Â

### nonchalant — 2012-12-16T19:49:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-hdl-33-reading-a-result-of-pnemonia-or-of-leaky-gut.5178/post-67660>

Well, if you weren't eating much animal fat before you got sick, I don't know if I'd worry about the lab result.Â  Eating animal fats usually brings the HDL number up very quickly.Â  Sounds like you are eating a good amount now.Â  I had/have a leaky gut and my HDL zoomed upward when I realized animal fats weren't bad.Â 

If you get retested and the number is still low, then you might need to take extra measures.Â  But realize that everyone has a leaky gut to some extent.Â  I'm glad you have changed your eating habits; I'm sure you have already noticed some health benefits.

# Thread: Help with Thyroid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-thyroid.5179/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Patty Cakes — 2012-12-15T13:17:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-thyroid.5179/post-67581>

I probably wouldn't skip the whole afternoon dose.Â  You may end up paying for it the next day.Â  If this was me I would eliminate one or both of the t3 doses and see how you feel.Â  Your FT3 lab is high but not that bad.Â  Plus it depends on when you took your last dose of either med compared to when you had your blood drawn. You want your FT3 in the top of the range and FT4 mid-high to top of range.Â  FT4 will go up with increasing the Armour but most likely so will your T3.

Â 

It could be the ABs reaction tot he gluten you said you may have ingested contributing as well. I'm wondering (out loud) what your adrenals are doing. Also another thought is that the light changes may be starting to affect you as well. There may be other things going on affecting the thyroid and causing it to react.

Â 

Is your rT3 ratio good?Â  http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-12-15T15:40:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-thyroid.5179/post-67588>

> I probably wouldn't skip the whole afternoon dose.Â  You may end up paying for it the next day.Â  If this was me I would eliminate one or both of the t3 doses and see how you feel.Â  Your FT3 lab is high but not that bad.Â  Plus it depends on when you took your last dose of either med compared to when you had your blood drawn. You want your FT3 in the top of the range and FT4 mid-high to top of range.Â  FT4 will go up with increasing the Armour but most likely so will your T3.
>
> Â 
>
> It could be the ABs reaction tot he gluten you said you may have ingested contributing as well. I'm wondering (out loud) what your adrenals are doing. Also another thought is that the light changes may be starting to affect you as well. There may be other things going on affecting the thyroid and causing it to react.
>
> Â 
>
> Is your rT3 ratio good?Â  http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/

Thanks for the reply. Â 

Â 

I take my blood/thyroid tests at the same time of day so I can accurately track them as much as possible. While T3/FT3 aren't the highest ever, they are really high for me. Â Esp considering that i wasnt able to get it up for so long. Â 

Â 

i'll probably just cut the aternoon dose of cytomel (wouldnt want to cut the armour as my T4 isnt really that great). this is very frustrating

Â 

your comment on the light is true. Â I have noticed i never feel as well during the winter and wonder if this is a connection. Â i do use a blue light though.Â 

Â 

according to that my ratio is good. Â sigh Â now i'm really confused

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-12-15T15:40:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-thyroid.5179/post-67589>

i would like to know what my adrenals are up to as well but as I am on a low dose of HC i believe that would mess with the cortisol testing i would normally do

### nuttmegs17 — 2013-01-03T20:10:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-thyroid.5179/post-69232>

Hello,

I reduced the afternoon cytomel...waited a week felt no difference (still had similar symptons etc) so upon Drs advice, took off the morning dose of Cytomel as well. Now I am just taking 1 grain of armour in the am and half grain at noon.

Its been a few weeks...how long should I wait before having labs drawn again? I'm curious what is going on, bc at night I've been extremely hot (my core especially). i am curious about my levels at this point.

How soon can I get my labs redone? (T3 would have been the only change I imagaine and as I understand it, cytomel has a shorter half life than T4 meds).

Thanks for any suggetions!

### Patty Cakes — 2013-01-03T21:46:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-thyroid.5179/post-69244>

It only has a 12 hour half life so should be out of your system within a day or two at most. Since you only eliminated the T3 I think your thyroid labs would be fairly true at this time. IMHO

### nuttmegs17 — 2013-01-04T18:10:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-thyroid.5179/post-69362>

Thanks! Thats what I'm hoping...

### Aussie Nana — 2013-01-12T23:39:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-thyroid.5179/post-70465>

If you are doing what Jack suggests - leptin reset, CT Rx, epipaleo, etc then you can expect to have changes occur.

I've just taken myself completely off my Ecothyoid (supposed to be equivalent to Armour, but I found it MUCH better). Anyway I started getting lots of symptoms of what I thought were low thyoid and wondered why when I thought I was doing everything right. Anyway someone on this forum indicated that it was possible that these symptoms were also due to too much supplemental thyroid. So I oh so cautiously stopped taking my thyroid. And my goodness, a week later and the only symptom I had was thickened skin on my heels. I hadn't got terribly cold in the late afternoon. I still had energy. Its been more than three weeks now and the skin on my heels is starting to improve, not quite back to where it was when I was on the thyroid but not cracking any more. So I think that the move to epipaleo has meant that I don't need my thyroid supplement any more. I'm aiming to get some labs drawn soon so it will be interesting to find out what they say.

Is (DBP)=Gc-MAF ??

\=========================================
http://www.jackkruse.com/the-sunshine-of-your-life/
11\. Humans have a Vitamin D savings bank in our body. It is supported by a good protein diet and a leads to a better Vitamin D level. **An Epi-paleo Rx is an optimal choice for this bank account. It works by making a protein called Vitamin D binding protein (DBP)

\=============================
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gc-MAF
Gc-MAF or Gc protein-derived macrophage activating factor is a functional change in the Gc protein (Vitamin D-binding protein) caused by serial deglycosylation. Gc-MAF is extremely potent and will at very low concentrations activate, regulate and expand macrophages. A Macrophage Activating Factor (MAF) is a lymphokine that primes macrophages to become cytotoxic to tumors. It also controls the expression of Ia antigens on the macrophage cell surface.\[1\]

Dr. Seyfried provides compelling evidence that cancer is a metabolic disease (NOT a genetic disease), and this has major implications for the treatment and prevention of cancer.

According to Otto Warburg's theory of cancer, mitochondrial dysfunction is the origin of cancer. Dr. Seyfried has amassed extensive evidence to support Warburg's theory and advances the idea that cancer arises from defects in energy metabolism (mitochondrial dysfunction), and that this metabolic dysfunction triggers genomic instability by activating oncogenes, inactivating tumor suppressor genes, etc. The author does an incredible job at convincing the reader that healthy mitochondria are the ultimate tumor suppressor.

It's clear that the strategy to treat cancer as a genetic disease is not working, but this fuels the pharmaceutical industry. Enormous amounts of money are spent on large cancer genome projects, but this has not advanced our understanding or treatment of cancer. The information from these genome projects has actually created more confusion amongst cancer researchers, and this is very clear if one reads the literature. On the other hand, when cancer is viewed as a metabolic disease the strategies to treat and prevent cancer become incredibly simplistic and economical. For example, animals studies, case reports and anecdotal evidence demonstrate that metabolic therapies that lower blood glucose and elevate ketones will shrink tumors, extend lifespan and in some cases cause complete remission. This strategy is effective because cancer cells are fueled by glucose and lack the ability to derive energy from ketones (due to mitochondrial defects). Metabolic therapies exploit the mitochondrial defects associated with cancer by targeting glucose metabolism and elevating ketones. These therapies are simple and include "therapeutic fasting", calorie restricted ketogenic diets and relatively cheap and safe drugs that target cancer-specific metabolic pathways. The information in this book is valuable to patients and supported by extensive references. This book is technical (in parts), but the author has the ability to describe complicated processes with elegant simplicity.

I read this book immediately after reading "The Secret History of the War on Cancer" and "The Emperor of All Maladies". These are excellent books, but I found Dr. Seyfried's book to be more informative and empowering because it gives the reader clear evidence for the cause of cancer and highly effective strategies to prevent and treat the disease.

I would highly recommend this book to all cancer researchers, oncologists and layman interested in understanding the origin, management and treatment of cancer as a metabolic disease. This book will also be an extremely valuable resource for patients diagnosed with cancer.
Dominic D'Agostino, PhD, University of South Florida

\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I am a board-certified medical oncologist with 30 years experience in caring for cancer patients and another 20 years of research in cancer medicine dating back to 1963. Seyfried's "Cancer as a Metabolic Disease" is the most significant book I have read in my 50 years in this field. It should be required reading of all cancer specialists, physicians in general, scientific researchers in the field of cancer and for medical students. I cannot overstate what a valuable contribution Thomas Seyfried has made in writing this masterpiece.
Stephen B. Strum, MD, FACP
Medical Oncologist, Member of ASCO (American Society of Clinical Oncology) since 1975

>
> Very interesting read.
> I was fascinated by this part:
>
>
> Experimental evidence shows that "Normal mitochondria suppress respiratory dysfunction and tumorigenicity, whereas abnormal mitochondria cannot suppress respiratory dysfunction or tumorigenicity."
>
> (a) Normal cells beget normal cells.
>
> (b) Tumor cells beget tumor cells.
>
> (c) Delivery of a tumor cell nucleus into a normal cell cytoplasm begets normal cells despite the persistence of tumor-associated genomic abnormalities.
>
> (d) Delivery of a normal cell nucleus into a tumor cell cytoplasm begets tumor cells or dead cells, but not normal cells.
>
> The results show that nuclear genomic defects alone cannot cause tumors and that normal mitochondria can suppress tumorigenesis.

All these researchers investigating genetic causes of cancer are just chasing red herrings. Profitable red herrings though they may be.
Take care of your mitochondria, people!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-24T22:06:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-report-on-ketogenic-diet-cancer.5188/post-156496>

Light is controlled by universal physical laws and biology. Every cancer on this planet has been linked to ubiquination defects. All ubiquination defects are associated with altered melatonin and sulfated Vitamin D levels. Everyone knows that lightcontrols the SCN........few realize the science that dictates light actions is QED and not biology. Therefore, you beliefs about food/biology link to cancer are not material nor accurate. Harsh.......but totally true. Most people know I support ketosis in cancer. Here is another shocker: ketosis in cancer won't work well, unless your SCN is working faster than your organ or tissue clocks. Why? the SCN has to run faster to control cell signaling. That is the point your missing. Regarding observational and non controlled studies about optical photonics and cancer. They will be USELESS, and are only a delay tactic. Why? The science that dictates this are universal laws, not biologic ones. They are not subject to a RCT or your beliefs. They are already firmly established by deep experimental proofs in physics. Don't forget all life is made up from atoms. Absence of evidence suitable to you is not really material in this arena. Moreover, it is not absence of effect when you begin to understand what a dielectric blocker does to water optically..........

LEARN from Yesterday
LIVE for today
HOPE for tomorrow
......................
ESSENTIALS FOR LIFE
Someone to love
Something to do
Something to look forward too
.......................
IF
you can imagine it

YOU
can create it

IF YOU
dream it

YOU .......CAN BECOME IT

Merry Christmas to all the Star Fish Here
and .... of course to
Dr. Kruse and his family

It has been an amazing year and a world class adventure!
x

# Thread: Does anyone here eat swordfish on a regular basis?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2012-12-17T16:04:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/post-67726>

Just curious as to whether or not anyone here eats swordfish on a regular basis as part of their Paleo/Epi-Paleo diet?

Â 

I'm planning on buying some this week, as I've always wanted to try it, and was interested in what those of you who have tried it think of it?

I'm also planning on buying some halibut, sablefish, salmon, tilapia, basa, clams, dungeness crab, lobster, prawns, snapper etc.

### Inger — 2012-12-17T16:21:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/post-67728>

Swordfish? I eat it sometimes but very seldom. It is very expensive here and not often to get. It is not my favorit fish even if good.. kinda dry? I love halibut. My favorit. I guess halibut, mackerel and salmon are the ones I maybe like the best when talking fish. Halibut, raw, with some limejuice and fresh herbs on it is delicious! I had it often in Germany. Here I get it almost.. never. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

If you can get it, wow! Absolutely mild and fatty and super tasty!

### caroline — 2012-12-17T16:46:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/post-67731>

I used to eat swordfish all the time in the Bahamas ....it was my very favorite .....but I am pretty sure it would have been caught on the day. Â It was pretty simply served Â ... Bahamian style with peas and rice. Â I have the most amazing memories of the Bahamas Â - brings tears to my eyes.Â

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-17T17:14:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/post-67740>

Swordfish is very good. Great for the grill. I don't eat big fish very often due to mercury concerns. I figure when eating a mostly seafood diet it's best to eat the big stuff sparingly. The bigger the fish , the higher the mercury content. I try to limit swordfish, tuna, striped bass to once a month.

### furface — 2012-12-17T21:26:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/post-67763>

Thanks everyone.

I just keep hearing a lot of bodybuilders eat swordfish, and have always wanted to try it. I found a place here in Vancouver, on Granville Island that sells frozen swordfish steaks @ 19.98 lb. So I may not make it a regular part of my diet, but to try it once, what the heck.

Â 

I love halibut! Living in Vancouver, we get a lot of fresh halibut since it comes from the Pacific Ocean up around northern B.C./Alaska. So deliicious.

Â 

That's awesome, Caroline. I'll bet it brings back a lot of wonderful memories of the Bahamas!

Question about tuna...does it taste ANYTHING like the canned stuff? I sure hope not! Not that I don't like the canned stuff, but I find it way too dry unless you add a whole bunch of mayonnaise. (I'm talking about eating it cooked, not raw. LOL)

Thanks.

### endless — 2012-12-17T21:45:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/post-67766>

I suggest you eat it raw....cooked it IS pretty dry. Try it in a restaurant first...seared ahi is available in lots of places in Van. Its mostly raw, and very tasty!

My idea of an ideal dinner would be pigging out on oysters at Joe Fortes!

### furface — 2012-12-17T22:19:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/post-67771>

Thanks Endless.

Not quite ready to eat fish or meat Raw. Never was a big fan of sushi. So maybe I'll hold off on the tuna for now. lol Lucky for us in B.C. that we have a wide variety of fish to choose from.

Have to get used to eating oysters. I've only had them twice...once breaded and fried, which is an Epi-Paleo no no, and the other time a few weeks ago when I had a can of smoked oysters, and IMHO they tasted like dirt. But I will give fresh, live ones another shot.

### caroline — 2012-12-18T03:25:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/post-67789>

My Gosh - you are a stubborn man! Â I am going to try one more time!! Â When we speak of raw fish ... I think you kinda have the wrong idea. Â We don't just grab a whole fish and start munching ...no, no, no!

The lomi lomi salmon that you loved \[you said so\] is basically what we are talking about. Â Have you heard of scallop ceviche? Â same thing. Steak Tartare - same thing. Â  They are more or less cooked with lemon or lime juice or balsamic vinegar Â \[something acidic\]. Â If you took a piece of your fresh halibut and squeezed on some lime juice and added green onions, herbs etc. etc. and you watched it Â .. you would see it change to more opaque - or "cooked "as they say in the trade! Â  just like your Lomi Lomi salmon. Â  Â You have so many wonderful restaurants in Vancouver so - like endless said - try some of this stuff when it has been prepared for you. Â You are such a guy!!

Â  Â  Don't make e come over there and take you to dinner!

### furface — 2012-12-18T10:00:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-here-eat-swordfish-on-a-regular-basis.5196/post-67803>

Hey, I never said never lol. But I figure if I do things on my terms, at my speed, I'm more bound to eventually actually DO them...maybe, then if I'm basically forced to do it, or if someone TELLS me to do it, which is something I've always hated, haha, and in turn may make me say "screw it, I won't do it at all then!" lol. So as I said before, if I do 80-90% of every aspect, it's better than doing 50-60% or less, or none at all.

I've always been one who HATES being told WHAT TO DO, esp if it's told almost as if given an ORDER! I think I'm doing pretty good as I am, and this is just the start. But we'll see. I may change my mind down the road, but if not, I'm cool with that.

### furface — 2012-12-18T13:15:54+0200

I might not even like it, but as I said, I've always wanted to try it, so I will, and take it from there. Hey whatever doesn't kill me can only make me stronger! Right?

Oh no, not at all. And no apologies necessary. I'm sorry if I sounded rude in any way as well.

Having a problem with being told what to do is another reason why I could never work FOR anyone, and why I'm a self-employed photographer. LOL.
